verses.kjv_text: Now these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הַבָּאִים מִצְרָיְמָה אֵת יַעֲקֹב אִישׁ וּבֵיתוֹ בָּאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'eleh shemot benei Yisra'el haba'im Mitzraymah et Ya'akov ish uveito ba'u.
verses.study_note: The opening word 'And' (ve) connects Exodus directly to Genesis — this is a continuation, not a new story. 'Shemot' (Names) is the Hebrew title of the entire book. God knows each name — even in Egypt's furnace of affliction, His people are not anonymous. The list recapitulates Genesis 46:8-27, emphasizing continuity of the covenant family. 'Every man and his household' — entire families entered Egypt together, and entire families will leave together.
verses.doctrinal_note: God preserves identity in suffering. The book that records the greatest national oppression in Israel's history opens not with statistics but with names. Each person is known to God (Isaiah 43:1).

verses.kjv_text: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah,
verses.original_text: רְאוּבֵן שִׁמְעוֹן לֵוִי וִיהוּדָה׃
verses.transliteration: Re'uven Shim'on Levi viYhudah.
verses.study_note: The sons of Leah are listed first (vv.2-4), in birth order. Reuben ("see, a son"), Simeon ("heard"), Levi ("joined"), and Judah ("praise"). These four names trace Leah's emotional journey — from longing to be seen, to being heard, to hoping for connection, to finally praising God. Judah would become the royal tribe (Gen 49:10), and Levi the priestly tribe — both born from Leah's pain, redeemed by God's purpose.

verses.kjv_text: Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin,
verses.original_text: יִשָׂשכָר זְבוּלֻן וּבִנְיָמִן׃
verses.transliteration: Yissaskhar Zevulun uVinyamin.
verses.study_note: Issachar ("reward/wages"), Zebulun ("dwelling/honor"), and Benjamin ("son of the right hand"). Benjamin was the only son born in Canaan (Gen 35:18) and the youngest. Rachel died giving birth to him. His inclusion completes the twelve — all are present, all are accounted for. God's covenant encompasses the entire family.

verses.kjv_text: Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.
verses.original_text: דָּן וְנַפְתָּלִי גָּד וְאָשֵׁר׃
verses.transliteration: Dan veNaftali Gad ve'Asher.
verses.study_note: The sons of the handmaids Bilhah and Zilpah. Dan ("he judged"), Naphtali ("my wrestling"), Gad ("fortune/troop"), Asher ("happy/blessed"). Even children of secondary wives are fully included in the covenant family. God does not discriminate by birth status — all twelve sons are named, all twelve tribes will be redeemed from Egypt.

verses.kjv_text: And all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls: for Joseph was in Egypt already.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כָּל־נֶפֶשׁ יֹצְאֵי יֶרֶךְ־יַעֲקֹב שִׁבְעִים נָפֶשׁ וְיוֹסֵף הָיָה בְמִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kol-nefesh yotze'ei yerekh-Ya'akov shiv'im nafesh veYosef hayah veMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'Seventy souls' — a number of completeness (7 × 10). The Septuagint reads 'seventy-five' (as Stephen quotes in Acts 7:14), likely including Joseph's grandsons. The Hebrew 'nefesh' (soul) emphasizes that each person is a living being known to God. 'Joseph was in Egypt already' — a single sentence bridging decades. Joseph went ahead as God's advance provision (Gen 50:20). From seventy souls will come a nation as numerous as the stars — the miracle of verses 7-12.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's covenant arithmetic: 70 souls become a multitude. What seems small in man's eyes is the seed of God's great purpose. The same principle applies to the church — from twelve apostles to every nation (Acts 1:8).

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמָת יוֹסֵף וְכָל־אֶחָיו וְכֹל הַדּוֹר הַהוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamot Yosef vekhol-echav vekhol haddor hahu.
verses.study_note: One of Scripture's most momentous summary statements. An entire generation — the patriarchal bridge between Abraham's promise and its national fulfillment — passes away in a single sentence. Joseph, the savior of Egypt and Israel, dies. His protection dies with him. This verse spans perhaps 60-80 years and sets the stage for everything that follows. Yet God's covenant does not depend on any human protector. When Joseph's generation dies, God's promise lives on.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל פָּרוּ וַיִּשְׁרְצוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ וַיַּעַצְמוּ בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד וַתִּמָּלֵא הָאָרֶץ אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yisra'el paru vayyishretzu vayyirbu vayyya'atzmu bim'od me'od vatimmale ha'aretz otam.
verses.study_note: This single verse contains FIVE verbs of increase, creating an explosive crescendo of growth. Remarkably, four of these five verbs echo Genesis 1: 'paru' (were fruitful — Gen 1:28), 'yishretzu' (swarmed/teemed — Gen 1:20), 'yirbu' (multiplied — Gen 1:28), and 'the land was filled' (Gen 1:28). Moses deliberately uses creation language — Israel's growth in Egypt is a new creation act. God is re-creating through this people. 'Bim'od me'od' (exceedingly, exceedingly) — the same superlative doubling used for Abraham's covenant promise (Gen 17:2,6). This is covenant fulfillment in action.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's promises cannot be stopped by time, death, or human opposition. The Abrahamic covenant (Gen 15:5, 22:17) is being fulfilled precisely as promised. What God speaks, He accomplishes — even across centuries of silence.

verses.kjv_text: Now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם מֶלֶךְ־חָדָשׁ עַל־מִצְרָיִם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־יָדַע אֶת־יוֹסֵף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaqom melekh-chadash al-Mitzrayim asher lo-yada et-Yosef.
verses.study_note: One of the most pivotal sentences in the Bible. 'Arose' (qum) — this king rises as an adversary. 'New' (chadash) — possibly a new dynasty entirely (perhaps the 18th Dynasty that expelled the Hyksos). 'Knew not Joseph' (lo-yada) — 'yada' implies intimate, relational knowledge, not mere information. This Pharaoh deliberately chose not to acknowledge the debt Egypt owed to Joseph and to his God. Political amnesia becomes the weapon of oppression. This pattern recurs throughout history — when a nation forgets God's past faithfulness through His people, persecution follows.
verses.doctrinal_note: The danger of willful ignorance. Pharaoh's refusal to 'know' Joseph anticipates his later refusal to 'know' Yahweh (Exodus 5:2: "Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice? I know not the LORD"). Both are acts of defiant rejection, not mere ignorance.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־עַמּוֹ הִנֵּה עַם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל רַב וְעָצוּם מִמֶּנּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer el-ammo hinneh am benei Yisra'el rav ve'atzum mimmennu.
verses.study_note: 'Behold' (hinneh) — Pharaoh draws his people's attention to a perceived threat. 'More and mightier than we' (rav ve'atzum mimmennu) — this is either hyperbole to stoke fear or a genuine admission that Israel's growth has become extraordinary. Pharaoh weaponizes Israel's blessing — the very fulfillment of God's covenant promise becomes the pretext for persecution. This is the first recorded instance of anti-Semitic propaganda: a ruler characterizing a minority as a dangerous demographic threat to justify oppression.

verses.kjv_text: Come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply, and it come to pass, that, when there falleth out any war, they join also unto our enemies, and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land.
verses.original_text: הָבָה נִתְחַכְּמָה לוֹ פֶּן־יִרְבֶּה וְהָיָה כִּי־תִקְרֶאנָה מִלְחָמָה וְנוֹסַף גַּם־הוּא עַל־שֹׂנְאֵינוּ וְנִלְחַם־בָּנוּ וְעָלָה מִן־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Havah nitchakkemah lo pen-yirbeh vehayah ki-tikre'nah milchamah venosaf gam-hu al-sone'einu venilcham-banu ve'alah min-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: 'Come, let us deal wisely' (havah nitchakkemah) — deliberate, calculated policy. The irony is devastating: Pharaoh's 'wisdom' is the opposite of true wisdom (Proverbs 9:10). His three fears reveal his insecurity: (1) they will multiply even more, (2) they will ally with enemies, (3) they will leave the land — meaning Egypt would lose its labor force. 'Get them up out of the land' — the very thing Pharaoh fears is the very thing God intends. Pharaoh's greatest nightmare is God's declared purpose. Note 'let US' — echoing the divine 'Let Us' of Genesis 1:26, but here it is a satanic counterfeit: human conspiracy against God's people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The futility of opposing God's purposes. Pharaoh fears Israel will leave Egypt — which is precisely what God has planned. Man proposes; God disposes. The counsel of the wicked cannot thwart the counsel of the Almighty (Psalm 33:10-11, Proverbs 21:30).

verses.kjv_text: Therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens. And they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשִׂימוּ עָלָיו שָׂרֵי מִסִּים לְמַעַן עַנֹּתוֹ בְּסִבְלֹתָם וַיִּבֶן עָרֵי מִסְכְּנוֹת לְפַרְעֹה אֶת־פִּתֹם וְאֶת־רַעַמְסֵס׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasimu alav sarei missim lema'an annoto besivlotam vayyiven arei miskenot leFar'oh et-Pitom ve'et-Ra'amses.
verses.study_note: 'Taskmasters' (sarei missim) — literally 'princes of forced labor,' official overseers of a state-run slavery system. 'To afflict them' (annoto) — the key verb of the chapter, the root of 'anah' (to oppress). This same word appears in God's prophecy to Abraham: 'they shall afflict them four hundred years' (Gen 15:13). Pharaoh unknowingly fulfills prophecy. 'Pithom' (Egyptian: Per-Atum, 'house of the god Atum') and 'Raamses' (Per-Ramesses, 'house of Ramesses') — storage cities in the eastern Delta. Archaeological evidence confirms massive construction projects using slave labor in this region.
verses.doctrinal_note: Affliction as prophetic fulfillment. God told Abraham in Genesis 15:13 that his descendants would be afflicted in a land not their own. Pharaoh's cruelty, while evil, operates within God's sovereign foreknowledge. God does not cause evil but incorporates it into His redemptive plan.

verses.kjv_text: But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew. And they were grieved because of the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְכַאֲשֶׁר יְעַנּוּ אֹתוֹ כֵּן יִרְבֶּה וְכֵן יִפְרֹץ וַיָּקֻצוּ מִפְּנֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vekha'asher ye'annu oto ken yirbeh vekhen yifrotz vayyakutzu mippenei benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The central theological statement of the chapter. A perfect divine paradox: oppression produces the opposite of its intention. 'The more... the more' — a proportional relationship. Pharaoh's policy backfires completely. 'Yifrotz' (burst forth/spread out) is a powerful verb — like water breaking through a dam. The same root describes Jacob's prosperity (Gen 30:43) and is related to 'Perez' (breach), in the line of Judah leading to Christ. 'Grieved' (yakutzu) — literally 'they felt dread/loathing.' Egypt's dread of Israel mirrors Israel's future dread-inspiring God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle of divine reversal: human persecution cannot diminish what God has blessed. This pattern repeats throughout biblical history — the church grows under persecution (Acts 8:1-4). The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church.

verses.kjv_text: And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour:
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲבִדוּ מִצְרַיִם אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּפָרֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'avidu Mitzrayim et-benei Yisra'el beparekh.
verses.study_note: 'Rigour' (parekh) — ruthlessness, harshness, crushing severity. This rare word appears only here and in v.14 and Leviticus 25:43,46,53. Later, God prohibits Israel from treating others with the same 'parekh' — they must remember how it felt. The verb 'serve' (avad) is the root of 'eved' (slave/servant). Tragically, the same word used of Adam's noble work in Eden (Gen 2:15) now describes degrading forced labor. Sin has corrupted work from calling into curse.

verses.kjv_text: And they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field: all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour.
verses.original_text: וַיְמָרְרוּ אֶת־חַיֵּיהֶם בַּעֲבֹדָה קָשָׁה בְּחֹמֶר וּבִלְבֵנִים וּבְכָל־עֲבֹדָה בַּשָּׂדֶה אֵת כָּל־עֲבֹדָתָם אֲשֶׁר־עָבְדוּ בָהֶם בְּפָרֶךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayemareru et-chayyeihem ba'avodah kashah bechomer uvilvenim uvekhol-avodah bassadeh et kol-avodatam asher-avdu vahem beparekh.
verses.study_note: 'Made bitter' (mareru) — the root of 'maror' (bitter herbs) that Israel will eat every Passover to remember this suffering (Exodus 12:8). The bitterness of Egypt is to be tasted annually, never forgotten. The verse lists four dimensions of suffering: (1) mortar, (2) bricks, (3) all field labor, (4) every kind of service. 'Avodah' (service/labor) appears FOUR times in this single verse, hammering the reality of enslavement. 'Parekh' (rigour) closes the section as a bookend with v.13. The catalogue is comprehensive — no aspect of life was free from crushing labor.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Passover maror (bitter herbs) is rooted in this verse. God commands Israel to taste their ancestors' bitterness each year — memory is a guard against complacency and a foundation for gratitude. Those who forget the bitterness of bondage will not appreciate the sweetness of redemption.

verses.kjv_text: And the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם לַמְיַלְּדֹת הָעִבְרִיֹּת אֲשֶׁר שֵׁם הָאַחַת שִׁפְרָה וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית פּוּעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer melekh Mitzrayim lamyalledot ha'Ivriyyot asher shem ha'achat Shifrah veshem hashenit Pu'ah.
verses.study_note: When forced labor fails to reduce Israel's population, Pharaoh escalates to genocide — beginning covertly, through the midwives. Stunning contrast: the mighty king of Egypt is unnamed, while two humble midwives are immortalized by name — Shiphrah ("beautiful/fair") and Puah ("splendid/crying out"). God honors the faithful, not the powerful. 'Hebrew' (Ivriyyot) — from 'ever' (to cross over), related to 'Eber' (Gen 10:21). The Hebrews are the people who 'crossed over' — immigrants, outsiders, sojourners. Whether the midwives were Hebrew women or midwives to the Hebrews is debated, but their faith is clear.
verses.doctrinal_note: God records the names of the faithful while leaving the oppressor unnamed. Pharaoh's name is lost to this text, but Shiphrah and Puah are remembered for eternity. In God's economy, faithfulness matters more than power (cf. Matthew 25:21).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women, and see them upon the stools; if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בְּיַלֶּדְכֶן אֶת־הָעִבְרִיּוֹת וּרְאִיתֶן עַל־הָאָבְנָיִם אִם־בֵּן הוּא וַהֲמִתֶּן אֹתוֹ וְאִם־בַּת הִיא וָחָיָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer beyalledkhen et-ha'Ivriyyot ur'iten al-ha'ovnayim im-ben hu vahammitten oto ve'im-bat hi vachayah.
verses.study_note: 'Stools' (ovnayim) — literally 'two stones,' a birthing stool. Pharaoh commands murder at the most vulnerable moment — during delivery itself. 'If a son... kill him' — targeting males specifically to destroy Israel's military capacity, lineage, and identity. 'If a daughter... she shall live' — daughters were spared presumably for assimilation into Egyptian society through intermarriage. This is calculated ethnic destruction: eliminate the men, absorb the women. Satan's strategy is always to destroy the covenant seed line from which the Messiah will come (cf. Gen 3:15). Every genocide in Scripture targets the Messianic line.

verses.kjv_text: But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive.
verses.original_text: וַתִּירֶאןָ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים וְלֹא עָשׂוּ כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֲלֵיהֶן מֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם וַתְּחַיֶּיןָ אֶת־הַיְלָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattirenah hamyalledot et-ha'Elohim velo asu ka'asher dibber aleihen melekh Mitzrayim vattechhayyenah et-hayladim.
verses.study_note: The theological climax of the chapter. 'Feared God' (tirenah et-ha'Elohim) — the 'fear of God' appears here for the first time in Exodus and stands in direct contrast to Pharaoh's godless fearfulness. The midwives feared a higher Authority than the most powerful ruler on earth. 'Did not as the king commanded' — deliberate, conscious civil disobedience grounded in the fear of God. 'Saved alive' (techhayyenah) — they actively preserved life, not merely failing to kill. This is one of Scripture's great models of righteous resistance to ungodly authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fear of God trumps the fear of man. When human law demands what God forbids, believers must obey God rather than men (Acts 5:29). Shiphrah and Puah are Scripture's first example of civil disobedience — and God rewards them for it (v.20-21). Their example establishes a biblical principle: obedience to God may require disobedience to human authority when that authority commands sin.

verses.kjv_text: And the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא מֶלֶךְ־מִצְרַיִם לַמְיַלְּדֹת וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶן מַדּוּעַ עֲשִׂיתֶן הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וַתְּחַיֶּיןָ אֶת־הַיְלָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra melekh-Mitzrayim lamyalledot vayyomer lahen maddua asitten haddavar hazzeh vattechhayyenah et-hayladim.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh summons the midwives to account. The fact that he confronts rather than simply executes them suggests they had some standing or that he needed their cooperation. 'Why have ye done this thing?' — the question assumes the answer is obvious. Pharaoh expected absolute obedience. The midwives now face the most powerful man on earth, knowing their defiance has been discovered. Their response will require both courage and wisdom.

verses.kjv_text: And the midwives said unto Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמַרְןָ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶל־פַּרְעֹה כִּי לֹא כַנָּשִׁים הַמִּצְרִיֹּת הָעִבְרִיֹּת כִּי־חָיוֹת הֵנָּה בְּטֶרֶם תָּבוֹא אֲלֵהֶן הַמְיַלֶּדֶת וְיָלָדוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomarnah hamyalledot el-Par'oh ki lo kannashim hamMitzriyyot ha'Ivriyyot ki-chayyot hennah beterem tavo aleihen hamyalledet veyaladu.
verses.study_note: 'Lively' (chayyot) — literally 'full of life, vigorous,' related to 'chayyah' (living, animal-like vitality). The midwives present the Hebrew women as exceptionally strong and fast in delivery. Whether this is a complete account or a strategic response is debated by scholars. Rashi and other rabbis noted the wordplay: 'chayyot' sounds like 'chayot' (animals/beasts) — the midwives may be subtly mocking Pharaoh's own dehumanization of the Hebrews. If he treats them like animals, well, they give birth like animals — quickly, without need of assistance. There is likely some truth in it: oppressed populations can indeed develop remarkable physical resilience.

verses.kjv_text: Therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּיטֶב אֱלֹהִים לַמְיַלְּדֹת וַיִּרֶב הָעָם וַיַּעַצְמוּ מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetev Elohim lamyalledot vayyirev ha'am vayyya'atzmu me'od.
verses.study_note: 'God dealt well' (yetev) — God actively rewarded the midwives. Their fear of God produced blessing, not punishment. The parallel structure is significant: (1) God blessed the midwives, and (2) the people continued to multiply. Both results flow from the midwives' obedience. Israel's growth continues unabated — Pharaoh's second strategy has failed completely, just like the first.
verses.doctrinal_note: God rewards faithful obedience, especially when it is costly. The midwives risked their lives and God honored them. The principle is consistent: "them that honour me I will honour" (1 Samuel 2:30).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי־יָרְאוּ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם בָּתִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki-yar'u hamyalledot et-ha'Elohim vayya'as lahem battim.
verses.study_note: 'Made them houses' (battim) — the specific nature of God's reward. 'Houses' likely means families/dynasties — God gave them their own enduring family lines. This is profoundly fitting: the women who preserved other women's sons were given sons (and descendants) of their own. The causal connection is explicit: 'BECAUSE the midwives feared God.' The fear of God that risked everything received everything in return. Some scholars connect this to the later "houses" of Israel — Shiphrah and Puah became mothers within the nation they saved.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fear of God is the foundation of every blessing. Those who feared God received 'houses' (families, legacy). Those who did not fear God (Pharaoh) would see his own household destroyed. The contrast between the midwives' 'houses' and Pharaoh's doomed house runs through the entire Exodus narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive.
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו פַּרְעֹה לְכָל־עַמּוֹ לֵאמֹר כָּל־הַבֵּן הַיִּלּוֹד הַיְאֹרָה תַּשְׁלִיכֻהוּ וְכָל־הַבַּת תְּחַיּוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav Par'oh lekhol-ammo lemor kol-habben hayyillod haYe'orah tashlichuhu vekhol-habbat techhayyun.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's third and most radical escalation. Having failed with forced labor (vv.11-14) and covert genocide through the midwives (vv.15-21), he now commands open, public infanticide. Key escalations: (1) 'All his people' — not just midwives but the entire Egyptian populace becomes complicit. (2) 'The river' (Ye'orah — the Nile) — Egypt's sacred, life-giving river becomes an instrument of death. The Nile that sustains Egypt will now swallow Israel's sons. The supreme irony: the very river Pharaoh uses for genocide will become the river that preserves the deliverer. Moses will be drawn FROM the Nile (Exodus 2:5-6), and the Nile will later be turned to blood in judgment (Exodus 7:20). This verse also establishes a typological parallel with Herod's massacre of the innocents in Matthew 2:16 — both are Satan's attempts to destroy the deliverer before he can act.
verses.doctrinal_note: The escalation of evil: when one level of oppression fails, the oppressor intensifies. But God is always one step ahead. The very instrument of destruction (the Nile) becomes the instrument of salvation (Moses's ark). God turns the enemy's weapons against him. This typological pattern culminates at the cross — Satan's greatest weapon (death) became God's instrument of eternal redemption.

meta.overview: Exodus 1 bridges the 400 years between Genesis and the birth of Moses. It opens with the names of Jacob's sons who came to Egypt — connecting directly to Genesis 46. The chapter traces how Israel's extraordinary growth fulfilled God's covenant promise to Abraham (Gen 15:5), and how a new Pharaoh's fear led to systematic oppression, forced labor, and finally genocide. Yet God's sovereign purpose cannot be thwarted: the more Israel is afflicted, the more they multiply. The chapter climaxes with the heroic faith of two midwives — Shiphrah and Puah — whose fear of God overrides their fear of Pharaoh, establishing a pattern of faithful resistance that will define the entire Exodus narrative.
meta.theme: Oppression & the Indestructible Covenant
meta.keyWordMeaning: To afflict, oppress, humble — the cry of suffering that ascends to God and triggers divine intervention (cf. Exodus 3:7)
outline: The Sons of Israel in Egypt (vv.1-7): Seventy souls multiply into a nation — covenant fulfillment
The New Pharaoh's Fear (vv.8-10): A king who did not know Joseph — political amnesia and paranoia
Forced Labor (vv.11-14): Taskmasters, store cities, bitter bondage — but Israel keeps growing
The Hebrew Midwives (vv.15-21): Shiphrah and Puah fear God, not Pharaoh — civil disobedience by faith
The Decree of Death (v.22): Every son cast into the Nile — setting the stage for Moses

verses.kjv_text: And there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ אִישׁ מִבֵּית לֵוִי וַיִּקַּח אֶת־בַּת־לֵוִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyelekh ish mibbeit Levi vayyiqqach et-bat-Levi.
verses.study_note: Neither parent is named here — they are simply "a man" and "a daughter" of Levi. Only later do we learn they are Amram and Jochebed (Exodus 6:20). The deliberate anonymity at this point emphasizes that God's deliverance begins not with great names but with ordinary, faithful people. "House of Levi" — the priestly tribe. The deliverer will come from the priestly line. Levi means "joined/attached" — this tribe will be joined to God's service. The marriage itself is an act of faith: marrying and bearing children under Pharaoh's death decree requires trust that God's promise of descendants is stronger than Pharaoh's decree of death.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's deliverers often come from obscure beginnings. An unnamed couple from a slave tribe, marrying under a genocide decree — this is how God launches the greatest redemption story of the Old Testament. The pattern repeats in Bethlehem (Luke 2:4-7).

verses.kjv_text: And the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months.
verses.original_text: וַתַּהַר הָאִשָּׁה וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתֵּרֶא אֹתוֹ כִּי־טוֹב הוּא וַתִּצְפְּנֵהוּ שְׁלֹשָׁה יְרָחִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattahar ha'ishah vateled ben vattere oto ki-tov hu vatitztpenehu sheloshah yerachim.
verses.study_note: 'She saw him that he was good' (ki-tov hu) — the exact phrase God uses in Genesis 1 when evaluating creation ("and God saw that it was good"). Moses deliberately echoes creation language: this child is a new creative act of God, a "very good" life emerging from chaos and death. Stephen says Moses was "exceeding fair" (literally "beautiful to God," Acts 7:20). Hebrews 11:23 says his parents acted "by faith," seeing he was "a proper child." They discerned something divinely appointed in this infant. 'Hid him three months' (sheloshah yerachim) — three months of secret defiance against the king's decree. Every day was a risk. This is quiet, sustained, dangerous faith — not a single dramatic act but daily courage.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faith sees what the world cannot. Jochebed saw not a condemned infant but a "good" child — using creation language. Parents of faith look at their children through God's eyes, not through the lens of cultural hostility or political oppression.

verses.kjv_text: And when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch, and put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river’s brink.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־יָכְלָה עוֹד הַצְּפִינוֹ וַתִּקַּח־לוֹ תֵּבַת גֹּמֶא וַתַּחְמְרָה בַחֵמָר וּבַזָּפֶת וַתָּשֶׂם בָּהּ אֶת־הַיֶּלֶד וַתָּשֶׂם בְּסוּף עַל־שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-yakhelah od hatztpino vattiqqach-lo tevat gome vattachmerah vachemer uvazzafet vattasem bah et-hayyeled vattasem besuf al-sefat haYe'or.
verses.study_note: The word 'ark' (tevah) appears only twice in the entire Old Testament — here and in the Noah narrative (Genesis 6-9). This is not coincidence but deliberate theological typology. Just as Noah's ark preserved life through the waters of judgment, Moses's ark preserves life through the waters of Pharaoh's decree. 'Bulrushes' (gome) — papyrus, the material of Egypt itself. 'Daubed with slime and pitch' — the same waterproofing technique used on Noah's ark (Gen 6:14). Jochebed technically obeys Pharaoh's command: she puts her son in the Nile. But she does so in a vessel of faith. She trusts the God who saved Noah through water to save her son through water. 'The flags' (suf) — reeds at the river's edge. The same word appears in "Yam Suf" (Sea of Reeds/Red Sea) — the place where God will later deliver the entire nation.
verses.doctrinal_note: Providence uses the enemy's own weapons. The Nile, instrument of genocide (1:22), becomes the instrument of salvation. The same water that killed Hebrew sons will carry the deliverer to safety. This divine irony is a hallmark of God's redemptive strategy — the cross itself is the supreme example (Colossians 2:15).

verses.kjv_text: And his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּתַצַּב אֲחֹתוֹ מֵרָחֹק לְדֵעָה מַה־יֵּעָשֶׂה לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattetatzav achoto merachok lede'ah mah-yye'aseh lo.
verses.study_note: 'His sister' — Miriam (Exodus 15:20, Numbers 26:59), though unnamed here like her parents. She would have been perhaps 7-12 years old. 'Stood afar off' (tetatzav merachok) — positioned herself at a watchful distance. This is not passive observation but strategic vigilance. The verb 'tetatzav' (stationed herself) implies intentionality — she was posted there, likely by her mother. 'To know what would be done to him' — the Hebrew 'lede'ah' (to know) carries the weight of discovering God's purpose. Miriam watches not merely to see what happens but to discern what God is doing. She will later become a prophetess (Exodus 15:20) — and here already she watches for the hand of God.

verses.kjv_text: And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river; and her maidens walked along by the river’s side; and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּרֶד בַּת־פַּרְעֹה לִרְחֹץ עַל־הַיְאֹר וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ הֹלְכֹת עַל־יַד הַיְאֹר וַתֵּרֶא אֶת־הַתֵּבָה בְּתוֹךְ הַסּוּף וַתִּשְׁלַח אֶת־אֲמָתָהּ וַתִּקָּחֶהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattered bat-Par'oh lirchotz al-haYe'or vena'aroteha holkhot al-yad haYe'or vattere et-hattevah betokh hassuf vattishlach et-amatah vattiqqacheha.
verses.study_note: 'The daughter of Pharaoh came down' — she descends to the Nile while Israel's deliverer floats upon it. Jewish tradition identifies her as Thermuthis or Bithiah (1 Chronicles 4:18, where 'Bithiah' means 'daughter of Yahweh'). The divine orchestration is breathtaking: the precise timing of the princess's bathing, the location, the visibility of the ark among the reeds, the princess's curiosity — every detail converges by providence. 'Sent her maid' (amatah) — this can also mean 'her arm' (stretched out her hand), a beautiful image of Pharaoh's own daughter reaching to save what Pharaoh commanded to destroy.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's providence operates through "coincidences" that are nothing of the sort. A princess decides to bathe at a particular spot at a particular time — and the entire course of redemptive history turns on it. God's sovereign plan works through human choices without violating them.

verses.kjv_text: And when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept. And she had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews’ children.
verses.original_text: וַתִּפְתַּח וַתִּרְאֵהוּ אֶת־הַיֶּלֶד וְהִנֵּה־נַעַר בֹּכֶה וַתַּחְמֹל עָלָיו וַתֹּאמֶר מִיַּלְדֵי הָעִבְרִים זֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vattiftach vattir'ehu et-hayyeled vehineh-na'ar bokheh vattachmol alav vattomer miyyaldei ha'Ivrim zeh.
verses.study_note: 'She saw the child: and behold, the babe wept' — the text shifts from 'yeled' (child) to 'na'ar' (boy/youth), perhaps emphasizing his full humanity to the princess. 'Behold' (hinneh) — an exclamation of surprise and emotion. The baby's cry pierces through Pharaoh's decree. 'She had compassion' (tachmol) — a deep gut-level mercy. The Hebrew word implies tender protectiveness. Pharaoh's own daughter feels the very compassion he has outlawed. 'This is one of the Hebrews' children' — she knows exactly what she is looking at. She knows the decree. She knows this child should die. And she chooses mercy over her father's law. This unnamed princess joins Shiphrah and Puah in the growing chain of women who defy Pharaoh — God uses female courage to unravel male tyranny.

verses.kjv_text: Then said his sister to Pharaoh’s daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר אֲחֹתוֹ אֶל־בַּת־פַּרְעֹה הַאֵלֵךְ וְקָרָאתִי לָךְ אִשָּׁה מֵינֶקֶת מִן־הָעִבְרִיֹּת וְתֵינִק לָךְ אֶת־הַיָּלֶד׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer achoto el-bat-Par'oh ha'elekh veqarati lakh ishah meineket min-ha'Ivriyyot veteiniq lakh et-hayyaled.
verses.study_note: Miriam's quick thinking transforms the situation. Her question is a masterpiece of wisdom — she offers a practical solution that serves the princess's needs while reuniting mother and child. 'Shall I go?' — bold initiative from a young slave girl addressing royalty. She does not hesitate. 'A nurse of the Hebrew women' — she specifies a Hebrew nurse, naturalizing the idea that a Hebrew woman should care for a Hebrew child. The princess could have used an Egyptian nurse; Miriam's suggestion ensures Moses will be raised knowing his identity. This young girl's courage and wisdom alter the course of history.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh’s daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child’s mother.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר־לָהּ בַּת־פַּרְעֹה לֵכִי וַתֵּלֶךְ הָעַלְמָה וַתִּקְרָא אֶת־אֵם הַיָּלֶד׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer-lah bat-Par'oh lekhi vattelekh ha'almah vatiqra et-em hayyaled.
verses.study_note: 'Go' (lekhi) — a single word of royal authorization. 'The maid' (ha'almah) — Miriam is called 'almah' (young woman/maiden), the same word used prophetically in Isaiah 7:14 for the virgin who will bear Immanuel. 'Called the child's mother' — the narrator reveals what the princess does not yet know: this is no random Hebrew nurse but the child's own mother. The irony is exquisite: Pharaoh commands death; his daughter commands life. Pharaoh tears families apart; God's providence reunites them. Jochebed will now nurse her own son legally, under royal protection, and be paid for it (v.9).

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh’s daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ בַּת־פַּרְעֹה הֵילִיכִי אֶת־הַיֶּלֶד הַזֶּה וְהֵינִקִהוּ לִי וַאֲנִי אֶתֵּן אֶת־שְׂכָרֵךְ וַתִּקַּח הָאִשָּׁה הַיֶּלֶד וַתְּנִיקֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomer lah bat-Par'oh heilikhi et-hayyeled hazzeh veheiniqihu li va'ani etten et-sekharekh vattiqqach ha'ishah hayyeled vattenikehu.
verses.study_note: The supreme divine irony of the chapter. 'Take this child... nurse it for me... I will give thee thy wages.' Pharaoh's daughter pays Moses's own mother to nurse Moses, using Pharaoh's own treasury. The king who decreed death for Hebrew sons now unknowingly funds the upbringing of Israel's deliverer. Jochebed is paid from the royal treasury to raise the child who will destroy Pharaoh's empire. During these nursing years (likely 3-5 years), Jochebed would have taught Moses his Hebrew identity, the stories of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and the covenant promises of God. These formative years shaped Moses's core identity even before his Egyptian education.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's economy inverts the world's: the oppressor funds his own undoing. Pharaoh's gold pays for the deliverer's upbringing. This pattern recurs — Haman builds the gallows on which he himself hangs (Esther 7:10). The cross itself was Rome's instrument of control, turned into God's instrument of eternal liberation.

verses.kjv_text: And the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh’s daughter, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses: and she said, Because I drew him out of the water.
verses.original_text: וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד וַתְּבִאֵהוּ לְבַת־פַּרְעֹה וַיְהִי־לָהּ לְבֵן וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ מֹשֶׁה וַתֹּאמֶר כִּי מִן־הַמַּיִם מְשִׁיתִהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyigdal hayyeled vattevi'ehu levat-Par'oh vayehi-lah leven vatiqra shemo Mosheh vattomer ki min-hammayim meshitihu.
verses.study_note: 'Moses' (Mosheh) — the princess gives an Egyptian-Hebrew hybrid name. In Egyptian, 'mose/mes' means 'born of' (as in Thut-mose, Ra-meses). But the narrator provides a Hebrew etymology: 'meshitihu' — 'I drew him out' (from 'mashah,' to draw out). The name is prophetic in both directions: (1) Moses was drawn out of water, and (2) Moses will draw Israel out of Egypt's waters at the Red Sea. 'He became her son' — Moses receives the full legal status of Egyptian royalty. Acts 7:22 says he was 'learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians.' For 40 years, God trains the future deliverer in the most advanced civilization on earth — military strategy, administration, writing, law. Nothing in Moses's formation is wasted.
verses.doctrinal_note: Moses's name carries his calling: the one "drawn out" will "draw out" God's people. God often encodes destiny in names. This dual identity — Hebrew by birth, Egyptian by education — uniquely qualifies Moses for his future role as mediator between God and Pharaoh.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in those days, when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens: and he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew, one of his brethren.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּיָּמִים הָהֵם וַיִּגְדַּל מֹשֶׁה וַיֵּצֵא אֶל־אֶחָיו וַיַּרְא בְּסִבְלֹתָם וַיַּרְא אִישׁ מִצְרִי מַכֶּה אִישׁ־עִבְרִי מֵאֶחָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bayyamim hahem vayyigdal Mosheh vayyetze el-echav vayyar besivlotam vayyar ish Mitzri makkeh ish-Ivri me'echav.
verses.study_note: 'In those days' — Acts 7:23 specifies Moses was 40 years old. 'Went out unto his brethren' — this is a pivotal act of identification. Moses leaves the palace to see the slaves. He could have remained in Egyptian privilege indefinitely. 'Looked on their burdens' (vayyar besivlotam) — 'ra'ah' (to see) here means more than observation; it is the same word used when God "looked upon" Israel's affliction (Ex 2:25, 3:7). Moses sees with eyes of compassion, not curiosity. 'One of his brethren' (me'echav) — repeated twice. Moses recognizes the Hebrews as 'his brethren' — his people, his family. Hebrews 11:24-26 interprets this moment as a faith decision: Moses chose 'rather to suffer affliction with the people of God.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Identification with the suffering is the first step toward deliverance. Moses's choice to leave the palace and "go out to his brethren" mirrors Christ leaving heaven's glory to identify with suffering humanity (Philippians 2:5-8). The deliverer must first share in the affliction of those he will deliver.

verses.kjv_text: And he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפֶן כֹּה וָכֹה וַיַּרְא כִּי אֵין אִישׁ וַיַּךְ אֶת־הַמִּצְרִי וַיִּטְמְנֵהוּ בַּחוֹל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifen koh vakhoh vayyar ki ein ish vayyakh et-haMitzri vayyitmeNehu bachol.
verses.study_note: 'Looked this way and that' — Moses looks for human witnesses but fails to look up to God. This is the critical flaw: right motive, wrong method. His compassion is genuine; his method is carnal. 'He slew the Egyptian' — Moses attempts deliverance through human power, 40 years before God's appointed time. Acts 7:25 says "he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them" — Moses sensed his calling but acted in the flesh rather than waiting for God's timing. 'Hid him in the sand' — what is hidden from men is not hidden from God. The buried Egyptian will be unearthed by the next verse. Sin always comes to light.
verses.doctrinal_note: Deliverance by human strength always fails. Moses's killing of the Egyptian is a type of all human attempts to accomplish God's purposes through fleshly means. God's deliverer must first be broken of self-reliance (the next 40 years in Midian) before God can use him. The contrast: Moses at 40 says "I can." Moses at 80 says "I cannot" (Ex 3:11). Only then does God say "I will" (Ex 3:12).

verses.kjv_text: And when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא בַּיּוֹם הַשֵּׁנִי וְהִנֵּה שְׁנֵי־אֲנָשִׁים עִבְרִים נִצִּים וַיֹּאמֶר לָרָשָׁע לָמָּה תַכֶּה רֵעֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze bayyom hasheni vehineh shenei-anashim Ivrim nitztzim vayyomer larasha lammah takkeh re'ekha.
verses.study_note: 'The second day' — Moses returns, perhaps expecting gratitude. Instead he finds Hebrews fighting each other. 'Two men strove together' — the very people Moses is trying to save are divided among themselves. 'He said to him that did the wrong' (larasha — to the wicked one) — Moses attempts to act as judge and mediator, a role God has not yet given him. His question 'Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?' is righteous, but his authority to ask it has not yet been established. Moses is trying to function as deliverer and judge before God has commissioned him.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us? intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian? And Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מִי שָׂמְךָ לְאִישׁ שַׂר וְשֹׁפֵט עָלֵינוּ הַלְהָרְגֵנִי אַתָּה אֹמֵר כַּאֲשֶׁר הָרַגְתָּ אֶת־הַמִּצְרִי וַיִּירָא מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמַר אָכֵן נוֹדַע הַדָּבָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer mi samekha le'ish sar veshofet aleinu halehorgeni attah omer ka'asher haragta et-haMitzri vayyira Mosheh vayyomar akhen noda haddavar.
verses.study_note: 'Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?' — the most cutting rejection Moses could face. The very people he sacrificed everything for reject his leadership. Stephen quotes this verse in Acts 7:27-28,35, then reveals its profound irony: the one they rejected as ruler and judge is the very one God sent as ruler and deliverer. This is the pattern of the rejected deliverer — repeated in Joseph (sold by his brothers), David (overlooked by his family), and supremely in Christ (John 1:11). 'Moses feared' — the man who had been fearless enough to kill an Egyptian now trembles at exposure. Human courage without divine commission is ultimately fragile. 'Surely this thing is known' — the hidden deed is now public. Moses is exposed, vulnerable, and about to become a fugitive.
verses.doctrinal_note: The rejected deliverer: Israel rejects Moses here, just as they will repeatedly reject him in the wilderness, just as they will reject the prophets, and ultimately reject Christ. Stephen's point in Acts 7 is devastating: "This Moses whom they refused... the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer" (Acts 7:35). God's chosen instruments are often initially rejected by those they come to save.

verses.kjv_text: Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע פַּרְעֹה אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וַיְבַקֵּשׁ לַהֲרֹג אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וַיִּבְרַח מֹשֶׁה מִפְּנֵי פַרְעֹה וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֶרֶץ־מִדְיָן וַיֵּשֶׁב עַל־הַבְּאֵר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Par'oh et-haddavar hazzeh vayevaqesh laharog et-Mosheh vayyivrach Mosheh mippenei Far'oh vayyeshev be'eretz-Midyan vayyeshev al-habbe'er.
verses.study_note: 'Sought to slay Moses' — the deliverer is now hunted by the very power he meant to challenge. 'Fled from the face of Pharaoh' — from palace prince to fugitive in a single day. 'Midian' — east of the Gulf of Aqaba, in the Arabian peninsula. The Midianites were descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Gen 25:2). Moses goes from the most advanced civilization to the desert wilderness. 'Sat down by a well' — wells are covenantal meeting places in Genesis. Abraham's servant found Rebekah at a well (Gen 24:11); Jacob met Rachel at a well (Gen 29:2). The pattern suggests that Moses, like the patriarchs before him, will meet his wife at this well. What appears to be the end of Moses's story is actually the beginning of God's preparation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's seminary is often the wilderness. Moses spends 40 years in Midian — the same duration as Israel's later wandering. The palace trained Moses's mind; the desert trains his character. God's preparation often looks like demotion, delay, and desolation.

verses.kjv_text: Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father’s flock.
verses.original_text: וּלְכֹהֵן מִדְיָן שֶׁבַע בָּנוֹת וַתָּבֹאנָה וַתִּדְלֶנָה וַתְּמַלֶּאנָה אֶת־הָרְהָטִים לְהַשְׁקוֹת צֹאן אֲבִיהֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Ulekhoken Midyan sheva banot vattavenah vattidlenah vattemalleanah et-harhatim lehashqot tzon avihen.
verses.study_note: 'Priest of Midian' — Reuel/Jethro (v.18, 3:1). As a descendant of Abraham through Keturah, he may have preserved some knowledge of the true God. 'Seven daughters' — seven being the number of completeness. They perform shepherd duties, drawing water for their father's flock. This scene echoes Genesis 29:9 where Rachel comes to the well with her father's sheep. The well-encounter pattern is now firmly established: the hero arrives at a well in a foreign land, meets women drawing water, and a marriage follows. God is writing Moses's story with the same pen He used for the patriarchs.

verses.kjv_text: And the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ הָרֹעִים וַיְגָרְשׁוּם וַיָּקָם מֹשֶׁה וַיּוֹשִׁעָן וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת־צֹאנָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u haro'im vayegarshu vayyaqom Mosheh vayyoshi'an vayyashq et-tzonam.
verses.study_note: 'The shepherds came and drove them away' — bullying and injustice, even at a desert well. Moses cannot stand by while the vulnerable are oppressed — this is the same impulse that led him to kill the Egyptian (v.12), but now expressed differently. 'Moses stood up and helped them' — 'vayyoshi'an' is from 'yasha' (to save/deliver). This is the first time the root of "salvation" is applied to Moses. He becomes a savior in miniature — rescuing seven women at a well before rescuing a nation from Egypt. 'Watered their flock' — the prince of Egypt now does a servant's work. Moses the deliverer is being reshaped into Moses the shepherd. From this humble work, God will call him to shepherd Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?
verses.original_text: וַתָּבֹאנָה אֶל־רְעוּאֵל אֲבִיהֶן וַיֹּאמֶר מַדּוּעַ מִהַרְתֶּן בֹּא הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vattavenah el-Re'u'el avihen vayyomer maddua mihartten bo hayyom.
verses.study_note: 'Reuel' — meaning 'friend of God' or 'shepherd of God.' He is also called Jethro (Exodus 3:1, 18:1) — possibly a title meaning 'his excellency.' The daughters' early return catches his attention. Their father's question reveals that the bullying shepherds were a regular occurrence — the daughters normally returned late because of harassment. Moses's intervention broke the pattern. Reuel's name as "friend of God" is significant: Moses finds refuge with a man whose name describes his own future relationship with God (Exodus 33:11 — God spoke to Moses "as a man speaketh unto his friend").

verses.kjv_text: And they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock.
verses.original_text: וַתֹּאמַרְןָ אִישׁ מִצְרִי הִצִּילָנוּ מִיַּד הָרֹעִים וְגַם־דָּלֹה דָלָה לָנוּ וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת־הַצֹּאן׃
verses.transliteration: Vattomarnah ish Mitzri hittzilanu miyyad haro'im vegam-daloh dalah lanu vayyashq et-hatzon.
verses.study_note: 'An Egyptian' — the daughters identify Moses by his appearance and dress. After 40 years in Pharaoh's court, Moses looked Egyptian. The irony is layered: the Hebrew fugitive appears Egyptian; the future deliverer of Israel is mistaken for a citizen of the oppressor nation. 'Delivered us' (hittzilanu) — from 'natsal' (to rescue, snatch away). Another salvation word applied to Moses. 'Drew water' (daloh dalah) — emphatic doubling meaning 'he drew and drew abundantly.' Moses serves generously. He who was 'drawn out' (mashoh) of the water now 'draws out' (daloh) water for others — his name becoming his pattern of life.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־בְּנֹתָיו וְאַיּוֹ לָמָּה זֶּה עֲזַבְתֶּן אֶת־הָאִישׁ קִרְאֶן לוֹ וְיֹאכַל לָחֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer el-benotav ve'ayyo lammah zeh azavten et-ha'ish qir'en lo veyokhal lachem.
verses.study_note: 'Where is he? Why have ye left the man?' — Reuel is surprised and perhaps rebukes his daughters for failing to show basic hospitality. 'Call him, that he may eat bread' — sharing bread is the first step of covenant hospitality in the ancient Near East. Reuel extends the invitation that will lead to Moses's 40-year sojourn in Midian. 'Eat bread' (yokhal lachem) — bread in Hebrew covers all food. This meal begins a relationship that will profoundly shape Moses. Reuel/Jethro will later give Moses essential wisdom for leading Israel (Exodus 18:13-27).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹאֶל מֹשֶׁה לָשֶׁבֶת אֶת־הָאִישׁ וַיִּתֵּן אֶת־צִפֹּרָה בִתּוֹ לְמֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyoel Mosheh lashevet et-ha'ish vayyitten et-Tzipporah bitto leMosheh.
verses.study_note: 'Was content' (vayyoel) — literally 'was willing, agreed, resolved.' Moses accepts his new life. The prince of Egypt becomes a son-in-law to a Midianite priest. 'Zipporah' means 'bird' or 'sparrow' — a beautiful name suggesting freedom and delicacy. The well-encounter pattern is now complete: the stranger arrives, helps the women, is invited home, and marries a daughter (cf. Genesis 24, 29). Moses begins 40 years of obscurity. From age 40 to 80, the future deliverer of Israel tends sheep in the desert. God's preparation is unhurried.

verses.kjv_text: And she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land.
verses.original_text: וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַיִּקְרָא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ גֵּרְשֹׁם כִּי אָמַר גֵּר הָיִיתִי בְּאֶרֶץ נָכְרִיָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vateled ben vayyiqra et-shemo Gershom ki amar ger hayiti be'eretz nokhriyyah.
verses.study_note: 'Gershom' — 'ger' (stranger/sojourner) + 'sham' (there). The name encodes Moses's exilic identity: 'I have been a stranger in a strange land.' This is both personal pain and theological truth. Moses is a stranger in Midian, but more profoundly, Israel is a stranger in Egypt — and all of God's people are strangers on earth (Hebrews 11:13). The name Gershom becomes a perpetual reminder that this is not home. Moses names his grief, and in doing so, he expresses the longing that will eventually drive the Exodus itself — the yearning for home, for belonging, for the Promised Land.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theology of exile: God's people are strangers and pilgrims in this world. Gershom's name captures the ache of displacement that every believer feels — not fully at home until we reach the Father's house (John 14:2-3, Hebrews 11:13-16).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַיָּמִים הָרַבִּים הָהֵם וַיָּמָת מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וַיֵּאָנְחוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִן־הָעֲבֹדָה וַיִּזְעָקוּ וַתַּעַל שַׁוְעָתָם אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים מִן־הָעֲבֹדָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bayyamim harabbim hahem vayyamot melekh Mitzrayim vayye'anchu venei-Yisra'el min-ha'avodah vayyiz'aqu vatta'al shav'atam el-ha'Elohim min-ha'avodah.
verses.study_note: 'In process of time' (bayyamim harabbim hahem — 'in those many days') — many years pass. The Pharaoh of the oppression dies, but slavery continues. A new king brings no relief. Three words describe Israel's response, escalating in intensity: (1) 'sighed' (ye'anchu) — groaning under the weight, (2) 'cried' (yiz'aqu) — a cry of distress, (3) 'their cry came up' (shav'atam — a shriek for help). This is the progression of prayer under extreme suffering: from sighs to groans to desperate outcry. 'Their cry came up unto God' — the most important phrase. The cry ascends. God is not deaf or indifferent. He hears from above what is suffered below. This verse transitions from the human story to the divine response.
verses.doctrinal_note: Prayer ascends even when it feels like it doesn't. Israel's cry during centuries of slavery was not lost in the void — it 'came up unto God.' The groaning of the oppressed always reaches heaven. God's timing is not indifference; His silence is not absence (Psalm 22:1-2, Romans 8:26).

verses.kjv_text: And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת־נַאֲקָתָם וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת־בְּרִיתוֹ אֶת־אַבְרָהָם אֶת־יִצְחָק וְאֶת־יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishma Elohim et-na'aqatam vayyizkor Elohim et-berito et-Avraham et-Yitzchaq ve'et-Ya'aqov.
verses.study_note: The first of four magnificent divine verbs (vv.24-25). 'God heard' (vayyishma) — the verb of attentive listening. God is not absent during the silence. He has been hearing every groan since the oppression began. 'God remembered' (vayyizkor) — 'zakhar' does not mean God forgot and then recalled. Divine remembrance means God activates His covenant commitment. When God 'remembers,' He acts. He 'remembered' Noah (Gen 8:1) and the waters receded. He 'remembers' His covenant here and the Exodus begins. The covenant with Abraham, Isaac, AND Jacob — all three patriarchs named, emphasizing the unbroken chain of promise. The basis of deliverance is not Israel's merit but God's covenant faithfulness.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's deliverance is rooted in His covenant, not in our worthiness. Israel does not earn the Exodus; God initiates it because He is faithful to His word. This is the pattern of grace throughout Scripture — salvation by covenant promise, not by human deserving (Ephesians 2:8-9).

verses.kjv_text: And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֵּדַע אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Elohim et-benei Yisra'el vayyeda Elohim.
verses.study_note: The final two divine verbs complete the quartet. 'God looked' (vayyar) — the same verb used when Jochebed "saw" the goodly child (v.2) and when Moses "looked" on Israel's burdens (v.11). Now God Himself sees. His gaze is not passive observation but active engagement. 'God knew' (vayyeda) — the Hebrew is strikingly abrupt. Literally: "And God knew." No object is given. The KJV adds "had respect unto them," but the Hebrew simply says "and God knew." This profound incompleteness suggests something beyond words: God knew their suffering, knew their need, knew His plan, knew the time had come. The verb 'yada' (to know) is the most intimate word in Hebrew — the knowledge of a husband for his wife (Gen 4:1). God enters into intimate, personal knowledge of Israel's anguish. These four verbs — HEARD, REMEMBERED, LOOKED, KNEW — are the theological engine that drives everything from Exodus 3 onward. The burning bush, the plagues, the Passover, the Red Sea — all flow from this moment when God turns His full covenant attention to His suffering people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The four verbs of divine engagement: God HEARD (He is not deaf), REMEMBERED (He is faithful), LOOKED (He is attentive), KNEW (He is intimately present). These four verbs demolish the lie that God is absent in suffering. He hears every groan, honors every promise, sees every tear, and knows every pain. The Exodus begins not with Moses's action but with God's attention.

meta.overview: Exodus 2 is the biography of Moses compressed into 25 verses — birth, rescue, royal upbringing, failed self-deliverance, exile, and the hinge moment when God "remembers" His covenant. The chapter opens with Pharaoh's death decree still in force (1:22), yet a Levite couple defies it by faith. The infant Moses is placed in an ark on the very Nile meant to destroy him, rescued by Pharaoh's own daughter, and nursed by his own mother — all orchestrated by God's sovereign providence. Moses grows up in Egypt's royal court but identifies with his enslaved people. His attempt to deliver Israel by human strength results in murder, exposure, and flight to Midian, where he spends 40 years as a shepherd. The chapter closes with four magnificent verbs: God heard, God remembered, God looked, God knew — signaling that the Exodus is about to begin.
meta.theme: Providence, Failed Deliverance & Divine Remembrance
meta.keyWordMeaning: To remember — not mere recall but active, covenant-keeping attention. When God "remembers," He acts.
outline: The Birth & Rescue of Moses (vv.1-10): An ark on the Nile — faith, providence, and Pharaoh's daughter
Moses Kills the Egyptian (vv.11-14): Identification with Israel, but deliverance by human strength fails
Flight to Midian (vv.15-22): From prince to shepherd — 40 years of divine preparation
God Hears, Remembers, Looks, Knows (vv.23-25): The four verbs that launch the Exodus

verses.kjv_text: Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law, the priest of Midian: and he led the flock to the backside of the desert, and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb.
verses.original_text: וּמֹשֶׁה הָיָה רֹעֶה אֶת־צֹאן יִתְרוֹ חֹתְנוֹ כֹּהֵן מִדְיָן וַיִּנְהַג אֶת־הַצֹּאן אַחַר הַמִּדְבָּר וַיָּבֹא אֶל־הַר הָאֱלֹהִים חֹרֵבָה׃
verses.transliteration: UMosheh hayah ro'eh et-tzon Yitro chotno kohen Midyan vayynhag et-hatzon achar hamidbar vayyavo el-har ha'Elohim Chorevah.
verses.study_note: 'Moses was keeping the flock' — the Hebrew uses the continuous past tense: this had been his life for 40 years. The prince of Egypt is now a shepherd of sheep. God's curriculum: 40 years in the palace learning to lead, 40 years in the desert learning to follow. 'Jethro' (Yitro) — meaning 'his excellency' or 'his abundance.' Called Reuel in 2:18, Jethro may be a title. 'Led the flock to the backside of the desert' (achar hamidbar) — literally 'behind the wilderness,' the farthest, most remote place. God meets Moses not in the temple or the palace but at the edge of nowhere. 'The mountain of God' — Horeb/Sinai. The narrator calls it 'the mountain of God' proleptically (anticipating what it will become). This mountain will become the site of the Law, the covenant, and the Tabernacle instructions. 'Horeb' (Chorev) means 'desolate, dry' — God reveals Himself in desolation.
verses.doctrinal_note: God prepares His servants in hiddenness. Moses's 40 years as a shepherd were not wasted time but essential formation. The skills of shepherding — patience, vigilance, leading the vulnerable, knowing the terrain — are precisely what leading Israel will require. God wastes nothing in our preparation.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he looked, and, behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֵלָיו בְּלַבַּת־אֵשׁ מִתּוֹךְ הַסְּנֶה וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה הַסְּנֶה בֹּעֵר בָּאֵשׁ וְהַסְּנֶה אֵינֶנּוּ אֻכָּל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyera mal'akh Yahweh elav belabbat-esh mittokh hasseneh vayyar vehineh hasseneh bo'er ba'esh vehasseneh einennu ukkal.
verses.study_note: 'The Angel of the LORD' (mal'akh Yahweh) — not an ordinary angel but a manifestation of God Himself. In v.4, 'the LORD saw' and 'God called' from the bush — the Angel IS God. This figure, the 'Angel of Yahweh,' appears throughout the OT and is widely understood as a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ (Christophany). 'A flame of fire' (labbat-esh) — fire in Scripture represents God's presence, holiness, judgment, and purification. The burning bush is the first of many fire theophanies: the pillar of fire (13:21), Sinai ablaze (19:18), and the fire on the mercy seat. 'The bush' (seneh) — a common, lowly thornbush, not a mighty cedar. God chooses the insignificant. Some scholars connect 'seneh' (bush) with 'Sinai' — the bush-mountain. 'Burned with fire... was not consumed' — the central miracle. The bush is on fire yet not destroyed. This is the image of Israel: afflicted in Egypt's furnace but not consumed. It is the image of God: His holiness blazes but does not annihilate sinners who approach in faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: The bush that burns but is not consumed reveals God's character: He is a consuming fire (Deut 4:24) who nevertheless does not destroy what He inhabits. His holiness is not merely destructive but sustaining. This paradox runs through Scripture to the cross — where God's wrath and God's love meet without either being diminished.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, I will now turn aside, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אָסֻרָה־נָּא וְאֶרְאֶה אֶת־הַמַּרְאֶה הַגָּדֹל הַזֶּה מַדּוּעַ לֹא־יִבְעַר הַסְּנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh asurah-nna ve'er'eh et-hammar'eh haggadol hazzeh maddua lo-yiv'ar hasseneh.
verses.study_note: 'I will now turn aside' (asurah-nna) — Moses makes a deliberate choice to investigate. God does not force the encounter; He attracts attention and waits for a response. The burning bush is an invitation, not a compulsion. 'This great sight' (hammar'eh haggadol) — Moses recognizes something extraordinary. The Hebrew 'mar'eh' (sight/vision) is the same word used for prophetic visions. Moses sees with spiritual perception, not just physical curiosity. 'Why the bush is not burnt' — the right question. Moses is drawn by the paradox. The bush should be consumed but is not. It is the anomaly that arrests him. God often works through what doesn't fit our expectations — the unexpected, the paradoxical, the thing that should not be possible. Verse 4 will show that God was waiting for this very turning: "when the LORD saw that he turned aside to see, God called."

verses.kjv_text: And when the LORD saw that he turned aside to see, God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי סָר לִרְאוֹת וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו אֱלֹהִים מִתּוֹךְ הַסְּנֶה וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Yahweh ki sar lir'ot vayyiqra elav Elohim mittokh hasseneh vayyomer Mosheh Mosheh vayyomer hinneni.
verses.study_note: 'When the LORD saw that he turned aside' — God waited for Moses's response before speaking. Divine revelation often requires human willingness. Notice the divine names: 'Yahweh saw' and 'Elohim called' — both names used together, confirming the Angel of Yahweh IS God. 'Moses, Moses' — the doubled name. In Scripture, God doubles the name at moments of urgent, intimate calling: 'Abraham, Abraham' (Gen 22:11), 'Jacob, Jacob' (Gen 46:2), 'Samuel, Samuel' (1 Sam 3:10), 'Saul, Saul' (Acts 9:4). Each marks a pivotal, life-altering summons. 'Here am I' (hinneni) — the classic response of availability. Abraham said it (Gen 22:1), Isaiah said it (Isa 6:8), Samuel said it (1 Sam 3:4). 'Hinneni' means more than physical presence — it means "I am ready, I am available, I am yours."
verses.doctrinal_note: God initiates, but He waits for responsiveness. The burning bush burns continuously — it was burning before Moses noticed it. God's revelation is always there; the question is whether we will turn aside to see. Divine calling is both sovereign (God initiates) and responsive (God waits for "hinneni").

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אַל־תִּקְרַב הֲלֹם שַׁל־נְעָלֶיךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶיךָ כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עוֹמֵד עָלָיו אַדְמַת־קֹדֶשׁ הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer al-tiqrav halom shal-ne'alekha me'al raglekha ki hamaqom asher attah omed alav admat-qodesh hu.
verses.study_note: 'Draw not nigh' (al-tiqrav) — the first command establishes distance before intimacy. God's holiness requires reverent approach. 'Put off thy shoes' — removing sandals signifies humility, submission, and direct contact with holy ground. The feet, the lowest part of the body, touch the holy earth without barrier. Joshua receives the same command at Jericho (Josh 5:15). 'Holy ground' (admat-qodesh) — this is the first use of 'qodesh' (holy) in Exodus. The ground is not inherently sacred — it is ordinary desert. It becomes holy because of God's presence. Holiness is not a property of places but of the Person who inhabits them. Where God is, there is holy ground. This transforms the concept of sacred space: anywhere God meets His people becomes a sanctuary.
verses.doctrinal_note: Holiness is communicative — it radiates from God's presence and transforms what it touches. Ordinary ground becomes holy ground. An ordinary bush becomes God's sanctuary. Ordinary people become vessels of the divine. The principle extends to the church: wherever God's Spirit dwells is holy (1 Corinthians 3:16-17).

verses.kjv_text: Moreover he said, I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. And Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב וַיַּסְתֵּר מֹשֶׁה פָּנָיו כִּי יָרֵא מֵהַבִּיט אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer anokhi Elohei avikha Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchaq ve'Elohei Ya'aqov vayyaster Mosheh panav ki yare mehabbit el-ha'Elohim.
verses.study_note: 'I am the God of thy father' — God identifies Himself through relationship, not abstraction. He is not a philosophical concept but a personal, covenant-keeping God. 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob' — each patriarch named individually. He is not merely 'the God of the ancestors' collectively but the God of each one personally. Jesus uses this very verse to prove the resurrection (Matt 22:31-32): God does not say 'I was' their God but 'I AM' — they are alive to Him. 'Moses hid his face' (vayyaster panav) — in contrast to his boldness in verse 3, Moses now covers his face in holy fear. The man who 'turned aside to see' now cannot bear to look. This is the proper progression: curiosity leads to encounter, encounter leads to reverence. 'Afraid to look upon God' — 'yare' (fear/reverence), the same root as the midwives' fear of God (1:17). But now it is Moses who fears.
verses.doctrinal_note: God reveals Himself as the God of history and relationship. He does not present abstract theology but personal covenant: "I am the God of your father." This grounds all theology in relationship. Christianity is not a philosophy but a relationship with the living God who has acted in history and who calls each person by name.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said, I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters; for I know their sorrows;
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה רָאֹה רָאִיתִי אֶת־עֳנִי עַמִּי אֲשֶׁר בְּמִצְרָיִם וְאֶת־צַעֲקָתָם שָׁמַעְתִּי מִפְּנֵי נֹגְשָׂיו כִּי יָדַעְתִּי אֶת־מַכְאֹבָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Yahweh ra'oh ra'iti et-oni ammi asher beMitzrayim ve'et-tza'aqatam shamati mippenei nogsav ki yadati et-makh'ovav.
verses.study_note: God now explains the burning bush. He has not been absent during the 400 years of slavery. Three verbs echo 2:24-25 but now from God's own lips: 'I have surely seen' (ra'oh ra'iti — emphatic doubling, 'seeing I have seen'). 'I have heard their cry.' 'I know their sorrows.' These are the same three dimensions: sight, hearing, and intimate knowledge. But there is an expansion: God now calls Israel 'MY people' (ammi) — the first time in Exodus God uses this possessive. They are not merely 'the children of Israel' but 'My people.' 'Their sorrows' (makh'ovav) — physical and emotional pain, anguish. God does not merely observe suffering clinically; He knows (yada) it with intimate, empathetic awareness. This is the God who 'in all their affliction, He was afflicted' (Isaiah 63:9).
verses.doctrinal_note: God sees what the world ignores, hears what the powerful silence, and knows what the suffering endure. The three verbs — seen, heard, known — are the divine answer to the problem of suffering: God is not absent, blind, deaf, or indifferent. He is fully aware, fully attentive, and about to act.

verses.kjv_text: And I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians, and to bring them up out of that land unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey; unto the place of the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites.
verses.original_text: וָאֵרֵד לְהַצִּילוֹ מִיַּד מִצְרַיִם וּלְהַעֲלֹתוֹ מִן־הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא אֶל־אֶרֶץ טוֹבָה וּרְחָבָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ אֶל־מְקוֹם הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ered lehatztzilo miyyad Mitzrayim uleha'aloto min-ha'aretz hahi el-eretz tovah urechavah el-eretz zavat chalav udevash el-meqom haKena'ani vehaChitti veha'Emori vehaPerizzi vehaChivvi vehaYevusi.
verses.study_note: 'I am come down' (va'ered) — God descends. The language of divine condescension. The God who is transcendent above all stoops to enter human history. 'To deliver them' (lehatztzilo) — from 'natsal' (to rescue, snatch away). God takes personal action. 'To bring them up' (leha'aloto) — up from Egypt, up to the Promised Land. The Exodus is not merely escape but elevation. 'A good land and a large' — 'tovah' (good) echoes Genesis 1's creation evaluations. The Promised Land is a restoration of Eden-like goodness. 'Flowing with milk and honey' (zavat chalav udevash) — the iconic description of Canaan. 'Milk' represents pastoral abundance; 'honey' represents agricultural sweetness. Together: a land of complete provision. This phrase appears 20 times in the Pentateuch alone. Six nations are listed as current occupants — but God's promise overrides their presence. The land belongs to God, and He gives it to whom He wills.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's salvation is never merely FROM something but always TO something. He delivers FROM bondage TO a promised land, FROM sin TO righteousness, FROM death TO life. Redemption is always purposeful, directional, and abundant — "a good land and a large."

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore, behold, the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה הִנֵּה צַעֲקַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָה אֵלָי וְגַם־רָאִיתִי אֶת־הַלַּחַץ אֲשֶׁר מִצְרַיִם לֹחֲצִים אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah hinneh tza'aqat benei-Yisra'el ba'ah elai vegam-ra'iti et-hallachatz asher Mitzrayim lochaztim otam.
verses.study_note: 'Now therefore' (ve'attah) — the transition from God's awareness (vv.7-8) to God's action (v.10). 'The cry... is come unto me' — God repeats for emphasis: the cry has reached Him. 'The oppression' (hallachatz) — from 'lachatz' (to press, squeeze, crush). The Egyptians are 'pressing' Israel. This physical imagery of compression and crushing conveys the lived reality of slavery — there is no room to breathe, no space to live. God sees both the cry AND the crushing. He knows the cause (oppression) and the effect (anguish). This verse serves as the final summary before the commission: God has stated His awareness (v.7), His plan (v.8), and now His attention to the ongoing reality (v.9). The ground is fully laid for verse 10.

verses.kjv_text: Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh, that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לְכָה וְאֶשְׁלָחֲךָ אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְהוֹצֵא אֶת־עַמִּי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lekhah ve'eshlachacha el-Par'oh vehotze et-ammi venei-Yisra'el miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'Come now' (lekhah) — the commission. After laying the entire theological foundation (vv.1-9), God now issues the call. 'I will send thee' (eshlachacha) — the root 'shalach' (to send) is the basis of 'shaliach' (sent one/apostle). Moses becomes God's authorized representative. The pattern: God sees the need, God chooses the agent, God sends. 'My people' (ammi) — repeated from v.7, reinforcing God's possessive claim. 'Bring forth' (hotze) — 'cause to go out.' Moses is the instrument; God is the power. Note the structure: GOD sees (v.7), GOD comes down (v.8), GOD sends (v.10). The Exodus is God's initiative from first to last. Moses is the agent, not the author, of deliverance.
verses.doctrinal_note: God works through human agents but remains the sovereign actor. "I will send thee" — the divine commission that transforms a reluctant shepherd into the instrument of the greatest deliverance in the Old Testament. The same pattern applies to all of God's servants: the call is God's, the power is God's, the agent is simply willing and available.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto God, Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים מִי אָנֹכִי כִּי אֵלֵךְ אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְכִי אוֹצִיא אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-ha'Elohim mi anokhi ki elekh el-Par'oh vekhi otzi et-benei Yisra'el miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'Who am I?' (mi anokhi) — Moses's first objection, and the most fundamental. At age 40, Moses never would have asked this question — he knew exactly who he was (a prince, a deliverer). Now at 80, after 40 years of obscurity, he has learned the most essential lesson: he is nobody. 'Who am I to go to Pharaoh?' — I am a fugitive with a murder charge. 'Who am I to bring forth Israel?' — I tried that 40 years ago and failed completely. This is not false humility but genuine brokenness. The Moses who asked "Who am I?" is ready in a way the Moses who killed the Egyptian never was. God's answer in v.12 is stunning: He does not answer the question. He does not tell Moses who Moses is. Instead, He redirects entirely: the issue was never who Moses is but who GOD is.
verses.doctrinal_note: The question "Who am I?" is the wrong question. God's answer is not "You are talented, courageous, qualified." His answer is "I will be with you." The sufficiency is not in the servant but in the Sender. This principle runs through all of Scripture: Paul's "when I am weak, then I am strong" (2 Cor 12:10) is the New Testament echo of Moses's "Who am I?"

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Certainly I will be with thee; and this shall be a token unto thee, that I have sent thee: When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי־אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ וְזֶה־לְּךָ הָאוֹת כִּי אָנֹכִי שְׁלַחְתִּיךָ בְּהוֹצִיאֲךָ אֶת־הָעָם מִמִּצְרַיִם תַּעַבְדוּן אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים עַל הָהָר הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer ki-Ehyeh immakh vezeh-lekha ha'ot ki anokhi shelachttikha behotzi'akha et-ha'am miMitzrayim ta'avdun et-ha'Elohim al hahar hazzeh.
verses.study_note: 'Certainly I will be with thee' (ki-Ehyeh immakh) — God's answer to "Who am I?" is not about Moses at all. It is about God: "I WILL BE with you." The word 'Ehyeh' (I will be) is the same word God will use as His name in v.14: 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh' (I AM THAT I AM). God's presence IS the answer to human insufficiency. 'This shall be a token' (ha'ot) — a sign. But the sign is unusual: you will know I sent you AFTER you succeed, when you worship on this mountain. This is a sign that requires faith — it is only confirmed in retrospect. God does not provide proof in advance; He provides a promise. 'Ye shall serve God upon this mountain' (ta'avdun) — 'avad' means both 'to serve' and 'to worship.' The Exodus is not freedom FOR freedom's sake but freedom FOR worship. Israel is delivered from serving Pharaoh to serving God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sign God gives requires faith, not sight. The proof that God sent Moses will only come AFTER obedience — when they worship at this mountain. This is the nature of biblical faith: you step out before you see the confirmation. "We walk by faith, not by sight" (2 Cor 5:7).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto God, Behold, when I come unto the children of Israel, and shall say unto them, The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you; and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי בָא אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתִּי לָהֶם אֱלֹהֵי אֲבוֹתֵיכֶם שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם וְאָמְרוּ־לִי מַה־שְּׁמוֹ מָה אֹמַר אֲלֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Mosheh el-ha'Elohim hinneh anokhi va el-benei Yisra'el ve'amarti lahem Elohei avoteikhem shelachani aleikhem ve'amru-li mah-shemo mah omar alehem.
verses.study_note: Moses's second objection/question: 'What is His name?' This is not casual curiosity but a profound theological question. In the ancient Near East, knowing a deity's name meant understanding its character and having access to its power. After 400 years in Egypt surrounded by hundreds of named gods — Ra, Osiris, Thoth, Isis — Israel would naturally ask: "Which god sent you? What is his name? What is his nature? What can he do?" The question 'What is his name?' really means 'What is his character? What distinguishes him from the gods of Egypt?' Moses anticipates that Israel needs more than 'the God of your fathers' — they need a name that reveals character, power, and covenant presence. God's answer in v.14 is the most profound self-revelation in the Old Testament.

verses.kjv_text: And God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה וַיֹּאמֶר כֹּה תֹאמַר לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶהְיֶה שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer Elohim el-Mosheh Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh vayyomer koh tomar livnei Yisra'el Ehyeh shelachani aleikhem.
verses.study_note: The supreme self-revelation of God in the Old Testament. 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh' — traditionally 'I AM THAT I AM' or 'I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE.' The Hebrew allows multiple translations, each capturing a facet of infinite truth: (1) I AM WHO I AM — self-existence, God depends on nothing outside Himself.
