(2) I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE — sovereign freedom, God cannot be controlled or predicted. (3) I AM BECAUSE I AM — absolute being, the ground of all existence. (4) I WILL BE THERE HOWSOEVER I WILL BE THERE — covenant faithfulness, God will show up however the situation requires. The name is built on the verb 'hayah' (to be/to become). God IS — not was, not will be, but eternally IS. This is the philosophical and theological foundation of all reality: God is the self-existent One from whom all other existence derives. 'I AM hath sent me' — Moses's credentials are the very being of God. He does not come with a message from a distant deity but as the envoy of Being Itself. Jesus claims this name directly in John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I AM' — and the Jews picked up stones, understanding the claim perfectly.
verses.doctrinal_note: Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh: the most profound three words in the Old Testament. God's name is not a label but an inexhaustible revelation of His nature: self-existent (uncaused), eternal (beyond time), sovereign (answerable to no one), faithful (He WILL BE there), and personal (He reveals Himself to a shepherd at a bush). Every other divine attribute flows from this name. When Jesus says "I AM" (John 8:58), He is claiming to be this God — the I AM of the burning bush standing in human flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And God said moreover unto Moses, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath sent me unto you: this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר עוֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כֹּה תֹאמַר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתֵיכֶם אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם זֶה־שְּׁמִי לְעֹלָם וְזֶה זִכְרִי לְדֹר דֹּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomer od Elohim el-Mosheh koh tomar el-benei Yisra'el Yahweh Elohei avoteikhem Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzchaq ve'Elohei Ya'aqov shelachani aleikhem zeh-shemi le'olam vezeh zikhri ledor dor.
verses.study_note: God now gives the third-person form of His name: 'Yahweh' (YHWH) — the Tetragrammaton. While 'Ehyeh' (I AM) is God's name from His own perspective, 'Yahweh' (HE IS) is God's name from the human perspective. YHWH is built on the same root 'hayah' (to be) but in the third person: 'He is' or 'He causes to be.' 'This is my name forever' (le'olam) — this is not a temporary designation but God's eternal, permanent, covenant name. The KJV renders YHWH as 'the LORD' (in small capitals). 'My memorial unto all generations' (zikhri ledor dor) — 'zikaron' (memorial) connects to 'zakhar' (remember, 2:24). God's name is to be remembered in every generation — it is the thread of identity that runs through all of redemptive history. God connects 'Yahweh' with 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — tying the eternal name to historical covenant relationships.
verses.doctrinal_note: YHWH — the covenant name of God. While "Ehyeh" (I AM) is God's name spoken about Himself, "Yahweh" (HE IS) is the name by which His people know and call upon Him. This is the name above all names in the Old Testament, used over 6,800 times. It appears in every biblical book except Esther and Song of Solomon. The name that is "forever" is the name Jesus carries: "I AM" (Ego Eimi) in the Greek New Testament.

verses.kjv_text: Go, and gather the elders of Israel together, and say unto them, The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, of Isaac, and of Jacob, appeared unto me, saying, I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt:
verses.original_text: לֵךְ וְאָסַפְתָּ אֶת־זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵיהֶם יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתֵיכֶם נִרְאָה אֵלַי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם יִצְחָק וְיַעֲקֹב לֵאמֹר פָּקֹד פָּקַדְתִּי אֶתְכֶם וְאֶת־הֶעָשׂוּי לָכֶם בְּמִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Lekh ve'asafta et-ziqnei Yisra'el ve'amarta aleihem Yahweh Elohei avoteikhem nir'ah elai Elohei Avraham Yitzchaq veYa'aqov lemor paqod paqadti etkhem ve'et-he'asui lakhem beMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: God gives Moses specific instructions for phase one. 'Gather the elders' — Israel had maintained a leadership structure even in slavery. The elders are the recognized heads of families and tribes. 'I have surely visited you' (paqod paqadti) — emphatic doubling: 'visiting, I have visited.' The word 'paqad' (to visit/attend to) is theologically loaded. It is the word Joseph used on his deathbed: 'God will surely visit you' (Gen 50:24-25). The elders would have known this tradition. When Moses says 'paqod paqadti,' it is the fulfillment of Joseph's dying prophecy — the code word that confirms the deliverer is genuine. The promise spoken 400 years earlier is now activated.

verses.kjv_text: And I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt unto the land of the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Amorites, and the Perizzites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites, unto a land flowing with milk and honey.
verses.original_text: וָאֹמַר אַעֲלֶה אֶתְכֶם מֵעֳנִי מִצְרַיִם אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי אֶל־אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'omar a'aleh etkhem me'oni Mitzrayim el-eretz haKena'ani vehaChitti veha'Emori vehaPerizzi vehaChivvi vehaYevusi el-eretz zavat chalav udevash.
verses.study_note: God instructs Moses to repeat the promise of v.8 to the elders. The repetition is for the elders' benefit — they will hear in their own ears the words God spoke from the bush. 'I will bring you UP' (a'aleh) — the Exodus is ascent, elevation, rising from the pit of bondage to the heights of the Promised Land. The six nations are repeated, and 'milk and honey' — the message Moses is to deliver is consistent, clear, and specific. God does not speak in vague generalities but in concrete geography and tangible promise.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall hearken to thy voice: and thou shalt come, thou and the elders of Israel, unto the king of Egypt, and ye shall say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go, we beseech thee, three days’ journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁמְעוּ לְקֹלֶךָ וּבָאתָ אַתָּה וְזִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָיו יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִיִּים נִקְרָה עָלֵינוּ וְעַתָּה נֵלֲכָה־נָּא דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר וְנִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vesham'u leqolekha uvata attah veziqnei Yisra'el el-melekh Mitzrayim va'amartem elav Yahweh Elohei ha'Ivriyyim niqrah aleinu ve'attah nelekhah-nna derekh sheloshet yamim bamidbar venizbechah laYahweh Eloheinu.
verses.study_note: 'They shall hearken to thy voice' — God assures Moses the elders will believe. This is confirmed in 4:31. 'Three days' journey' — the initial request is modest: a short trip to sacrifice. Some see this as a test of Pharaoh — a reasonable request that reveals his tyranny when refused. Others see it as the full truth expressed in part — the sacrifice in the wilderness is Sinai, three months away. Either way, Pharaoh's refusal exposes his heart: he will not yield even an inch. 'The LORD God of the Hebrews' — God is now identified by name (YHWH) to Pharaoh. The God Pharaoh does not know (5:2) introduces Himself. 'That we may sacrifice' (venizbechah) — from 'zabach' (to slaughter/sacrifice). The Exodus purpose: worship. Freedom is for worship.

verses.kjv_text: And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, no, not by a mighty hand.
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי לֹא־יִתֵּן אֶתְכֶם מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם לַהֲלֹךְ וְלֹא בְּיָד חֲזָקָה׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ani yadati ki lo-yitten etkhem melekh Mitzrayim lahalokh velo beyad chazaqah.
verses.study_note: 'I am sure' (yadati — I know) — God knows the outcome in advance. Pharaoh will refuse. This is not a surprise to God but part of the plan. 'Not by a mighty hand' (velo beyad chazaqah) — this phrase is debated. It could mean: (1) Pharaoh will not let you go, not even compelled by a mighty hand (i.e., he is stubborn beyond reason), or (2) Pharaoh will not let you go UNLESS by a mighty hand (i.e., only God's power will break him). Either reading leads to the same conclusion: the plagues are coming. God is transparent with Moses about the difficulty ahead. He does not promise easy success but promised presence through hard reality.
verses.doctrinal_note: God does not hide the cost of obedience. He tells Moses plainly: Pharaoh will refuse. The path to the Promised Land goes through confrontation, plagues, and suffering. Honest calling includes honest warnings. Jesus similarly warned His disciples: "In the world ye shall have tribulation" (John 16:33).

verses.kjv_text: And I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof: and after that he will let you go.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁלַחְתִּי אֶת־יָדִי וְהִכֵּיתִי אֶת־מִצְרַיִם בְּכֹל נִפְלְאֹתַי אֲשֶׁר אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּקִרְבּוֹ וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן יְשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Veshalachti et-yadi vehikkeiti et-Mitzrayim bekhol nifl'otai asher e'eseh beqirbo ve'acharei-khen yeshallach etkhem.
verses.study_note: 'I will stretch out MY hand' — God Himself will strike Egypt. Moses is the messenger; God is the warrior. 'Smite Egypt with all my wonders' (nifl'otai) — 'nifla'ot' are miraculous works beyond human capacity. The ten plagues are God's 'wonders' — each one dismantling an Egyptian deity and demonstrating Yahweh's supremacy. 'After that, he will let you go' — the outcome is guaranteed. God declares the end from the beginning. The plagues are not experiments hoping for success but a predetermined campaign with a certain conclusion. 'He will send you out' (yeshallach) — the same root as 'I will send you' (eshlachacha, v.10). God sends Moses; eventually Pharaoh will send Israel. God's 'sending' initiates; Pharaoh's 'sending' capitulates.
verses.doctrinal_note: The plagues are not random disasters but targeted, systematic dismantling of Egyptian idolatry. Each plague confronts a specific Egyptian deity: the Nile god (Hapi), the frog goddess (Heqet), the sun god (Ra), etc. God wages war not merely against Pharaoh but "against all the gods of Egypt" (Exodus 12:12).

verses.kjv_text: And I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians: and it shall come to pass, that, when ye go, ye shall not go empty:
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתִּי אֶת־חֵן הָעָם־הַזֶּה בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרָיִם וְהָיָה כִּי תֵלֵכוּן לֹא תֵלְכוּ רֵיקָם׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatti et-chen ha'am-hazzeh be'einei Mitzrayim vehayah ki telekun lo telkhu reqam.
verses.study_note: 'I will give this people favour' (chen) — grace, favor, kindness. God will sovereignly cause the Egyptians to feel goodwill toward Israel at the moment of departure. This is supernatural intervention in human hearts (Proverbs 21:1). 'Ye shall not go empty' (reqam) — they will leave Egypt wealthy. This fulfills God's promise to Abraham: 'afterward shall they come out with great substance' (Gen 15:14). The slaves will receive wages for 400 years of unpaid labor. God ensures just compensation even when human systems refuse it.

verses.kjv_text: But every woman shall borrow of her neighbour, and of her that sojourneth in her house, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment: and ye shall put them upon your sons, and upon your daughters; and ye shall spoil the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁאֲלָה אִשָּׁה מִשְּׁכֶנְתָּהּ וּמִגָּרַת בֵּיתָהּ כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּשְׂמָלֹת וְשַׂמְתֶּם עַל־בְּנֵיכֶם וְעַל־בְּנֹתֵיכֶם וְנִצַּלְתֶּם אֶת־מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vesha'alah ishah mishkhentah umiggarat beitah kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav usemalot vesamtem al-beneikhem ve'al-benoteikhem venitztzaltem et-Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'Borrow' (sha'alah) — the Hebrew 'sha'al' means 'to ask, request,' not 'borrow' with intent to return. There is no deception here; the Israelites simply ask, and the Egyptians give. 'Jewels of silver and gold and raiment' — precious metals and clothing. These will later be used to construct the Tabernacle (Exodus 25:2-3, 35:22). Egypt's gold becomes God's Tabernacle. 'Ye shall spoil the Egyptians' (venitztzaltem) — from 'natsal' (to plunder, strip). The same word used for 'deliver' (v.8)! Israel will be 'delivered' FROM Egypt and will 'plunder' Egypt. There is poetic justice: the nation that plundered Israel's labor for 400 years is now plundered in return. God is a God of recompense. The wealth of Egypt becomes the furnishing of God's dwelling place.
verses.doctrinal_note: God redeems even material loss. Israel's 400 years of unpaid labor is compensated in a single night. The gold of Egypt becomes the gold of the Tabernacle — what was used to enslave now serves to worship. This is the pattern of divine reversal: what the enemy meant for evil, God transforms for holy purpose (Genesis 50:20).

meta.overview: Exodus 3 is the theological heart of the Pentateuch. After 40 years of obscurity in Midian, Moses encounters God in a bush that burns without being consumed — the first theophany (visible manifestation of God) in Exodus. From the fire, God reveals Himself by His covenant name YHWH, declares that He has seen Israel's affliction, announces His plan to deliver them and bring them to a land flowing with milk and honey, and commissions Moses as His agent. Moses responds with five objections (spanning chapters 3-4), beginning here with "Who am I?" God's answer redirects the question entirely: the issue is not who Moses is but who God is. The chapter climaxes with the most profound self-revelation in the Old Testament: "I AM THAT I AM" (Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh) — the divine name that Jesus later claims for Himself (John 8:58). This chapter establishes that the Exodus is God's initiative from first to last: He sees, He descends, He sends, He will be present.
meta.theme: The Self-Revelation of God & the Commission of Moses
meta.keyWordMeaning: I AM / I Will Be — God's self-revealed name expressing eternal, self-existent, covenant-keeping presence
outline: The Burning Bush (vv.1-3): Moses at Horeb — fire that does not consume
Holy Ground (vv.4-6): God calls by name, reveals Himself as the God of the fathers
I Have Seen (vv.7-9): God declares He has heard, seen, and known Israel's suffering
I Will Send You (vv.10-12): The commission — and Moses's first objection: Who am I?
I AM THAT I AM (vv.13-15): The divine name revealed — Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh / YHWH
The Plan Declared (vv.16-22): Gather the elders, confront Pharaoh, plunder Egypt

verses.kjv_text: And Moses answered and said, But, behold, they will not believe me, nor hearken unto my voice: for they will say, The LORD hath not appeared unto thee.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַן מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר וְהֵן לֹא יַאֲמִינוּ לִי וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ בְּקֹלִי כִּי יֹאמְרוּ לֹא נִרְאָה אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'an Moshe vayomer vehen lo ya'aminu li velo yishme'u bekholi ki yomeru lo nir'ah eleikha YHVH.
verses.study_note: Moses's fourth objection in the burning bush dialogue (cf. 3:11, 3:13, 4:1, 4:10, 4:13 — five total). The previous objections concerned identity ('Who am I?' — 3:11) and knowledge ('What is His name?' — 3:13). Now Moses raises the problem of credibility: 'They will not believe me' (lo ya'aminu li — from 'aman,' the root of 'Amen'). This is the concern of every human messenger: being believed. Remarkably, God does not argue with Moses — He demonstrates. The three signs that follow are God's answer to the credibility problem. Note that Moses's fear proves partly wrong: the elders DO believe (v.31), though Pharaoh does not.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto him, What is that in thine hand? And he said, A rod.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוָה מַה זֶּה בְיָדֶךָ וַיֹּאמֶר מַטֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav YHVH mah zzeh veyadekha vayomer matteh.
verses.study_note: God's question — 'What is that in thine hand?' — is one of Scripture's great divine inquiries. Like 'Where are you?' (Gen 3:9) and 'Where is Abel thy brother?' (Gen 4:9), God does not ask for information but to draw attention to what is already present. Moses holds a shepherd's rod (matteh) — the ordinary implement of his forty-year vocation in Midian. God's method is to take what is already in the servant's hand and transform it into an instrument of divine power. The principle recurs throughout Scripture: five loaves, a jawbone, a sling and stone. The rod of a shepherd becomes the rod of God (v.20).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Cast it on the ground. And he cast it on the ground, and it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הַשְׁלִיכֵהוּ אַרְצָה וַיַּשְׁלִכֵהוּ אַרְצָה וַיְהִי לְנָחָשׁ וַיָּנָס מֹשֶׁה מִפָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hashlikkhu artzah vayyashlikkehu artzah vayehi lenachash vayyanas Moshe mippanav.
verses.study_note: The first sign: the rod becomes a 'nachash' (serpent). Moses flees — an honest, human response to a suddenly animate snake. The serpent carried deep theological freight in the ancient world: in Egypt, the uraeus cobra on Pharaoh's crown was a symbol of his divine power and sovereignty. The transformation of Moses's humble rod into a serpent — and then back (v.4) — directly challenges Pharaonic authority on its own symbolic territory. Later, Pharaoh's own magicians replicate this sign with their rods (7:11-12), but Aaron's rod swallows theirs. The serpent on the rod anticipates the bronze serpent of Numbers 21 and ultimately Christ lifted up on the cross (John 3:14-15).
verses.doctrinal_note: The rod/serpent typology runs deep: (1) the serpent in Eden was the instrument of the Fall (Gen 3); (2) Moses's rod becoming a serpent shows God's mastery over the curse; (3) the bronze serpent on the pole (Num 21) — looking = living — is explicitly cited by Jesus as a type of His own crucifixion (John 3:14). He who knew no sin became sin (a serpent, accursed thing — Gal 3:13) that we might live. The rod of judgment becomes the instrument of salvation.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Put forth thine hand, and take it by the tail. And he put forth his hand, and caught it, and it became a rod in his hand:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה שְׁלַח יָדְךָ וֶאֱחֹז בִּזְנָבוֹ וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וַיַּחֲזֶק בּוֹ וַיְהִי לְמַטֶּה בְּכַפּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Moshe shelach yadekha ve'echaz biznavvo vayyishlach yado vayyachazek bo vayehi lematteh bekhappo.
verses.study_note: Moses is commanded to grab the serpent 'by the tail' — the most dangerous way to handle a snake. Snake handlers seize them behind the head. Grabbing the tail leaves you fully exposed to the serpent's strike. This is a test of trust: Moses must reach out in obedience, accepting vulnerability, and the serpent reverts to a rod 'in his palm' (bekhappo — literally 'in his hollow hand'). The restoration is immediate. The pattern throughout Scripture: death and resurrection, curse and redemption, judgment reversed in the hand of the obedient servant.

verses.kjv_text: That they may believe that the LORD God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath appeared unto thee.
verses.original_text: לְמַעַן יַאֲמִינוּ כִּי נִרְאָה אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתָם אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Lema'an ya'aminu ki nir'ah eleikha YHVH Elohei avotam Elohei Avraham Elohei Yitzhak vElohei Ya'akov.
verses.study_note: The purpose clause of the first sign: 'that they may believe' (lema'an ya'aminu). Signs are not for entertainment but for belief — faith grounded in evidence of divine action. God identifies Himself by the triple covenant formula: 'the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.' This is the same name He revealed at the burning bush (3:6) and which Jesus quotes to the Sadducees to prove resurrection: 'God is not the God of the dead, but of the living' (Matt 22:32). The covenant God is a God of continuity — His commitments span generations.
verses.doctrinal_note: The triple patriarchal formula appears repeatedly as the definitive self-identification of YHWH (3:6, 3:15, 3:16; Exod 32:13). It grounds Israel's entire identity in promise rather than achievement. Jesus uses it in Matthew 22:32 as proof of bodily resurrection: these men are still 'in the hand' of a living God — the covenant cannot be annulled by death.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said furthermore unto him, Put now thine hand into thy bosom. And he put his hand into his bosom: and when he took it out, behold, his hand was leprous as snow.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לוֹ עוֹד הָבֵא נָא יָדְךָ בְּחֵיקֶךָ וַיָּבֵא יָדוֹ בְּחֵיקוֹ וַיּוֹצִאָהּ וְהִנֵּה יָדוֹ מְצֹרַעַת כַּשָּׁלֶג׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH lo od have na yadekha becheikekha vayyave yado becheiko vayyotzi'ah vehineh yado metzora'at kashaleg.
verses.study_note: The second sign: Moses's hand becomes 'leprous as snow' (metzora'at kashaleg). Leprosy (tzara'at) in Israel was the quintessential symbol of divine judgment and ritual impurity — the living death. A man struck with tzara'at was cut off from the congregation, forced to cry 'Unclean! Unclean!' (Lev 13:45), and treated as a walking corpse. That God visits leprosy upon Moses's hand and immediately reverses it is a sign of God's absolute sovereignty over both affliction and healing, judgment and restoration. The description 'white as snow' (kashaleg) echoes Miriam's later judgment for opposing Moses — also described as 'white as snow' (Num 12:10).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Put thine hand into thy bosom again. And he put his hand into his bosom again; and plucked it out of his bosom, and, behold, it was turned again as his other flesh.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר הָשֵׁב יָדְךָ אֶל חֵיקֶךָ וַיָּשֶׁב יָדוֹ אֶל חֵיקוֹ וַיּוֹצִאָהּ מֵחֵיקוֹ וְהִנֵּה שָׁבָה כִּבְשָׂרוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer hashev yadekha el-cheikekha vayyashev yado el-cheiko vayyotzi'ah mecheiko vehineh shavah kivesaro.
verses.study_note: The reversal is immediate: 'it was turned again as his other flesh' (shavah kivesaro — restored like the rest of his body). The word 'shavah' (turned again, returned) is the word of restoration and return. The hand that was dead comes back to life. This sign carries a profound mini-gospel embedded within it: judgment entered (leprosy = death and exclusion), hidden in the bosom (burial), brought out again whole (resurrection). The pattern of death and resurrection is built into the very structure of the sign.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, if they will not believe thee, neither hearken to the voice of the first sign, that they will believe the voice of the latter sign.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה אִם לֹא יַאֲמִינוּ לָךְ וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ לְקֹל הָאֹת הָרִאשׁוֹן וְהֶאֱמִינוּ לְקֹל הָאֹת הָאַחֲרוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah im lo ya'aminu lakh velo yishme'u lekol ha'ot harrishon veheeminu lekol ha'ot ha'acharon.
verses.study_note: The escalating structure of the three signs reflects God's pastoral patience. He does not give one sign and demand acceptance. He provides layers of confirmation — a principle seen in Gideon's fleece (Judg 6:36-40), in the double Sabbath manna (Exod 16), and in the two-or-three witnesses principle (Deut 19:15; Matt 18:16). 'The voice of the sign' (kol ha'ot) is a striking phrase: signs 'speak,' they communicate a divine message. The sign is not mute power but articulate revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, if they will not believe also these two signs, neither hearken unto thy voice, that thou shalt take of the water of the river, and pour it upon the dry land: and the water which thou takest out of the river shall become blood upon the dry land.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה אִם לֹא יַאֲמִינוּ גַּם לִשְׁנֵי הָאֹתוֹת הָאֵלֶּה וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּן לְקֹלֶךָ וְלָקַחְתָּ מִמֵּימֵי הַיְאֹר וְשָׁפַכְתָּ הַיַּבָּשָׁה וְהָיוּ הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר תִּקַּח מִן הַיְאֹר וְהָיוּ לְדָם בַּיַּבָּשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah im lo ya'aminu gam lishnei ha'otot ha'elleh velo yishme'un lekoleikha velakachta mimmei haye'or veshafakhta hayabbashah vehayu hammayim asher tikkach min-haye'or vehayu ledam babbasheshet.
verses.study_note: The third sign escalates dramatically: Nile water becomes blood on dry land (yabbashah — the 'dry ground'). The Nile was the lifeblood of Egypt — Hapi, the god of the Nile, was among the most revered Egyptian deities. This sign previews the first of the ten plagues (7:20-21). The transformation of water to blood on dry ground is deeply symbolic: what gives life (water) becomes death (blood); what was gathered is poured out. The specific mention of 'dry land' (yabbashah) anticipates the dry ground of the parted sea (14:22). God gives this sign as a private preview before Moses even arrives in Egypt, establishing it as a prophetic foretaste of coming judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The three signs are arranged in an intentional theological progression: (1) the rod/serpent — authority over the symbol of Pharaoh's divine power; (2) the leprous and restored hand — authority over life, death, and the human body; (3) Nile water to blood — authority over Egypt's very source of life. Together, they declare: YHWH is Lord over Pharaoh's throne, over human flesh, and over Egypt's most sacred natural resource. They are a compressed anticipation of the ten plagues.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the LORD, O my LORD, I am not eloquent, neither heretofore, nor since thou hast spoken unto thy servant: but I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל יְהוָה בִּי אֲדֹנָי לֹא אִישׁ דְּבָרִים אָנֹכִי גַּם מִתְּמוֹל גַּם מִשִּׁלְשֹׁם גַּם מֵאָז דַּבֶּרְךָ אֶל עַבְדֶּךָ כִּי כְבַד פֶּה וּכְבַד לָשׁוֹן אָנֹכִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Moshe el-YHVH bi Adonai lo ish devarim anokhi gam mittmol gam mishilshom gam me'az dabberekha el-avdekha ki khevad peh ukhevad lashon anokhi.
verses.study_note: Moses's fifth objection (and his most poignant): 'I am not a man of words' (lo ish devarim anokhi). He reinforces it with triple 'gam' (also) — 'neither heretofore, nor since thou hast spoken unto thy servant.' Even this divine encounter has not cured his reticence! The description 'heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue' (kevad peh ukevad lashon) uses the word 'kaved' — heavy, weighty, the same root as 'glory' (kavod). There is deep irony: what Moses calls his weightiest deficiency (kevad — heavy tongue) is linguistically related to the word for God's greatest attribute (kavod — glory). Stephen's speech in Acts 7:22 tells us Moses was 'mighty in words and deeds' — raising the possibility that his affliction was a learned helplessness born from the decades in Midian, or a genuine speech difficulty. Either way, God's answer is definitive.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto him, Who hath made man’s mouth? or who maketh the dumb, or deaf, or the seeing, or the blind? have not I the LORD?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלָיו מִי שָׂם פֶּה לָאָדָם אוֹ מִי יָשׂוּם אִלֵּם אוֹ חֵרֵשׁ אוֹ פִקֵּחַ אוֹ עִוֵּר הֲלֹא אָנֹכִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH elav mi sam peh la'adam o mi yasum illem o cheresh o pikkei'ach o ivver halo anokhi YHVH.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically dense verses in the Torah. God answers Moses's speech objection not with a cure or a reassurance, but with a question: 'Who made man's mouth?' The rhetorical answer encompasses all human sensory states — mute, deaf, seeing, and blind. God claims sovereign authorship over every form of human faculty and limitation. This verse became foundational in Jewish and Christian theology of disability: suffering and limitation are not outside God's purview but within His sovereign design and purpose. Jesus explicitly references this theological framework in John 9:3 when His disciples ask whose sin caused a man's blindness: 'Neither hath this man sinned, nor his parents: but that the works of God should be made manifest in him.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine sovereignty over human disability and limitation. God does not promise Moses a new tongue — He claims that the slow tongue He already has is His design, and that He will speak through it. This cuts through every performance-based view of ministry: the deliverer of Israel is not chosen for his eloquence but despite its absence. Paul echoes this in 2 Corinthians 12:9 — 'My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness.' God is glorified most when His power operates through what the world considers inadequate.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore go, and I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לֵךְ וְאָנֹכִי אֶהְיֶה עִם פִּיךָ וְהוֹרֵיתִיךָ אֲשֶׁר תְּדַבֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lekh ve'anokhi ehyeh im pikha vehoretikha asher tedabber.
verses.study_note: God's promise is not to change Moses's mouth but to be present with it: 'I will be with thy mouth' (anokhi ehyeh im pikha). The phrase 'ehyeh' (I will be) is the name God revealed at the burning bush (3:14 — 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh,' I AM WHO I AM). The divine presence itself is Moses's eloquence. God will 'teach' (yoreh — from the same root as 'Torah') what to say — the Lawgiver teaches the law before it is given. Jesus echoes this promise to His disciples: 'Take no thought beforehand what ye shall speak... for it is not ye that speak, but the Holy Ghost' (Mark 13:11).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, O my LORD, send, I pray thee, by the hand of him whom thou wilt send.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר בִּי אֲדֹנָי שְׁלַח נָא בְּיַד תִּשְׁלָח׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer bi Adonai shelach na beyad tishlach.
verses.study_note: Moses's most naked and honest objection — and the most transparent: 'Send by the hand of whomever You will send.' In Hebrew it is almost cryptic: 'shelach na beyad tishlach' — 'send, please, by the hand you will send.' He does not say 'send someone else' explicitly but the implication is unmistakable: 'anyone but me.' This is not a theological objection but a personal one — the purest expression of inadequacy and fear. Remarkably, God has answered every objection with promise and provision. Here He does the same — but for the first time, His 'anger is kindled' (v.14). Grace absorbs the refusals; even so, human refusal has consequences.

verses.kjv_text: And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Moses, and he said, Is not Aaron the Levite thy brother? I know that he can speak well. And also, behold, he cometh forth to meet thee: and when he seeth thee, he will be glad in his heart.
verses.original_text: וַיִּחַר אַף יְהוָה בְּמֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר הֲלֹא אַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ הַלֵּוִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי דַבֵּר יְדַבֵּר הוּא וְגַם הִנֵּה הוּא יֹצֵא לִקְרָאתֶךָ וְרָאֲךָ וְשָׂמַח בְּלִבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyichar af YHVH beMoshe vayomer halo Aharon achikha halevi yada'ti ki dabber yedabber hu vegam hineh hu yotze likratekha vera'akha vesamach belibbo.
verses.study_note: The first time in the Exodus narrative that God's anger is said to be 'kindled' (vayyichar af — literally 'the nose of the LORD burned'). There is a limit to divine patience with human refusal even under gracious persuasion. Yet remarkably, even in anger, God provides: He announces that Aaron is already on his way ('he cometh forth to meet thee' — a God-orchestrated meeting). God's anger does not rescind the call; it modifies the arrangement. Aaron's gladness when he sees Moses ('he will be glad in his heart') is a touching detail: while Moses has spent this conversation arguing with God, Aaron has been walking toward his brother with joy. The appointment of Aaron as spokesman, however, is not without cost — it introduces a mediatorial structure that later leads to the golden calf (Exod 32:1-6).
verses.doctrinal_note: The appointment of Aaron reveals the double-edged nature of accommodation to human weakness. God grants Moses's implicit request for a spokesman — but this is presented as accommodation, not ideal. Aaron becomes a layer of mediation between Moses and the people that introduces vulnerabilities. Yet God's sovereign purposes are not frustrated: Aaron is already moving in God's providence ('he cometh forth') even before Moses has agreed to go. God's plans do not depend on human willingness — they include human reluctance.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt speak unto him, and put words in his mouth: and I will be with thy mouth, and with his mouth, and will teach you what ye shall do.
verses.original_text: וְדִבַּרְתָּ אֵלָיו וְשַׂמְתָּ אֶת הַדְּבָרִים בְּפִיו וְאָנֹכִי אֶהְיֶה עִם פִּיךָ וְעִם פִּיהוּ וְהוֹרֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם אֵת אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשׂוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vedibarta elav vesamta et-haddevarim befiv ve'anokhi ehyeh im pikha ve'im pihu vehoretikha etkhem et asher ta'asun.
verses.study_note: The chain of communication is established: God → Moses → Aaron → People. Moses puts the words in Aaron's mouth; God puts the words in Moses's mouth. This layered structure of divine speech — God speaking through a mediator who speaks through another mediator — is a foreshadowing of prophetic inspiration itself, and points ultimately to Christ who is the Word of God (John 1:1) speaking through anointed vessels by His Spirit (2 Pet 1:21). The double 'ehyeh im' (I will be with): 'I will be with thy mouth AND with his mouth' — God's presence accompanies both men simultaneously.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall be thy spokesman unto the people: and he shall be, even he shall be to thee instead of a mouth, and thou shalt be to him instead of God.
verses.original_text: וְדִבֶּר הוּא לְךָ אֶל הָעָם וְהָיָה הוּא יִהְיֶה לְּךָ לְפֶה וְאַתָּה תִּהְיֶה לּוֹ לֵאלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vedibber hu lekha el-ha'am vehayah hu yihyeh lekha lefeh ve'attah tihyeh lo le'Elohim.
verses.study_note: The most theologically charged phrase in the chapter: 'thou shalt be to him as God' (le'Elohim). Moses stands to Aaron in the same relationship that God stands to Moses — the ultimate source of the message, the one behind the words. Aaron will be Moses's 'mouth' (lefe — literally, his 'face/front'); Moses will be Aaron's 'Elohim.' This establishes a profound typological pattern: the relationship between Moses and Aaron mirrors in miniature the relationship between God and His human spokesmen. Later, this language is applied inversely to prophecy: the prophet is as the 'mouth' of God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Moses/Aaron relationship as a type of divine-human communication: (1) God speaks to Moses (direct revelation); (2) Moses speaks to Aaron (mediated transmission); (3) Aaron speaks to Israel (public proclamation). This three-stage model of inspired speech (revelation → transmission → proclamation) anticipates the pattern of Old Testament prophecy and ultimately New Testament apostolic ministry. In Christ, the mediation collapses into unity: He is simultaneously the Word of God (revelation), the bearer of the message (transmission), and the preacher (proclamation) — Hebrews 1:1-2.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take this rod in thine hand, wherewith thou shalt do signs.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת הַמַּטֶּה הַזֶּה תִּקַּח בְּיָדֶךָ אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂה בּוֹ אֶת הָאֹתֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et hammatteh hazzeh tikkach beyadekha asher ta'aseh bo et-ha'otot.
verses.study_note: The closing charge of the burning bush encounter: take the rod. All the dialogue about signs, speech, Aaron — it reduces to this: take the ordinary shepherd's staff. God ends the entire commissioning with a tangible object. The rod will do the signs (otot); the rod of a shepherd becomes the rod of God (matteh ha'Elohim — v.20). Ordinary things, held in surrendered hands, become instruments of divine power. The 'this rod' (hammatteh hazzeh) is emphatic — the specific, familiar, unimpressive stick already in Moses's possession is the vehicle of all that follows.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father in law, and said unto him, Let me go, I pray thee, and return unto my brethren which are in Egypt, and see whether they be yet alive. And Jethro said to Moses, Go in peace.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ מֹשֶׁה וַיָּשָׁב אֶל יֶתֶר חֹתְנוֹ וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֵלְכָה נָּא וְאָשׁוּבָה אֶל אַחַי אֲשֶׁר בְּמִצְרַיִם וְאֶרְאֶה הַיּוֹשְׁבוּ עוֹד וַיֹּאמֶר יֶתְרוֹ לְמֹשֶׁה לֵךְ לְשָׁלוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeylekh Moshe vayyashav el-Yeter chotno vayomer lo elkhah na ve'ashuva el-achai asher beMitzrayim ve'er'eh hayoshvu od vayomer Yitro leMoshe lekh leshalom.
verses.study_note: Moses leaves the burning bush and returns to Jethro with a carefully worded request: not 'God has sent me to deliver Israel' but 'let me return to my brothers in Egypt to see if they are still alive.' Moses is not yet fully transparent about his commission — perhaps to protect Jethro from anxiety, or still processing the enormity of what has been asked. Jethro is here called 'Yeter' — an alternate name meaning 'abundance/excellence.' His dismissal, 'Go in peace' (lekh leshalom), is the most generous and freeing response possible: the old father-in-law releases his servant-son with the full blessing of shalom.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses in Midian, Go, return into Egypt: for all the men are dead which sought thy life.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה בְּמִדְיָן לֵךְ שֻׁב מִצְרָיִם כִּי מֵתוּ כָּל הָאֲנָשִׁים הַמְבַקְשִׁים אֶת נַפְשֶׁךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Moshe beMidyan lekh shuv Mitzrayim ki metu kol ha'anashim hamevakkeshim et nafshekha.
verses.study_note: God provides one final divine assurance for Moses's journey: the men who sought his life (for killing the Egyptian overseer — 2:12-15) are dead. After 40 years in Midian, the old threat has expired. This detail closely parallels Matthew 2:20 — the angel's instruction to Joseph to return from Egypt with Jesus because 'they are dead which sought the young child's life.' The Matthean echo is deliberate: Jesus's early life recapitulates Moses's, including the flight from a murderous ruler, the sojourn in Egypt, and the return after death of the persecutors. Moses the type; Jesus the antitype.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took his wife and his sons, and set them upon an ass, and he returned to the land of Egypt: and Moses took the rod of God in his hand.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת אִשְׁתּוֹ וְאֶת בָּנָיו וַיַּרְכִּבֵם עַל הַחֲמֹר וַיָּשָׁב אַרְצָה מִצְרָיִם וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת מַטֵּה הָאֱלֹהִים בְּיָדוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach Moshe et-ishto ve'et-banav vayyarkivem al-hachamor vayyashav artzah Mitzrayim vayyikkach Moshe et-matteh ha'Elohim beyado.
verses.study_note: The transition from ordinary life to divine mission is captured in two parallel actions: Moses takes his family and then takes the rod of God. The shepherd's rod (matteh — v.2) has become 'the rod of God' (matteh ha'Elohim) — the same object, transformed by divine appointment. The simple donkey, the family, the staff — Moses carries all of ordinary life into the extraordinary commission. This is not a dramatic hero's departure but a quiet, domestic journey into history. 'The rod of God in his hand' — the last phrase of the verse is a statement of divine empowerment carried in the everyday.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, When thou goest to return into Egypt, see that thou do all those wonders before Pharaoh, which I have put in thine hand: but I will harden his heart, that he shall not let the people go.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה בְּלֶכְתְּךָ לָשׁוּב מִצְרַיְמָה רְאֵה כָּל הַמֹּפְתִים אֲשֶׁר שַׂמְתִּי בְיָדֶךָ וַעֲשִׂיתָם לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וַאֲנִי אֲחַזֵּק אֶת לִבּוֹ וְלֹא יְשַׁלַּח אֶת הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Moshe belekhtekha lashuv Mitzraymah re'eh kol hamofetim asher samti veyadekha va'asitam lifnei Far'oh va'ani achazzek et-libbo velo yeshalach et-ha'am.
verses.study_note: The first announcement of Pharaoh's hardened heart — stated before Moses even reaches Egypt. 'I will harden' (achazzek — I will strengthen/harden — from 'chazak,' to be strong). Throughout the plague narrative, both 'hardening' verbs are used: 'chazak' (God strengthens Pharaoh's heart) and 'kashah' (Pharaoh hardens his own heart). The biblical text distributes both forms of hardening across the ten plagues: Pharaoh hardens his own heart (8:15, 8:32, 9:34), and God hardens it (9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10, 14:8). The wonders are called 'mofetim' (portents, prodigies) here rather than 'otot' (signs) — a related but distinct word emphasizing their astonishing, overwhelming character.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is one of Scripture's most challenging theological statements about divine sovereignty and human responsibility. Paul treats it directly in Romans 9:17-18: 'For this cause have I raised thee up, that I might show my power in thee, and that my name might be declared throughout all the earth.' The key hermeneutical principle: hardening must be understood not as the creation of evil in Pharaoh but as the judicial confirmation of a heart already committed to rebellion. God's hardening is the divine sealing of a choice Pharaoh has already made — a righteous judgment on persistent, informed, defiant refusal. The same principle appears in Romans 1:24-28: God 'gave them over' to what they had already chosen.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt say unto Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD, Israel is my son, even my firstborn:
verses.original_text: וְאָמַרְתָּ אֶל פַּרְעֹה כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה בְּנִי בְכֹרִי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amarta el-Far'oh koh amar YHVH beni vekhori Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically loaded statements in the Old Testament: 'Israel is my son, my firstborn' (beni vekhori Yisra'el). This is the first time in Scripture that God calls a people His 'son.' The term 'bekhor' (firstborn) carried the highest honor in the ancient world — the firstborn son held the birthright, the double portion, the patriarchal blessing, the leadership of the family. God assigns to Israel the supreme status of firstborn son in the family of nations. This is not biological sonship but covenantal: adopted by grace, chosen not by merit. 'My son' (beni) — singular possessive, as intimate as a father's word to his child.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'firstborn son' language in this verse is one of Scripture's great typological foundations: (1) Israel as God's firstborn son, rescued from Egypt through the blood of the Passover lamb; (2) Jesus as God's true and ultimate Firstborn Son (Col 1:15, 18; Heb 1:6; Rom 8:29), the one in whom and through whom all the Israel-sonship is fulfilled. Matthew's citation of Hosea 11:1 — 'Out of Egypt have I called my son' (Matt 2:15) — deliberately applies the Israel-narrative to Jesus: He is the true Son who recapitulates Israel's story, succeeds where Israel failed, and fulfills what Israel could only foreshadow. Believers are then adopted as 'sons of God' in Him (Gal 4:4-7, Rom 8:15-17).

verses.kjv_text: And I say unto thee, Let my son go, that he may serve me: and if thou refuse to let him go, behold, I will slay thy son, even thy firstborn.
verses.original_text: וָאֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ שַׁלַּח אֶת בְּנִי וְיַעַבְדֵנִי וַתְּמָאֵן לְשַׁלְּחוֹ הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי הֹרֵג אֶת בִּנְךָ בְּכֹרֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'omar eleikha shallach et-beni veya'avdeni vattime'en leshalleho hinneh anokhi horeg et-binkha bekhorekha.
verses.study_note: The devastating logic of divine judgment: Pharaoh holds God's firstborn (Israel) in bondage; therefore, God will strike Pharaoh's firstborn. This is the first explicit announcement of the tenth plague (Exod 12), declared before Moses has even entered Egypt, before the first plague has fallen. The symmetry is precise: My son → your son; My firstborn → your firstborn. The punishment fits the crime in the most direct possible way. 'Thou hast refused to let him go' (wattime'en) — Pharaoh's refusal is stated as accomplished fact even before the confrontation begins, because God sees the end from the beginning.
verses.doctrinal_note: The firstborn son judgment forms the decisive theological center of the Exodus: God's firstborn (Israel) is redeemed through the death of Egypt's firstborns, and ultimately through the death of the Passover lamb. The New Testament reads this as a type of the ultimate exchange: God's own Firstborn Son (Jesus) dies so that God's many adopted sons might live (Rom 8:29-32; Heb 12:23). 'He that spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all' (Rom 8:32) — God does to His own Firstborn what He threatened to do to Pharaoh's. The great reversal of the gospel is embedded in Exodus 4:23.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass by the way in the inn, that the LORD met him, and sought to kill him.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַדֶּרֶךְ בַּמָּלוֹן וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ יְהוָה וַיְבַקֵּשׁ הֲמִיתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi vaderrekh bammalon vayyifgeshehu YHVH vayvakkesh hamito.
verses.study_note: The most enigmatic and disturbing verse in Exodus 4 — perhaps in all the Torah. God, who has just commissioned Moses as Israel's deliverer, now seeks to kill him at the lodging place. The text gives no immediate explanation. The resolution (vv.25-26) makes clear: Moses's son had not been circumcised. The deliverer of God's covenant people had neglected to circumcise his own son — the foundational covenant sign given to Abraham (Gen 17:10-14). It is inconceivable for the leader of circumcised Israel to have an uncircumcised son. The one called to lead Israel in covenant faithfulness must himself be in covenant faithfulness. The 'inn' (malon — a lodging place for the night) becomes the scene of mortal divine confrontation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theological principle embedded in this disturbing scene: God's calling and commission do not exempt a person from covenant obligation. The deliverer must first be delivered. The priest must first be consecrated. Before Moses can demand that Pharaoh release God's son (v.22), Moses's own son must bear the covenant mark. This is not legalism but integrity: you cannot lead others into covenant while your own household is outside it. The New Testament parallel: 'one that ruleth well his own house' (1 Tim 3:4-5). Circumcision is not a minor formality — it is the blood sign of covenant, the mark that says 'this life belongs to God.' Without it, Moses stands in the same position as Pharaoh: holding a son who does not bear the covenant mark.

verses.kjv_text: Then Zipporah took a sharp stone, and cut off the foreskin of her son, and cast it at his feet, and said, Surely a bloody husband art thou to me.
verses.original_text: וַתִּקַּח צִפֹּרָה צֹר וַתִּכְרֹת אֶת עָרְלַת בְּנָהּ וַתַּגַּע לְרַגְלָיו וַתֹּאמֶר כִּי חֲתַן דָּמִים אַתָּה לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vattikkach Tzipporoah tzor vattikhrot et-orlat benah vattagga lereglav vattomer ki chatan damim attah li.
verses.study_note: Zipporah acts decisively where Moses could not (or would not). She takes a 'flint stone' (tzor — volcanic rock used as a blade) and circumcises her son. The phrase 'cast it at his feet' (vattagga lereglav) — the antecedent of 'his feet' is debated: most commentators refer it to Moses, with Zipporah touching the foreskin to Moses's feet as a dramatic gesture of performed covenant. 'A bloody husband' (chatan damim) — the word 'chatan' means both 'bridegroom' and 'son-in-law.' In Arabic cognate usage, 'chatan' specifically refers to 'one who is circumcised' — making this phrase a covenant-naming act: 'You are a blood-covenant husband to me.' The blood of circumcision became the blood of the covenant that spared Moses's life.

verses.kjv_text: So he let him go: then she said, A bloody husband thou art, because of the circumcision.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרֶף מִמֶּנּוּ אָז אָמְרָה חֲתַן דָּמִים לַמּוּלֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiref mimmenu az amrah chatan damim lammulot.
verses.study_note: The crisis is resolved as suddenly as it came: 'he let him go' (vayyiref — released, left off). The covenant blood of circumcision satisfied the divine demand. Zipporah's declaration is now given in full explanation: 'a bridegroom of bloods because of the circumcision' (lammulot — 'because of the circumcisions'). The plural 'damim' (bloods) may refer to the father's life and the son's circumcision blood — two lives covered by one act. Zipporah, a Midianite woman, becomes the unlikely instrument of covenant faithfulness that preserves the deliverer's life. The outsider acts where the insider had been passive.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Bridegroom of Blood as a type of Christ: just as Moses's life was spared through covenant blood (circumcision), and just as Moses was constituted a 'blood-husband' by that act, Christ is the ultimate 'Bridegroom' who purchased His Bride (the Church) through His own blood (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7-9). He who had no sin became 'made sin' (2 Cor 5:21), the ultimate covenant blood act, that all who are united to Him — circumcised of heart (Col 2:11-12) — might be spared the divine judgment their uncircumcision deserved.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said to Aaron, Go into the wilderness to meet Moses. And he went, and met him in the mount of God, and kissed him.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל אַהֲרֹן לֵךְ לִקְרַאת מֹשֶׁה הַמִּדְבָּרָה וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ בְּהַר הָאֱלֹהִים וַיִּשַּׁק לוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Aharon lekh likrat Moshe hamidbarah vayyeylekh vayyifgeshehu behar ha'Elohim vayyishak lo.
verses.study_note: God speaks to Aaron — the only direct divine communication to Aaron in the chapter. While Moses has been at the burning bush and on the road, God has been simultaneously preparing Aaron. Aaron walks into the desert not knowing why, only that God said to go — and meets his brother 'at the mountain of God' (har ha'Elohim), the very place of the burning bush theophany. The kiss (vayyishak) is the greeting of deep familial affection. These brothers have not seen each other for forty years. The reunion is arranged by God and sealed with covenant family love — a touching, human moment in the middle of an immense divine drama.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses told Aaron all the words of the LORD who had sent him, and all the signs which he had commanded him.
verses.original_text: וַיַּגֵּד מֹשֶׁה לְאַהֲרֹן אֵת כָּל דִּבְרֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר שְׁלָחוֹ וְאֵת כָּל הָאֹתֹת אֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyagged Moshe le'Aharon et kol divrei YHVH asher shelaho ve'et kol ha'otot asher tzivvahu.
verses.study_note: Moses fulfills the first function of his commission: he speaks to Aaron. The transmission of revelation begins. Two categories of content are passed on: 'all the words of the LORD' (divrei YHVH — the message content) and 'all the signs' (ha'otot — the authenticating miracles). Word and sign together: proclamation without authentication is bare assertion; authentication without proclamation lacks content. The combination — the word accompanied by signs — is the New Testament model of apostolic ministry (Mark 16:20, Acts 14:3).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron went and gathered together all the elders of the children of Israel:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלֶךְ מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן וַיַּאַסְפוּ אֶת כָּל זִקְנֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyeylekh Moshe ve'Aharon vayyasfu et kol ziknei venei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: Moses and Aaron immediately and jointly execute the mission — there is no delay, no further negotiation. 'All the elders of the children of Israel' (kol ziknei venei Yisra'el) — the 'elders' (zekhenim — literally 'bearded ones,' the tribal and clan leaders) are the official representatives of the people. This assembly is the fulfillment of God's instruction in 3:16: 'Go, and gather the elders of Israel together.' The commission moves from burning bush to assembly in this one verse — all the dialogue, all the objections, all the promises, now become concrete action.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron spake all the words which the LORD had spoken unto Moses, and did the signs in the sight of the people.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אַהֲרֹן אֵת כָּל הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה וַיַּעַשׂ הָאֹתֹת לְעֵינֵי הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Aharon et kol haddevarim asher dibber YHVH el-Moshe vayya'as ha'otot le'einei ha'am.
verses.study_note: The chain of divine communication reaches its human terminus: Aaron speaks the words, Moses (or Aaron) performs the signs 'in the sight of the people' (le'einei ha'am — before the eyes of the people). Public, visible, verifiable — the signs are not performed secretly but openly before the assembled elders. 'All the words' and 'the signs' — the same dual content transmitted to Aaron in v.28 is now delivered to Israel. The communication chain is complete: YHWH → Moses → Aaron → Israel. Notice that the text says Aaron spoke 'all the words which the LORD had spoken unto Moses' — Aaron is faithful to the transmission without addition or subtraction.

verses.kjv_text: And the people believed: and when they heard that the LORD had visited the children of Israel, and that he had looked upon their affliction, then they bowed their heads and worshipped.
verses.original_text: וַיַּאֲמֵן הָעָם וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ כִּי פָקַד יְהוָה אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְכִי רָאָה אֶת עָנְיָם וַיִּקְּדוּ וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaamen ha'am vayyishme'u ki fakad YHVH et benei Yisra'el vekhi ra'ah et-anyam vayyikkodu vayyishtachavu.
verses.study_note: The chapter's final verse is one of the most moving in Exodus. Moses feared they would not believe (v.1); they believed (vayyaamen — from the root of 'Amen,' the same root as 'believed' in v.1 and v.5, creating a beautiful inclusion). Two truths ignite their faith: (1) God has 'visited' (pakad) them — the word means to number, to attend to, to intervene with intent; (2) God has 'seen' (ra'ah) their affliction (anyyam — their misery, oppression). The slave people who had no voice hear that Heaven has taken notice. Their response is not celebration or combat planning — it is worship. 'They bowed their heads and worshipped' (vayyikkodu vayyishtachavu) — the instinctive response of a people who have just learned that God has remembered them. The chapter that opened with Moses's objections closes with Israel's worship — a complete theological arc.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine 'visitation' (pekudah) is one of Scripture's great theological concepts: God's purposeful, intervening attention to His people. The same word is used when God 'visited' Sarah (Gen 21:1), when Joseph in Egypt predicted that God would 'visit' Israel and bring them out (Gen 50:24-25), and when Zechariah in the New Testament blesses God for 'visiting and redeeming his people' (Luke 1:68 — epeskepsato). The entire chapter's movement — from Moses's doubting objections (v.1) to Israel's unanimous worship (v.31) — is a parable of the gospel: the reluctant messenger, the authenticating signs, the announcement of redemption, and the response of faith and worship. Moses's fear that Israel would not believe (v.1) is answered: the Amen-people say Amen to the Amen-God.

meta.overview: Exodus 4 is a chapter of divine confrontation and human capitulation. Moses's final objection — 'they will not believe me' — is met not with argument but with demonstration: God turns a rod into a serpent, a hand leprous and whole again, and water into blood. Three signs, each a miniature gospel of death and resurrection power. When Moses pleads his inadequacy of speech, God's response is stunning: 'Who made man's mouth?' The chapter reaches its theological peak in vv.22-23 where God declares Israel to be His 'firstborn son,' and the threat to slay Pharaoh's firstborn — introducing the Passover logic before the Passover arrives. The harrowing 'Bridegroom of Blood' incident (vv.24-26) reveals that the deliverer himself must first submit to the covenant sign. Aaron is appointed, the elders believe, and Israel bows in worship for the first time since their oppression began.
meta.theme: Divine Authentication, the Deliverer's Weakness & Israel as God's Firstborn Son
meta.keyWordMeaning: Sign/Miracle — a supernatural act that points beyond itself to authenticate a divine messenger and reveal God's character. The root means 'to mark, to designate.' Signs in Exodus are not ends in themselves but witnesses to YHWH's identity and power.
outline: Moses's Final Objection and the Three Authenticating Signs (vv.1-9): Rod→Serpent, Leprous Hand, Water→Blood
Moses Pleads Inadequacy of Speech (vv.10-12): 'Who made man's mouth?'
Moses's Ultimate Refusal and God's Anger — Aaron Appointed (vv.13-17)
Moses Returns to Egypt — Permission from Jethro and Commission from God (vv.18-20)
The Rod of God and the Hardened Heart — Israel My Firstborn Son (vv.21-23)
The Bridegroom of Blood — Zipporah Circumcises Her Son (vv.24-26)
Aaron Meets Moses — The Signs Performed Before the Elders (vv.27-30)
Israel Believes and Worships (v.31)

verses.kjv_text: And afterward Moses and Aaron went in, and told Pharaoh, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto me in the wilderness.
verses.original_text: וְאַחַר בָּאוּ מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל פַּרְעֹה כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל שַׁלַּח אֶת עַמִּי וְיָחֹגּוּ לִי בַּמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'achar ba'u Moshe ve'Aharon vayyomeru el-Far'oh koh amar YHVH Elohei Yisra'el shallach et-ammi veyachogu li bamidbar.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the word 'afterward' (ve'achar) — signaling that Moses and Aaron went first to the elders (4:29-31) before approaching Pharaoh. The chain of authority is honored: Israel first, then Pharaoh. The demand is delivered with the full prophetic messenger formula: 'Thus saith the LORD God of Israel' (koh amar YHVH Elohei Yisra'el). The initial request is deliberately modest and religiously framed: 'Let my people go that they may hold a feast' (veyachogu — from 'chagag,' to celebrate a festival, to dance in a circle). The word 'chag' is the source of the three great pilgrimage feasts of Israel. Importantly, Moses does not open with 'free the slaves permanently' but with a request for a three-day wilderness festival — testing Pharaoh's response at its mildest. The contrast between 'my people' (ammi — God's claim) and Pharaoh's later 'my people' (ammi — Pharaoh's claim, v.5) makes the confrontation explicit: Who owns Israel?
verses.doctrinal_note: The liberation demand is inseparable from the worship imperative: 'Let my people go, that they may hold a feast unto ME.' Freedom and worship are linked from the beginning of the Exodus narrative. Israel is not freed for self-determination but for covenant worship. This pattern is foundational: redemption always moves toward worship (cf. Rev 5:9-10 — 'Thou wast slain, and hast redeemed us... and we shall reign on the earth'). The New Testament parallel: 'You are not your own; you are bought with a price: therefore glorify God in your body' (1 Cor 6:19-20). Liberation from slavery is liberation for worship, not liberation from all obligation.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said, Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice to let Israel go? I know not the LORD, neither will I let Israel go.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה מִי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר אֶשְׁמַע בְּקֹלוֹ לְשַׁלַּח אֶת יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא יָדַעְתִּי אֶת יְהוָה וְגַם אֶת יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Far'oh mi YHVH asher eshma bekolo leshalach et-Yisra'el lo yada'ti et-YHVH vegam et-Yisra'el lo ashalleach.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically charged statements in the Old Testament: 'Who is YHWH?' (mi YHVH). Pharaoh's question is not philosophical curiosity — it is contemptuous dismissal. In the ancient Near East, to know a god's name was to acknowledge his power and domain. Pharaoh denies both the knowledge of YHWH and any obligation to obey Him: 'I know not (lo yada'ti) the LORD.' The word 'yada' (to know) here is covenantal acknowledgment, not merely cognitive familiarity. 'Lo yada'ti' — I have no covenantal relationship with, I do not recognize the authority of, YHWH. The entire plague narrative is God's progressive answer to this question — each plague is a lesson in the knowledge of YHWH ('that you may KNOW that I am the LORD' — 7:17, 8:10, 8:22, 9:14, etc.). The ten plagues are ten chapters in God's self-revelation to a man who said 'I know not YHWH.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Pharaoh's 'I know not the LORD' becomes the theological problem the entire plague sequence is designed to solve. God will MAKE Himself known through judgment when He refuses to be known through testimony. This principle runs through Scripture: Pharaoh who says 'lo yada'ti' (I know not) is progressively educated in who YHWH is through catastrophe. Paul quotes this episode in Romans 9:17: 'For this purpose I raised thee up, that I might show my power in thee, and that my name might be declared throughout all the earth.' The willful ignorance of divine authority does not exempt one from divine accountability — it escalates the revelation until the name is known, if necessary through judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And they said, The God of the Hebrews hath met with us: let us go, we pray thee, three days’ journey into the desert, and sacrifice unto the LORD our God; lest he fall upon us with pestilence, or with the sword.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים נִקְרָא עָלֵינוּ נֵלְכָה נָּא דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר וְנִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ פֶּן יִפְגָּעֵנוּ בַּדֶּבֶר אוֹ בֶחָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru Elohei ha'Ivrim nikra aleinu nelkhah na derekh sheloshet yamim bamidbar venizevchah la'YHVH Eloheinu pen yifga'enu baddefer o vecharev.
verses.study_note: Moses and Aaron shift their language slightly before Pharaoh: not 'God of Israel' (covenantal name) but 'God of the Hebrews' (Elohei ha'Ivrim — the ethnic-geographic designation used with outsiders). They add a note of urgency: if Israel does not worship, God will fall upon them 'with pestilence (dever) or with the sword (cherev).' This is not merely a rhetorical strategy — it is theologically true. The God who demands worship from His people does not hold their failure to worship costless. The word 'nikra aleinu' (has met with us, has encountered us — literally 'has called upon us') describes a divine summons that cannot be ignored. The three-day journey is the same modest request as 3:18 — God's initial escalation strategy begins small.

verses.kjv_text: And the king of Egypt said unto them, Wherefore do ye, Moses and Aaron, let the people from their works? get you unto your burdens.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם לָמָּה מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן תַּפְרִיעוּ אֶת הָעָם מִמַּעֲשָׂיו לְכוּ לְסִבְלֹתֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer alehem melekh Mitzrayim lammah Moshe ve'Aharon tafri'u et-ha'am mimma'asav lekhu lesiveloteikhem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh now addresses Moses and Aaron directly by name — a significant shift. He reframes the religious request as political subversion: 'Why do you make the people idle (tafri'u — disturb, let loose, make unruly) from their works?' The verb 'para' (to let loose, to disturb order) is the same root used in Exodus 32:25 when the people 'ran wild' (para) under Aaron's leadership at the golden calf — suggesting Pharaoh's language frames Moses as an agitator. His counter-command: 'Get you unto your burdens' (seveloteikhem — from 'sevalah,' forced labor). He addresses them as slaves, not as ambassadors of a divine king. The title 'king of Egypt' (melekh Mitzrayim) is used here — a deliberate literary contrast with the 'LORD God of Israel' (v.1): two kings, one territory, one people.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said, Behold, the people of the land now are many, and ye make them rest from their burdens.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה הֵן רַבִּים עַתָּה עַם הָאָרֶץ וְהִשְׁבַּתֶּם אֹתָם מִסִּבְלֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Far'oh hen rabbim attah am ha'aretz vehishbattem otam missivelotam.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's assessment is strategic and reveals his fear: 'the people of the land now are many' (rabbim — numerous). This echoes 1:9 where a previous Pharaoh said 'the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we.' Population = threat in Pharaoh's political calculus. 'Ye make them rest' (hishbattem — from 'shabbat') — the word is the Sabbath-word. Pharaoh unconsciously uses the Sabbath-rest word to describe what he most fears and refuses: rest for Israel. The one who should be giving Israel Sabbath rest uses 'shabbat' as a word of accusation against the very men God commissioned to bring that rest.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh commanded the same day the taskmasters of the people, and their officers, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְצַו פַּרְעֹה בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת הַנֹּגְשִׂים בָּעָם וְאֶת שֹׁטְרָיו לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayetzav Far'oh bayyom hahu et-hannogeshim ba'am ve'et shoterav lemor.
verses.study_note: The immediacy of Pharaoh's response is striking: 'the same day' (bayyom hahu). There is no deliberation, no period of consideration — the moment Moses and Aaron leave, Pharaoh issues a punitive decree. The word 'taskmasters' (nogesim — from 'nagas,' to press, to oppress, to drive with force) appears in Isaiah 9:4 for oppressors, and Isaiah 53:7 says of the Suffering Servant: 'He was oppressed (nagas) and he was afflicted.' A two-tiered supervisory structure is revealed: Egyptian 'nogesim' (taskmasters) over Israelite 'shoterim' (officers/foremen). The Israelite overseers are responsible to the Egyptian taskmasters and accountable to their own people — a system designed to create internal pressure and division.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall no more give the people straw to make brick, as heretofore: let them go and gather straw for themselves.
verses.original_text: לֹא תֹאסִפוּן לָתֵת תֶּבֶן לָעָם לִלְבֹּן הַלְּבֵנִים כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם הֵם יֵלְכוּ וְקֹשְׁשׁוּ לָהֶם תֶּבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tosilfun latet teven la'am livbon hallevenim kitmol shileshom hem yelekhu vekosheshu lahem teven.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's punishment is calculated cruelty: the quota of bricks is maintained but the straw supply is withdrawn. In Egyptian brick-making, straw or chaff was mixed with Nile mud to bind the clay and prevent cracking — a technique archaeologically confirmed in ancient Egyptian brickmaking. Removing the straw while keeping the quota is not merely impractical — it is designed to make success physically impossible while the failure to meet the target can be punished. The word 'teven' (straw/stubble/chaff) becomes a rich theological symbol: Malachi 4:1 describes the wicked as 'stubble' (kash — related to the 'kosheshu' of this verse) consumed on the day of judgment. The straw Israel must gather in humiliation becomes the image of what God will do to their oppressors.

verses.kjv_text: And the tale of the bricks, which they did make heretofore, ye shall lay upon them; ye shall not diminish ought thereof: for they be idle; therefore they cry, saying, Let us go and sacrifice to our God.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת מַתְכֹּנֶת הַלְּבֵנִים אֲשֶׁר הֵם עֹשִׂים תְּמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם תָּשִׂימוּ עֲלֵיהֶם לֹא תִגְרְעוּ מִמֶּנּוּ כִּי נִרְפִּים הֵם עַל כֵּן הֵם צֹעֲקִים לֵאמֹר נֵלְכָה נִזְבְּחָה לֵאלֹהֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et matkonet hallevenim asher hem osim temol shileshom tasimu aleihem lo tigre'u mimmenu ki nirpim hem al ken hem tzo'akim lemor nelkhah nizvechah le'Eloheinu.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh reveals his theological interpretation of the exodus request: Israel cries 'Let us go and sacrifice' because 'they are idle' (nirpim — slack, lazy, loose). The verb 'para' (to let loose, to disturb order) is the same root used in Exodus 32:25 when the people 'ran wild' (para) under Aaron at the golden calf. To Pharaoh, worship is a symptom of idleness; to God, idleness from worship is a symptom of bondage. He has inverted the entire moral framework. The word 'tzo'akim' (they are crying out) is the same word as Israel's cry in 2:23 that 'went up to God.' Pharaoh quotes the very cry of oppression — God's cry of liberation — and reinterprets it as laziness.
verses.doctrinal_note: Pharaoh's theology is the theology of all totalitarianism: the slaves' desire for worship and rest is laziness; their cry to God is weakness; their religious identity is a productivity problem. Every system that subordinates human dignity to economic output echoes Pharaoh. The irony is perfect: the people whose cry reached God (2:23) are told by Pharaoh that their crying is merely idleness. God heard the cry as the voice of suffering; Pharaoh hears it as the complaint of the comfortable. The same cry is interpreted in diametrically opposite ways by the divine and the demonic.

verses.kjv_text: Let there more work be laid upon the men, that they may labour therein; and let them not regard vain words.
verses.original_text: תִּכְבַּד הָעֲבֹדָה עַל הָאֲנָשִׁים וְיַעֲשׂוּ בָהּ וְאַל יִשְׁעוּ בְּדִבְרֵי שָׁקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Tikbad ha'avodah al-ha'anashim veya'asu vah ve'al yish'u bedivrei shaker.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's counter-theology in concentrated form: 'Let the work be heavy' (tikbad ha'avodah — from 'kaved,' heavy/weighty/glorious). The same root as 'kavod' (glory) and 'kevad peh' (Moses's heavy mouth, 4:10). What Moses called his heaviest burden (kevad — slow tongue) Pharaoh applies to the slaves' backs. He further dismisses God's message as 'vain words' (divrei shaker — false words, lying words). The word of God — 'let my people go' — is classified by Pharaoh as 'shaker' (falsehood). This is the ultimate inversion: the word of ultimate truth labeled as the ultimate lie.

verses.kjv_text: And the taskmasters of the people went out, and their officers, and they spake to the people, saying, Thus saith Pharaoh, I will not give you straw.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצְאוּ נֹגְשֵׂי הָעָם וְשֹׁטְרָיו וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל הָעָם לֵאמֹר כֹּה אָמַר פַּרְעֹה אֵינֶנִּי נֹתֵן לָכֶם תֶּבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetz'u nogsei ha'am veshoterav vayyomeru el-ha'am lemor koh amar Far'oh eineni noten lakhem teven.
verses.study_note: The taskmasters deliver the decree using the prophetic messenger formula: 'Thus saith Pharaoh' (koh amar Far'oh) — the exact same formula as 'Thus saith YHWH' (koh amar YHVH) from v.1. The narrator juxtaposes the two declarations deliberately: YHWH says 'Let my people go'; Pharaoh says 'I will not give you straw.' Both use 'koh amar' — but one is the voice of liberation, the other of oppression. The counterfeit divine claim of Pharaoh (he was considered a god in Egypt) is exposed by the identical formula applied to diametrically opposite ends. Every human authority that adopts divine language and form to oppress stands in this line.

verses.kjv_text: Go ye, get you straw where ye can find it: yet not ought of your work shall be diminished.
verses.original_text: אַתֶּם לְכוּ קְחוּ לָכֶם תֶּבֶן מֵאֲשֶׁר תִּמְצָאוּ כִּי אֵין נִגְרָע מֵעֲבֹדַתְכֶם דָּבָר׃
verses.transliteration: Attem lekhu kechu lakhem teven me'asher timtza'u ki ein nigra me'avodatkhem davar.
verses.study_note: The full weight of the impossible demand crystallizes here: find your own straw, from wherever you can find it (me'asher timtza'u — wherever you may find it), but not one 'word' (davar) of your work will be reduced. The clause 'not a word' (davar) echoes and inverts YHWH's 'davar' (word) from v.1. God's word says 'release'; Pharaoh's word says 'not a word (davar) will be lessened.' The Hebrew wordplay pits two 'devarim' against each other: the liberating word of God and the enslaving word of Pharaoh.

verses.kjv_text: So the people were scattered abroad throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble instead of straw.
verses.original_text: וַיָּפֶץ הָעָם בְּכָל אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לְקֹשֵׁשׁ קַשׁ לַתֶּבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyafetz ha'am bekhol eretz Mitzrayim lekoshesh kash latteven.
verses.study_note: The people are 'scattered' (vayyafetz — from 'puts,' to scatter, to disperse). This word is loaded with theological significance: 'puts' is the verb used for the scattering of nations at Babel (Gen 11:8-9), and repeatedly for Israel's exile and scattering among the nations (Deut 30:3, Jer 9:15). Before God gathers and brings out Israel in a great assembly (the Exodus), Pharaoh scatters them across Egypt in humiliation. The people go from organized community (gathered before the elders, 4:29) to scattered gleaning parties. Egypt's oppression imposes exile-dispersion even before exile begins.

verses.kjv_text: And the taskmasters hasted them, saying, Fulfil your works, your daily tasks, as when there was straw.
verses.original_text: וְהַנֹּגְשִׂים אָצִים לֵאמֹר כַּלּוּ מַעֲשֵׂיכֶם דְּבַר יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר בִּהְיוֹת הַתֶּבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Vehannogeshim atzim lemor kallu ma'aseikhem devar yom beyomo ka'asher bihyot hatteven.
verses.study_note: The taskmasters drive (atzim — hurry, press, urge) the people with the impossible standard: 'as when there was straw.' The phrase 'devar yom beyomo' (the matter of a day in its day) is a phrase of precise daily accounting — every day's quota, on the day it is due. God will later use this identical phrase for the daily manna in Exodus 16:4: 'gather a day's portion (devar yom beyomo) each day.' The oppressive daily accounting of Pharaoh's brick-making becomes the gracious daily provision of God's bread-giving. The same phrase, the same daily rhythm — one produces crushing bondage, the other liberating abundance.

verses.kjv_text: And the officers of the children of Israel, which Pharaoh’s taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and demanded, Wherefore have ye not fulfilled your task in making brick both yesterday and to day, as heretofore?
verses.original_text: וַיֻּכּוּ שֹׁטְרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר שָׂמוּ עֲלֵהֶם נֹגְשֵׂי פַרְעֹה לֵאמֹר מַדּוּעַ לֹא כִלִּיתֶם חָקְכֶם לִלְבֹּן כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם גַּם תְּמוֹל גַּם הַיּוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyukku shoterei venei Yisra'el asher samu aleihem nogsei Far'oh lemor maddua lo killitem chokekhem livbon kitmol shileshom gam temol gam hayyom.
verses.study_note: The Israelite foremen are beaten (vayyukku — struck, the passive of 'nakah,' the plague-verb). The same verb used for God striking Egypt in the plagues is first used here for Pharaoh striking Israel's leaders. Physical punishment falls on those in the middle — the Israelite overseers who represent their people to the taskmasters. The 'chukkah' (quota/portion/statute) is the same word later used for God's 'statutes' in the Law. What Pharaoh makes a law of pain, God transforms into laws of covenant life. The vocabulary of bondage is redeemed into the vocabulary of covenant.

verses.kjv_text: Then the officers of the children of Israel came and cried unto Pharaoh, saying, Wherefore dealest thou thus with thy servants?
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ שֹׁטְרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר לָמָּה תַעֲשֶׂה כֹה לַעֲבָדֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u shoterei venei Yisra'el vayyitze'aku el-Far'oh lemor lammah ta'aseh kho la'avadeikha.
verses.study_note: The beaten foremen 'cried out' (vayyitze'aku — from 'tza'ak,' the same cry as 2:23 and 5:8). But here they cry to Pharaoh, not to God. This is the chapter's great irony: Israel's legitimate cry of suffering goes up to God in 2:23 and prompts divine action; but under the pressure of the increased oppression, the foremen bring their cry to the wrong address — to the very one causing the problem. Pharaoh cannot answer the cry that only God can answer. This misdirected appeal is a perennial pattern: in suffering, the temptation is to appeal to earthly power rather than to the God who has already heard and is already acting.

verses.kjv_text: There is no straw given unto thy servants, and they say to us, Make brick: and, behold, thy servants are beaten; but the fault is in thine own people.
verses.original_text: תֶּבֶן אֵין נִתָּן לַעֲבָדֶיךָ וּלְבֵנִים אֹמְרִים לָנוּ עֲשׂוּ וְהִנֵּה עֲבָדֶיךָ מֻכִּים וְחָטָאת עַמֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Teven ein nittan la'avadeikha ulevenem omrim lanu asu vehineh avadeikha mukkim vechata'at amekha.
verses.study_note: The foremen's plea contains a brilliant piece of political audacity: 'the fault is in thine own people' (vechata'at amekha — the sin is with your people). They redirect blame toward the Egyptian taskmasters. The word 'chata'at' (sin, fault, guilt) is the same word for 'sin offering' in Leviticus — what is guilt here becomes atonement there. The foremen's language unwittingly points to a coming reality: the fault that lies with Egypt's people (Pharaoh's taskmasters) will be visited upon them through the plagues, while the guilt (chata'at) of Israel will be atoned by blood sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: But he said, Ye are idle, ye are idle: therefore ye say, Let us go and do sacrifice to the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר נִרְפִּים אַתֶּם נִרְפִּים עַל כֵּן אַתֶּם אֹמְרִים נֵלְכָה נִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer nirpim attem nirpim al ken attem omrim nelkhah nizvechah la'YHVH.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh repeats 'idle' (nirpim) twice — the only emphatic repetition in his speeches. The doubling (nirpim attem nirpim) intensifies the accusation: 'You are utterly, completely idle.' His diagnosis remains unchanged despite the foremen's evidence of unjust treatment: the problem is the workers, not the system. This is the recurring logic of oppression — the suffering of the oppressed is always redefined as a character defect of the oppressed themselves. Their desire to worship YHWH is, in Pharaoh's framework, both the evidence and the cause of their laziness. The one who wants to worship God is, to the world, the one who is avoiding real work.

verses.kjv_text: Go therefore now, and work; for there shall no straw be given you, yet shall ye deliver the tale of bricks.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה לְכוּ עִבְדוּ וְתֶבֶן לֹא יִנָּתֵן לָכֶם וְתֹכֶן לְבֵנִים תִּתֵּנּוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah lekhu ivdu veteven lo yinnatein lakhem vetokhen levenum tittenu.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's final word to the foremen is terse and absolute: Go, work (lekhu ivdu — go, serve/labor). The two verbs stand as Pharaoh's complete theology: movement and labor. No mercy, no negotiation, no modification. The 'tokhen' (quota/weight) must be delivered. The word 'tokhen' (to weigh out, to account) is the measuring word — God will later 'weigh' human actions (1 Sam 2:3; Dan 5:27: 'TEKEL — you have been weighed and found wanting'). Pharaoh's demand to deliver the 'tokhen' (weight) of bricks foreshadows the divine weighing of Pharaoh himself in the scales of justice.

verses.kjv_text: And the officers of the children of Israel did see that they were in evil case, after it was said, Ye shall not minish ought from your bricks of your daily task.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ שֹׁטְרֵי בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֹתָם בְּרָע לֵאמֹר לֹא תִגְרְעוּ מִלְּבַנֵיכֶם דְּבַר יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyir'u shoterei venei Yisra'el otam bera lemor lo tigre'u millevaneikhem devar yom beyomo.
verses.study_note: The Israelite foremen 'saw' (vayyir'u — the perception-word) that they were 'in evil' (bera — in trouble, in a bad condition). The word 'ra' (evil, bad) is the word Pharaoh had been associated with throughout the chapter. What Pharaoh has done to them is 'ra' — and now the foremen perceive themselves to be 'in ra.'
