The perception of their condition is delayed — they had hoped to persuade Pharaoh; now they understand the full horror of their situation. Seeing (ra'ah) the 'evil' (ra) is the beginning of the cry that will again reach God.

verses.kjv_text: And they met Moses and Aaron, who stood in the way, as they came forth from Pharaoh:
verses.original_text: וַיִּפְגְּעוּ אֶת מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן נִצָּבִים לִקְרָאתָם בְּצֵאתָם מֵאֵת פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifge'u et-Moshe ve'Aharon nitzavim likratam betzetam me'et Far'oh.
verses.study_note: The encounter between the defeated foremen and the waiting Moses and Aaron is poignant. Moses and Aaron are 'standing' (nitzavim — the verb of steadfast, purposeful standing) to meet them as they come from Pharaoh. Moses has been waiting. The word 'vayyifge'u' (they met/encountered) uses the same root as God's frightening encounter with Moses at the inn (4:24 — vayyifgeshehu). The verb 'paga' carries the weight of unexpected, significant confrontation. Moses waiting at the crossroads is about to receive the full force of Israel's disillusionment.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto them, The LORD look upon you, and judge; because ye have made our savour to be abhorred in the eyes of Pharaoh, and in the eyes of his servants, to put a sword in their hand to slay us.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵהֶם יֵרֶא יְהוָה עֲלֵיכֶם וְיִשְׁפֹּט אֲשֶׁר הִבְאַשְׁתֶּם אֶת רֵיחֵנוּ בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו לָתֶת חֶרֶב בְּיָדָם לְהָרְגֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru aleihem yera YHVH aleikhem veyishpot asher hiv'ashtem et-reichenu be'einei Far'oh uve'einei avadav latet cherev beyadam lehargenu.
verses.study_note: The bitterest speech in the Exodus narrative. The beaten foremen curse Moses and Aaron in the name of YHWH: 'May the LORD look upon you and judge' — turning the very name of the liberating God into a word of condemnation against His own messenger. 'Ye have made our savour to be abhorred' (hiv'ashtem et-reichenu — literally 'you have made our smell to stink'). In Hebrew thought, 'a good smell' (reyach nichoach) is the expression of divine favor (used of sacrifices throughout Leviticus). To 'make a bad smell' is to make oneself and one's people repulsive in the sight of those in power. The accusation is devastating: Moses's intervention has made things catastrophically worse, and the people hold him responsible.
verses.doctrinal_note: The foremen's accusation against Moses — 'you have made our savour to stink' — is the pattern of the true prophet rejected in his own generation. Moses is accused of causing harm when he is in fact the instrument of coming liberation. The people who worshipped and believed (4:31) are now — within days — cursing their deliverer. This is one of Scripture's most honest portrayals of the gap between calling and fulfillment. Jesus identified this pattern: 'A prophet is not without honor save in his own country' (Matt 13:57). Paul named it in 2 Corinthians 2:15-16: the gospel is to some 'the savour of death unto death' — but this does not make it false.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, LORD, wherefore hast thou so evil entreated this people? why is it that thou hast sent me?
verses.original_text: וַיָּשָׁב מֹשֶׁה אֶל יְהוָה וַיֹּאמַר אֲדֹנָי לָמָּה הֲרֵעֹתָה לָעָם הַזֶּה לָמָּה זֶּה שְׁלַחְתָּנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashav Moshe el-YHVH vayomar Adonai lammah hare'otah la'am hazzeh lammah zzeh shelachtani.
verses.study_note: Moses's response to the foremen's accusation is one of the most honest prayers in Scripture. He does not defend himself to the people — he returns (vayyashav — from 'shuv,' the return word) to God. But what he brings to God is raw complaint: 'Lord, why hast thou so evil entreated this people?' (lammah hare'otah — literally, 'why have you done evil to this people?'). Moses uses the word 'ra' (evil) of God's actions — the same word Pharaoh is associated with throughout the chapter. Moses lays the responsibility for Israel's suffering directly at God's feet. This is not blasphemy but lament — the kind of fierce, honest complaint prayer that runs from Moses through Job to the Psalms to Jeremiah. The double 'lammah' (why... why?) is the lament structure of the Psalms (cf. Ps 22:1 — 'My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?').
verses.doctrinal_note: Moses's 'why have you done evil?' (hare'otah) addressed to God is one of Scripture's great lament prayers. It stands in the tradition of Job ('Why dost thou contend against me?' — Job 10:2), Jeremiah ('Cursed be the day wherein I was born' — Jer 20:14), and the Psalmists ('My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?' — Ps 22:1). The capacity to bring honest, raw complaint to God — accusing Him of wrongdoing — without abandoning Him (Moses 'returned to YHWH') is one of the distinctive marks of biblical faith. The lament is not faithlessness but the deepest form of trust: bringing the worst to God because He is the only one who can answer it. Jesus demonstrates the same pattern on the cross (Matt 27:46) — and continuing in trust through to resurrection.

verses.kjv_text: For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in thy name, he hath done evil to this people; neither hast thou delivered thy people at all.
verses.original_text: וּמֵאָז בָּאתִי אֶל פַּרְעֹה לְדַבֵּר בִּשְׁמֶךָ הֵרַע לָעָם הַזֶּה וְהַצֵּל לֹא הִצַּלְתָּ אֶת עַמֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ume'az ba'ti el-Far'oh ledabber bishmeikha hera la'am hazzeh vehatzzel lo hitzalta et-amekha.
verses.study_note: The chapter's devastating last verse. Moses lays out the evidence of God's apparent failure in two devastating clauses: (1) 'Since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil (hera) to this people' — obedience has made things worse; (2) 'And you have not at all delivered this people' (vehatzzel lo hitzalta — the emphatic infinitive absolute: 'delivering, you have NOT delivered'). The emphatic construction (infinitive absolute + negation) makes the accusation as strong as the Hebrew language allows. This is the nadir of the Exodus narrative — the darkest moment before the dawn of chapters 6-12. God does not answer in chapter 5 — the silence is part of the theology. Chapter 6 will open with God's magnificent response. But the chapter ends here, in darkness, with Moses's unanswered lament.
verses.doctrinal_note: Exodus 5 is the theology of the dark night of obedience — when faithfulness produces not relief but intensified suffering, when God is silent, and when the deliverer himself is accused. The chapter ends without resolution and without a divine answer — that comes in chapter 6. This is Scripture's most honest treatment of the hidden God (Deus absconditus — Isaiah 45:15). The pattern is: (1) divine commission (ch.3-4), (2) first obedience — suffering intensifies (ch.5), (3) renewed revelation and promise (ch.6), (4) divine action through judgment and salvation (chs.7-14). The dark valley of chapter 5 is not a detour from God's plan but the very road that leads to the Exodus. Gethsemane precedes the resurrection; the cross precedes the empty tomb. God does not answer Moses in chapter 5 — He acts in chapters 7-14.

meta.overview: Exodus 5 is the chapter of the first great disappointment. Moses and Aaron have received the burning bush commission, Aaron has been appointed, the elders have believed, and the people have worshipped (4:31). Everything is in place. Then reality hits: Pharaoh refuses. Not merely refuses — he increases the oppression, removes the straw supply while keeping the brick quota, and the Israelite overseers are beaten. The people turn on Moses with bitter accusation: 'You have made us a stench in Pharaoh's sight.' And Moses, barely commissioned, brings his complaint directly to God: 'Why hast thou so evil entreated this people? Why is it that thou hast sent me?' The chapter is a profound theology of the dark valley between calling and fulfillment — the period when obedience produces not relief but intensified suffering, and when God appears silent. It is the Old Testament's most honest account of the gap between divine promise and human experience, and it sets the stage for God's magnificent self-revelation in chapter 6.
meta.theme: The Cost of Obedience, the Silence of God & the Dark Valley of Faith
meta.keyWordMeaning: Burden/Forced labor — from the root 'saval,' to bear a heavy load. The word of crushing servitude that defines Israel's condition in Egypt. Appears 6 times in Exodus and encapsulates the full weight of oppression that God has promised to remove.
outline: The First Audience with Pharaoh: Let My People Go (vv.1-3)
Pharaoh's Contemptuous Rejection: Who is YHWH? (vv.4-5)
Pharaoh's Decree: No More Straw (vv.6-9)
The Taskmasters Execute the Edict (vv.10-14)
The Israelite Officers Appeal to Pharaoh — Rejected (vv.15-19)
The Officers Confront Moses and Aaron (vv.20-21)
Moses's Lament Before God: Why Have You Done Evil? (vv.22-23)

verses.kjv_text: Then the LORD said unto Moses, Now shalt thou see what I will do to Pharaoh: for with a strong hand shall he let them go, and with a strong hand shall he drive them out of his land.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה עַתָּה תִרְאֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶעֱשֶׂה לְפַרְעֹה כִּי בְיָד חֲזָקָה יְשַׁלְּחֵם וּבְיָד חֲזָקָה יְגָרְשֵׁם מֵאַרְצוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Moshe attah tir'eh asher e'eseh leFar'oh ki veyad chazakah yeshallechem uveyad chazakah yegareshem me'artzo.
verses.study_note: God's first word in response to Moses's lament (5:22-23) is not an explanation but a declaration of imminent action: 'Now shalt thou see' (attah tir'eh — you will now see). The word 'attah' (now) signals a turning point — the hinge between chapter 5's suffering and the coming liberation. 'With a strong hand' (yad chazakah) appears twice in this verse — the phrase becomes a refrain of the Exodus (13:9, 13:14, 13:16; Deut 6:21). Notably, it is 'Pharaoh's strong hand' that drives them out — the compulsion will come from Pharaoh himself, not Israel's own escape. God answers Moses's 'why hast thou not delivered?' by revealing that the deliverer is the oppressor, who will be broken by divine force into acting as his own people's expeller.
verses.doctrinal_note: God does not answer the 'why' of suffering but the 'what next' of sovereign action. Moses asked 'why?' (5:22 — lammah); God answers 'now watch' (attah tir'eh). The pastoral principle: in the depth of the lament, God often does not explain the delay but reveals the coming act. This is the pattern of all great divine responses to suffering: Job is not given a theodicy but a theophany (Job 38-42); Habakkuk is not given an explanation but a vision (Hab 2:2-4). The answer to the lament is not information but encounter with the acting God.

verses.kjv_text: And God spake unto Moses, and said unto him, I am the LORD:
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים אֶל מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Elohim el-Moshe vayomer elav ani YHVH.
verses.study_note: The divine speech begins with the most fundamental theological assertion in the Old Testament: 'I am the LORD' (ani YHVH). This three-word declaration functions as the theological foundation of all that follows. In the Decalogue it introduces the Ten Commandments (Exod 20:2); throughout Leviticus it punctuates each law ('I am the LORD your God'); in Ezekiel it closes virtually every prophetic oracle. Here, before the seven 'I will' promises of the covenant, God establishes His identity. The response to Moses's lament begins not with a plan but with a person — not 'here is what I will do' first, but 'I am who I am.' The doing flows from the being. The promises of vv.6-8 are guaranteed because of the identity declared in v.2.
verses.doctrinal_note: 'Ani YHVH' (I am the LORD) is the self-authenticating declaration of the covenant God. In grammar it is a nominal clause with no verb — not 'I was' or 'I will be' but the timeless, continuous 'I am.' Jesus appropriates this formula seven times in John's Gospel with the 'I AM' declarations (John 6:35, 8:12, 10:9, 10:11, 11:25, 14:6, 15:1) and uses the absolute 'I AM' (ego eimi) in John 8:58 — 'Before Abraham was, I am' — provoking the accusation of blasphemy because He identifies Himself with the divine name. The 'I am the LORD' of Exodus 6:2 and the 'I AM' of John 8:58 are the same divine self-disclosure, separated by centuries, united in the one God.

verses.kjv_text: And I appeared unto Abraham, unto Isaac, and unto Jacob, by the name of God Almighty, but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them.
verses.original_text: וָאֵרָא אֶל אַבְרָהָם אֶל יִצְחָק וְאֶל יַעֲקֹב בְּאֵל שַׁדַּי וּשְׁמִי יְהוָה לֹא נוֹדַעְתִּי לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Va'era el-Avraham el-Yitzhak ve'el-Ya'akov be'El Shaddai ushmi YHVH lo noda'ti lahem.
verses.study_note: One of the most exegetically discussed verses in the Pentateuch. The patriarchs are said to have known God as 'El Shaddai' (God Almighty) but not by His name 'YHVH' in the same way Moses's generation will. The challenge: Genesis uses the name YHVH repeatedly in the patriarchal narratives (Gen 15:7 — 'I am the LORD who brought you from Ur'; Gen 28:13; etc.). Interpreters have offered several resolutions: (1) Documentary hypothesis assigns the two naming traditions to different source documents (J and P) — but this is rejected by a grammatical solution; (2) The more compelling reading is that the patriarchs knew the name YHVH but did not experience its full covenant-fulfilment content — 'El Shaddai' (the God who is abundantly sufficient, who promises) was their functional experience; 'YHVH' (the covenant-keeping God who acts in history to fulfil every promise) is the experience about to begin. The name is not new; its experiential reality is. 'Noda'ti' (was I made known) — Niphal of 'yada': God was not made known in the full dimension of His keeping-power.
verses.doctrinal_note: The distinction between El Shaddai and YHWH is a distinction between promise and fulfilment, between covenant announced and covenant enacted. El Shaddai — the name of the Abrahamic covenant — is the God of the promise; YHWH — the name of the Exodus covenant — is the God of the kept promise. Hebrews 11:13 says the patriarchs 'died in faith, not having received the promises' but having seen them 'afar off.' The generation of Moses is the generation that does not just receive the promise but lives inside its fulfilment. This is the supreme theological privilege of the Exodus generation — and, by typology, of the New Covenant generation: we live not in the age of promise (the Old Testament) but in the age of the down-payment of fulfilment (the gift of the Spirit — 2 Cor 1:22; Eph 1:13-14).

verses.kjv_text: And I have also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their pilgrimage, wherein they were strangers.
verses.original_text: וְגַם הֲקִמֹתִי אֶת בְּרִיתִי אִתָּם לָתֵת לָהֶם אֶת אֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן אֵת אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר גָּרוּ בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam hakimoti et-beriti ittam latet lahem et-eretz Kena'an et eretz megurihem asher garu vah.
verses.study_note: God grounds His coming action in the Abrahamic covenant (beriti — my covenant). 'I have established' (hakimoti — Hiphil perfect of 'kum,' to rise, to stand firm) — the covenant stands firm, erected and immovable. The land of Canaan is described as 'eretz megurihem' (the land of their sojourning) — the same root as 'ger' (stranger/sojourner). The patriarchs lived as aliens (gerim) in the very land God had promised them. This is the supreme act of faith: inhabiting a promise without possessing it. Hebrews 11:9 captures this: 'By faith he sojourned in the land of promise, as in a strange country.' The covenant is not new; what is new is its moment of fulfilment.

verses.kjv_text: And I have also heard the groaning of the children of Israel, whom the Egyptians keep in bondage; and I have remembered my covenant.
verses.original_text: וְגַם אֲנִי שָׁמַעְתִּי אֶת נַאֲקַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר מִצְרַיִם מַעֲבִדִים אֹתָם וָאֶזְכֹּר אֶת בְּרִיתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam ani shamatti et na'akat benei Yisra'el asher Mitzrayim ma'avidim otam va'ezkhor et-beriti.
verses.study_note: Two divine actions anchor this verse: 'I have heard' (shamatti) and 'I have remembered' (va'ezkhor). Both are perfect tenses — completed divine acts. 'The groaning' (na'akat — from 'na'ak,' to groan under pressure) is distinct from the 'za'akah' (cry) of 2:23 and the 'tze'akah' (outcry) of 3:7 — it is the inarticulate moan of those too crushed even to form words. God hears what cannot be spoken. 'I have remembered my covenant' (va'ezkhor et-beriti) — divine 'remembering' in the Hebrew Bible always means covenantal engagement, not merely mental recollection. When God 'remembers,' He acts. The same word is used of God remembering Noah (Gen 8:1), remembering Abraham to save Lot (Gen 19:29), and of Israel's prayer to God to 'remember' them (Ps 25:6-7). Memory in this usage is the covenant activated.

verses.kjv_text: Wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments:
verses.original_text: לָכֵן אֱמֹר לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲנִי יְהוָה וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם מִתַּחַת סִבְלֹת מִצְרַיִם וְהִצַּלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם מֵעֲבֹדָתָם וְגָאַלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם בִּזְרוֹעַ נְטוּיָה וּבִשְׁפָטִים גְּדֹלִים׃
verses.transliteration: Lakhen emor livnei Yisra'el ani YHVH vehotze'ti etkhem mittachat sivlot Mitzrayim vehitzalti etkhem me'avodatam vega'alti etkhem bizroa netuyah uvishfatim gedolim.
verses.study_note: The first of the seven 'I will' promises — and the most theologically loaded: 'I will redeem you' (vega'alti etkhem). The verb 'ga'al' makes its first appearance in the Exodus narrative. In Israelite law, the 'go'el' (kinsman-redeemer — Lev 25:25, 47-55; Ruth 4) was the nearest male relative obligated to redeem an enslaved family member, buy back alienated property, or avenge blood. God here identifies Himself as Israel's divine Go'el — their nearest Kin who buys them back from slavery. The redemption is accomplished 'with a stretched out arm' (bizroa netuyah) — the divine arm, extended in power, becomes one of the Exodus's most enduring images (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 26:8). 'Great judgments' (shefatim gedolim) — the ten plagues are God's legal judgments on Egypt, the penalty for holding God's son (4:22-23) in unauthorized bondage.
verses.doctrinal_note: The theology of redemption as kinsman-redeemer: In Israel's law, the go'el (redeemer) had to be: (1) a near kinsman; (2) willing to redeem; (3) able to redeem (pay the price). God fulfils all three: (1) He is Israel's Father/next-of-kin (4:22 — 'my firstborn son'); (2) He is willing — His entire self-disclosure in chs. 3-6 is His declaration of willing engagement; (3) He is able — the stretched-out arm and great judgments demonstrate omnipotence. The New Testament applies exactly this framework to Christ: in Hebrews 2:14-17, Jesus must become 'like his brethren in all things' in order to function as high priest and redeemer — the incarnation is the kinship-qualification for the go'el role. He became our nearest kin in order to have the standing to redeem us (Gal 4:4-5; Ruth 4:1-10 as type).

verses.kjv_text: And I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God: and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God, which bringeth you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתִּי אֶתְכֶם לִי לְעָם וְהָיִיתִי לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם הַמּוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם מִתַּחַת סִבְלֹת מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Velakachti etkhem li le'am vehayiti lakhem le'Elohim viyda'tem ki ani YHVH Eloheikhem hammotzi etkhem mittachat sivlot Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The covenant formula at the center of the seven promises: 'I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God' (welakachti etkhem li le'am vehayiti lakhem le'Elohim). This bilateral declaration — You will be My people; I will be your God — is the heartbeat of the entire covenant relationship, repeated throughout the Old Testament (Lev 26:12; Jer 7:23; 11:4; 30:22; Ezek 36:28) and reaching its New Covenant fulfilment in Revelation 21:3: 'They shall be his people, and God himself shall be with them, and be their God.' The purpose of redemption is not merely escape from slavery but entry into covenant relationship. Israel is 'taken' (lakachti — the word of betrothal in marriage, cf. Ruth 4:13) as a bride is taken by a husband — into an intimate, exclusive, covenantal bond.
verses.doctrinal_note: The covenant formula 'my people / your God' is the recurring declaration of biblical covenant across every dispensation: given first as promise to Abraham (Gen 17:7), enacted at Sinai (Exod 19:5-6), renewed through the prophets (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:28), and consummated in the New Covenant (2 Cor 6:16; Rev 21:3). Each occurrence is not a new covenant but a deeper unfolding of the same eternal bond. The New Covenant version (Jer 31:31-34) does not replace the formula but internalizes it: instead of the law written on stone, written on the heart. The culmination (Rev 21:3) shows the formula at its fullest: God dwelling with His people, personally present, without the mediation of temple or distance.

verses.kjv_text: And I will bring you in unto the land, concerning the which I did swear to give it to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it you for an heritage: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהֵבֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם אֶל הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר נָשָׂאתִי אֶת יָדִי לָתֵת אֹתָהּ לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיַעֲקֹב וְנָתַתִּי אֹתָהּ לָכֶם מוֹרָשָׁה אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veheve'ti etkhem el-ha'aretz asher nasati et-yadi latet otah le'Avraham leYitzhak uLeYa'akov venatatti otah lakhem morashah ani YHVH.
verses.study_note: The seventh and final 'I will' — the culminating promise: 'I will bring you in' (veheve'ti etkhem). The sequence of the seven promises maps the entire arc of redemption: (1) bring out — liberation from bondage; (2) rid out — separation from slavery's grip; (3) redeem — the go'el act of purchasing; (4) take for a people — covenant formation; (5) be your God — covenant intimacy; (6) bring you into — land inheritance. The oath formula 'I did swear to give' (nasati et-yadi — literally 'I lifted my hand') is the ancient gesture of a solemn oath — raising the hand to heaven (Gen 14:22; Deut 32:40; Rev 10:5-6). God has sworn this by Himself, since there is no higher oath-taker (Heb 6:13). The land is given as 'morashah' (heritage, inheritance) — not earned but inherited, a gift flowing from relationship.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sevenfold 'I will' structure of vv.6-8 is a theological masterpiece. The seven promises move from liberation (out of Egypt) through covenant formation (my people, your God) to inheritance (the land). In the New Testament, this sevenfold structure finds its ultimate fulfilment in Christ: He brings us out of the domain of darkness (Col 1:13), redeems us as our kinsman (Gal 4:4-5), takes us as His people (1 Pet 2:9-10), dwells with us as our God (John 14:23), and will bring us into our inheritance (1 Pet 1:3-4; Rev 21:1-7). The 'I am the LORD' that closes v.8 forms an inclusion with v.2 — the entire covenant speech is bracketed by the divine name, as if to say: every promise is as certain as the name behind it.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses spake so unto the children of Israel: but they hearkened not unto Moses for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה כֵּן אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְלֹא שָׁמְעוּ אֶל מֹשֶׁה מִקֹּצֶר רוּחַ וּמֵעֲבֹדָה קָשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Moshe ken el-benei Yisra'el velo shame'u el-Moshe mikkotzer ruach ume'avodah kashah.
verses.study_note: The most heart-rending verse in the chapter. Moses delivers the greatest covenant speech in Israelite history — the sevenfold 'I will' of the living God — and Israel cannot hear it. 'They hearkened not... for anguish of spirit' (mikkotzer ruach — literally 'from shortness of spirit,' from constricted breath). The word 'kotzer' (shortness, constriction) describes the shallow, gasping breath of someone under crushing burden — the opposite of the 'neshamah' (deep breath of life) God breathed into Adam. 'Cruel bondage' (avodah kashah — hard, severe labor) has compressed their capacity for hope. The inability to hear God's promise is not a moral failure here but a physiological and psychological reality of extreme oppression. The text records this without condemnation. Sometimes people are too broken to receive the word. God does not withdraw the promise because the audience cannot hear it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The failure to hear the word of God due to 'kotzer ruach' (constricted spirit) is a pastoral reality the text validates without condemning. Oppression can damage a person's capacity for hope to the point where the gospel itself cannot penetrate — not from hardness of heart but from the crushing of the very capacity to hope. This is the pastoral challenge of ministry in contexts of severe trauma, poverty, and generational suffering. The Reformers noted that the law must be preached before the gospel can be received — not because the gospel is withheld but because the capacity to hear it must be awakened. In Isaiah 42:3 — 'a bruised reed shall he not break, and the smoking flax shall he not quench' — God's answer to 'kotzer ruach' is not condemnation but the tender, patient work of the Spirit.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor.
verses.study_note: The briefest transitional verse in this section, yet theologically significant: Israel has not heard; Moses is struggling; yet 'the LORD spoke' (vaydabber YHVH). The divine speech does not pause because the audience is unavailable or the messenger is inadequate. God continues speaking, commanding, and commissioning independent of human receptivity. The gap between Israel's inability to hear (v.9) and God's continued speaking (v.10) is bridged by nothing except God's sovereign purpose. He speaks into the silence of failed communication. This is the pattern of prophetic persistence: Jeremiah speaks to a deaf generation for forty years; Ezekiel prophesies to dry bones; Isaiah is told his message will fall on closed ears (Isa 6:9-10) — and yet he speaks.

verses.kjv_text: Go in, speak unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the children of Israel go out of his land.
verses.original_text: בֹּא דַבֵּר אֶל פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וִישַׁלַּח אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאַרְצוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Bo dabber el-Far'oh melekh Mitzrayim vishallach et-benei Yisra'el me'artzo.
verses.study_note: The reissued commission is spare and unadorned: 'Go in, speak, that he let them go.' God does not modify the mission because Israel could not hear or because the first audience with Pharaoh failed catastrophically (ch.5). The commission stands exactly as before. This is the nature of the prophetic calling: the servant is not responsible for outcomes, only for obedience. The message and the mission remain unchanged regardless of the reception. 'Let... go' (vishallach — Piel of 'shallach') repeats the central demand of every subsequent encounter. The commission is to speak the same word into the same closed ear until God decides the moment of breakthrough.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses spake before the LORD, saying, Behold, the children of Israel have not hearkened unto me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, who am of uncircumcised lips?
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לֵאמֹר הֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא שָׁמְעוּ אֵלַי וְאֵיךְ יִשְׁמָעֵנִי פַרְעֹה וַאֲנִי עֲרַל שְׂפָתָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Moshe lifnei YHVH lemor hen benei Yisra'el lo shame'u elai ve'eikh yishma'eni Far'oh va'ani aral sefatayim.
verses.study_note: Moses's objection is now sharpened by recent experience: if his own people could not hear him (v.9), how will Pharaoh? The a fortiori argument is logical but faithless. 'Uncircumcised lips' (aral sefatayim) — one of the most distinctive metaphors in the Torah. Circumcision removes the 'covering' that prevents full function. Uncircumcised lips are lips still covered, still obstructed, unable to speak with full effectiveness. The metaphor extends throughout the Old Testament: 'uncircumcised heart' (Lev 26:41; Jer 9:26), 'uncircumcised ears' (Acts 7:51 — 'stiffnecked... uncircumcised in heart and ears'). Every faculty has a 'foreskin' of the flesh that prevents it from full operation before God. Moses's spiritual self-assessment applies the circumcision covenant to his own mouth.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, and gave them a charge unto the children of Israel, and unto Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל אַהֲרֹן וַיְצַוֵּם אֶל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְאֶל פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם לְהוֹצִיא אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon vayetzavvem el-benei Yisra'el ve'el-Far'oh melekh Mitzrayim lehotzi et-benei Yisra'el me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: God's response to Moses's objection is simply to re-commission — to both Moses and Aaron ('unto Moses and unto Aaron'), acknowledging that the two-man team stands. God does not argue with Moses's weakness; He overrules it by charging them jointly. 'Gave them a charge' (vayetzavvem — Piel of 'tzavah,' to command, to charge solemnly) is the commissioning verb used for the most solemn divine mandates (the Law, the creation command, the priestly charges). The commission is issued as a formal, binding charge regardless of the commissioners' sense of inadequacy. Between Moses's lament (v.12) and God's reissued charge (v.13) there is no argument — only the sovereign word overcoming human hesitation.

verses.kjv_text: These be the heads of their fathers’ houses: The sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel; Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi: these be the families of Reuben.
verses.original_text: אֵלֶּה רָאשֵׁי בֵית אֲבֹתָם בְּנֵי רְאוּבֵן בְּכֹר יִשְׂרָאֵל חֲנוֹךְ וּפַלּוּא חֶצְרֹן וְכַרְמִי אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת רְאוּבֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Elleh rashei veit avotam benei Re'uven bekhor Yisra'el Chanokh uFallu Chetzron veKharmi elleh mishpechot Re'uven.
verses.study_note: The genealogy (vv.14-27) begins with Reuben and Simeon before focusing on Levi — the tribe of Moses and Aaron. The purpose of inserting a genealogy here is theological and structural: (1) it grounds Moses and Aaron in the covenantal tribal structure of Israel, establishing their legitimacy as leaders; (2) it slows the narrative at the moment of greatest tension, creating a literary pause that heightens the drama when the commission resumes; (3) it connects the Exodus generation back to Jacob's twelve sons, showing that God's promise to the patriarchs is now reaching its fulfilment in these specific named descendants. Reuben leads (bekhor Yisra'el — firstborn of Israel) but the list moves quickly to Levi, where Moses and Aaron's lineage lies.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman: these are the families of Simeon.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן יְמוּאֵל וְיָמִין וְאֹהַד וְיָכִין וְצֹחַר וְשָׁאוּל בֶּן הַכְּנַעֲנִית אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת שִׁמְעוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Shimon Yemu'el veYamin ve'Ohad veYakhin veTzochar veSha'ul ben hakKena'anit elleh mishpechot Shimon.
verses.study_note: Simeon's genealogy includes the notation 'the son of a Canaanitish woman' for Shaul — a rare personal ethnic note in a genealogy. The Torah preserves this detail not as condemnation but as honest record. Simeon is listed second because Levi was third in birth order (Gen 29:33-34). The genealogy moves in birth order only long enough to establish context before focusing entirely on Levi's line. The tribal list exists in this chapter to emphasize that Moses and Aaron are not self-appointed figures but men deeply embedded in Israel's genealogical identity and covenant inheritance.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations; Gershon, and Kohath, and Merari: and the years of the life of Levi were an hundred thirty and seven years.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי לֵוִי לְתֹלְדֹתָם גֵּרְשׁוֹן וּקְהָת וּמְרָרִי וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי לֵוִי שֶׁבַע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמֵאַת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh shemot benei Levi letoledotam Gershon uKehat uMerari usheni chayyei Levi sheva ushloshin ume'at shanah.
verses.study_note: The genealogy of Levi begins. The tribe of Levi will be set apart for priestly service — receiving no land inheritance but God Himself as their portion (Num 18:20; Deut 18:2). The three sons of Levi — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari — become the founders of the three great Levitical families that carry, maintain, and guard the tabernacle (Num 3-4). Kohath's line (v.18) leads to Amram, who leads to Moses and Aaron. Levi lived 137 years — the text preserves these ancestral ages because the long lifespan of the patriarchal line (Levi the son of Jacob) connects the narrative directly to the covenant promises: these are not distant mythological figures but recent ancestors. Jochebed (Moses's mother — v.20) could have known Levi personally.

verses.kjv_text: The sons of Gershon; Libni, and Shimi, according to their families.
verses.original_text: בְּנֵי גֵרְשׁוֹן לִבְנִי וְשִׁמְעִי לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Benei Gershon Livni veShimi lemishpechotam.
verses.study_note: The Gershonite families (Libni and Shimi) are listed briefly. They will be appointed to carry the coverings and curtains of the tabernacle (Num 4:21-28). The brevity of the Gershon entry compared to the Kohath entry (which receives the most expansion) reflects the narrative focus: the reader is being conducted through the genealogy toward Amram, Moses, and Aaron as quickly as possible while maintaining the appearance of comprehensive tribal record.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Kohath; Amram, and Izhar, and Hebron, and Uzziel: and the years of the life of Kohath were an hundred thirty and three years.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי קְהָת עַמְרָם וְיִצְהָר וְחֶבְרוֹן וְעֻזִּיאֵל וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי קְהָת שָׁלֹשׁ וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמֵאַת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Kehat Amram veYitzhar veChevron ve'Uzzi'el usheni chayyei Kehat shalosh ushloshin ume'at shanah.
verses.study_note: The Kohathite line now carries the central narrative — Amram is the father of Moses and Aaron. The Kohathites are assigned the most sacred duties: carrying the ark, the table, the lampstand, the altars, and the vessels of the sanctuary (Num 4:4-20). Only they may approach and carry these objects, though even they must not look upon them or touch them uncovered (Num 4:15). Uzziel's line is also significant: his son Mishael and his son Elzaphan are later summoned to carry the bodies of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:4), and Korah — who leads the great rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Num 16) — is also of Kohath's line through Izhar.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Merari; Mahali and Mushi: these are the families of Levi according to their generations.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי מְרָרִי מַחְלִי וּמוּשִׁי אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת הַלֵּוִי לְתֹלְדֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Merari Machli uMushi elleh mishpechot haLevi letoledotam.
verses.study_note: The Merarites (Mahali and Mushi) complete the three Levitical families. They are charged with the boards, bars, pillars, and foundations of the tabernacle — the heavy structural framework (Num 4:29-33). The three families thus represent a complete division of sacred labor: Gershon carries the fabric and coverings; Kohath carries the most sacred vessels; Merari carries the structural skeleton. Together they form the portable temple of the wilderness. The genealogy of all three families is listed before focusing on the Kohathite line of Moses and Aaron in the next verses.

verses.kjv_text: And Amram took him Jochebed his father’s sister to wife; and she bare him Aaron and Moses: and the years of the life of Amram were an hundred and thirty and seven years.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח עַמְרָם אֶת יוֹכֶבֶד דֹּדָתוֹ לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ אֶת אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת מֹשֶׁה וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי עַמְרָם שֶׁבַע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמֵאַת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach Amram et-Yokheved dodato lo le'ishah vatteleld lo et-Aharon ve'et-Moshe usheni chayyei Amram sheva ushloshin ume'at shanah.
verses.study_note: The genealogy reaches its narrative destination: Amram and Jochebed — the parents of Moses and Aaron. Jochebed (Yokheved — 'YHVH is glory') is named here and in Numbers 26:59 as the first person in Scripture to bear a name compounded with the divine name YHVH. That the mother of the lawgiver carries the name of YHWH is deeply significant: the name Israel is about to experience in its fullest redemptive dimension is already embedded in the name of the woman who gave birth to Israel's deliverer. Amram's marriage to his father's sister (dodato) would later be prohibited by Levitical law (Lev 18:12-13), but this marriage predates the giving of the law — it is an example of the principle that the Mosaic law codifies what was previously informal or variable.
verses.doctrinal_note: The name Jochebed (Yokheved — YHWH is glory/weighty) is the first theophoric name using the divine name YHWH in Scripture. It is remarkable that the very name Israel is about to 'know' experientially (v.3 — 'by my name YHWH was I not known') has already been enshrined in the name of Moses's mother. The theology of the name precedes its experience: Jochebed's very existence in Israel is a testimony that the name YHWH was already embedded in Israel's life before its full revelation. Grace names us before we can pronounce the name.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Izhar; Korah, and Nepheg, and Zichri.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יִצְהָר קֹרַח וָנֶפֶג וְזִכְרִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yitzhar Korach vaNefeg veZikhri.
verses.study_note: The sons of Izhar (Amram's brother) include Korah — whose name here in the genealogy carries no commentary but whose future rebellion against Moses and Aaron (Num 16) makes this a moment of foreshadowing. Korah is Moses's first cousin, a fellow Kohathite, a carrier of the sacred vessels. His rebellion is not from the margins of Israel but from within the Levitical inner circle — making it one of the most personally devastating challenges Moses faces. The genealogy records him without judgment, as it records all names. The shadow of Num 16 falls across this verse for every reader who knows the story.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Uzziel; Mishael, and Elzaphan, and Zithri.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי עֻזִּיאֵל מִישָׁאֵל וְאֶלְצָפָן וְסִתְרִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Uzzi'el Misha'el ve'Eltzafan veSitri.
verses.study_note: The sons of Uzziel (another brother of Amram) include Mishael and Elzaphan — who will later be called by Moses to carry the bodies of Nadab and Abihu from the sanctuary after their unauthorized fire offering (Lev 10:4-5). They are summoned precisely because of their Kohathite kinship to the dead men (Moses says 'your brethren'). The genealogy here quietly places these men on the page before their moment of solemn duty arises. The genealogical record serves as a roster of those whose lives intersect with the great events of the Exodus and wilderness periods at critical moments.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron took him Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Naashon, to wife; and she bare him Nadab, and Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח אַהֲרֹן אֶת אֱלִישֶׁבַע בַּת עַמִּינָדָב אֲחוֹת נַחְשׁוֹן לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ אֶת נָדָב וְאֶת אֲבִיהוּא אֶת אֶלְעָזָר וְאֶת אִיתָמָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikkach Aharon et-Elisheva bat Amminadav achot Nachshon lo le'ishah vatteleld lo et-Nadav ve'et-Avihu et-Ele'azar ve'et-Itamar.
verses.study_note: Aaron's marriage links the tribe of Levi to the tribe of Judah: Elisheba (Elisheva — 'My God is seven/my God is abundance') is the daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon — and Nahshon is a prince of the tribe of Judah (Num 2:3) and an ancestor of Boaz, David, and ultimately Jesus (Ruth 4:20; Matt 1:4). The union of Levi (priesthood) and Judah (kingship) in Aaron's marriage foreshadows the ultimate union of the priestly and royal offices in the Messiah (Heb 7:14 — Christ is of the tribe of Judah; Ps 110:4 — 'a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek'). Aaron's four sons — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar — will become the first priests of Israel. Nadab and Abihu's death (Lev 10) will be one of the most solemn moments in Israel's history.

verses.kjv_text: And the sons of Korah; Assir, and Elkanah, and Abiasaph: these are the families of the Korhites.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי קֹרַח אַסִּיר וְאֶלְקָנָה וַאֲבִיאָסָף אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת הַקָּרְחִי׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Korach Assir ve'Elkanah va'Avi'asaf elleh mishpechot haKorchi.
verses.study_note: The sons of Korah are listed after Korah himself appears in v.21 — and remarkably, they survive Korah's rebellion. Numbers 26:11 explicitly states: 'the children of Korah died not.' Despite their father's overthrow, his sons continue and become the founders of a guild of temple singers — the 'Sons of Korah' are credited with eleven psalms (Ps 42-49, 84-85, 87-88). The mercy embedded in this verse is extraordinary: the children of the great rebel become the great worshippers of Israel. The genealogy preserves a seed of grace within judgment. Abiasaph's line extends through the Korahite guilds to the great musician Heman, David's singer (1 Chr 6:33-38).

verses.kjv_text: And Eleazar Aaron’s son took him one of the daughters of Putiel to wife; and she bare him Phinehas: these are the heads of the fathers of the Levites according to their families.
verses.original_text: וְאֶלְעָזָר בֶּן אַהֲרֹן לָקַח לוֹ מִבְּנוֹת פּוּטִיאֵל לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ אֶת פִּינְחָס אֵלֶּה רָאשֵׁי אֲבוֹת הַלְּוִיִּם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'Ele'azar ben-Aharon lakach lo mibbenot Puti'el lo le'ishah vatteleld lo et-Pinchas elleh rashei avot haLevi'im lemishpechotam.
verses.study_note: The genealogy extends one more generation to Eleazar and his son Phinehas — who will become one of the most zealous figures in Israel's wilderness history. Phinehas's act of zeal at Baal-Peor (Num 25:7-8) — driving a spear through an Israelite man and a Midianite woman — earns him God's 'covenant of peace' and 'covenant of an everlasting priesthood' (Num 25:12-13). The high priesthood descends through Phinehas's line. His father Eleazar succeeds Aaron as High Priest (Num 20:26-28). The genealogy thus projects forward into the entire high-priestly succession of Israel. The mention of Phinehas here quietly forecasts the Messianic priest-king who will exhibit the same zeal for God's holiness.

verses.kjv_text: These are that Aaron and Moses, to whom the LORD said, Bring out the children of Israel from the land of Egypt according to their armies.
verses.original_text: הוּא אַהֲרֹן וּמֹשֶׁה אֲשֶׁר אָמַר יְהוָה לָהֶם הוֹצִיאוּ אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם עַל צִבְאֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Hu Aharon uMoshe asher amar YHVH lahem hotzi'u et-benei Yisra'el me'eretz Mitzrayim al tziv'otam.
verses.study_note: The genealogy comes to its destination and makes its identification explicit: 'These are that Aaron and Moses' (hu Aharon uMoshe). The pronoun 'hu' (he/they — singular used emphatically for identification) points at the men who have just been positioned in their full genealogical context. The word 'tziv'otam' (according to their armies/hosts) is significant: Israel is described as 'tzva'ot' (armies, hosts), the same word as in the divine name 'YHVH Tzva'ot' (the LORD of Hosts). Before Israel becomes the LORD's army in the wilderness, they are called armies while still in Egypt — their identity as a host precedes their military history. The deliverers are given; the delivered are identified as God's army.

verses.kjv_text: These are they which spake to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the children of Israel from Egypt: these are that Moses and Aaron.
verses.original_text: הֵם הַמְדַבְּרִים אֶל פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם לְהוֹצִיא אֶת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמִּצְרָיִם הוּא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Hem hamedabberim el-Far'oh melekh Mitzrayim lehotzi et-benei Yisra'el miMitzrayim hu Moshe ve'Aharon.
verses.study_note: The genealogy closes with a parallel confirmation: 'These are they which spake to Pharaoh... these are that Moses and Aaron' (hu Moshe ve'Aharon). The order is reversed from v.26 (Aaron and Moses → Moses and Aaron), a stylistic chiasm that brackets the identification. The narrator uses the closing genealogical notation to anchor the narrative firmly: the readers now know exactly who these men are, where they come from, and what they have been commissioned to do. The genealogy served as a credentials document — establishing legitimacy before the next audience with Pharaoh begins.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the day when the LORD spake unto Moses in the land of Egypt,
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּיוֹם דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi beyom dibber YHVH el-Moshe be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The narrative resumes after the genealogical interruption with the formula 'vayehi' (and it came to pass) — the narrator's standard signal for resumed action after a digression. 'On the day when the LORD spoke to Moses' (beyom dibber YHVH) — this temporal phrase ties the resuming narrative back to the commission of vv.10-13, completing the frame around the genealogy. The phrase 'in the land of Egypt' (be'eretz Mitzrayim) is emphatic — placed at the end of the verse for stress. This is not a theophany in the wilderness (as chs. 3-4) but God speaking directly into the heart of the enemy's territory. The commission is issued inside Egypt, where the battle must be fought.

verses.kjv_text: That the LORD spake unto Moses, saying, I am the LORD: speak thou unto Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say unto thee.
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר אֲנִי יְהוָה דַּבֵּר אֶל פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם אֵת כָּל אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי דֹבֵר אֵלֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Moshe lemor ani YHVH dabber el-Far'oh melekh Mitzrayim et kol asher ani dover eleikha.
verses.study_note: The commission restated in its simplest and most authoritative form, anchored once again by the divine name: 'I am the LORD' (ani YHVH). The command is unqualified: speak 'all' (kol) that I say to you — no editing, no softening, no omission. The repeated 'dabber' (speak) is characteristic of the prophetic calling: speak regardless of response. The divine speech is in the present tense: 'all that I am speaking' (asher ani dover — ongoing, continuous speech). God is not giving Moses a script to memorize but a living stream of ongoing divine communication. The deliverer is not a herald with a prepared message but a vessel for continuous revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said before the LORD, Behold, I am of uncircumcised lips, and how shall Pharaoh hearken unto me?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה הֵן אֲנִי עֲרַל שְׂפָתַיִם וְאֵיךְ יִשְׁמַע אֵלַי פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Moshe lifnei YHVH hen ani aral sefatayim ve'eikh yishma elai Far'oh.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with Moses repeating his objection word for word from v.12 — the same uncircumcised lips, the same rhetorical question about Pharaoh. The repetition is deliberate: it creates a structural frame (vv.12, 30) around the commission of vv.13-29. But it also shows the character of Moses: even after the sevenfold 'I will' covenant speech of vv.6-8, even after the genealogy that grounds him in Israel's sacred history, he returns to the same self-deprecating objection. The persistence of Moses's weakness is not presented as moral failure but as a repeated opportunity for God's power to be the answer. Exodus 7:1-2 provides God's definitive answer: 'I have made thee a god to Pharaoh; and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet.' The chapter ends on the objection; the next chapter will begin with God overruling it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The inclusion of Moses's repeated objection at both v.12 and v.30 creates a deliberate literary frame: inside the frame is the genealogy (who Moses is) and the commission (what Moses is called to do). The frame says: despite knowing exactly who he is and exactly what he is called to do, Moses still returns to his sense of inadequacy. This is not lack of faith but the honest texture of the believing soul. Paul describes the same dynamic in 2 Corinthians 12:8-9 — three times he asked God to remove his 'thorn,' and three times God answered: 'My grace is sufficient.' Moses's repeated 'I am of uncircumcised lips' is met by God's repeated commission, which is itself the grace that makes speech possible. The book of Exodus will record Moses speaking some of the most powerful, eloquent, and effective words in human history — all through lips he called 'uncircumcised.'

meta.overview: Exodus 6 is God's direct answer to Moses's lament at the end of chapter 5. Where chapter 5 ends with 'thou hast not delivered thy people at all,' chapter 6 opens with the most concentrated self-disclosure of the divine name and covenant in the entire Torah. God answers Moses not with an explanation of the delay but with a cascade of seven 'I will' promises rooted in His eternal covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the Abrahamic covenant is now entering its fulfilment phase. The new revelation is not the name YHWH (which the patriarchs used) but the experiential dimension of that name: 'I appeared unto Abraham... by the name of God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name YHWH was I not known to them.' The full covenant-redemption content of the name — the God who keeps every promise across centuries — is now being demonstrated. The chapter introduces the crucial word 'ga'al' (to redeem as a kinsman-redeemer — v.6), the first of its kind in Exodus. Israel cannot hear the promise due to crushed spirits. Moses cannot speak due to 'uncircumcised lips.' Yet God reissues the commission through both weaknesses. The genealogy (vv.14-27) grounds the deliverers in Israel's tribal history before the narrative resumes.
meta.theme: The Self-Revealing God, the Covenant Kept & the Kinsman-Redeemer
meta.keyWordMeaning: To redeem as a kinsman — the duty of the nearest male relative to buy back an enslaved family member, restore lost property, or avenge blood. The first occurrence in the Exodus narrative (v.6) of the word that becomes the theology of redemption. God acts as Israel's Go'el — their divine next-of-kin Redeemer.
outline: The Sevenfold 'I Will' Covenant Response (vv.1-8): God answers Moses's lament with His name and seven promises
Israel Cannot Hear — Moses Cannot Speak (vv.9-13): Anguish of spirit and uncircumcised lips
The Genealogy of Reuben, Simeon, and Levi — Moses and Aaron's Lineage (vv.14-25)
The Commission Restated: 'Bring out the children of Israel' (vv.26-27)
Moses's Repeated Objection and God's Repeated Commission (vv.28-30)

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, See, I have made thee a god to Pharaoh: and Aaron thy brother shall be thy prophet.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה רְאֵה נְתַתִּיךָ אֱלֹהִים לְפַרְעֹה וְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ יִהְיֶה נְבִיאֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh re'eh netattikha elohim leFar'oh ve'Aharon achikha yihyeh nevi'ekha.
verses.study_note: God addresses Moses' objection from 6:30 ('I am of uncircumcised lips'). The stunning declaration: 'I have made thee elohim (god) to Pharaoh.' Not that Moses becomes divine, but that he stands in God's stead — bearing divine authority and speaking divine words. Pharaoh claimed to be divine (the incarnation of Horus, son of Ra); now God installs Moses as the true representative of the true God. Aaron becomes Moses' 'prophet' (navi) — the one who speaks on behalf of another. This mirrors the God-prophet relationship: God gives the word, the prophet delivers it. The pattern: YHVH → Moses → Aaron → Pharaoh parallels the prophetic chain throughout Scripture.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prophetic office defined: A prophet (navi) is one who speaks on behalf of another. Aaron speaks for Moses as Moses receives from God. This establishes the foundational pattern of biblical prophecy — the prophet does not originate the message but transmits what he receives. Christ fulfills this as the ultimate Prophet who speaks what He hears from the Father (John 12:49-50).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt speak all that I command thee: and Aaron thy brother shall speak unto Pharaoh, that he send the children of Israel out of his land.
verses.original_text: אַתָּה תְדַבֵּר אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר אֲצַוֶּךָּ וְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ יְדַבֵּר אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְשִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאַרְצוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Attah tedabber et kol-asher atzavvekka ve'Aharon achikha yedabber el-Far'oh veshillach et-benei-Yisra'el me'artzo.
verses.study_note: The command chain is explicit: Moses speaks 'all that I command thee' — completeness and faithfulness are required. A prophet cannot add, subtract, or modify (cf. Deut 4:2, Rev 22:18-19). Aaron then delivers the message to Pharaoh. The goal remains: 'that he send' (veshillach) — the Piel intensive of 'shalach,' meaning to release, to let go, to send away. This is the demand that will echo throughout the plague narrative: 'Let my people go.'

verses.kjv_text: And I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, and multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי אַקְשֶׁה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת־אֹתֹתַי וְאֶת־מוֹפְתַי בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ani aksheh et-lev Far'oh vehirbeiti et-ototai ve'et-mofetai be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The first explicit statement that God will 'harden' (aksheh — from 'qashah,' to make hard, stiff, severe) Pharaoh's heart. Three different Hebrew verbs describe this hardening throughout Exodus: 'qashah' (to make hard/severe — used here), 'chazaq' (to strengthen/make firm — 4:21), and 'kaved' (to make heavy/dull — 8:15). In the first five plagues, Pharaoh hardens his own heart; from the sixth plague onward, God confirms that hardening. God does not create evil in Pharaoh but confirms the trajectory Pharaoh has chosen. 'Signs' (otot) and 'wonders' (mofetim) — signs point to truth; wonders produce awe. Together they authenticate God's message.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is one of the great theological questions of Scripture. Three verbs are used: qashah (make hard), chazaq (strengthen), and kaved (make heavy). Pharaoh first hardens his own heart (8:15, 8:32, 9:34); God then confirms that trajectory (9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27). This is not arbitrary predestination but judicial confirmation — God gives Pharaoh over to what Pharaoh has chosen. Paul cites this in Romans 9:17-18 as an example of divine sovereignty, while the narrative itself preserves Pharaoh's agency. Both truths stand together.

verses.kjv_text: But Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you, that I may lay my hand upon Egypt, and bring forth mine armies, and my people the children of Israel, out of the land of Egypt by great judgments.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־יִשְׁמַע אֲלֵכֶם פַּרְעֹה וְנָתַתִּי אֶת־יָדִי בְּמִצְרָיִם וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶת־צִבְאֹתַי אֶת־עַמִּי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בִּשְׁפָטִים גְּדֹלִים׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-yishma aleikhem Far'oh venatatti et-yadi beMitzrayim vehotzeti et-tziv'otai et-ammi venei-Yisra'el me'eretz Mitzrayim bishfatim gedolim.
verses.study_note: God foreknows Pharaoh's refusal and integrates it into His plan. The phrase 'lay my hand' (natatti et-yadi) — a powerful anthropomorphism for direct divine intervention. Israel is called 'mine armies' (tziv'otai) — the origin of 'YHVH Tzva'ot' (LORD of Hosts). Even in slavery, Israel is God's army. The 'great judgments' (shefatim gedolim) are the ten plagues — not random calamities but judicial acts against Egypt's gods and Pharaoh's claims.
verses.doctrinal_note: The plagues are 'judgments' (shefatim) — legal verdicts against Egypt's entire religious system. Exodus 12:12 confirms: 'against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment.' Each plague targets a specific Egyptian deity, systematically dismantling the cosmological claims of Egypt's religion. This is theological warfare, not mere spectacle.

verses.kjv_text: And the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I stretch forth mine hand upon Egypt, and bring out the children of Israel from among them.
verses.original_text: וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה בִּנְטֹתִי אֶת־יָדִי עַל־מִצְרָיִם וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶת־בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִתּוֹכָם׃
verses.transliteration: Veyade'u Mitzrayim ki-ani YHVH binetoti et-yadi al-Mitzrayim vehotzeti et-benei-Yisra'el mittokham.
verses.study_note: The purpose clause of the entire plague narrative: 'the Egyptians shall KNOW that I am the LORD' (veyade'u... ki-ani YHVH). This 'recognition formula' appears repeatedly in Exodus (7:17, 8:10, 8:22, 9:14, 9:29, 10:2, 11:7, 14:4, 14:18) and is foundational in Ezekiel (used over 60 times). God's judgments are revelatory — they force recognition of who YHVH is. The stretching forth of God's hand (binetoti et-yadi) is the gesture of sovereign power and judicial action.
verses.doctrinal_note: The ultimate purpose of the plagues is not merely punishment but revelation: that all nations 'shall know that I am the LORD.' Judgment is a form of self-disclosure. What mercy does not teach, judgment will. This purpose extends beyond Egypt — Rahab in Jericho (Josh 2:10-11) and the Gibeonites (Josh 9:9) both acknowledge YHVH because of what they heard about the Exodus.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron did as the LORD commanded them, so did they.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתָם כֵּן עָשׂוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as Mosheh ve'Aharon ka'asher tzivvah YHVH otam ken asu.
verses.study_note: A brief but critical statement of obedience. 'As the LORD commanded... so did they' (ka'asher tzivvah... ken asu) — this obedience formula becomes a refrain in Exodus (7:6, 7:10, 7:20, 12:28, 12:50, 39:32, 40:16). Exact obedience — not partial, not modified, not delayed. The contrast with Moses' earlier reluctance (chs. 3-4) is striking. The man who asked 'Who am I?' (3:11) now acts without hesitation. This is the fruit of the commissioning in chapters 3-6.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses was fourscore years old, and Aaron fourscore and three years old, when they spake unto Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וּמֹשֶׁה בֶּן־שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה וְאַהֲרֹן בֶּן־שָׁלֹשׁ וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה בְּדַבְּרָם אֶל־פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: UMosheh ben-shemonim shanah ve'Aharon ben-shalosh ushemonim shanah bedabberam el-Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Moses is 80, Aaron is 83. Moses' life divides into three periods of 40 years: 40 years in Egypt as prince (Acts 7:23), 40 years in Midian as shepherd (Acts 7:30), and 40 years leading Israel in the wilderness (Deut 34:7). He begins his greatest work at 80 — an age when human strength is spent. God's timing and God's power, not human vitality, accomplish deliverance. Stephen's speech in Acts 7 traces these three periods as God's providential preparation.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon lemor.
verses.study_note: A new section begins. God speaks to both Moses and Aaron together — they are a prophetic team. The word 'lemor' (saying) introduces a new divine instruction. What follows (vv.9-13) is the first confrontation sign before Pharaoh — the rod-to-serpent sign — which differs from the rod-to-snake sign given to Moses at the burning bush (4:3). There, the word was 'nachash' (snake); here it will be 'tannin' (serpent/dragon) — a word loaded with Egyptian royal and mythological significance.

verses.kjv_text: When Pharaoh shall speak unto you, saying, Shew a miracle for you: then thou shalt say unto Aaron, Take thy rod, and cast it before Pharaoh, and it shall become a serpent.
verses.original_text: כִּי יְדַבֵּר אֲלֵכֶם פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר תְּנוּ לָכֶם מוֹפֵת וְאָמַרְתָּ אֶל־אַהֲרֹן קַח אֶת־מַטְּךָ וְהַשְׁלֵךְ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה יְהִי לְתַנִּין׃
verses.transliteration: Ki yedabber aleikhem Far'oh lemor tenu lakhem mofet ve'amarta el-Aharon kach et-mattekha vehashlekh lifnei Far'oh yehi letannin.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh will demand a 'wonder' (mofet) — authentication of their divine commission. God anticipates this and prepares the response: Aaron's rod cast down becomes a 'tannin' — not the common 'nachash' (snake) of Exodus 4:3, but a 'tannin,' a term meaning serpent, dragon, or sea monster. In Egyptian religion, the cobra (uraeus) adorned Pharaoh's crown as a symbol of divine power and protection. The tannin also represented Apophis (Apep), the chaos serpent of Egyptian mythology. By transforming the rod into a tannin, God directly confronts the symbols of Egyptian royal and religious authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: God fights on the enemy's own ground. The tannin is Egypt's symbol — and YHVH demonstrates absolute sovereignty over it. The rod (matteh) of Moses/Aaron represents delegated divine authority. It is the same rod that will part the Red Sea (14:16), strike the rock (17:6), and be raised in battle (17:9). In Scripture, God consistently uses weak instruments (a rod, a sling, jawbone of a donkey) to shame the mighty (1 Cor 1:27).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron went in unto Pharaoh, and they did so as the LORD had commanded: and Aaron cast down his rod before Pharaoh, and before his servants, and it became a serpent.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ אַהֲרֹן אֶת־מַטֵּהוּ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וְלִפְנֵי עֲבָדָיו וַיְהִי לְתַנִּין׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Mosheh ve'Aharon el-Far'oh vayya'asu khen ka'asher tzivvah YHVH vayyashlekh Aharon et-mattehu lifnei Far'oh velifnei avadav vayehi letannin.
verses.study_note: Again the obedience formula: 'they did so as the LORD had commanded.' The rod is cast before Pharaoh 'and before his servants' — this is a public confrontation in the royal court, likely in the throne room with priests, magicians, and officials watching. The rod becoming a tannin in the very court where the uraeus cobra adorns Pharaoh's crown is a deliberate provocation — YHVH challenges Pharaoh's claimed authority on his own ground.

verses.kjv_text: Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers: now the magicians of Egypt, they also did in like manner with their enchantments.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא גַּם־פַּרְעֹה לַחֲכָמִים וְלַמְכַשְּׁפִים וַיַּעֲשׂוּ גַם־הֵם חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם בְּלַהֲטֵיהֶם כֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra gam-Far'oh lachakhamim velamkhashfim vayya'asu gam-hem chartummei Mitzrayim belahatehem ken.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh summons three categories of occult practitioners: 'wise men' (chakhamim — advisors versed in sacred lore), 'sorcerers' (mekhashfim — those who practice dark arts, later condemned in Deut 18:10), and 'magicians' (chartummim — possibly from Egyptian 'hery-tp,' chief lector priests who read sacred texts). They replicate the sign 'with their enchantments' (belahatehem — from 'lahat,' meaning flames, secret arts, or perhaps sleight of hand). Paul identifies two of these magicians by name: Jannes and Jambres (2 Tim 3:8). The replication gives Pharaoh a pretext to dismiss YHVH's sign as just another form of magic.
verses.doctrinal_note: The existence of counterfeit miracles does not disprove the genuine. Satan can produce convincing imitations (2 Thess 2:9). The magicians' ability to replicate the sign reveals a critical spiritual truth: the test is not merely 'can you do wonders?' but 'whose power is supreme?' Verse 12 will answer decisively. Discernment is needed to distinguish divine power from demonic imitation (1 John 4:1).

verses.kjv_text: For they cast down every man his rod, and they became serpents: but Aaron’s rod swallowed up their rods.
verses.original_text: וַיַּשְׁלִיכוּ אִישׁ מַטֵּהוּ וַיִּהְיוּ לְתַנִּינִם וַיִּבְלַע מַטֵּה־אַהֲרֹן אֶת־מַטֹּתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashlikhu ish mattehu vayyihyu letanninim vayyivla matteh-Aharon et-mattotam.
verses.study_note: The decisive detail: 'Aaron's rod swallowed up their rods' (vayyivla matteh-Aharon et-mattotam). The magicians can imitate — but Aaron's rod DEVOURS theirs. 'Swallowed' (bala) is the same verb used when the earth swallows Korah's rebellion (Num 16:32) and when death is 'swallowed up in victory' (Isa 25:8, 1 Cor 15:54). The verb indicates complete consumption and total defeat. Pharaoh should have recognized the implication: YHVH's authority does not merely match but utterly consumes every rival power. The serpent that represents Pharaoh's crown has been devoured by the rod of the God of slaves.
verses.doctrinal_note: God does not merely compete with rival powers — He consumes them. Aaron's rod swallowing the magicians' rods is a prophetic enactment: YHVH will ultimately swallow everything that opposes Him. This principle culminates in 1 Corinthians 15:54: 'Death is swallowed up in victory.' Christ's resurrection is the ultimate 'swallowing' of the last enemy. What begins in Exodus 7 reaches its fulfillment at the empty tomb.

verses.kjv_text: And he hardened Pharaoh’s heart, that he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyechezak lev Far'oh velo shama alehem ka'asher dibber YHVH.
verses.study_note: Here 'chazaq' (strengthened/hardened) is used. The grammar is ambiguous — it can be translated 'Pharaoh's heart was strengthened' (reflexive: he hardened himself) or 'He (God) strengthened Pharaoh's heart.' This deliberate ambiguity preserves both divine sovereignty and human responsibility. 'As the LORD had said' (ka'asher dibber YHVH) — God's prediction in v.4 is fulfilled precisely. Nothing surprises God; Pharaoh's resistance is already factored into the divine plan.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh’s heart is hardened, he refuseth to let the people go.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כָּבֵד לֵב פַּרְעֹה מֵאֵן לְשַׁלַּח הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh kaved lev Far'oh me'en leshallach ha'am.
verses.study_note: Now the third hardening verb appears: 'kaved' (heavy, dull, insensible — from the same root as 'kavod,' glory/weight). Pharaoh's heart is 'heavy' — weighed down, unresponsive. 'Me'en' (refuses) — a deliberate, willful refusal. God diagnoses the condition before prescribing the remedy. The first plague is about to begin — the section vv.14-25 constitutes the complete account of Plague 1: Water to Blood.

verses.kjv_text: Get thee unto Pharaoh in the morning; lo, he goeth out unto the water; and thou shalt stand by the river’s brink against he come; and the rod which was turned to a serpent shalt thou take in thine hand.
verses.original_text: לֵךְ אֶל־פַּרְעֹה בַּבֹּקֶר הִנֵּה יֹצֵא הַמַּיְמָה וְנִצַּבְתָּ לִקְרָאתוֹ עַל־שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר וְהַמַּטֶּה אֲשֶׁר־נֶהְפַּךְ לְנָחָשׁ תִּקַּח בְּיָדֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Lekh el-Far'oh babboker hinneh yotze hammaymah venitzavta likrato al-sefat haYe'or vehammatteh asher-nehpakh lenachash tikkach beyadekha.
verses.study_note: God directs Moses to meet Pharaoh 'in the morning' at the Nile — likely during a ritual morning procession. Egyptian texts confirm that Pharaoh visited the Nile for religious ablutions and ceremonies honoring Hapi (the Nile god). God arranges the confrontation at the very site of Egyptian worship. 'Thou shalt stand by the river's brink' (al-sefat haYe'or) — 'Ye'or' is the Hebrew rendering of the Egyptian word for the Nile ('itrw'). Moses is to stand with the rod 'which was turned to a serpent' — a reminder of YHVH's demonstrated power. The setting is deliberately chosen: judgment falls at the site of false worship.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath sent me unto thee, saying, Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness: and, behold, hitherto thou wouldest not hear.
verses.original_text: וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים שְׁלָחַנִי אֵלֶיךָ לֵאמֹר שַׁלַּח אֶת־עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי בַּמִּדְבָּר וְהִנֵּה לֹא־שָׁמַעְתָּ עַד־כֹּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amarta elav YHVH Elohei ha'Ivrim shelachani eleikha lemor shallach et-ammi veya'avduni bamidbar vehineh lo-shama'ta ad-koh.
verses.study_note: The message reintroduces the divine name: 'YHVH, God of the Hebrews' — identifying God not by Egyptian categories but by His covenant relationship with His people. 'Let my people go, that they may serve me' (shallach... veya'avduni) — the purpose of liberation is worship. Freedom from Pharaoh is freedom FOR God. The word 'serve' (avad) is the same word used for Israel's slavery in Egypt — they will exchange servitude to Pharaoh for service to YHVH. 'Hitherto thou wouldest not hear' (lo-shama'ta ad-koh) — a judicial indictment. Pharaoh has been given opportunity and has refused.

verses.kjv_text: Thus saith the LORD, In this thou shalt know that I am the LORD: behold, I will smite with the rod that is in mine hand upon the waters which are in the river, and they shall be turned to blood.
verses.original_text: כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה בְּזֹאת תֵּדַע כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי מַכֶּה בַּמַּטֶּה אֲשֶׁר־בְּיָדִי עַל־הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר וְנֶהֶפְכוּ לְדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Koh amar YHVH bezot teda ki ani YHVH hinneh anokhi makkeh bammatteh asher-beyadi al-hammayim asher baYe'or venehefkhu ledam.
verses.study_note: 'In this thou shalt KNOW that I am the LORD' — the recognition formula applied to the first plague. God announces His judgment before executing it — giving Pharaoh one more chance to repent. The Nile will be struck and 'turned to blood' (venehefkhu ledam). The Nile was the lifeblood of Egypt: annual flooding deposited fertile soil, its waters irrigated crops, its fish fed the population. Hapi, the Nile god, was one of Egypt's most revered deities. By striking the Nile, God strikes the foundation of Egyptian civilization and religion. 'I will smite' (anokhi makkeh) — the emphatic pronoun 'anokhi' (I myself) stresses that this is YHVH's personal act.
verses.doctrinal_note: The first plague establishes the pattern: God announces judgment, giving opportunity for repentance; judgment targets the specific false god being worshipped; the purpose is revelatory — 'thou shalt know that I am the LORD.' This pattern of announced, targeted, purposeful judgment runs through all of Scripture to the book of Revelation. God does not strike without warning.

verses.kjv_text: And the fish that is in the river shall die, and the river shall stink; and the Egyptians shall lothe to drink of the water of the river.
verses.original_text: וְהַדָּגָה אֲשֶׁר־בַּיְאֹר תָּמוּת וּבָאַשׁ הַיְאֹר וְנִלְאוּ מִצְרַיִם לִשְׁתּוֹת מַיִם מִן־הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehadagah asher-baYe'or tamut uva'ash haYe'or venil'u Mitzrayim lishtot mayim min-haYe'or.
verses.study_note: Three devastating consequences: (1) the fish die (tamut) — destroying a major food source; (2) the river stinks (uva'ash) — the symbol of fertility becomes revolting; (3) the Egyptians cannot drink (venil'u... lishtot) — 'nil'u' means they will be exhausted, unable, disgusted. The word for 'lothe' is the same root as the weariness of the Sodomites in Genesis 19:11. Fish were a staple of the Egyptian diet (cf. Num 11:5 — Israel remembers 'the fish which we did eat freely'), and the fish goddess Hatmehit was worshipped. Every consequence strikes a different aspect of Egypt's dependence on the Nile.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Take thy rod, and stretch out thine hand upon the waters of Egypt, upon their streams, upon their rivers, and upon their ponds, and upon all their pools of water, that they may become blood; and that there may be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood, and in vessels of stone.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֱמֹר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן קַח מַטְּךָ וּנְטֵה־יָדְךָ עַל־מֵימֵי מִצְרַיִם עַל־נַהֲרֹתָם עַל־יְאֹרֵיהֶם וְעַל־אַגְמֵיהֶם וְעַל כָּל־מִקְוֵה מֵימֵיהֶם וְיִהְיוּ דָם וְהָיָה דָם בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וּבָעֵצִים וּבָאֲבָנִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh emor el-Aharon kach mattekha unteh-yadekha al-meimei Mitzrayim al-naharotam al-ye'oreihem ve'al-agmeihem ve'al kol-mikveh meimeihem veyihyu dam vehayah dam bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim uva'etzim uva'avanim.
verses.study_note: The scope is comprehensive: streams (naharot), rivers (ye'orim — plural of Ye'or, the Nile and its channels), ponds (agamim — marshes, pools), 'all their pools of water' (kol-mikveh meimeihem — every collection of water). Even water stored in wooden and stone vessels turns to blood. This is not localized — it is 'throughout ALL the land of Egypt.' No water source is exempt. The totality leaves no escape and no natural explanation. The God who created water now transforms it — demonstrating absolute sovereignty over the elements He made.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron did so, as the LORD commanded; and he lifted up the rod, and smote the waters that were in the river, in the sight of Pharaoh, and in the sight of his servants; and all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה וַיָּרֶם בַּמַּטֶּה וַיַּךְ אֶת־הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר לְעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה וּלְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו וַיֵּהָפְכוּ כָּל־הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר לְדָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu khen Mosheh ve'Aharon ka'asher tzivvah YHVH vayyarem bammatteh vayyakh et-hammayim asher baYe'or le'einei Far'oh ule'einei avadav vayyehafkhu kol-hammayim asher baYe'or ledam.
verses.study_note: The obedience formula again: 'as the LORD commanded.' Aaron lifts the rod and strikes the Nile 'in the sight of Pharaoh and in the sight of his servants' — a public, undeniable, witnessed act. 'All the waters... were turned to blood' (vayyehafkhu... ledam). The verb 'hafakh' (turned/overturned) is the same word used for the destruction of Sodom (Gen 19:25) — God's reversing power. The Nile, which Pharaoh had used to murder Hebrew infants (1:22), now runs with blood. The river of death becomes the river of judgment. The connection is not accidental — God's justice is poetic.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Nile that was the instrument of Pharaoh's genocide (Exodus 1:22 — 'cast every son into the river') now turns to blood. Divine justice is precise: the instrument of oppression becomes the site of judgment. This principle — the measure you use will be measured back to you (Matt 7:2) — operates throughout Scripture. Egypt's idolatry of the Nile is answered by the Nile's desecration. The first plague typologically connects to the final plague (blood of the firstborn) and ultimately to the blood of Christ, which brings not judgment but redemption.

verses.kjv_text: And the fish that was in the river died; and the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink of the water of the river; and there was blood throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהַדָּגָה אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר מֵתָה וַיִּבְאַשׁ הַיְאֹר וְלֹא־יָכְלוּ מִצְרַיִם לִשְׁתּוֹת מַיִם מִן־הַיְאֹר וַיְהִי הַדָּם בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehadagah asher baYe'or metah vayyiv'ash haYe'or velo-yakhlu Mitzrayim lishtot mayim min-haYe'or vayehi haddam bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The fulfillment matches the prediction (v.18) exactly — fish die, the river stinks, Egyptians cannot drink. 'Blood throughout all the land of Egypt' — the scope is total and national. The Hymn to Hapi, an ancient Egyptian text, praised the Nile: 'Lord of fishes... who makes the grass for the cattle... who makes every beloved tree to grow.' Now the Nile is dead, its fish rotting, its water undrinkable. The god Hapi has been exposed as impotent before YHVH. This is the beginning of Egypt's entire cosmological collapse.

verses.kjv_text: And the magicians of Egypt did so with their enchantments: and Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, neither did he hearken unto them; as the LORD had said.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם בְּלָטֵיהֶם וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא־שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen chartummei Mitzrayim belateihem vayyechezak lev Far'oh velo-shama alehem ka'asher dibber YHVH.
verses.study_note: The magicians replicate the plague — turning more water into blood. The bitter irony: they can make the problem WORSE but cannot reverse it. They can imitate destruction but cannot produce restoration. They cannot turn blood back into water. This is the fundamental limitation of counterfeit power — it can only destroy, never heal. 'Belateihem' (their enchantments) uses a slightly different form than v.11 (belahatehem) — both refer to secret, occult arts. Pharaoh uses the replication as justification for hardness: if his magicians can do the same, then Moses' God is not unique. But the magicians cannot undo what God has done.
verses.doctrinal_note: Counterfeit miracles can replicate but not reverse. The magicians could turn water to blood but could not turn blood back to water. This exposes the nature of demonic power: it can imitate judgment but never produce redemption. Only God heals, restores, and makes new. This distinction is critical for discernment in every age (1 John 4:1-3).

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh turned and went into his house, neither did he set his heart to this also.
verses.original_text: וַיִּפֶן פַּרְעֹה וַיָּבֹא אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ וְלֹא־שָׁת לִבּוֹ גַּם־לָזֹאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyifen Far'oh vayyavo el-beto velo-shat libbo gam-lazot.
verses.study_note: A devastating portrait of spiritual hardness: Pharaoh simply 'turned and went into his house' (vayyifen... vayyavo el-beto). He walks away. The Nile is blood, fish are dying, the nation is in crisis — and Pharaoh goes inside. 'Neither did he set his heart to this also' (velo-shat libbo gam-lazot) — he did not 'put his heart' (shat libbo) to the matter. 'Gam-lazot' (even to this) implies this is not his first dismissal. The most dangerous spiritual condition is not outrage against God but indifference — the ability to witness divine power and shrug.

verses.kjv_text: And all the Egyptians digged round about the river for water to drink; for they could not drink of the water of the river.
verses.original_text: וַיַּחְפְּרוּ כָל־מִצְרַיִם סְבִיבֹת הַיְאֹר מַיִם לִשְׁתּוֹת כִּי לֹא יָכְלוּ לִשְׁתֹּת מִמֵּימֵי הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyachperu khol-Mitzrayim sevivot haYe'or mayim lishtot ki lo yakhlu lishtot mimmeimei haYe'or.
verses.study_note: While Pharaoh goes inside, the people suffer.
