'All the Egyptians dug round about the river' — desperately seeking filtered groundwater that seeped through the soil and might be drinkable. The contrast is stark: the ruler dismisses the crisis while the population scrambles for survival. This detail reveals that the filtered subterranean water was apparently not turned to blood — suggesting God preserved a means of survival even within judgment, extending mercy even in wrath. The people bear the consequences of their king's rebellion.

verses.kjv_text: And seven days were fulfilled, after that the LORD had smitten the river.
verses.original_text: וַיִּמָּלֵא שִׁבְעַת יָמִים אַחֲרֵי הַכּוֹת־יְהוָה אֶת־הַיְאֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyimmale shiv'at yamim acharei hakkot-YHVH et-haYe'or.
verses.study_note: The plague lasts seven days — a complete period. 'Seven' (sheva) in Hebrew is from the root meaning 'completeness' or 'oath' (cf. 'shevuah' — oath/week). The seven days indicate a full, measured, complete judgment. God's judgments are not chaotic or endless but precisely measured. The phrase 'after that the LORD had smitten' (acharei hakkot-YHVH) — the Hiphil infinitive construct attributes the striking directly to YHVH. Though Aaron wielded the rod, the smiter is God. The first plague concludes; Pharaoh remains unchanged. Nine more will follow.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's judgments are measured and complete — seven days, not more, not less. The number seven (sheva) connotes completeness throughout Scripture: seven days of creation, seven seals, seven trumpets, seven bowls. Even in wrath, God operates with precision and self-restraint. This is not the fury of a capricious deity but the calibrated justice of a holy God who measures even His judgments.

meta.overview: Exodus 7 marks the dramatic escalation of the confrontation between YHVH and Pharaoh — the opening salvo of the ten plagues. God redefines Moses' role: he will be 'as God to Pharaoh' and Aaron will be his prophet. God announces that He will harden Pharaoh's heart and multiply His signs in Egypt. The chapter presents two pivotal signs: (1) Aaron's rod becomes a serpent (tannin) before Pharaoh, swallowing the magicians' serpents — a direct assault on Egyptian serpent worship and royal authority; (2) the first plague transforms the Nile into blood, striking at Hapi, the Nile god, and the very lifeblood of Egyptian civilization. The Egyptian magicians replicate both signs, giving Pharaoh a pretext for hardness. Throughout, the narrative establishes the theological framework for the entire plague cycle: YHVH wages systematic war against the gods of Egypt (12:12) to demonstrate that He alone is LORD.
meta.theme: Divine Authority, Serpent Sovereignty & the Nile Struck — Judgment on Egypt's Gods Begins
meta.keyWordMeaning: Rod/Staff — the instrument of divine authority and judgment. Moses' and Aaron's rod becomes the vehicle through which God demonstrates His power over Pharaoh and Egypt's deities.
outline: Moses as God, Aaron as Prophet (vv.1-7): The divine commission restated and the ages of Moses and Aaron
Aaron's Rod Becomes a Serpent (vv.8-13): The sign of the tannin — swallowing the magicians' serpents
The First Plague Announced (vv.14-18): YHVH confronts Pharaoh at the Nile with a warning
The Nile Turned to Blood (vv.19-21): Judgment on Hapi — water becomes blood throughout Egypt
Pharaoh's Heart Hardened (vv.22-25): The magicians replicate, Pharaoh turns away, Egypt digs for water

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, Go unto Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה בֹּא אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה שַׁלַּח אֶת־עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh bo el-Par'oh ve'amarta elav koh amar YHVH shallach et-ammi veya'avduni.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with the recurring demand: 'Let my people go, that they may serve me' (shallach et-ammi veya'avduni). The verb 'bo' (come/go in) rather than 'lekh' (go) suggests boldness — Moses is to enter Pharaoh's presence with divine authority. The demand is not merely political liberation but theological reorientation: Israel must be freed FROM Pharaoh's service TO YHVH's service. 'Serve' (avad) is the same word for both slavery and worship — the question is not whether Israel will serve but whom.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's redemption always has a purpose beyond mere freedom. Israel is not freed into autonomy but into worship. The New Testament echoes this: believers are freed from sin not to live for themselves but to serve God (Romans 6:18, 22). True freedom is not the absence of a master but the presence of the right one.

verses.kjv_text: And if thou refuse to let them go, behold, I will smite all thy borders with frogs:
verses.original_text: וְאִם־מָאֵן אַתָּה לְשַׁלֵּחַ הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נֹגֵף אֶת־כָּל־גְּבוּלְךָ בַּצְפַרְדְּעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-ma'en attah leshalle'ach hinneh anokhi nogef et-kol-gevulekha batzfarde'im.
verses.study_note: The warning precedes the plague — God gives Pharaoh opportunity to repent before judgment falls. 'I will smite' (nogef) — God Himself strikes; this is not a natural event but divine action. 'All thy borders' (kol-gevulekha) — the plague will be comprehensive, reaching every corner of Egypt. The frog (tzefarde'a) was sacred in Egypt — Heqet, the frog-headed goddess, presided over fertility and childbirth. God turns Egypt's object of worship into an instrument of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And the river shall bring forth frogs abundantly, which shall go up and come into thine house, and into thy bedchamber, and upon thy bed, and into the house of thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thine ovens, and into thy kneadingtroughs:
verses.original_text: וְשָׁרַץ הַיְאֹר צְפַרְדְּעִים וְעָלוּ וּבָאוּ בְּבֵיתֶךָ וּבַחֲדַר מִשְׁכָּבְךָ וְעַל־מִטָּתֶךָ וּבְבֵית עֲבָדֶיךָ וּבְעַמֶּךָ וּבְתַנּוּרֶיךָ וּבְמִשְׁאֲרוֹתֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vesharatz haye'or tzfarde'im ve'alu uva'u beveiteka uvachadar mishkavekha ve'al-mittatekha uveveit avadekha uve'ammekha uvetannurekha uvemish'arotekha.
verses.study_note: The invasion is total and intimate: house, bedroom, bed, servants' quarters, ovens, kneading troughs. No space is private or sacred. The Nile (ye'or) — Egypt's lifeblood, already turned to blood (7:20) — now spawns an uncontrollable infestation. 'Sharatz' (to swarm/teem) is the same word used for creation's abundance (Gen 1:20) — God's creative power turned to judgment. The frogs penetrate the most intimate and essential spaces: bedrooms (rest), ovens and kneading troughs (food production). Egypt's goddess of fertility now brings a plague of her own creatures.

verses.kjv_text: And the frogs shall come up both on thee, and upon thy people, and upon all thy servants.
verses.original_text: וּבְכָה וּבְעַמְּךָ וּבְכָל־עֲבָדֶיךָ יַעֲלוּ הַצְפַרְדְּעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Uvekha uve'ammekha uvekhol-avadekha ya'alu hatzfarde'im.
verses.study_note: The frogs come 'upon thee' — even Pharaoh himself is not exempt. The one who claims divine status is overrun by the creatures of the deity he worships. The deliberate escalation (you, your people, your servants) mirrors the social hierarchy of Egypt: no rank provides immunity. Pharaoh, who drowns Hebrew babies in the Nile, is now overrun by what comes from the Nile.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch forth thine hand with thy rod over the streams, over the rivers, and over the ponds, and cause frogs to come up upon the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֱמֹר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן נְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ בְּמַטֶּךָ עַל־הַנְּהָרוֹת עַל־הַיְאֹרִים וְעַל־הָאֲגַמִּים וְהַעַל אֶת־הַצְפַרְדְּעִים עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh emor el-Aharon neteh et-yadekha bematekha al-hanneharot al-haye'orim ve'al-ha'agammim veha'al et-hatzfarde'im al-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Aaron acts as Moses' instrument — stretching out the rod over all water sources: streams (neharot), rivers/canals (ye'orim), and ponds/pools (agammim). The comprehensive water vocabulary covers every water source in Egypt. The rod of God (matteh) that turned the Nile to blood now summons the second plague. Each water source that Egyptians depended upon becomes a source of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron stretched out his hand over the waters of Egypt; and the frogs came up, and covered the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט אַהֲרֹן אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל מֵימֵי מִצְרָיִם וַתַּעַל הַצְּפַרְדֵּעַ וַתְּכַס אֶת־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyet Aharon et-yado al meimei Mitzrayim vatta'al hatzefarddea vattekhas et-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Note the Hebrew singular 'hatzefarddea' (the frog) though the plague involves millions — this may emphasize the singular overwhelming nature of the judgment, or it may be a collective singular (common in Hebrew). 'Covered the land' (vattekhas) — the same verb used for water covering the earth in the Flood (Gen 7:19). Total, comprehensive inundation. The land that enslaved Israel is now enslaved by frogs.

verses.kjv_text: And the magicians did so with their enchantments, and brought up frogs upon the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן הַחַרְטֻמִּים בְּלָטֵיהֶם וַיַּעֲלוּ אֶת־הַצְפַרְדְּעִים עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen hachartummim belatehem vayya'alu et-hatzfarde'im al-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The magicians replicate the plague — but this only worsens Egypt's condition. They can add frogs but cannot remove them. The absurdity is striking: if their power were real, they should remove the frogs, not produce more. The magicians' 'enchantments' (belatehem — from 'lat,' to conceal/do secretly) involve occult practices. Satan can mimic God's works but cannot reverse God's judgments. The pattern is significant: the magicians matched blood (7:22) and now frogs — but their power is about to reach its limit.

verses.kjv_text: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron, and said, Intreat the LORD, that he may take away the frogs from me, and from my people; and I will let the people go, that they may do sacrifice unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר הַעְתִּירוּ אֶל־יְהוָה וְיָסֵר הַצְפַרְדְּעִים מִמֶּנִּי וּמֵעַמִּי וַאֲשַׁלְּחָה אֶת־הָעָם וְיִזְבְּחוּ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Far'oh leMosheh ule'Aharon vayomer ha'tiru el-YHVH veyaser hatzfarde'im mimmenni ume'ammi va'ashallachah et-ha'am veyizbechu laYHVH.
verses.study_note: A pivotal moment: Pharaoh does not appeal to his own gods or magicians — he asks Moses to 'intreat the LORD' (ha'tiru el-YHVH). He uses God's covenant name and implicitly acknowledges YHVH's power over the frogs. His promise 'I will let the people go' (va'ashallachah) is the first of several empty pledges. 'Sacrifice' (veyizbechu) — Pharaoh concedes the need for Israel to worship YHVH, but his words are insincere, as verse 15 will reveal.
verses.doctrinal_note: Pharaoh's appeal to Moses to pray illustrates a principle seen throughout Scripture: the pressure of judgment can produce the appearance of repentance without genuine heart change. True repentance is not mere discomfort with consequences but a genuine turning from sin to God. Pharaoh wants relief, not righteousness — a pattern Jesus warns against (Luke 6:46).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto Pharaoh, Glory over me: when shall I intreat for thee, and for thy servants, and for thy people, to destroy the frogs from thee and thy houses, that they may remain in the river only?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה לְפַרְעֹה הִתְפָּאֵר עָלַי לְמָתַי אַעְתִּיר לְךָ וְלַעֲבָדֶיךָ וּלְעַמְּךָ לְהַכְרִית הַצְפַרְדְּעִים מִמְּךָ וּמִבָּתֶּיךָ רַק בַּיְאֹר תִּשָּׁאַרְנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh leFar'oh hitpa'er alai lematai a'tir lekha vela'avadekha ule'ammekha lehakhrit hatzfarde'im mimmekha umibattekha rak baye'or tishsha'arnah.
verses.study_note: Moses offers Pharaoh the extraordinary privilege of choosing the timing: 'Glory over me: when shall I intreat?' (hitpa'er alai lematai). 'Hitpa'er' means to have the honor/glory — Moses lets Pharaoh set the schedule to demonstrate that YHVH's power is not bound by natural timing or magical coincidence. This eliminates any excuse that the plague ended naturally. The frogs will remain only in the Nile (baye'or) — their natural habitat — proving divine control over nature.

verses.kjv_text: And he said, To morrow. And he said, Be it according to thy word: that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר לְמָחָר וַיֹּאמֶר כִּדְבָרְךָ לְמַעַן תֵּדַע כִּי־אֵין כַּיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer lemachar vayomer kidvarekha lema'an teda ki-ein kaYHVH Eloheinu.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh says 'tomorrow' (lemachar) — remarkably choosing to endure one more night with frogs rather than requesting immediate relief. This reveals either an attempt to test whether Moses can truly deliver, or a heart so hardened that even urgent suffering does not produce immediate repentance. Moses' response contains the theological purpose of all the plagues: 'that thou mayest know that there is none like unto the LORD our God' (ein kaYHVH Eloheinu). The plagues are pedagogy — divine instruction through judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And the frogs shall depart from thee, and from thy houses, and from thy servants, and from thy people; they shall remain in the river only.
verses.original_text: וְסָרוּ הַצְפַרְדְּעִים מִמְּךָ וּמִבָּתֶּיךָ וּמֵעֲבָדֶיךָ וּמֵעַמֶּךָ רַק בַּיְאֹר תִּשָּׁאַרְנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesaru hatzfarde'im mimmekha umibattekha ume'avadekha ume'ammekha rak baye'or tishsha'arnah.
verses.study_note: Moses declares the precise scope of removal: from Pharaoh personally, from houses, servants, and people. The frogs will remain 'only in the river' — their natural habitat, demonstrating that God does not destroy creation itself but restores order by removing the plague. The precision of the promise underscores the precision of divine control. Every detail is under YHVH's sovereign direction.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron went out from Pharaoh: and Moses cried unto the LORD because of the frogs which he had brought against Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה וַיִּצְעַק מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוָה עַל־דְּבַר הַצְפַרְדְּעִים אֲשֶׁר־שָׂם לְפַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Mosheh ve'Aharon me'im Par'oh vayyitz'ak Mosheh el-YHVH al-devar hatzfarde'im asher-sam leFar'oh.
verses.study_note: Moses 'cried' (vayyitz'ak) unto the LORD — the same intense word used for Israel's cry under oppression (Exod 2:23). Moses does not wave the rod to end the plague; he prays. This reveals the essential truth: the rod is a symbol, but prayer is the real instrument of power. Moses intercedes even for his oppressor — a foreshadowing of Christ's intercession and of the command to pray for enemies (Matt 5:44).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and the frogs died out of the houses, out of the villages, and out of the fields.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה וַיָּמֻתוּ הַצְפַרְדְּעִים מִן־הַבָּתִּים מִן־הַחֲצֵרֹת וּמִן־הַשָּׂדֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as YHVH kidvar Mosheh vayyamutu hatzfarde'im min-habbattim min-hachatzarot umin-hassadot.
verses.study_note: God acts 'according to the word of Moses' (kidvar Mosheh) — precisely as promised, at the appointed time. The frogs die rather than simply returning to the water, which ensures visible evidence of the miracle. The death of the frogs in houses, villages, and fields creates the massive cleanup described in verse 14. God honors His servant's word, establishing Moses' prophetic authority.

verses.kjv_text: And they gathered them together upon heaps: and the land stank.
verses.original_text: וַיִּצְבְּרוּ אֹתָם חֳמָרִם חֳמָרִם וַתִּבְאַשׁ הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitzberu otam chamarim chamarim vattiv'ash ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The aftermath is vivid and nauseating: the dead frogs are gathered into 'heaps upon heaps' (chamarim chamarim — the doubling intensifies the image). 'The land stank' (vattiv'ash) — the stench of rotting frogs fills Egypt. This lingering corruption becomes a physical memorial of the plague even after its conclusion. The sacred animal of Heqet lies rotting in piles — a devastating commentary on the impotence of Egypt's gods. The word 'tiv'ash' (stank) later describes Israel's reputation under Pharaoh's increased oppression (Exod 5:21).

verses.kjv_text: But when Pharaoh saw that there was respite, he hardened his heart, and hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא פַּרְעֹה כִּי הָיְתָה הָרְוָחָה וְהַכְבֵּד אֶת־לִבּוֹ וְלֹא שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Par'oh ki hayetah harvachah vehakhbed et-libbo velo shama alehem ka'asher dibber YHVH.
verses.study_note: The chapter's most critical verse on Pharaoh's spiritual condition. 'When Pharaoh saw that there was respite' (harvachah — relief, breathing room), 'he hardened his heart' (vehakhbed et-libbo). The word 'hakhbed' is the Hiphil of 'kaved' (heavy/glory) — he made his heart heavy, insensible. The pattern is devastating: pressure produces promises; relief produces hardening. Pharaoh's repentance lasted exactly as long as his discomfort. The narrator adds: 'as the LORD had said' — this was predicted, not accidental.
verses.doctrinal_note: False repentance is one of Scripture's most sobering themes. Pharaoh's pattern — repenting under pressure, relapsing in relief — illustrates what Paul calls 'worldly sorrow' that 'works death' versus 'godly sorrow' that 'works repentance' (2 Cor 7:10). True repentance is not produced by the consequences of sin but by a genuine encounter with God's holiness. The hardening of the heart is progressive: what begins as self-hardening (Pharaoh's own act) will eventually become divine hardening (God confirming the choice).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Say unto Aaron, Stretch out thy rod, and smite the dust of the land, that it may become lice throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֱמֹר אֶל־אַהֲרֹן נְטֵה אֶת־מַטְּךָ וְהַךְ אֶת־עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ וְהָיָה לְכִנִּם בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh emor el-Aharon neteh et-mattekha vehakh et-afar ha'aretz vehayah lekhinnim bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The third plague comes without warning — a significant shift. Plagues 1-2 included warnings; plague 3 arrives unannounced. This establishes a pattern: in each cycle of three plagues, the first two are warned and the third comes suddenly (plagues 3, 6, 9 all arrive without warning). Aaron strikes the 'dust of the ground' (afar ha'aretz) — the very soil of Egypt. Geb, the earth god, cannot protect his own domain. 'Kinnim' is variously translated as lice, gnats, or mosquitoes — small biting insects arising from the dust itself. The dust that God used to create man (Gen 2:7) now produces a plague.
verses.doctrinal_note: The plague of lice arises from the dust of the earth — the very element from which God formed humanity (Gen 2:7). This connection is theologically significant: God demonstrates sovereignty over the basic elements of creation. What He used for life, He can use for judgment. The Egyptian priests were obsessively concerned with ritual cleanliness, shaving their bodies to prevent vermin — this plague strikes at the heart of their ability to worship their own gods.

verses.kjv_text: And they did so; for Aaron stretched out his hand with his rod, and smote the dust of the earth, and it became lice in man, and in beast; all the dust of the land became lice throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן וַיֵּט אַהֲרֹן אֶת־יָדוֹ בְמַטֵּהוּ וַיַּךְ אֶת־עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ וַתְּהִי הַכִּנָּם בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה כָּל־עֲפַר הָאָרֶץ הָיָה כִנִּם בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen vayyet Aharon et-yado vemattehu vayyakh et-afar ha'aretz vattehi hakkinnam ba'adam uvabbehemah kol-afar ha'aretz hayah khinnim bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The scope is staggering: 'all the dust of the land became lice.' The plague affects both man (adam) and beast (behemah) — no living creature is exempt. Egypt's carefully maintained temple hygiene is shattered. The priests who shaved their entire bodies to be ritually clean are now infested. Temple sacrifices and priestly functions would be disrupted, as no priest could approach the gods in an impure state. This plague strikes at the functioning of Egypt's entire religious system.

verses.kjv_text: And the magicians did so with their enchantments to bring forth lice, but they could not: so there were lice upon man, and upon beast.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן הַחַרְטֻמִּים בְּלָטֵיהֶם לְהוֹצִיא אֶת־הַכִּנִּים וְלֹא יָכֹלוּ וַתְּהִי הַכִּנָּם בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen hachartummim belatehem lehotzi et-hakkinnim velo yakholu vattehi hakkinnam ba'adam uvabbehemah.
verses.study_note: The critical turning point in the contest between YHVH and Egypt's powers: 'the magicians did so... but they could not' (velo yakholu). After matching the blood and the frogs, their power reaches its limit at the dust of the earth. They cannot create life, even insect life, from inanimate matter. The exposure of their limitation is devastating — their occult arts (belatehem) are shown to be finite imitations, not genuine creative power. The magicians themselves are afflicted (the lice are upon them too), rendering them ritually unclean.

verses.kjv_text: Then the magicians said unto Pharaoh, This is the finger of God: and Pharaoh’s heart was hardened, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had said.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ הַחַרְטֻמִּם אֶל־פַּרְעֹה אֶצְבַּע אֱלֹהִים הִוא וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב־פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא־שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru hachartummim el-Par'oh etzba Elohim hi vayyechezak lev-Par'oh velo-shama alehem ka'asher dibber YHVH.
verses.study_note: One of the most significant confessions in Exodus. Egypt's own occult practitioners declare: 'This is the finger of God' (etzba Elohim hi). 'Etzba' (finger) denotes the smallest expression of divine power — if this is merely God's finger, what would His hand or arm accomplish? Jesus later references this phrase when casting out demons: 'If I with the finger of God cast out devils' (Luke 11:20). The magicians recognize the divine source, yet Pharaoh's heart 'was hardened' (vayyechezak — from 'chazak,' to be strong/hard). Notably, this is the first use of 'chazak' for Pharaoh's hardening — a different word from 'kaved' in v.15, suggesting an intensification.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'finger of God' confession establishes that even the enemies of God are compelled to testify to His power. The same phrase appears in three critical contexts: (1) here, where Egypt's magicians confess, (2) the tablets of the Law 'written with the finger of God' (Exod 31:18, Deut 9:10), and (3) Jesus casting out demons by 'the finger of God' (Luke 11:20). From judgment to law to deliverance — the finger of God touches every sphere. If the lice plague is merely God's 'finger,' the full demonstration of His power is immeasurably greater.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh; lo, he cometh forth to the water; and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הַשְׁכֵּם בַּבֹּקֶר וְהִתְיַצֵּב לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה הִנֵּה יוֹצֵא הַמָּיְמָה וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה שַׁלַּח עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh hashkem babboker vehityatzev lifnei Far'oh hinneh yotze hammaymah ve'amarta elav koh amar YHVH shallach ammi veya'avduni.
verses.study_note: A new plague cycle begins with the fourth plague. Moses is to 'rise up early' (hashkem) and confront Pharaoh as he goes to the water (hammaymah) — likely for a morning ritual at the Nile. God knows Pharaoh's schedule and orchestrates the encounter. 'Stand before' (hityatzev) carries military connotations — to take one's position, to present oneself for battle. Moses stands as God's ambassador, confronting Egypt's most powerful figure at his place of religious devotion.
verses.doctrinal_note: God meets people where they are, even in their idolatry. Pharaoh goes to the Nile for worship; God sends Moses to the Nile to confront him. The New Testament parallel is Christ going to the places of sinners — tax collectors' tables, wells, fishing boats — to bring the word of God where people actually live.

verses.kjv_text: Else, if thou wilt not let my people go, behold, I will send swarms of flies upon thee, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people, and into thy houses: and the houses of the Egyptians shall be full of swarms of flies, and also the ground whereon they are.
verses.original_text: כִּי אִם־אֵינְךָ מְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת־עַמִּי הִנְנִי מַשְׁלִיחַ בְּךָ וּבַעֲבָדֶיךָ וּבְעַמְּךָ וּבְבָתֶּיךָ אֶת־הֶעָרֹב וּמָלְאוּ בָּתֵּי מִצְרַיִם אֶת־הֶעָרֹב וְגַם הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר־הֵם עָלֶיהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki im-einekha meshalle'ach et-ammi hineni mashli'ach bekha uva'avadekha uve'ammekha uvevattekha et-he'arov umale'u battei Mitzrayim et-he'arov vegam ha'adamah asher-hem aleha.
verses.study_note: The fourth plague introduces 'he'arov' — traditionally translated 'swarms of flies,' though the Hebrew literally means 'the swarm' or 'the mixture' (from 'arav,' to mix). Some rabbinical traditions interpret this as a mixed swarm of various dangerous creatures. The wordplay is powerful: God 'sends' (mashli'ach) what Pharaoh refuses to 'send' (meshalle'ach — the same root). The infestation affects persons, houses, and the ground itself. The god Khepri (the scarab beetle deity) who represented the morning sun is powerless before this invasion of insects.
verses.doctrinal_note: There is a divine wordplay in verse 21 that reveals the lex talionis (law of retribution) embedded in the plagues. The Hebrew root 'shalach' (to send) appears in both God's demand and His judgment: 'If you refuse to send (meshalle'ach) my people, I will send (mashli'ach) upon you.' What Pharaoh refuses to release, God sends against him. The measure-for-measure principle of divine justice — what you withhold, God takes; what you hoard, God consumes.

verses.kjv_text: And I will sever in that day the land of Goshen, in which my people dwell, that no swarms of flies shall be there; to the end thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth.
verses.original_text: וְהִפְלֵיתִי בַיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת־אֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן אֲשֶׁר עַמִּי עֹמֵד עָלֶיהָ לְבִלְתִּי הֱיוֹת־שָׁם עָרֹב לְמַעַן תֵּדַע כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehifleiti bayyom hahu et-eretz Goshen asher ammi omed aleha levilti heyot-sham arov lema'an teda ki ani YHVH bekerev ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: This is the first explicit mention of divine 'distinction' or 'separation' (vehifleiti — from 'palah,' to make wonderful/distinct) between Israel and Egypt. The land of Goshen, where Israel dwells, will be supernaturally exempt from the plague. The theological purpose is stated: 'that thou mayest know that I am the LORD in the midst of the earth' (bekerev ha'aretz — literally 'in the middle of the land'). YHVH is not a distant, remote deity — He is actively present, governing the boundaries of judgment with surgical precision. This distinction becomes a central theme of the Exodus: culminating in the Passover, where death enters every Egyptian home but passes over every Israelite door.
verses.doctrinal_note: The principle of divine distinction (separation) is foundational to biblical theology. God separated light from darkness at creation (Gen 1:4), Israel from Egypt at the Exodus, and believers from the world in Christ (2 Cor 6:17). The Hebrew word 'hivdil' (to separate/distinguish) and its synonym 'palah' (to set apart/make distinct) express God's redemptive activity: He always makes a difference between His own and the world. This is not favoritism but covenant faithfulness — God protects those who are His.

verses.kjv_text: And I will put a division between my people and thy people: to morrow shall this sign be.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתִּי פְדֻת בֵּין עַמִּי וּבֵין עַמֶּךָ לְמָחָר יִהְיֶה הָאֹת הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamti fedut bein ammi uvein ammekha lemachar yihyeh ha'ot hazzeh.
verses.study_note: God declares 'a division' (fedut) between 'my people' and 'thy people.' The word 'fedut' is remarkable — from 'padah,' to ransom or redeem. The 'division' is literally a 'redemption' — God's separation is itself an act of ransom. This is not merely geographic but covenantal: Israel belongs to YHVH, and their exemption from judgment is a function of their relationship to Him. 'My people' versus 'thy people' — the possessive language underscores the contest: whose people are they? The sign (ot) will occur 'tomorrow' (lemachar), giving Pharaoh time to repent.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD did so; and there came a grievous swarm of flies into the house of Pharaoh, and into his servants’ houses, and into all the land of Egypt: the land was corrupted by reason of the swarm of flies.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה כֵּן וַיָּבֹא עָרֹב כָּבֵד בֵּיתָה פַרְעֹה וּבֵית עֲבָדָיו וּבְכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם תִּשָּׁחֵת הָאָרֶץ מִפְּנֵי הֶעָרֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as YHVH ken vayyavo arov kaved beitah Far'oh uveit avadav uvekhol-eretz Mitzrayim tishachet ha'aretz mippenei he'arov.
verses.study_note: God executes precisely as promised. The swarm is 'grievous' (kaved — heavy, the same root word used for Pharaoh's hardened heart). 'The land was corrupted' (tishachet — from 'shachat,' to corrupt/destroy) — the same word used for the corruption before the Flood (Gen 6:11-12). Egypt is being un-created, its order dissolving into chaos. The flies enter Pharaoh's house — no palace walls can keep out God's judgment. The word 'kaved' (heavy/grievous) ironically echoes the hardening of Pharaoh's heart — his heavy heart produces heavy plagues.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh called for Moses and for Aaron, and said, Go ye, sacrifice to your God in the land.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר לְכוּ זִבְחוּ לֵאלֹהֵיכֶם בָּאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Far'oh el-Mosheh ule'Aharon vayomer lekhu zivchu le'Eloheikhem ba'aretz.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's first compromise: 'sacrifice... in the land' (ba'aretz). He concedes that Israel may worship YHVH but insists they remain in Egypt. This is a calculated concession — allow the religious practice but maintain political control. It is the strategy of every tyrant: permit the form of religion while controlling its substance. 'Your God' (Eloheikhem) — Pharaoh still refuses to acknowledge YHVH as the universal God; He is merely Israel's tribal deity in Pharaoh's mind.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, It is not meet so to do; for we shall sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians to the LORD our God: lo, shall we sacrifice the abomination of the Egyptians before their eyes, and will they not stone us?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה לֹא נָכוֹן לַעֲשׂוֹת כֵּן כִּי תּוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם נִזְבַּח לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ הֵן נִזְבַּח אֶת־תּוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם לְעֵינֵיהֶם וְלֹא יִסְקְלֻנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh lo nakhon la'asot ken ki to'avat Mitzrayim nizbach laYHVH Eloheinu hen nizbach et-to'avat Mitzrayim le'einehem velo yiskelunu.
verses.study_note: Moses gives a brilliant theological and practical refusal. Israelite sacrifices include animals sacred to Egypt — cattle (Hathor/Apis), sheep, and goats. Slaughtering these 'before their eyes' (le'einehem) would provoke a violent response. The word 'abomination' (to'avat) is loaded: what Egypt considers sacred, Israel sacrifices to YHVH. The religious systems are fundamentally incompatible. Moses understands that genuine worship requires separation from the religious sensitivities of idolaters — compromise is structurally impossible.
verses.doctrinal_note: True worship of the true God cannot be accommodated within the framework of false religion. Moses' refusal to sacrifice 'in the land' anticipates the New Testament principle of spiritual separation (2 Cor 6:14-17). The incompatibility is not merely practical but theological: the very animals Egypt worships, Israel offers to YHVH. There can be no syncretism between the worship of the living God and the worship of idols. What the world considers sacred, God may require as an offering.

verses.kjv_text: We will go three days’ journey into the wilderness, and sacrifice to the LORD our God, as he shall command us.
verses.original_text: דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים נֵלֵךְ בַּמִּדְבָּר וְזָבַחְנוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ כַּאֲשֶׁר יֹאמַר אֵלֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Derekh sheloshet yamim nelekh bammidbar vezavachnu laYHVH Eloheinu ka'asher yomar eleinu.
verses.study_note: Moses restates the original demand: a three-day journey into the wilderness. 'As he shall command us' (ka'asher yomar eleinu) — this phrase is essential. Worship is not performed according to human preference but according to divine instruction. God determines the form, place, and manner of acceptable worship. Moses refuses to negotiate on the terms God has established. The 'three days' journey has often been seen typologically as pointing to resurrection — the third-day pattern that culminates in Christ's rising.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said, I will let you go, that ye may sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness; only ye shall not go very far away: intreat for me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אָנֹכִי אֲשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם וּזְבַחְתֶּם לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם בַּמִּדְבָּר רַק הַרְחֵק לֹא־תַרְחִיקוּ לָלֶכֶת הַעְתִּירוּ בַּעֲדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Par'oh anokhi ashalach etkhem uzevachtem laYHVH Eloheikhem bammidbar rak harchek lo-tarchiqu lalekhet ha'tiru ba'adi.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's second compromise: 'you may go... only ye shall not go very far away' (rak harchek lo-tarchiqu). Each concession comes with a controlling condition. Pharaoh yields ground grudgingly, always retaining a measure of control. 'Not very far away' keeps Israel within reach for recall. 'Intreat for me' (ha'tiru ba'adi) — again Pharaoh asks Moses to pray, revealing his implicit acknowledgment of YHVH's power while refusing genuine submission. The pattern of compromise mirrors Satan's strategy: concede the form of religion while emptying it of substance.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, Behold, I go out from thee, and I will intreat the LORD that the swarms of flies may depart from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people, to morrow: but let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more in not letting the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי יוֹצֵא מֵעִמָּךְ וְהַעְתַּרְתִּי אֶל־יְהוָה וְסָר הֶעָרֹב מִפַּרְעֹה מֵעֲבָדָיו וּמֵעַמּוֹ מָחָר רַק אַל־יֹסֵף פַּרְעֹה הָתֵל לְבִלְתִּי שַׁלַּח אֶת־הָעָם לִזְבֹּחַ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh hinneh anokhi yotze me'immakh veha'tarti el-YHVH vesar he'arov miPar'oh me'avadav ume'ammo machar rak al-yosef Par'oh hatel levilti shallach et-ha'am lizboch laYHVH.
verses.study_note: Moses agrees to intercede but delivers a sharp warning: 'let not Pharaoh deal deceitfully any more' (al-yosef hatel). The word 'hatel' (to deceive, to mock) exposes Pharaoh's pattern — his promises of release are lies. Moses names the sin directly: not merely stubbornness but active deception. This prophetic confrontation of royal dishonesty recalls Nathan's boldness before David (2 Sam 12) and anticipates the prophetic tradition of speaking truth to power.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא מֹשֶׁה מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה וַיֶּעְתַּר אֶל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Mosheh me'im Par'oh vayye'tar el-YHVH.
verses.study_note: Moses intercedes faithfully despite knowing Pharaoh's history of deception. The brevity of this verse belies its significance: Moses does what he promised. His integrity stands in stark contrast to Pharaoh's deceit. 'Vayye'tar' (intreated) carries the sense of abundant, overflowing prayer — pouring out supplication. Moses is the faithful intercessor, foreshadowing Christ who intercedes even for those who reject Him.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD did according to the word of Moses; and he removed the swarms of flies from Pharaoh, from his servants, and from his people; there remained not one.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה וַיָּסַר הֶעָרֹב מִפַּרְעֹה מֵעֲבָדָיו וּמֵעַמּוֹ לֹא נִשְׁאַר אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as YHVH kidvar Mosheh vayyasar he'arov miPar'oh me'avadav ume'ammo lo nish'ar echad.
verses.study_note: Complete, total removal: 'there remained not one' (lo nish'ar echad). God's deliverance is as thorough as His judgment. Unlike the frogs that died in heaps and stank (v.14), the flies are simply removed — no residue, no lingering evidence. This demonstrates God's absolute control: He can bring plague and remove it with equal precision. 'According to the word of Moses' — again, God honors His servant's intercession.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh hardened his heart at this time also, neither would he let the people go.
verses.original_text: וַיַּכְבֵּד פַּרְעֹה אֶת־לִבּוֹ גַּם בַּפַּעַם הַזֹּאת וְלֹא שִׁלַּח אֶת־הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakhbed Par'oh et-libbo gam bappa'am hazzo't velo shillach et-ha'am.
verses.study_note: The chapter ends as it began — with Pharaoh's refusal. 'Hardened his heart' (vayyakhbed) — the Hiphil of 'kaved' (heavy) returns from verse 15. The pattern is now established: plague, plea, promise, relief, hardening. 'Also this time' (gam bappa'am hazzo't) — the narrator notes the repetition, building cumulative evidence of willful, deliberate rebellion. Pharaoh has now broken his promise twice in one chapter (vv.8,28). Each cycle deepens the hardening. The human heart's capacity for self-deception is nearly limitless when it prefers comfort to obedience.
verses.doctrinal_note: Pharaoh's repeated hardening illustrates the theology of progressive spiritual blindness. Each act of rebellion makes the next easier. What begins as resistance ('I will not') becomes inability ('I cannot'). Paul describes this trajectory in Romans 1:21-28: God eventually 'gives them over' to the consequences of their own choices. Pharaoh's story is a warning to every generation: there is no guarantee that the opportunity to repent will remain open indefinitely. 'Today, if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts' (Heb 3:15).

meta.overview: Exodus 8 continues the plague narrative with three devastating judgments against Egypt: frogs, lice (gnats), and flies. Each plague escalates in severity and theological significance. The frog plague directly assaults Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of fertility and childbirth. Pharaoh begs Moses to pray for relief and makes his first false promise of release — establishing a pattern of hardened deceit that intensifies throughout the plague cycle. The lice plague introduces a critical turning point: Egypt's magicians fail to replicate it and confess 'This is the finger of God' (v.19), yet Pharaoh's heart remains hardened. The fly plague introduces the principle of divine distinction — God separates Goshen from the rest of Egypt, protecting His people while judging their oppressors. Pharaoh offers a series of compromises, each designed to maintain partial control, but Moses insists on complete obedience. The chapter reveals the systematic dismantling of Egypt's religious worldview and the progressive revelation of YHVH's absolute sovereignty over all creation.
meta.theme: The Finger of God, False Repentance & Divine Distinction
meta.keyWordMeaning: To separate, distinguish, set apart — God's act of making a division between His people and Egypt. The same root used in creation (Gen 1:4) now applied to redemption.
outline: The Plague of Frogs (vv.1-7): Frogs cover the land — assault on Heqet
Pharaoh's First False Repentance (vv.8-15): Relief brings hardening, not gratitude
The Plague of Lice/Gnats (vv.16-19): The magicians fail — 'This is the finger of God'
The Plague of Flies (vv.20-24): Divine distinction — Goshen is spared
Pharaoh's Attempted Compromises (vv.25-32): Negotiation, deception, and hardened refusal

verses.kjv_text: Then the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה בֹּא אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וְדִבַּרְתָּ אֵלָיו כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים שַׁלַּח אֶת־עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh bo el-Par'oh vedibarta elav koh amar YHVH Elohei ha'Ivrim shallach et-ammi veya'avduni.
verses.study_note: The fifth plague opens with the now-familiar prophetic formula: 'Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews' (YHVH Elohei ha'Ivrim). The title 'God of the Hebrews' is a deliberate confrontation — YHVH identifies Himself not as a local deity but as the covenant God of a specific people whom Pharaoh holds captive. 'Let my people go that they may serve me' (shallach... veya'avduni) — the verb 'avad' (to serve/worship) is the same word used for Israel's slavery; true freedom is not autonomy but service to the true God.

verses.kjv_text: For if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still,
verses.original_text: כִּי אִם־מָאֵן אַתָּה לְשַׁלֵּחַ וְעוֹדְךָ מַחֲזִיק בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki im-ma'en attah leshalle'ach ve'odekha machazik bam.
verses.study_note: 'If thou refuse' (ma'en) — the refusal is now willful and deliberate. 'Hold them still' (machazik bam) — from 'chazak' (to strengthen, seize), the very word used for Pharaoh's hardened heart. He 'holds firmly' to Israel as he 'holds firmly' to his defiance. The word links Pharaoh's political oppression to his spiritual condition — his grip on God's people mirrors his grip against God's word.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field, upon the horses, upon the asses, upon the camels, upon the oxen, and upon the sheep: there shall be a very grievous murrain.
verses.original_text: הִנֵּה יַד־יְהוָה הוֹיָה בְּמִקְנְךָ אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה בַּסּוּסִים בַּחֲמֹרִים בַּגְּמַלִּים בַּבָּקָר וּבַצֹּאן דֶּבֶר כָּבֵד מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Hinneh yad-YHVH hoyah bemikhnekha asher basadeh bassusim bachamorim baggemallim babbaka uvattzon dever kaved me'od.
verses.study_note: The 'hand of the LORD' (yad-YHVH) — anthropomorphic language for divine power in direct action. The plague targets livestock specifically: horses, donkeys, camels, cattle, and sheep — the complete economic infrastructure of Egypt. 'Dever' (pestilence/murrain) is a 'very grievous' (kaved me'od) epidemic. The word 'kaved' (heavy/grievous) is from the same root as Pharaoh's 'hardened' (hikbid) heart — the heaviness of judgment mirrors the heaviness of his stubbornness.
verses.doctrinal_note: The fifth plague strikes Egypt's agricultural economy and religious system simultaneously. The bull-god Apis, the cow-goddess Hathor, and the ram-god Khnum were among Egypt's most venerated deities. YHVH demonstrates that He has power over the very creatures Egypt worships. The hand that Egypt trusted (its livestock wealth) is the hand God strikes.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt: and there shall nothing die of all that is the children’s of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְהִפְלָה יְהוָה בֵּין מִקְנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל וּבֵין מִקְנֵה מִצְרָיִם וְלֹא יָמוּת מִכָּל־לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל דָּבָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiflah YHVH bein mikneh Yisra'el uvein mikneh Mitzrayim velo yamut mikkol-livnei Yisra'el davar.
verses.study_note: God 'shall sever' (hiflah — to make a distinction, to set apart) between Israel and Egypt. This is the second explicit mention of divine distinction (after 8:22). The word 'hiflah' derives from 'pala' — to be wonderful, extraordinary. God's separating work is itself a wonder. 'Nothing shall die' (lo yamut... davar) — not a single animal belonging to Israel will perish. The precision of divine judgment — devastating to Egypt yet surgically avoiding Israel — testifies to God's sovereign control.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine distinction: God separates His covenant people from the world under judgment. This principle runs through the entire Bible — from Goshen to the Passover blood, from the Ark to the sealing of the 144,000 (Rev 7). The plague falls on all of Egypt but not one Israelite animal dies. This foreshadows the ultimate distinction: those covered by the blood of the Lamb are untouched by judgment (1 Thess 5:9).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD appointed a set time, saying, To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֶׂם יְהוָה מוֹעֵד לֵאמֹר מָחָר יַעֲשֶׂה יְהוָה הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה בָּאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasem YHVH mo'ed lemor machar ya'aseh YHVH haddavar hazzeh ba'aretz.
verses.study_note: God sets an 'appointed time' (mo'ed) — the same word later used for Israel's sacred festivals and the Tabernacle of Meeting (ohel mo'ed). Even judgment operates on God's timetable, not random chance. 'Tomorrow' (machar) gives Pharaoh one final night to repent — grace precedes judgment. The word 'davar' (thing/matter) echoes 'dever' (pestilence) — God's word and God's plague are intertwined.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died: but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה מִמָּחֳרָת וַיָּמָת כֹּל מִקְנֵה מִצְרָיִם וּמִמִּקְנֵה בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא־מֵת אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'as YHVH et-haddavar hazzeh mimmocharot vayyamot kol mikneh Mitzrayim umimmikneh venei-Yisra'el lo-met echad.
verses.study_note: Exact fulfillment: 'all the cattle of Egypt died' yet 'not one' of Israel's perished. The Hebrew emphasizes the totality — 'kol' (all) on Egypt's side, 'echad' (one) on Israel's. 'All' versus 'not one' — this is the language of absolute divine precision. The word 'met' (died) versus 'lo-met' (did not die) creates a stark binary that illustrates the chasm between judgment and protection.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וְהִנֵּה לֹא־מֵת מִמִּקְנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל עַד־אֶחָד וַיִּכְבַּד לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שִׁלַּח אֶת־הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Par'oh vehineh lo-met mimmikneh Yisra'el ad-echad vayyikhbad lev Par'oh velo shillach et-ha'am.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh investigates — he 'sent' (vayyishlach) messengers to verify. The report confirms: 'not so much as one' (ad-echad) of Israel's animals died. The evidence is incontrovertible. Yet 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened' (vayyikhbad lev Par'oh). Here the verb is 'kabad' (became heavy) in the Qal — Pharaoh's own heart grows heavier. He sees the evidence, verifies the miracle, and still refuses. This is the deepest tragedy of hardening: even proof of God's power and mercy produces only deeper resistance.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hardening of heart can be self-inflicted even in the face of overwhelming evidence. Pharaoh saw the distinction, verified it personally, and still refused. This pattern — evidence met with resistance — recurs throughout Scripture. Jesus warns that those who refuse to hear Moses and the Prophets 'will not be persuaded, though one rose from the dead' (Luke 16:31). Knowledge without submission produces deeper hardening, not conversion.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses and unto Aaron, Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן קְחוּ לָכֶם מְלֹא חָפְנֵיכֶם פִּיחַ כִּבְשָׁן וּזְרָקוֹ מֹשֶׁה הַשָּׁמַיְמָה לְעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon kechu lakhem melo chofneikhem pi'ach kivshan uzerako Mosheh hashamaimah le'einei Par'oh.
verses.study_note: The sixth plague begins with no warning — a shift in the pattern. God commands Moses and Aaron to take 'ashes of the furnace' (pi'ach kivshan). The word 'kivshan' (furnace/kiln) is laden with meaning: (1) It recalls the brick kilns where Israel labored as slaves — the instrument of oppression becomes the instrument of judgment. (2) It connects to Genesis 19:28 where Sodom's smoke rose 'like the smoke of a furnace.' (3) It foreshadows Deuteronomy 4:20 where Egypt itself is called 'the iron furnace.' Moses sprinkles it 'toward heaven' (hashamaimah) — a priestly gesture of consecration turned to judgment, performed 'in the sight of Pharaoh' (le'einei Par'oh) as a public sign.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sixth plague — boils — has no warning, no negotiation, no opportunity for Pharaoh to relent beforehand. This marks an escalation: God's patience, while vast, is not infinite. The furnace ash is a powerful symbol: Egypt's own instruments of oppression (the kilns where Israel made bricks) become the source of Egypt's suffering. God turns the tools of injustice against the oppressor — a principle of divine justice that echoes throughout Scripture (Psalm 7:15-16).

verses.kjv_text: And it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast, throughout all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה לְאָבָק עַל כָּל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְהָיָה עַל־הָאָדָם וְעַל־הַבְּהֵמָה לִשְׁחִין פֹּרֵחַ אֲבַעְבֻּעֹת בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah le'avak al kol-eretz Mitzrayim vehayah al-ha'adam ve'al-habbehemah lishchin pore'ach ava'bu'ot bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The furnace soot becomes fine dust (avak) spreading over all Egypt, producing 'boils breaking forth with blains' (shchin pore'ach ava'bu'ot). 'Shchin' (boil) is a painful, inflamed skin eruption — the same word appears in the diseases threatened for covenant-breaking (Deut 28:27,35) and in Job's affliction (Job 2:7). This plague strikes both 'man and beast' (ha'adam ve'al-habbehemah) — the first plague to directly afflict human bodies. The escalation from property (livestock) to persons signals that judgment is drawing closer to Pharaoh himself.

verses.kjv_text: And they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת־פִּיחַ הַכִּבְשָׁן וַיַּעַמְדוּ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וַיִּזְרֹק אֹתוֹ מֹשֶׁה הַשָּׁמָיְמָה וַיְהִי שְׁחִין אֲבַעְבֻּעֹת פֹּרֵחַ בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikchu et-pi'ach hakkivshan vayya'amdu lifnei Par'oh vayyizrok oto Mosheh hashamaimah vayehi shchin ava'bu'ot pore'ach ba'adam uvabbehemah.
verses.study_note: Moses performs the act publicly before Pharaoh — a prophetic sign-act. The Hebrew word order emphasizes the visual drama: they 'stood before Pharaoh' (vayya'amdu lifnei Par'oh), then Moses 'sprinkled it heavenward' (vayyizrok... hashamaimah). The soot rises toward the sky — toward the realm of the gods — and returns as plague upon the people. It is as if Moses offers the ashes of Israel's slavery to heaven, and heaven responds with retribution upon the oppressor.

verses.kjv_text: And the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־יָכְלוּ הַחַרְטֻמִּים לַעֲמֹד לִפְנֵי מֹשֶׁה מִפְּנֵי הַשְּׁחִין כִּי־הָיָה הַשְּׁחִין בַּחַרְטֻמִּים וּבְכָל־מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-yakhelu hachartummim la'amod lifnei Mosheh mippenei hashchin ki-hayah hashchin bachartummim uvkhol-Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: A devastating detail: the magicians 'could not stand before Moses' (lo-yakhelu... la'amod lifnei Mosheh). This is their final appearance in the plague narrative. They who could replicate some early plagues (7:22, 8:7), who admitted 'this is the finger of God' (8:19), are now themselves stricken and incapacitated. The phrase 'could not stand' (lo-yakhelu la'amod) is a total defeat — they are physically and spiritually prostrate before the servant of YHVH.
verses.doctrinal_note: The defeat of the magicians represents the complete collapse of Egyptian religious authority. Pharaoh's spiritual advisors — the men who sustained his resistance by offering counterfeits — are now broken by the very God they opposed. This is the theological climax of the magicians' narrative arc: from imitation (7:22), to acknowledgment (8:19), to incapacitation (9:11). All false spiritual authority ultimately falls before the true God. Paul references this arc in 2 Timothy 3:8-9: 'Jannes and Jambres withstood Moses' but 'their folly shall be manifest.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had spoken unto Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaychazek YHVH et-lev Par'oh velo shama alehem ka'asher dibber YHVH el-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: For the first time in the actual plague narrative, God is the subject of the hardening: 'the LORD hardened' (vaychazek YHVH) — Piel form, causative. In plagues 1-5, the text says Pharaoh's heart 'was hardened' or 'Pharaoh hardened his heart.' Now, after Pharaoh has repeatedly hardened himself, God confirms that trajectory. This fulfills Exodus 4:21 and 7:3. The phrase 'as the LORD had spoken' (ka'asher dibber YHVH) emphasizes that even this hardening operates within God's declared purpose.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is one of Scripture's deepest theological realities. In the early plagues, Pharaoh hardens his own heart (8:15, 8:32). After persistent self-hardening, God confirms and intensifies that state. This is not arbitrary but judicial — God gives Pharaoh over to the direction he has chosen. Paul explicates this principle in Romans 1:24,26,28: 'God gave them over.' God does not harden a willing heart; He confirms an unwilling one. This serves God's larger purpose: the display of His power and the proclamation of His name (v.16).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh, and say unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הַשְׁכֵּם בַּבֹּקֶר וְהִתְיַצֵּב לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו כֹּה־אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים שַׁלַּח אֶת־עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh hashkem babboker vehityatzev lifnei Par'oh ve'amarta elav koh-amar YHVH Elohei ha'Ivrim shallach et-ammi veya'avduni.
verses.study_note: The seventh plague begins with the prophetic commission repeated for the third time in this chapter. 'Rise up early' (hashkem) — the same urgency Abraham showed (Gen 19:27, 22:3). 'Stand before Pharaoh' (vehityatzev) — the Hithpael form suggests presenting oneself with authority, taking a firm stand. Moses, who once trembled before Pharaoh, now confronts him with prophetic boldness. The same demand — 'Let my people go that they may serve me' — rings out, unchanged and non-negotiable.

verses.kjv_text: For I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart, and upon thy servants, and upon thy people; that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth.
verses.original_text: כִּי בַּפַּעַם הַזֹּאת אֲנִי שֹׁלֵחַ אֶת־כָּל־מַגֵּפֹתַי אֶל־לִבְּךָ וּבַעֲבָדֶיךָ וּבְעַמֶּךָ בַּעֲבוּר תֵּדַע כִּי אֵין כָּמֹנִי בְּכָל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki bappa'am hazzo't ani shole'ach et-kol-maggerfotai el-libbkha uva'avadekha uve'ammekha ba'avur teda ki ein kamoni bekhol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: God declares His purpose with unprecedented directness. 'All my plagues upon thine heart' (kol-maggerfotai el-libbkha) — the plagues target Pharaoh's heart, not merely his land. Physical disasters serve a spiritual purpose: 'that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth' (teda ki ein kamoni). This is the great monotheistic declaration embedded in the plague narrative. The plagues are not arbitrary destruction — they are revelation. Each plague dismantles a piece of Egyptian theology until only YHVH remains standing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The purpose of divine judgment is revelation, not mere destruction. God sends plagues so that Pharaoh will 'know' (teda) — the same epistemological language used throughout the plague narrative (7:5, 7:17, 8:10, 8:22). The incomparability formula — 'there is none like me' (ein kamoni) — becomes a central confession of Israel's faith (Deut 33:26; Psalm 86:8; Isaiah 46:9). Judgment serves worship: through seeing God's power, all creation is to acknowledge His uniqueness.

verses.kjv_text: For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth.
verses.original_text: כִּי עַתָּה שָׁלַחְתִּי אֶת־יָדִי וָאַךְ אוֹתְךָ וְאֶת־עַמְּךָ בַּדָּבֶר וַתִּכָּחֵד מִן־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki attah shalachti et-yadi va'akh otkha ve'et-ammekha baddaver vatikkached min-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: God reveals what He could have done: 'I will stretch out my hand' and 'smite thee... with pestilence' and 'thou shalt be cut off' (vatikkached — destroyed, exterminated). The verb 'kachad' (Niphal: to be annihilated) indicates total destruction. God could have ended Egypt entirely with the pestilence — He chose restraint. This sets up the crucial theological explanation in the next verse.

verses.kjv_text: And in very deed for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth.
verses.original_text: וְאוּלָם בַּעֲבוּר זֹאת הֶעֱמַדְתִּיךָ בַּעֲבוּר הַרְאֹתְךָ אֶת־כֹּחִי וּלְמַעַן סַפֵּר שְׁמִי בְּכָל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ulam ba'avur zo't he'emadtikha ba'avur har'otekha et-kochi ulema'an sapper shemi bekhol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically significant verses in the entire Old Testament. 'I have raised thee up' (he'emadtikha — literally 'I have caused you to stand/remain') — God has sustained Pharaoh through the plagues, not because Pharaoh deserves mercy, but for two purposes: (1) 'to show in thee my power' (har'otekha et-kochi) and (2) 'that my name may be declared throughout all the earth' (sapper shemi bekhol-ha'aretz). Pharaoh's very existence serves God's revelatory purpose. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:17 as the definitive statement on God's sovereignty in salvation and judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: Romans 9:17 quotes this verse to explain God's sovereign purpose in election and hardening. God did not create Pharaoh to be evil but sustained him through judgment for a purpose: the global declaration of YHVH's name. This verse establishes that even resistance to God serves God's purposes. Pharaoh intended defiance; God used it for universal revelation. The Exodus became the defining act of God's self-disclosure for all subsequent history — Rahab in Jericho (Josh 2:10), the Philistines (1 Sam 4:8), and the nations generally (Psalm 106:8) all learn of YHVH through these events.

verses.kjv_text: As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people, that thou wilt not let them go?
verses.original_text: עוֹדְךָ מִסְתּוֹלֵל בְּעַמִּי לְבִלְתִּי שַׁלְּחָם׃
verses.transliteration: 'Odekha mistolel be'ammi levilti shallecham.
verses.study_note: 'Exaltest thou thyself' (mistolel) — Hithpolel of 'salal,' meaning to heap up, to build a raised highway against someone. Pharaoh is building a wall of resistance against God's people. The verb carries connotations of arrogant self-elevation — literally 'lifting yourself up as a rampart' against God's purposes. This is the sin of presumption: setting oneself as an obstacle to the God of the universe. The verse is brief — just seven Hebrew words — but devastating in its directness.

verses.kjv_text: Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now.
verses.original_text: הִנְנִי מַמְטִיר כָּעֵת מָחָר בָּרָד כָּבֵד מְאֹד אֲשֶׁר לֹא־הָיָה כָמֹהוּ בְּמִצְרַיִם לְמִן־הַיּוֹם הִוָּסְדָה וְעַד־עָתָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Hineni mamtir ka'et machar barad kaved me'od asher lo-hayah khamohu beMitzrayim lemin-hayyom hivvasdah ve'ad-attah.
verses.study_note: God announces the seventh plague: hail of unprecedented severity — 'such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof' (lemin-hayyom hivvasdah). The word 'hivvasdah' (was founded/established) points back to Egypt's origin as a nation. The hail will be historically unprecedented. 'I will cause it to rain' (mamtir) uses the same verb as Genesis 19:24 where God 'rained' fire on Sodom — the parallel is deliberate. What comes from heaven can be blessing (rain) or judgment (hail/fire).

verses.kjv_text: Send therefore now, and gather thy cattle, and all that thou hast in the field; for upon every man and beast which shall be found in the field, and shall not be brought home, the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה שְׁלַח הָעֵז אֶת־מִקְנְךָ וְאֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר לְךָ בַּשָּׂדֶה כָּל־הָאָדָם וְהַבְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר־יִמָּצֵא בַשָּׂדֶה וְלֹא יֵאָסֵף הַבַּיְתָה וְיָרַד עֲלֵהֶם הַבָּרָד וָמֵתוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah shelach ha'ez et-mikhnekha ve'et kol-asher lekha basadeh kol-ha'adam vehabbehemah asher-yimmatze vasadeh velo ye'asef habbayetah veyarad alehem habbarad vametu.
verses.study_note: A remarkable act of mercy within judgment: God warns Pharaoh to shelter his remaining livestock and servants. 'Gather thy cattle and all that thou hast in the field' (ha'ez et-mikhnekha). Despite six plagues of rejection, God offers a way to minimize the devastation. This is grace in the midst of wrath — even the enemy is given an opportunity to respond to God's word. The warning creates a test: will Pharaoh (and his people) believe YHVH's word enough to act on it?
verses.doctrinal_note: Grace embedded in judgment: God warns before He strikes. Even at this advanced stage of confrontation, God provides a means of protection for those who will heed His word. This establishes a pattern completed at Calvary: judgment is real, but mercy precedes it for all who respond. The warning in v.19 anticipates the Passover in chapter 12, where obedience to God's word (applying the blood) means the difference between life and death.

verses.kjv_text: He that feared the word of the LORD among the servants of Pharaoh made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses:
verses.original_text: הַיָּרֵא אֶת־דְּבַר יְהוָה מֵעַבְדֵי פַּרְעֹה הֵנִיס אֶת־עֲבָדָיו וְאֶת־מִקְנֵהוּ אֶל־הַבָּתִּים׃
verses.transliteration: Hayyare et-devar YHVH me'avdei Par'oh henis et-avadav ve'et-miknehu el-habbattim.
verses.study_note: A turning point: some of Pharaoh's own servants now 'feared the word of the LORD' (hayyare et-devar YHVH). The plagues are accomplishing their revelatory purpose — the knowledge of YHVH is penetrating Egyptian society. 'Feared' (yare) is the same word used for the fear of the LORD that is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10). These Egyptians are beginning to transfer their allegiance from Pharaoh to YHVH. They act on God's word — faith expressing itself in obedience.
verses.doctrinal_note: Faith reveals itself in action: those who feared God's word acted on it and were preserved. Those who disregarded it suffered loss. This binary — belief that acts versus disbelief that ignores — runs through all of Scripture, from Noah building the ark to Rahab hiding the spies to the response to the gospel itself. Saving faith is never merely intellectual assent; it is trust that moves the feet (James 2:17-26). These God-fearing Egyptians prefigure the 'mixed multitude' (Exod 12:38) that joins Israel at the Exodus.

verses.kjv_text: And he that regarded not the word of the LORD left his servants and his cattle in the field.
verses.original_text: וַאֲשֶׁר לֹא־שָׂם לִבּוֹ אֶל־דְּבַר יְהוָה וַיַּעֲזֹב אֶת־עֲבָדָיו וְאֶת־מִקְנֵהוּ בַּשָּׂדֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Va'asher lo-sam libbo el-devar YHVH vayya'azov et-avadav ve'et-miknehu basadeh.
verses.study_note: 'Regarded not' is literally 'did not set his heart to' (lo-sam libbo) — the heart was not directed toward God's word. This is not ignorance but willful inattention. 'Left' (vayya'azov — abandoned) his servants and cattle in the field. The result is devastation of their own making. The contrast between vv.20-21 creates a miniature judgment scene: the same word, the same warning, two different responses, two different outcomes. This is the pattern of all divine judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch forth thine hand toward heaven, that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, upon man, and upon beast, and upon every herb of the field, throughout the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ עַל־הַשָּׁמַיִם וִיהִי בָרָד בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם עַל־הָאָדָם וְעַל־הַבְּהֵמָה וְעַל כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh neteh et-yadkha al-hashamayim vihi varad bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim al-ha'adam ve'al-habbehemah ve'al kol-esev hasadeh be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Moses stretches his hand 'toward heaven' (al-hashamayim) — a prophetic gesture invoking divine authority over the atmospheric realm. The Egyptian sky-goddess Nut was believed to protect Egypt beneath her body arched over the earth. Moses' upraised hand summons destruction from the very sky Egypt's gods were supposed to control. The threefold target — man, beast, and every herb — signals comprehensive devastation striking every level of the created order.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses stretched forth his rod toward heaven: and the LORD sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground; and the LORD rained hail upon the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־מַטֵּהוּ עַל־הַשָּׁמַיִם וַיהוָה נָתַן קֹלֹת וּבָרָד וַתִּהֲלַךְ אֵשׁ אָרְצָה וַיַּמְטֵר יְהוָה בָּרָד עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyet Mosheh et-mattehu al-hashamayim vaYHVH natan kolot uvarad vattihalakh esh artzah vayyamter YHVH barad al-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: A terrifying theophany: thunder (kolot — literally 'voices'), hail, and fire running along the ground. The word 'kolot' means both 'thunder' and 'voices' — the voice of God manifests as the thunder of judgment (cf. Psalm 29). 'Fire ran along upon the ground' (vattihalakh esh artzah) — lightning or fire mingled with the hailstones, creating an otherworldly scene of simultaneous ice and fire. This combination is physically paradoxical, emphasizing its supernatural origin. The storm-god Seth, whom Egyptians associated with thunder, is shown to be powerless.

verses.kjv_text: So there was hail, and fire mingled with the hail, very grievous, such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בָרָד וְאֵשׁ מִתְלַקַּחַת בְּתוֹךְ הַבָּרָד כָּבֵד מְאֹד אֲשֶׁר לֹא־הָיָה כָמֹהוּ בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מֵאָז הָיְתָה לְגוֹי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi varad ve'esh mitlakachat betokh habbarad kaved me'od asher lo-hayah khamohu bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim me'az hayetah legoi.
verses.study_note: 'Fire mingled with the hail' (esh mitlakachat betokh habbarad) — the word 'mitlakachat' means 'catching itself,' 'flashing continuously.' This is fire that feeds upon itself within the hailstones — a physically impossible combination that underscores divine power. 'Very grievous' (kaved me'od) — again the word 'kaved' (heavy), linking this plague to Pharaoh's heart condition. The narrator confirms: nothing like this since Egypt became a nation (me'az hayetah legoi).

verses.kjv_text: And the hail smote throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and beast; and the hail smote every herb of the field, and brake every tree of the field.
verses.original_text: וַיַּךְ הַבָּרָד בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה מֵאָדָם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָה וְאֵת כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה הִכָּה הַבָּרָד וְאֶת־כָּל־עֵץ הַשָּׂדֶה שִׁבֵּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakh habbarad bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim et kol-asher basadeh me'adam ve'ad-behemah ve'et kol-esev hasadeh hikkah habbarad ve'et-kol-etz hasadeh shibber.
verses.study_note: Comprehensive destruction: the hail strikes everything in the field — man, beast, herbs, and trees. 'Brake every tree' (shibber — shattered) — the trees are not merely damaged but broken. The repeated 'kol' (all/every) five times in this verse emphasizes totality. Egypt's agricultural infrastructure — trees that take decades to grow — is destroyed in a single storm. The goddess Isis, patroness of agriculture, and Osiris, god of vegetation, are powerless.

verses.kjv_text: Only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were, was there no hail.
verses.original_text: רַק בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן אֲשֶׁר־שָׁם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא הָיָה בָּרָד׃
verses.transliteration: Rak be'eretz Goshen asher-sham benei Yisra'el lo hayah barad.
verses.study_note: 'Only' (rak) — the particle of exception. Goshen stands as an island of calm in a sea of devastation. The same storm system that destroys all of Egypt simply does not touch the land where Israel dwells. This is not a natural weather pattern — it is supernatural protection. The distinction that began in 8:22 and was reaffirmed in 9:4 reaches its most dramatic expression here: the hail, which struck 'all the land of Egypt,' did not fall in Goshen. The land itself testifies to the difference between YHVH's people and the world.
verses.doctrinal_note: Goshen as a type of divine protection: throughout Scripture, God preserves His people in the midst of, not apart from, the world's judgment. Israel in Goshen, Noah in the Ark, Rahab in Jericho, the sealed 144,000 in Revelation — the pattern is consistent. God does not always remove His people from the place of danger but shields them within it. This is the theological foundation for Christ's prayer: 'I pray not that thou shouldest take them out of the world, but that thou shouldest keep them from the evil' (John 17:15).

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh sent, and called for Moses and Aaron, and said unto them, I have sinned this time: the LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וַיִּקְרָא לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם חָטָאתִי הַפָּעָם יְהוָה הַצַּדִּיק וַאֲנִי וְעַמִּי הָרְשָׁעִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyishlach Par'oh vayyikra leMosheh ule'Aharon vayomer alehem chatati happa'am YHVH hatzaddik va'ani ve'ammi haresha'im.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh makes his most extensive confession yet: 'I have sinned' (chatati), 'the LORD is righteous' (YHVH hatzaddik), 'I and my people are wicked' (haresha'im). This is theologically precise language — acknowledging God's righteousness and his own wickedness. But confession alone is not repentance. Pharaoh uses the word 'happa'am' (this time) — limiting his admission, as if his previous refusals were somehow different. True repentance acknowledges a pattern, not merely a single instance.

verses.kjv_text: Intreat the LORD (for it is enough) that there be no more mighty thunderings and hail; and I will let you go, and ye shall stay no longer.
verses.original_text: הַעְתִּירוּ אֶל־יְהוָה וְרַב מִהְיֹת קֹלֹת אֱלֹהִים וּבָרָד וַאֲשַׁלְּחָה אֶתְכֶם וְלֹא תֹסִפוּן לַעֲמֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Ha'tiru el-YHVH verav miheyot kolot Elohim uvarad va'ashalechah etkhem velo tosifun la'amod.
verses.study_note: 'Intreat the LORD' (ha'tiru) — Pharaoh asks Moses to pray on his behalf, acknowledging Moses' access to YHVH. 'Mighty thunderings' is literally 'voices of God' (kolot Elohim) — Pharaoh uses a divine superlative, recognizing these as no ordinary thunder. His promise — 'I will let you go' (va'ashalechah) — is conditional on relief. This is the pattern of Pharaoh: concession under pressure, reversal upon relief. His repentance is driven by suffering, not by genuine submission to YHVH.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto him, As soon as I am gone out of the city, I will spread abroad my hands unto the LORD; and the thunder shall cease, neither shall there be any more hail; that thou mayest know how that the earth is the LORD’s.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו מֹשֶׁה כְּצֵאתִי אֶת־הָעִיר אֶפְרֹשׂ אֶת־כַּפַּי אֶל־יְהוָה הַקֹּלוֹת יֶחְדָּלוּן וְהַבָּרָד לֹא יִהְיֶה־עוֹד לְמַעַן תֵּדַע כִּי לַיהוָה הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav Mosheh ketze'ti et-ha'ir efros et-kappai el-YHVH hakkolot yechddalun vehabbarad lo yihyeh-od lema'an teda ki laYHVH ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: Moses will leave the city to pray — he prays outside the idolatrous city, in the open before YHVH. 'Spread abroad my hands' (efros et-kappai) — the posture of prayer with open palms. The purpose is again revelatory: 'that thou mayest know that the earth is the LORD'S' (ki laYHVH ha'aretz). This claim is staggering in an Egyptian context: Pharaoh was considered the divine ruler of the earth. Moses declares that the very ground Pharaoh stands on belongs to YHVH.
verses.doctrinal_note: The earth belongs to YHVH, not to Pharaoh or any human ruler. 'The earth is the LORD'S and the fulness thereof' (Psalm 24:1) is the foundational claim of biblical theology. All human authority is derivative and accountable. The stopping of the hail at Moses' prayer demonstrates this: YHVH controls the weather, the land, and the outcome. Pharaoh's pretension to divine sovereignty is exposed as empty.

verses.kjv_text: But as for thee and thy servants, I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה וַעֲבָדֶיךָ יָדַעְתִּי כִּי טֶרֶם תִּירְאוּן מִפְּנֵי יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah va'avadekha yada'ti ki terem tire'un mippenei YHVH Elohim.
verses.study_note: Moses speaks prophetically: 'I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God' (yada'ti ki terem tire'un). He sees through Pharaoh's confession. 'Not yet' (terem) — perhaps implying that the fear will eventually come, but only at the catastrophic end. Moses uses the full title 'YHVH Elohim' — the covenant God who is also the sovereign Creator. Moses discerns the difference between distress-driven confession and genuine God-fearing submission. This is prophetic insight at its sharpest.

verses.kjv_text: And the flax and the barley was smitten: for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was bolled.
verses.original_text: וְהַפִּשְׁתָּה וְהַשְּׂעֹרָה נֻכָּתָה כִּי הַשְּׂעֹרָה אָבִיב וְהַפִּשְׁתָּה גִּבְעֹל׃
verses.transliteration: Vehappishtah vehasse'orah nukkatah ki hasse'orah aviv vehappishtah giv'ol.
verses.study_note: A precise agricultural detail: flax and barley were destroyed because they were mature — barley 'in the ear' (aviv — in bud/head) and flax 'bolled' (giv'ol — in bud/flower). The word 'aviv' later gives its name to the first month of the Hebrew calendar (Aviv/Nisan), the month of Passover (Exod 13:4). The barley harvest was imminent — Egypt's grain supply was struck at the worst possible moment. This detail also precisely dates the plague to late January or February in Egypt's agricultural cycle.

verses.kjv_text: But the wheat and the rie were not smitten: for they were not grown up.
verses.original_text: וְהַחִטָּה וְהַכֻּסֶּמֶת לֹא נֻכּוּ כִּי אֲפִילֹת הֵנָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehachittah vehakkussemet lo nukku ki afilot hennah.
verses.study_note: Wheat and rie (spelt/emmer) survived because they were 'not grown up' (afilot — late-ripening, still tender). This is not merely an agricultural footnote — it sets up the next plague. The surviving grain will be destroyed by the locusts in chapter 10:15. God's judgment operates in stages; what survives one plague falls to the next. This detail also reveals divine restraint: enough grain survives to sustain life temporarily, but the threat of total famine looms.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh, and spread abroad his hands unto the LORD: and the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured upon the earth.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא מֹשֶׁה מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה אֶת־הָעִיר וַיִּפְרֹשׂ כַּפָּיו אֶל־יְהוָה וַיַּחְדְּלוּ הַקֹּלוֹת וְהַבָּרָד וּמָטָר לֹא־נִתַּךְ אָרְצָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze Mosheh me'im Par'oh et-ha'ir vayyifros kappav el-YHVH vayyachdelu hakkolot vehabbarad umatar lo-nittakh artzah.
verses.study_note: Moses fulfills his word: he leaves the city, spreads his hands to YHVH, and the storm immediately stops. 'Ceased' (vayyachdelu) — complete cessation. 'The rain was not poured upon the earth' (matar lo-nittakh artzah) — literally the rain was 'not poured out.' The same God who sent the storm withdraws it instantly at the prophet's prayer. Moses' intercessory authority over nature points forward to Christ's authority over wind and wave (Mark 4:39).

verses.kjv_text: And when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders were ceased, he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart, he and his servants.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא פַּרְעֹה כִּי־חָדַל הַמָּטָר וְהַבָּרָד וְהַקֹּלֹת וַיֹּסֶף לַחֲטֹא וַיַּכְבֵּד לִבּוֹ הוּא וַעֲבָדָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Par'oh ki-chadal hammatar vehabbarad vehakkolot vayyosef lachato vayyakhbed libbo hu va'avadav.
verses.study_note: 'He sinned yet more' (vayyosef lachato) — literally 'he added to sin.' Pharaoh's pattern is now explicit: relief leads to regression. 'Hardened his heart' (vayyakhbed libbo — 'made his heart heavy') — the Hiphil causative: Pharaoh actively, deliberately hardens himself. This time his servants join him: 'he and his servants' (hu va'avadav). The fear some servants showed in v.20 is overridden by the court's collective return to defiance. Moses' prediction (v.30) is fulfilled within verses.

verses.kjv_text: And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, neither would he let the children of Israel go; as the LORD had spoken by Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה בְּיַד־מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyechezak lev Par'oh velo shillach et-benei Yisra'el ka'asher dibber YHVH beyad-Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes with the summary verdict: 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened' (vayyechezak lev Par'oh) — the root 'chazak' (strong/firm) indicates his heart has become rigid, immovable. 'As the LORD had spoken by Moses' (ka'asher dibber YHVH beyad-Mosheh) — literally 'by the hand of Moses.' Everything unfolds exactly as God declared. Pharaoh's most sincere-sounding confession (v.27) has proved empty within eight verses. The chapter that opened with the demand 'Let my people go' closes with 'he would not let the children of Israel go.' Three plagues, one confession, zero change.
verses.doctrinal_note: The closing formula — 'as the LORD had spoken by Moses' — reaffirms divine sovereignty over the entire narrative.
