Pharaoh's decisions, even his reversals, operate within the framework God has declared. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is both his own act (v.34, 'he hardened his heart') and God's act (v.12, 'the LORD hardened'). This is not contradiction but mystery: human responsibility and divine sovereignty operate simultaneously. Paul resolves this tension not by eliminating either pole but by affirming both and pointing to God's right as Creator (Romans 9:19-21).

meta.overview: Exodus 9 records three devastating plagues — the fifth (pestilence on livestock), sixth (boils on man and beast), and seventh (hail mixed with fire) — that escalate God's judgment against Egypt and its gods. The chapter marks a critical turning point in the plague narrative: God begins to explicitly state the purpose of the plagues — that His name might be declared throughout all the earth (v.16). A new element emerges in the seventh plague: mercy within judgment, as God warns Pharaoh to shelter his remaining livestock and servants. Some Egyptians now 'fear the word of the LORD' (v.20), showing that the plagues are accomplishing their revelatory purpose. Pharaoh's heart continues to harden, fulfilling the divine declaration of 4:21. The systematic dismantling of Egypt's religious, economic, and agricultural infrastructure reaches a new intensity. Each plague targets specific Egyptian deities: pestilence strikes Apis and Hathor (cattle gods), boils humiliate the healing arts of Sekhmet and Imhotep, and hail demonstrates YHVH's supremacy over Nut (sky goddess) and Seth (storm god). Throughout, the distinction between Israel and Egypt — Goshen untouched while Egypt is devastated — powerfully foreshadows the separation between God's covenant people and the world under judgment.
meta.theme: The Escalation of Divine Judgment & the Declaration of God's Name
meta.keyWordMeaning: Pestilence, plague — a devastating epidemic sent by divine agency. The word carries the sense of divine speech (davar/word) turned to destruction — God's word brings both life and judgment.
outline: The Fifth Plague — Pestilence on Livestock (vv.1-7): All Egyptian cattle die; Israel's livestock untouched
The Sixth Plague — Boils on Man and Beast (vv.8-12): Furnace soot becomes boils; magicians cannot stand
The Seventh Plague — Hail Announced with Purpose (vv.13-21): God declares His name; mercy offered before judgment
The Seventh Plague — Hail Falls (vv.22-26): Fire and hail destroy Egypt; Goshen preserved
Pharaoh's False Repentance (vv.27-35): Confession without change; heart hardened again

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go in unto Pharaoh: for I have hardened his heart, and the heart of his servants, that I might shew these my signs before him:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה בֹּא אֶל־פַּרְעֹה כִּי־אֲנִי הִכְבַּדְתִּי אֶת־לִבּוֹ וְאֶת־לֵב עֲבָדָיו לְמַעַן שִׁתִי אֹתֹתַי אֵלֶּה בְּקִרְבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh bo el-Par'oh ki-ani hikhbadti et-libbo ve'et-lev avadav lema'an shiti ototai elleh bekirbo.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with a staggering theological revelation: 'I have hardened his heart' (ani hikhbadti et-libbo). The verb 'hikhbadti' is the Hiphil (causative) of 'kaved' — to make heavy, to cause to be weighty. God now claims direct agency in the hardening process. In the earlier plagues, Pharaoh hardened his own heart (Exod 8:15, 8:32); now God confirms and seals that self-chosen trajectory. The purpose clause 'lema'an' (in order that) reveals the divine intent: the plagues are not random acts of power but deliberately staged revelations of God's character and authority.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hardening of Pharaoh's heart raises the deepest question of divine sovereignty and human responsibility. Scripture maintains both truths without contradiction: Pharaoh freely chose resistance (8:15, 8:32), and God judicially confirmed that choice (9:12, 10:1). Paul addresses this directly in Romans 9:17-18, quoting Exodus 9:16. God does not create evil in the human heart; He allows, confirms, and uses the evil already present for His redemptive purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And that thou mayest tell in the ears of thy son, and of thy son’s son, what things I have wrought in Egypt, and my signs which I have done among them; that ye may know how that I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּלְמַעַן תְּסַפֵּר בְּאָזְנֵי בִנְךָ וּבֶן־בִּנְךָ אֵת אֲשֶׁר הִתְעַלַּלְתִּי בְּמִצְרַיִם וְאֶת־אֹתֹתַי אֲשֶׁר־שַׂמְתִּי בָם וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulema'an tesapper be'oznei vinkha uven-binkha et asher hit'alalti beMitzrayim ve'et-ototai asher-samti vam vidatem ki-ani YHVH.
verses.study_note: The second purpose of the plagues: generational testimony. 'That thou mayest tell' (tesapper) — from 'saphar,' to recount, to narrate. This becomes the foundation of the Passover Haggadah, where fathers recount the Exodus to their children (Exod 13:8, 14). The word 'hit'alalti' is striking — it means 'I have dealt severely with' or even 'I have made a mockery of.' God openly declares He is making sport of Egypt's pretensions. The ultimate purpose: 'that ye may know that I am YHVH' — the revelation formula that echoes throughout Exodus.
verses.doctrinal_note: The plagues serve a pedagogical purpose extending far beyond the immediate historical moment. God designs His mighty acts to be told and retold across generations. Deuteronomy 6:20-25 and Psalm 78:1-7 develop this principle: God's acts in history become the curriculum of faith for every generation. The Exodus is not ancient history but living testimony.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron came in unto Pharaoh, and said unto him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me? let my people go, that they may serve me.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו כֹּה־אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים עַד־מָתַי מֵאַנְתָּ לֵעָנֹת מִפָּנָי שַׁלַּח עַמִּי וְיַעַבְדֻנִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vayavo Mosheh ve'Aharon el-Par'oh vayyomeru elav koh-amar YHVH Elohei ha'Ivrim ad-matai me'anta le'anot mippanai shallach ammi veya'avduni.
verses.study_note: God's demand now contains a stinging question: 'How long wilt thou refuse to humble thyself before me?' (ad-matai me'anta le'anot mippanai). The verb 'le'anot' (to humble oneself, to be afflicted) is the same word used of Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exod 1:11-12) and later of the Day of Atonement fasting (Lev 16:29, 23:27). What Pharaoh has done to Israel — affliction/humbling — he now refuses to do before God. The irony is devastating: the great Pharaoh, self-proclaimed deity, is called to humble himself before the true God.

verses.kjv_text: Else, if thou refuse to let my people go, behold, to morrow will I bring the locusts into thy coast:
verses.original_text: כִּי אִם־מָאֵן אַתָּה לְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת־עַמִּי הִנְנִי מֵבִיא מָחָר אַרְבֶּה בִּגְבֻלֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki im-ma'en attah leshalle'ach et-ammi hineni mevi machar arbeh bigvulekha.
verses.study_note: The locust plague is announced with a deadline: 'tomorrow' (machar). God gives Pharaoh one more night to repent. 'Arbeh' (locusts) comes from the root 'ravah' (to be many, to multiply) — the locust is named for its terrifying capacity to multiply. In the ancient Near East, locust swarms could number in the billions and strip entire regions bare in hours. The locust was associated with the deity Serapia, protector of crops — another Egyptian god about to be proven impotent.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall cover the face of the earth, that one cannot be able to see the earth: and they shall eat the residue of that which is escaped, which remaineth unto you from the hail, and shall eat every tree which groweth for you out of the field:
verses.original_text: וְכִסָּה אֶת־עֵין הָאָרֶץ וְלֹא יוּכַל לִרְאֹת אֶת־הָאָרֶץ וְאָכַל אֶת־יֶתֶר הַפְּלֵטָה הַנִּשְׁאֶרֶת לָכֶם מִן־הַבָּרָד וְאָכַל אֶת־כָּל־הָעֵץ הַצֹּמֵחַ לָכֶם מִן־הַשָּׂדֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhissah et-ein ha'aretz velo yukhal lir'ot et-ha'aretz ve'akhal et-yeter happeletah hannish'eret lakhem min-habbarad ve'akhal et-kol-ha'etz hattzome'ach lakhem min-hassadeh.
verses.study_note: The description is catastrophic: the locusts will 'cover the face of the earth' (khissah et-ein ha'aretz — literally 'cover the eye of the land') so completely that the ground cannot be seen. They will finish what the hail began — eating the 'residue' (yeter happeletah — 'the remnant of the escape'). The hail of plague seven had destroyed the flax and barley but left the wheat and spelt (9:31-32); now the locusts will take everything remaining. This connection between plagues shows God's comprehensive, systematic judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall fill thy houses, and the houses of all thy servants, and the houses of all the Egyptians; which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers’ fathers have seen, since the day that they were upon the earth unto this day. And he turned himself, and went out from Pharaoh.
verses.original_text: וּמָלְאוּ בָתֶּיךָ וּבָתֵּי כָל־עֲבָדֶיךָ וּבָתֵּי כָל־מִצְרַיִם אֲשֶׁר לֹא־רָאוּ אֲבֹתֶיךָ וַאֲבוֹת אֲבֹתֶיךָ מִיּוֹם הֱיוֹתָם עַל־הָאֲדָמָה עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה וַיִּפֶן וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Umal'u vatekha uvattei khol-avadekha uvattei khol-Mitzrayim asher lo-ra'u avotekha va'avot avotekha miyyom heyotam al-ha'adamah ad hayyom hazzeh vayyifen vayyetze me'im Par'oh.
verses.study_note: The scope is unprecedented: 'which neither thy fathers, nor thy fathers' fathers have seen.' This is not merely a natural locust swarm but something beyond all historical experience. The locusts will invade not just fields but houses — Pharaoh's palace, his servants' homes, every Egyptian dwelling. After delivering this devastating oracle, Moses 'turned himself, and went out' (vayyifen vayyetze) — an abrupt, dramatic departure. He does not wait for Pharaoh's response; the word has been spoken, and God will act.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh’s servants said unto him, How long shall this man be a snare unto us? let the men go, that they may serve the LORD their God: knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ עַבְדֵי פַרְעֹה אֵלָיו עַד־מָתַי יִהְיֶה זֶה לָנוּ לְמוֹקֵשׁ שַׁלַּח אֶת־הָאֲנָשִׁים וְיַעַבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם הֲטֶרֶם תֵּדַע כִּי אָבְדָה מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru avdei Far'oh elav ad-matai yihyeh zeh lanu lemokesh shallach et-ha'anashim veya'avdu et-YHVH Eloheihem haterem teda ki avedah Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: A watershed moment: Pharaoh's own court breaks ranks. His servants echo God's question 'how long?' (ad-matai, v.3) back at Pharaoh — the question posed to him by God is now posed to him by his own advisors. They call Moses 'a snare' (mokesh — a trap, a lure) — acknowledging his devastating effectiveness. Most remarkably, they use YHVH's covenant name and acknowledge Him as 'their God' — the Egyptian court has been forced to concede YHVH's reality. 'Knowest thou not yet that Egypt is destroyed?' (avedah Mitzrayim) — the verb 'avad' means to perish, to be lost. Egypt is already ruined; Pharaoh is the last to admit it.
verses.doctrinal_note: When God's judgment falls, even the resistant eventually recognize reality. Pharaoh's servants see what he refuses to see. This pattern recurs in Scripture: Nebuchadnezzar's courtiers, Pilate's wife, the Philippian jailer — secondary witnesses often grasp the truth before the primary antagonist. God's acts testify to all, not just to the faithful.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron were brought again unto Pharaoh: and he said unto them, Go, serve the LORD your God: but who are they that shall go?
verses.original_text: וַיּוּשַׁב אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶת־אַהֲרֹן אֶל־פַּרְעֹה וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם לְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם מִי וָמִי הַהֹלְכִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyushav et-Mosheh ve'et-Aharon el-Par'oh vayomer alehem lekhu ivdu et-YHVH Eloheikhem mi vami haholkhim.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh summons Moses and Aaron back — a retreat from his hard position, forced by his servants' rebellion. He appears to concede: 'Go, serve the LORD your God.' But the conditional question 'who are they that shall go?' (mi vami haholkhim) reveals his intent to negotiate the terms of obedience. Pharaoh will permit worship but on his terms, not God's. This is the first of two compromises in this chapter — partial obedience dressed as concession.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, We will go with our young and with our old, with our sons and with our daughters, with our flocks and with our herds will we go; for we must hold a feast unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה בִּנְעָרֵינוּ וּבִזְקֵנֵינוּ נֵלֵךְ בְּבָנֵינוּ וּבִבְנוֹתֵנוּ בְּצֹאנֵנוּ וּבִבְקָרֵנוּ נֵלֵךְ כִּי חַג־יְהוָה לָנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh bine'areinu uvizkenenu nelekh bevaneinu uvivnotenu betzoneinu uvivkarenu nelekh ki chag-YHVH lanu.
verses.study_note: Moses' reply is comprehensive and non-negotiable: young and old, sons and daughters, flocks and herds — everyone and everything. Six categories are named, forming a complete picture of the covenant community. 'For we must hold a feast unto the LORD' (chag-YHVH lanu) — a festival/pilgrimage feast. The word 'chag' (feast, from 'chagag,' to celebrate in a circle/dance) implies a joyful, corporate celebration. God demands not partial but total worship from the entire community — no one left behind, no resource withheld.
verses.doctrinal_note: God requires total commitment from His people — not selective obedience. Partial worship is not worship at all. Moses' insistence that every person and every possession must go foreshadows Christ's call to discipleship: 'If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children... he cannot be my disciple' (Luke 14:26). Compromise with the world's ruler is never an option.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go, and your little ones: look to it; for evil is before you.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יְהִי כֵן יְהוָה עִמָּכֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר אֲשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם וְאֶת־טַפְּכֶם רְאוּ כִּי רָעָה נֶגֶד פְּנֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer alehem yehi khen YHVH immakhem ka'asher ashallach etkhem ve'et-tappekhem re'u ki ra'ah neged peneikhem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's response drips with sarcasm and menace. 'Let the LORD be so with you, as I will let you go' — this is an oath formula turned into a threat: 'The LORD will be with you about as much as I intend to release you' — meaning not at all. 'Evil is before your face' (ra'ah neged peneikhem) — a veiled accusation that Moses has hostile intentions. Pharaoh imputes evil motive to Moses while he himself embodies it. The word 'ra'ah' (evil, calamity) ironically describes Pharaoh's own conduct, not Moses'.

verses.kjv_text: Not so: go now ye that are men, and serve the LORD; for that ye did desire. And they were driven out from Pharaoh’s presence.
verses.original_text: לֹא כֵן לְכוּ־נָא הַגְּבָרִים וְעִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה כִּי אֹתָהּ אַתֶּם מְבַקְשִׁים וַיְגָרֶשׁ אֹתָם מֵאֵת פְּנֵי פַרְעֹה׃
verses.transliteration: Lo khen lekhu-na haggevarim ve'ivdu et-YHVH ki otah attem mevakshim vayegaresh otam me'et penei Far'oh.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's first compromise is revealed: 'go now ye that are men' (haggevarim — the adult males only). Women, children, and livestock must stay — human hostages guaranteeing Israel's return. This compromise exposes the strategy of the world: allow limited worship but retain control over the most precious things. 'They were driven out' (vayegaresh) — physically expelled from Pharaoh's court. The verb 'garash' (to drive out, to expel) is the same word used of Adam's expulsion from Eden (Gen 3:24) and will later describe God driving out the Canaanites (Exod 23:28-31). Pharaoh's act of expulsion ironically foreshadows his ultimate forced release of all Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts, that they may come up upon the land of Egypt, and eat every herb of the land, even all that the hail hath left.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה יָדְךָ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בָּאַרְבֶּה וְיַעַל עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְיֹאכַל אֶת־כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הָאָרֶץ אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר הִשְׁאִיר הַבָּרָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh neteh yadekha al-eretz Mitzrayim ba'arbeh veya'al al-eretz Mitzrayim veyokhal et-kol-esev ha'aretz et kol-asher hish'ir habbarad.
verses.study_note: God commands Moses to 'stretch out thine hand' (neteh yadekha) — the extended hand/staff of Moses has become the symbol of divine power channeled through human agency. 'Even all that the hail hath left' (kol-asher hish'ir habbarad) — the plagues work in concert, each completing what the previous one began. This systematic, cumulative approach reveals God's methodical judgment: nothing is overlooked, nothing escapes.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses stretched forth his rod over the land of Egypt, and the LORD brought an east wind upon the land all that day, and all that night; and when it was morning, the east wind brought the locusts.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־מַטֵּהוּ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וַיהוָה נִהַג רוּחַ קָדִים בָּאָרֶץ כָּל־הַיּוֹם הַהוּא וְכָל־הַלָּיְלָה הַבֹּקֶר הָיָה וְרוּחַ הַקָּדִים נָשָׂא אֶת־הָאַרְבֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayet Mosheh et-mattehu al-eretz Mitzrayim vaYHVH nihag ruach kadim ba'aretz kol-hayyom hahu vekhol-hallaylah habboker hayah veruach hakkadim nasa et-ha'arbeh.
verses.study_note: God uses an 'east wind' (ruach kadim) — the hot, dry wind from the Arabian desert — to carry the locusts. The east wind is God's instrument of judgment throughout Scripture: it parts the Red Sea (14:21), withers Jonah's plant (Jonah 4:8), and symbolizes divine wrath (Jer 18:17, Ezek 17:10). The timing is precise: the wind blows all day and night, and 'when it was morning' (habboker hayah) the locusts arrive. God commands the natural world with absolute precision. The ruach (wind/spirit) is the same word used for God's Spirit — the Creator directs His creation as an instrument of justice.

verses.kjv_text: And the locusts went up over all the land of Egypt, and rested in all the coasts of Egypt: very grievous were they; before them there were no such locusts as they, neither after them shall be such.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעַל הָאַרְבֶּה עַל כָּל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וַיָּנַח בְּכֹל גְּבוּל מִצְרָיִם כָּבֵד מְאֹד לְפָנָיו לֹא־הָיָה כֵן אַרְבֶּה כָּמֹהוּ וְאַחֲרָיו לֹא יִהְיֶה־כֵּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'al ha'arbeh al kol-eretz Mitzrayim vayyannach bekhol gevul Mitzrayim kaved me'od lefanav lo-hayah khen arbeh kamohu ve'acharav lo yihyeh-ken.
verses.study_note: The locust plague is superlative: 'before them there were no such locusts... neither after them shall be such.' This unique-in-history language is later echoed by Joel (Joel 2:2) for the eschatological locust army and by Jesus for the Great Tribulation (Matt 24:21). The word 'kaved' (grievous/heavy) is from the same root as Pharaoh's hardened heart ('hikhbadti,' v.1) — the weight of Pharaoh's stubborn pride is matched by the weight of God's judgment. A devastating wordplay: Pharaoh's 'heavy' heart produces 'heavy' locusts.
verses.doctrinal_note: The unprecedented nature of this plague points beyond the immediate historical event. Joel 1-2 uses the locust imagery to describe both a historical invasion and an eschatological Day of the Lord. Revelation 9:3-11 draws on Exodus locust imagery for the fifth trumpet judgment. The plagues of Egypt serve as a pattern and preview of God's final judgment on all who resist His rule.

verses.kjv_text: For they covered the face of the whole earth, so that the land was darkened; and they did eat every herb of the land, and all the fruit of the trees which the hail had left: and there remained not any green thing in the trees, or in the herbs of the field, through all the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיְכַס אֶת־עֵין כָּל־הָאָרֶץ וַתֶּחְשַׁךְ הָאָרֶץ וַיֹּאכַל אֶת־כָּל־עֵשֶׂב הָאָרֶץ וְאֵת כָּל־פְּרִי הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר הוֹתִיר הַבָּרָד וְלֹא־נוֹתַר כָּל־יֶרֶק בָּעֵץ וּבְעֵשֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayekhas et-ein kol-ha'aretz vattech'shakh ha'aretz vayyokhal et-kol-esev ha'aretz ve'et kol-peri ha'etz asher hotir habbarad velo-notar kol-yerek ba'etz uve'esev hassadeh bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The fulfillment matches the prediction precisely (cf. vv.5-6). 'The land was darkened' (vattech'shakh ha'aretz) — the locust swarm is so dense it blocks the sun, a foretaste of the ninth plague. 'There remained not any green thing' (lo-notar kol-yerek) — total agricultural annihilation. The triple 'kol' (all/every) — all herbs, all fruit, all the land — emphasizes completeness. Egypt, the breadbasket of the ancient world, famous for its agricultural abundance, is stripped bare. The creation bounty of Genesis 1:11-12 is reversed — God unmakes what He made.

verses.kjv_text: Then Pharaoh called for Moses and Aaron in haste; and he said, I have sinned against the LORD your God, and against you.
verses.original_text: וַיְמַהֵר פַּרְעֹה לִקְרֹא לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן וַיֹּאמֶר חָטָאתִי לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְלָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayemaher Par'oh likro leMosheh uleAharon vayomer chatati laYHVH Eloheikhem velakhem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh summons Moses 'in haste' (vayemaher) and confesses: 'I have sinned' (chatati). This is Pharaoh's second confession of sin (cf. 9:27). He names YHVH and acknowledges wrongdoing against both God and Moses. But the confession lacks true repentance — it is crisis-driven, not heart-driven. The phrase 'your God' (Eloheikhem) reveals the distance: YHVH is still 'your' God, not 'my' God. Pharaoh's confession is a negotiating tool, not a surrender.

verses.kjv_text: Now therefore forgive, I pray thee, my sin only this once, and intreat the LORD your God, that he may take away from me this death only.
verses.original_text: וְעַתָּה שָׂא נָא חַטָּאתִי אַךְ הַפַּעַם וְהַעְתִּירוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְיָסֵר מֵעָלַי רַק אֶת־הַמָּוֶת הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah sa na chattati akh happa'am veha'tiru laYHVH Eloheikhem veyaser me'alai rak et-hammavet hazzeh.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh begs forgiveness 'only this once' (akh happa'am) — a telling qualifier that limits his repentance to the present crisis. He asks Moses to 'entreat' (ha'tiru — to pray, to intercede) — acknowledging that Moses has access to God that Pharaoh lacks. 'Take away from me this death only' (hammavet hazzeh) — the locusts are described as 'death' itself. Pharaoh calls the locusts 'death' because they represent the death of Egypt's economy and food supply. Yet even in desperation, YHVH remains 'your' God, not his.

verses.kjv_text: And he went out from Pharaoh, and intreated the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם פַּרְעֹה וַיֶּעְתַּר אֶל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyetze me'im Par'oh vayye'tar el-YHVH.
verses.study_note: Moses intercedes — again. Despite Pharaoh's superficial repentance, Moses faithfully prays. The verb 'vayye'tar' (entreated) implies earnest, persistent prayer. Moses serves as mediator between Pharaoh and God — a priestly role that foreshadows Christ's intercession. Even for an enemy who repeatedly betrays his word, Moses prays. The brevity of this verse highlights the contrast: Pharaoh uses many words (vv.16-17); Moses simply goes to God.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD turned a mighty strong west wind, which took away the locusts, and cast them into the Red sea; there remained not one locust in all the coasts of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיַּהֲפֹךְ יְהוָה רוּחַ־יָם חָזָק מְאֹד וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת־הָאַרְבֶּה וַיִּתְקָעֵהוּ יָמָּה סּוּף לֹא נִשְׁאַר אַרְבֶּה אֶחָד בְּכֹל גְּבוּל מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyahafokh YHVH ruach-yam chazak me'od vayyissa et-ha'arbeh vayyitka'ehu yammah Suf lo nish'ar arbeh echad bekhol gevul Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: God 'turned' (vayyahafokh — the same 'haphakh' from Gen 19:25, the Sodom destruction) a 'mighty strong west wind' (ruach-yam chazak me'od — literally 'sea wind extremely strong'). The locusts are hurled into the 'Red Sea' (yam Suf — literally 'Sea of Reeds') — the first mention of this body of water, which will become the site of Israel's supreme deliverance in Exodus 14. 'Not one locust' (lo nish'ar arbeh echad) — the removal is as total as the plague was. God's ability to remove judgment is as absolute as His power to send it.

verses.kjv_text: But the LORD hardened Pharaoh’s heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go.
verses.original_text: וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא שִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechazzek YHVH et-lev Par'oh velo shillach et-benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The pattern repeats with grim predictability. 'The LORD hardened' (vayechazzek — from 'chazak,' to strengthen, to make firm) — God strengthens Pharaoh's resolve in the direction Pharaoh has already chosen. This is the third Hebrew verb used for hardening: 'kaved' (to make heavy, v.1), 'kashah' (to make hard/stubborn, 7:3), and 'chazak' (to make strong/firm). The threefold vocabulary reveals three dimensions of hardening: heaviness (insensitivity), hardness (inflexibility), and strength (entrenched defiance).
verses.doctrinal_note: The eighth plague ends exactly as the previous ones — Pharaoh's heart remains hard. His confession (v.16) evaporated the moment the crisis passed. This is the pattern of false repentance: crisis produces confession, relief produces regression. True repentance, by contrast, persists after the crisis has passed. 'Godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of: but the sorrow of the world worketh death' (2 Cor 7:10).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה יָדְךָ עַל־הַשָּׁמַיִם וִיהִי חֹשֶׁךְ עַל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וְיָמֵשׁ חֹשֶׁךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh neteh yadekha al-hashamayim vihi choshekh al-eretz Mitzrayim veyamesh choshekh.
verses.study_note: The ninth plague — Darkness — arrives without warning to Pharaoh. Unlike most previous plagues, there is no prophetic announcement, no demand to Pharaoh, no opportunity for avoidance. God simply commands Moses to act. 'Darkness which may be felt' (veyamesh choshekh) — the word 'yamesh' means to grope, to feel, to touch. This is not ordinary nighttime but a thick, tangible, supernatural darkness — a darkness with physical substance. This plague strikes at the very heart of Egyptian religion. Ra (also Amon-Ra, Aten, Khepri, Atum) was the supreme deity of Egypt — the sun god in his various forms was the king of the gods. Egypt literally worshipped light. The ninth plague declares: YHVH is Lord of the sun; He can extinguish it at will.
verses.doctrinal_note: The plague of darkness is a direct assault on Ra, the supreme god of Egypt. Pharaoh himself was considered the 'Son of Ra' — the living incarnation of the sun god on earth. By extinguishing the sun for three days, YHVH demonstrates absolute sovereignty over Egypt's highest deity. This plague reverses creation itself: 'And God said, Let there be light' (Gen 1:3). In Egypt, God says, 'Let there be darkness.' The Creator who made light can unmake it. This anticipates the cosmic darkness at Christ's crucifixion (Matt 27:45) — when the sun was darkened as God judged sin.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses stretched forth his hand toward heaven; and there was a thick darkness in all the land of Egypt three days:
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַשָּׁמָיִם וַיְהִי חֹשֶׁךְ־אֲפֵלָה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayet Mosheh et-yado al-hashamayim vayehi choshekh-afelah bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim sheloshet yamim.
verses.study_note: The darkness is described as 'choshekh-afelah' — a double expression combining two words for darkness: 'choshekh' (darkness) and 'afelah' (deep gloom, thick darkness). This is darkness squared — an intensified, supernatural pitch-blackness. 'Three days' (sheloshet yamim) — the same duration as Jonah in the fish (Jonah 1:17), Jesus in the tomb (Matt 12:40), and Paul's blindness after his Damascus Road encounter (Acts 9:9). Three days of darkness precede resurrection and transformation throughout Scripture.

verses.kjv_text: They saw not one another, neither rose any from his place for three days: but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings.
verses.original_text: לֹא־רָאוּ אִישׁ אֶת־אָחִיו וְלֹא־קָמוּ אִישׁ מִתַּחְתָּיו שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים וּלְכָל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָיָה אוֹר בְּמוֹשְׁבֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Lo-ra'u ish et-achiv velo-kamu ish mittachtav sheloshet yamim ulekhol-benei Yisra'el hayah or bemoshevotam.
verses.study_note: The effects of the darkness are total paralysis: 'they saw not one another, neither rose any from his place.' Egyptian society — commerce, government, religion, daily life — comes to a complete standstill. The most powerful civilization on earth is immobilized by darkness. Meanwhile, 'all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings' (hayah or bemoshevotam). This is the most dramatic display yet of the distinction ('havdalah') between Israel and Egypt, first established in the fourth plague (8:22). Light and darkness coexist in the same land at the same time — but only by God's sovereign decree.
verses.doctrinal_note: The distinction between Israel (light) and Egypt (darkness) is a powerful type of the spiritual reality described throughout Scripture. 'Ye are the light of the world' (Matt 5:14). 'God is light, and in him is no darkness at all' (1 John 1:5). God's people dwell in light even while surrounded by a world in darkness. This distinction is not earned but given — it is grace, not merit, that places Israel in the light. The physical separation foreshadows the spiritual separation of the redeemed from the unredeemed.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh called unto Moses, and said, Go ye, serve the LORD; only let your flocks and your herds be stayed: let your little ones also go with you.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמֶר לְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה רַק צֹאנְכֶם וּבְקַרְכֶם יֻצָּג גַּם־טַפְּכֶם יֵלֵךְ עִמָּכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Far'oh el-Mosheh vayomer lekhu ivdu et-YHVH rak tzonekhem uvekarkhem yutzag gam-tappekhem yelekh immakhem.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh's second compromise: he now concedes what he previously denied — the children can go (cf. v.11). But he adds a new condition: 'let your flocks and herds be stayed' (yutzag — be stationed, be left behind). This is a significant concession from his previous position but still falls short of God's demand. Pharaoh's compromises are always partial — each time he gives a little more but never gives all. The livestock represents economic capital; retaining it keeps Israel dependent on Egypt for their livelihood. Satan's strategy mirrors Pharaoh's: he bargains for whatever he can retain.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, Thou must give us also sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice unto the LORD our God.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה גַּם־אַתָּה תִּתֵּן בְּיָדֵנוּ זְבָחִים וְעֹלוֹת וְעָשִׂינוּ לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh gam-attah titten beyadenu zevachim ve'olot ve'asinu laYHVH Elohenu.
verses.study_note: Moses not only rejects the compromise — he escalates the demand. 'Thou must give us also' (gam-attah titten) — not only will Israel take all their animals, but Pharaoh himself must contribute sacrifices! 'Sacrifices' (zevachim — fellowship/peace offerings) and 'burnt offerings' (olot — whole burnt offerings) represent the two main categories of Levitical sacrifice. Moses demands that the oppressor supply the very offerings that will celebrate Israel's liberation. This is a stunning reversal of power dynamics.

verses.kjv_text: Our cattle also shall go with us; there shall not an hoof be left behind; for thereof must we take to serve the LORD our God; and we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither.
verses.original_text: וְגַם־מִקְנֵנוּ יֵלֵךְ עִמָּנוּ לֹא תִשָּׁאֵר פַּרְסָה כִּי מִמֶּנּוּ נִקַּח לַעֲבֹד אֶת־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ וַאֲנַחְנוּ לֹא־נֵדַע מַה־נַּעֲבֹד אֶת־יְהוָה עַד־בֹּאֵנוּ שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam-miknennu yelekh immanu lo tisha'er parsah ki mimmennu nikkach la'avod et-YHVH Elohenu va'anachnu lo-neda mah-na'avod et-YHVH ad-bo'enu shammah.
verses.study_note: Moses' most absolute statement: 'there shall not an hoof be left behind' (lo tisha'er parsah — literally 'not a hoof shall remain'). This is total, unconditional departure — zero compromise. His reasoning reveals a profound theological principle: 'we know not with what we must serve the LORD, until we come thither.' God's demands may exceed our current understanding; therefore, we must bring everything and withhold nothing. We cannot predetermine how much God will require. True obedience brings everything to God and lets Him decide what He needs.
verses.doctrinal_note: Moses' principle — 'we know not with what we must serve the LORD' — teaches that obedience must be open-ended and total. We do not negotiate with God over what we will surrender and what we will retain. Paul echoes this: 'I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice' (Rom 12:1). Complete consecration means holding nothing back and nothing in reserve.

verses.kjv_text: But the LORD hardened Pharaoh’s heart, and he would not let them go.
verses.original_text: וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא אָבָה לְשַׁלְּחָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayechazzek YHVH et-lev Par'oh velo avah leshallecham.
verses.study_note: Again the formula: 'the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart.' But this time a new word appears: 'he would not' (lo avah) — from 'avah,' to be willing, to consent. This combines divine hardening with personal unwillingness. Pharaoh is both judicially hardened by God and personally unwilling. The two realities coexist without contradiction — God's sovereignty and Pharaoh's responsibility are both affirmed in the same verse.

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh said unto him, Get thee from me, take heed to thyself, see my face no more; for in that day thou seest my face thou shalt die.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר־לוֹ פַרְעֹה לֵךְ מֵעָלָי הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ אַל־תֹּסֶף רְאוֹת פָּנַי כִּי בְּיוֹם רְאֹתְךָ פָנַי תָּמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer-lo Par'oh lekh me'alai hishamer lekha al-tosef re'ot panai ki beyom re'otekha fanai tamut.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh erupts in fury: 'Get thee from me... see my face no more... thou shalt die!' Three escalating commands: (1) leave my presence, (2) never return, (3) death upon sight. This is Pharaoh's ultimate display of royal power — the threat of execution. Yet the irony is devastating: the man who sits in darkness (v.22), who could not even see his own servants for three days, now threatens Moses with 'see my face no more.' Pharaoh wields the language of power while possessing none. His death threat is hollow — it is Pharaoh's firstborn, not Moses, who will die (ch.12).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, Thou hast spoken well, I will see thy face again no more.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה כֵּן דִּבַּרְתָּ לֹא־אֹסִף עוֹד רְאוֹת פָּנֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh ken dibbarta lo-osif od re'ot panekha.
verses.study_note: Moses' reply is breathtaking in its calm authority: 'Thou hast spoken well' (ken dibbarta — 'you have spoken correctly/rightly'). Moses agrees — they will not meet again. But the meaning is inverted from Pharaoh's intent: Pharaoh means it as a threat; Moses accepts it as a prophecy. The next time Pharaoh seeks Moses, it will be at midnight, after the death of the firstborn, and he will beg Israel to leave (12:31). Moses needs nothing more from Pharaoh. God's dealings with Egypt's king through the medium of prophetic confrontation are essentially over. What remains is direct divine action — the tenth and final plague.
verses.doctrinal_note: The confrontation between Moses and Pharaoh reaches its climax and end. Moses represents the prophet who speaks God's word without fear, even before the most powerful ruler on earth. This pattern continues through Elijah before Ahab, Nathan before David, Jeremiah before Zedekiah, John the Baptist before Herod, and ultimately Christ before Pilate. The prophet's authority exceeds that of the king because the prophet speaks for the King of kings.

meta.overview: Exodus 10 brings the plague narrative to its penultimate climax with the eighth and ninth plagues — Locusts and Darkness. The chapter opens with a profound theological statement: God has hardened Pharaoh's heart specifically so that He might display His signs and that Israel would recount them to future generations. The locust plague devastates what the hail left standing, stripping Egypt bare. For the first time, Pharaoh's own servants break ranks and plead with him to release Israel before Egypt is completely destroyed. Pharaoh offers partial compromises — first allowing only the men to go, then later the people but not the livestock — but Moses demands total obedience: every person, every animal, every possession. The ninth plague of thick, tangible darkness strikes at the heart of Egyptian religion — a direct assault on Ra, the supreme sun god. For three days, impenetrable darkness paralyzes Egypt while Israel enjoys light in Goshen. The chapter ends with a furious Pharaoh threatening Moses with death if he appears again. Each plague systematically dismantles the Egyptian pantheon, proving YHVH's absolute sovereignty over all creation and all false gods.
meta.theme: The Dismantling of Egypt — Locusts, Darkness & the Sovereignty of YHVH
meta.keyWordMeaning: Darkness — thick, palpable darkness that could be felt. The ninth plague strikes at Ra, Egypt's supreme deity, demonstrating YHVH's power over the sun itself.
outline: God's Purpose in Hardening Pharaoh (vv.1-2): Signs for future generations
The Eighth Plague Announced — Locusts (vv.3-6): Moses warns of unprecedented devastation
Pharaoh's Servants Break Ranks (v.7): 'How long shall this man be a snare?'
Pharaoh's First Compromise — Men Only (vv.8-11): Partial obedience rejected
The Locusts Devour Egypt (vv.12-15): Everything the hail left is consumed
Pharaoh's Hasty Repentance (vv.16-20): Confession without transformation
The Ninth Plague — Darkness Over Egypt (vv.21-23): Three days of tangible darkness while Israel has light
Pharaoh's Second Compromise — Leave the Livestock (vv.24-26): Moses demands total departure
The Final Break (vv.27-29): Pharaoh threatens death; Moses agrees they will meet no more

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Yet will I bring one plague more upon Pharaoh, and upon Egypt; afterwards he will let you go hence: when he shall let you go, he shall surely thrust you out hence altogether.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה עוֹד נֶגַע אֶחָד אָבִיא עַל־פַּרְעֹה וְעַל־מִצְרַיִם אַחֲרֵי־כֵן יְשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה כְּשַׁלְּחוֹ כָּלָה גָּרֵשׁ יְגָרֵשׁ אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh od nega echad avi al-Par'oh ve'al-Mitzrayim acharei-khen yeshallach etkhem mizzeh keshallecho kalah garesh yegaresh etkhem mizzeh.
verses.study_note: God reveals the endgame to Moses: 'one plague more' (nega echad) — literally 'one more stroke.' The word 'nega' (plague/stroke) is used rather than the general 'makkah,' emphasizing the percussive, striking nature of this final blow. The emphatic infinitive 'garesh yegaresh' ('surely thrust out') means Pharaoh will not merely permit Israel to leave — he will forcefully expel them, driving them out in haste. The verb 'garash' (to drive out) is the same word used for the expulsion from Eden (Gen 3:24). What began with Pharaoh refusing to 'send away' (shalach) Israel ends with Pharaoh violently expelling them — a complete reversal of power.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's sovereignty over the timing and means of deliverance: the number of plagues is not random. Nine plagues dismantled Egypt's theological and economic infrastructure; the tenth targets the heart of succession and inheritance. God controls not only the event but Pharaoh's response to it — 'he shall surely thrust you out.'

verses.kjv_text: Speak now in the ears of the people, and let every man borrow of his neighbour, and every woman of her neighbour, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold.
verses.original_text: דַּבֶּר־נָא בְּאָזְנֵי הָעָם וְיִשְׁאֲלוּ אִישׁ מֵאֵת רֵעֵהוּ וְאִשָּׁה מֵאֵת רְעוּתָהּ כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber-na be'oznei ha'am veyish'alu ish me'et re'ehu ve'ishah me'et re'utah kelei-khesef ukhelei zahav.
verses.study_note: The word 'borrow' (KJV) is misleading — 'yish'alu' means 'ask, request, demand.' This is not borrowing but claiming. Israel is receiving back-wages for generations of slave labor. God promised this to Abraham in Genesis 15:14: 'afterward shall they come out with great substance.' The 'kelei' (vessels/articles) of silver and gold will later be used in constructing the Tabernacle (Exodus 25:3, 35:5) — Egypt's wealth is repurposed for God's dwelling. The instruction to both 'ish' (each man) and 'ishah' (each woman) emphasizes the comprehensive scope: every household participates in the plundering.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's justice includes restitution. Israel's 430 years of unpaid labor (Exod 12:40) is compensated through Egyptian wealth. This is not theft but divinely commanded reparation — the wages of slavery collected at the point of liberation. What Egypt accumulated through injustice, God redistributes through justice.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. Moreover the man Moses was very great in the land of Egypt, in the sight of Pharaoh’s servants, and in the sight of the people.
verses.original_text: וַיִּתֵּן יְהוָה אֶת־חֵן הָעָם בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרָיִם גַּם הָאִישׁ מֹשֶׁה גָּדוֹל מְאֹד בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּעֵינֵי עַבְדֵי־פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitten YHVH et-chen ha'am be'einei Mitzrayim gam ha'ish Mosheh gadol me'od be'eretz Mitzrayim be'einei avdei-Far'oh uve'einei ha'am.
verses.study_note: God supernaturally gives Israel 'favour' (chen — grace, attractiveness) in Egypt's eyes. The Egyptians do not give grudgingly but willingly — God has changed their disposition. Moses himself is described as 'very great' (gadol me'od) — the same superlative used of creation in Genesis 1:31 (tov me'od). The one whom Pharaoh despised now commands awe from Pharaoh's own servants. The phrase 'be'einei' (in the eyes of) appears three times — Pharaoh's servants, the Egyptian populace, and by implication, the reader: everyone now sees Moses' stature except Pharaoh himself, whose eyes are blinded by hardness.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, Thus saith the LORD, About midnight will I go out into the midst of Egypt:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה כַּחֲצֹת הַלַּיְלָה אֲנִי יוֹצֵא בְּתוֹךְ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh koh amar YHVH kachatzot hallaylah ani yotze betokh Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Moses delivers the climactic oracle. 'Thus saith the LORD' (koh amar YHVH) — the prophetic messenger formula establishing that what follows is not Moses' word but God's own speech. 'About midnight' (kachatzot hallaylah) — the darkest, most vulnerable hour. The prepositional 'ka-' (about/around) indicates precision with divine flexibility. 'I go out' (ani yotze) — God Himself, not an angel or a mediator, will walk through Egypt. This is personal divine action. The first person 'ani' (I) is emphatic — God stakes His own identity on this act. The same God who 'went out' in creation now 'goes out' in judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: God acts personally in both salvation and judgment. 'I will go out' (ani yotze) — this is not delegated. Just as God Himself walked in Eden (Gen 3:8), He now walks through Egypt at midnight. This personal involvement is fulfilled in Christ, who came 'in the fulness of time' (Gal 4:4) to execute both judgment on sin and deliverance for His people — in His own person, at the cost of His own life.

verses.kjv_text: And all the firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the first born of Pharaoh that sitteth upon his throne, even unto the firstborn of the maidservant that is behind the mill; and all the firstborn of beasts.
verses.original_text: וּמֵת כָּל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכוֹר פַּרְעֹה הַיֹּשֵׁב עַל־כִּסְאוֹ עַד בְּכוֹר הַשִּׁפְחָה אֲשֶׁר אַחַר הָרֵחָיִם וְכֹל בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Umet kol-bekhor be'eretz Mitzrayim mibekhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashifchah asher achar harechayim vekhol bekhor behemah.
verses.study_note: The scope of the tenth plague is total: from the apex of society (Pharaoh's heir on the throne) to the absolute bottom (the slave girl behind the grinding mill) and extending even to the animal kingdom. The word 'bekhor' (firstborn) occurs three times, hammering the theme. In the ancient Near East, the firstborn was the heir, the family's future, the guarantor of legacy. To lose the firstborn was to lose the future itself. The 'shifchah achar harechayim' (maidservant behind the mill) represents the lowest social position — even there, no household is exempt. God's judgment recognizes no class privilege. Egypt's entire socioeconomic structure, from palace to mill to pasture, is stripped of its future in a single night.
verses.doctrinal_note: The firstborn theology: Israel is God's 'firstborn son' (Exod 4:22). Egypt enslaved God's firstborn; God strikes Egypt's firstborn in just retaliation. This 'measure for measure' (middah keneged middah) judgment reveals that God takes the oppression of His people as a personal offense against His own family. Christ, the 'firstborn over all creation' (Col 1:15) and 'firstborn from the dead' (Col 1:18), becomes the ultimate Firstborn who dies so that God's people might live — the reversal of this very plague.

verses.kjv_text: And there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more.
verses.original_text: וְהָיְתָה צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹהוּ לֹא נִהְיָתָה וְכָמֹהוּ לֹא תֹסִף׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayetah tze'akah gedolah bekhol-eretz Mitzrayim asher kamohu lo nihyatah vekhamohu lo tosif.
verses.study_note: A 'great cry' (tze'akah gedolah) — the word 'tze'akah' is the same root as the cry of the oppressed Israelites that rose to God in Exodus 2:23 and 3:7. There is profound poetic justice here: the cry of the oppressed summoned God's intervention; now the cry of the oppressor responds to God's judgment. What Israel cried under the lash, Egypt will cry under the plague. The intensifier — 'such as there was none like it, nor shall be like it any more' — places this cry in a category of unique, unrepeatable grief. The only other event given this 'none like it' designation is the plague of locusts (Exod 10:14) and the reign of Solomon (1 Kings 3:12). This is superlative, epoch-defining anguish.

verses.kjv_text: But against any of the children of Israel shall not a dog move his tongue, against man or beast: that ye may know how that the LORD doth put a difference between the Egyptians and Israel.
verses.original_text: וּלְכֹל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא יֶחֱרַץ־כֶּלֶב לְשֹׁנוֹ לְמֵאִישׁ וְעַד־בְּהֵמָה לְמַעַן תֵּדְעוּן אֲשֶׁר יַפְלֶה יְהוָה בֵּין מִצְרַיִם וּבֵין יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ulekhol benei Yisra'el lo yecheratz-kelev leshono leme'ish ve'ad-behemah lema'an ted'un asher yafleh YHVH bein Mitzrayim uvein Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The most extraordinary verse in the chapter. While Egypt screams, in Israel's camp even a dog will not 'sharpen its tongue' (yecheratz-kelev leshono) — a vivid idiom for growling or barking aggressively. Complete, supernatural silence surrounds God's people while deafening grief engulfs Egypt. The verb 'yafleh' (to put a difference, to make distinct, to do wondrously) is from the root 'pala' — the same root as 'wonderful' and 'miracle' (pele). The distinction between Israel and Egypt is itself miraculous. This is not geographic separation — Israelites and Egyptians lived in proximity — but divine, supernatural discrimination. The purpose clause 'that ye may know' (lema'an ted'un) reveals that the distinction is pedagogical: it teaches that God actively distinguishes between His covenant people and the world under judgment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The doctrine of divine distinction (havdalah): God separates and distinguishes His people from the world under judgment. This principle runs through all of Scripture — Noah from the Flood, Israel from Egypt, Rahab from Jericho, the Church from the coming wrath (1 Thess 1:10). The distinction is not earned by Israel's merit but established by God's sovereign 'yafleh' — His miraculous, wonder-working separation. In the New Testament, this distinction is made through the blood of Christ: those under the blood are passed over; those without it face judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And all these thy servants shall come down unto me, and bow down themselves unto me, saying, Get thee out, and all the people that follow thee: and after that I will go out. And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger.
verses.original_text: וְיָרְדוּ כָל־עֲבָדֶיךָ אֵלֶּה אֵלַי וְהִשְׁתַּחֲווּ־לִי לֵאמֹר צֵא אַתָּה וְכָל־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר־בְּרַגְלֶיךָ וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן אֵצֵא וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם־פַּרְעֹה בָּחֳרִי־אָף׃
verses.transliteration: Veyaredu khol-avadekha elleh elai vehishtachavu-li lemor tze attah vekhol-ha'am asher-beraglekha ve'acharei-khen etze vayyetze me'im-Par'oh bachari-af.
verses.study_note: Moses prophesies a dramatic reversal: Pharaoh's own servants will come 'down' (yaredu — descend from their position) and 'bow down' (hishtachavu) to Moses — the very posture of submission that Pharaoh had demanded from Israel. The Hebrew 'asher-beraglekha' ('who are at your feet/who follow you') is the idiom for loyal followers. Moses carefully says 'thy servants' rather than addressing Pharaoh directly — a calculated diplomatic insult that spares Pharaoh's person while prophesying the humiliation of his court. 'And he went out from Pharaoh in a great anger' (bachari-af — literally 'in the burning of nostrils/wrath') — this is one of the only times Moses shows visible, holy anger. Moses departs with the finality of a prophet who has delivered his last oracle. He will not return to Pharaoh; Pharaoh will come to him.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Pharaoh shall not hearken unto you; that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֹא־יִשְׁמַע אֲלֵיכֶם פַּרְעֹה לְמַעַן רְבוֹת מוֹפְתַי בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh lo-yishma aleikhem Par'oh lema'an revot mofetai be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: God provides the theological rationale for Pharaoh's sustained resistance: it is 'lema'an' (in order that) — a divine purpose clause. Pharaoh's refusal is not thwarting God's plan but fulfilling it. Each refusal provides the occasion for another 'mofet' (wonder/sign) — multiplying (revot) the evidence of God's supremacy. The verb 'yishma' (to hear/listen) carries the connotation of obedient response: Pharaoh does not merely fail to hear but refuses to respond obediently. This verse is the hermeneutical key to the hardening narrative: God's sovereignty operates through, not against, Pharaoh's own culpable choices.
verses.doctrinal_note: Divine sovereignty and human responsibility intersect in the hardening of Pharaoh. God's purpose ('that my wonders may be multiplied') operates through Pharaoh's own willful refusal ('shall not hearken'). Paul explicitly cites this dynamic in Romans 9:17: 'For the Scripture says to Pharaoh, 'For this very purpose I have raised you up, that I might show my power in you.' God does not create evil in Pharaoh; He uses Pharaoh's own stubborn rebellion to display His glory and deliver His people.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron did all these wonders before Pharaoh: and the LORD hardened Pharaoh’s heart, so that he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land.
verses.original_text: וּמֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן עָשׂוּ אֶת־כָּל־הַמֹּפְתִים הָאֵלֶּה לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא־שִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאַרְצוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: UMosheh ve'Aharon asu et-kol-hammoftim ha'elleh lifnei Far'oh vaychazzek YHVH et-lev Par'oh velo-shillach et-benei-Yisra'el me'artzo.
verses.study_note: The chapter's closing verse is a summary statement encompassing the entire plague narrative. 'Moses and Aaron did all these wonders' — the 'all' (kol) is comprehensive, covering all nine plagues preceding the tenth. 'The LORD hardened' (vaychazzek YHVH) uses the Piel stem of 'chazak' (to strengthen, to make firm) — God strengthened Pharaoh's own resolve. The progression throughout Exodus is instructive: in the early plagues, Pharaoh hardens his own heart (using 'kaved' — to make heavy, Exod 8:15, 8:32, 9:34); later, God hardens it (using 'chazak' — to make strong, Exod 9:12, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10). God confirms what Pharaoh chose. The final clause — 'he would not let the children of Israel go out of his land' — uses 'shillach' (to send away), the verb that has echoed since Exodus 5:1. The contest between 'Let my people go' (shallach) and Pharaoh's refusal to 'shallach' reaches its climax. What Pharaoh refuses to do willingly, God will force him to do violently (v.1, garesh yegaresh).
verses.doctrinal_note: The hardening of Pharaoh teaches that God's purposes cannot be frustrated by human resistance. Pharaoh's obstinacy does not delay God's plan — it is incorporated into it. The three Hebrew verbs for hardening tell the full story: 'kaved' (to make heavy — Pharaoh's own choice), 'chazak' (to make strong — God's confirmation), and 'qashah' (to make stiff — the settled condition). This pattern serves as a solemn warning: persistent rebellion against clear revelation leads to a point where God confirms the rebel in their chosen path (cf. Romans 1:24,26,28 — 'God gave them over').

meta.overview: Exodus 11 is the hinge of the entire Exodus narrative — the announcement of the tenth and final plague, the death of every firstborn in Egypt. After nine plagues that systematically dismantled Egypt's gods and Pharaoh's pretensions, God now reveals the ultimate stroke of judgment. The chapter opens with God informing Moses that one more plague will break Pharaoh's resistance. Israel is instructed to ask the Egyptians for silver and gold — the plundering of Egypt prophesied to Abraham (Gen 15:14). Moses delivers a scorching oracle to Pharaoh: at midnight, every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from Pharaoh's throne to the slave's mill to the cattle in the field. But against Israel, not even a dog shall sharpen its tongue — a supernatural distinction between God's people and the world under judgment. The chapter closes with the narrator's confirmation that Pharaoh's heart was hardened, fulfilling God's declared purpose. This announcement sets the stage for the Passover (ch.12), the central redemptive event of the Old Testament and the supreme type of Christ's atoning death.
meta.theme: The Final Plague Announced — Judgment, Distinction & the Coming Redeemer
meta.keyWordMeaning: Firstborn — the preeminent one, the heir, the one who carries the family's future. God strikes the firstborn of Egypt to redeem His own firstborn, Israel (4:22).
outline: God's Final Promise to Moses (vv.1-3): One more plague; Israel to plunder Egypt
The Oracle of Midnight (vv.4-6): Moses announces the death of every firstborn
The Great Distinction (v.7): Not a dog shall move its tongue against Israel
Pharaoh's Refusal & God's Purpose (vv.8-10): Moses departs in anger; hardened hearts multiply God's wonders

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron in the land of Egypt, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh ve'el-Aharon be'eretz Mitzrayim lemor.
verses.study_note: God speaks to both Moses and Aaron together — a dual address reflecting their complementary roles: Moses as prophet-lawgiver, Aaron as priestly mediator. The location 'in the land of Egypt' is significant — God gives Israel's foundational redemptive ordinance while they are still in bondage. Grace precedes deliverance; the instruction for salvation comes before the act of salvation. This is revelation in the midst of oppression.
verses.doctrinal_note: God reveals the means of salvation before executing it. Israel must act in faith on God's Word before they experience deliverance — the pattern for all salvation: faith precedes sight (Heb 11:1). The instructions come in Egypt, not after the Exodus — salvation is by grace through faith, not by works already accomplished.

verses.kjv_text: This month shall be unto you the beginning of months: it shall be the first month of the year to you.
verses.original_text: הַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה לָכֶם רֹאשׁ חֳדָשִׁים רִאשׁוֹן הוּא לָכֶם לְחָדְשֵׁי הַשָּׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Hachodesh hazzeh lakhem rosh chodashim rishon hu lakhem lechodeshei hashanah.
verses.study_note: God resets Israel's calendar. The month of Nisan (Abib) — the month of redemption — becomes month one. Before this, Israel's year began in Tishrei (autumn). Now their national identity begins with deliverance, not creation. Redemption becomes the starting point of all reckoning. The word 'chodesh' (month) comes from 'chadash' (new) — everything starts fresh. This is the Old Testament equivalent of 'if any man be in Christ, he is a new creation' (2 Cor 5:17).
verses.doctrinal_note: Redemption resets the clock. When God saves, He doesn't merely improve the old — He inaugurates the new. Israel's calendar, identity, and self-understanding now begin with the Passover. In the New Testament, believers reckon time from the cross. The redeemed life starts at the moment of salvation — all things become new (2 Cor 5:17).

verses.kjv_text: Speak ye unto all the congregation of Israel, saying, In the tenth day of this month they shall take to them every man a lamb, according to the house of their fathers, a lamb for an house:
verses.original_text: דַּבְּרוּ אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר בֶּעָשֹׂר לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה וְיִקְחוּ לָהֶם אִישׁ שֶׂה לְבֵית־אָבֹת שֶׂה לַבָּיִת׃
verses.transliteration: Dabberu el-kol-adat Yisra'el lemor be'asor lachodesh hazzeh veyikchu lahem ish seh levet-avot seh labbayit.
verses.study_note: On the 10th of Nisan, each household selects a lamb. The 'seh' (lamb/kid) is chosen four days before slaughter — during which the family lives with it, bonds with it, and can verify it has no blemish. This four-day inspection period is profoundly typological: Jesus entered Jerusalem on the 10th of Nisan (Palm Sunday), was examined and questioned for four days by Pharisees, Sadducees, and Pilate, and was found 'without fault' (John 18:38) before being crucified on the 14th. The lamb is 'for a house' — redemption has a household dimension.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Passover lamb is the clearest Old Testament type of Christ. Selected on the 10th, examined for four days, slaughtered on the 14th — precisely mirroring Jesus' final week. The household principle ('a lamb for a house') shows that salvation, while personal, has communal dimensions (cf. Acts 16:31, 'thou and thy house').

verses.kjv_text: And if the household be too little for the lamb, let him and his neighbour next unto his house take it according to the number of the souls; every man according to his eating shall make your count for the lamb.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־יִמְעַט הַבַּיִת מִהְיוֹת מִשֶּׂה וְלָקַח הוּא וּשְׁכֵנוֹ הַקָּרֹב אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ בְּמִכְסַת נְפָשֹׁת אִישׁ לְפִי אָכְלוֹ תָּכֹסּוּ עַל־הַשֶּׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-yim'at habbayit miheyot misseh velakach hu ushkheno hakkarov el-beto bemikhsat nefashot ish lefi okhlo takhosu al-hasseh.
verses.study_note: If a household is too small to consume an entire lamb, they join with a neighbor. The lamb must be fully consumed — nothing wasted. The 'number of souls' (mikhsat nefashot) determines the sharing. Later rabbinic tradition set the minimum at ten persons per lamb. This provision ensures that the Passover lamb is sufficient for all who partake — a picture of the sufficiency of Christ's sacrifice. The lamb is never too much; the household might be too small.

verses.kjv_text: Your lamb shall be without blemish, a male of the first year: ye shall take it out from the sheep, or from the goats:
verses.original_text: שֶׂה תָמִים זָכָר בֶּן־שָׁנָה יִהְיֶה לָכֶם מִן־הַכְּבָשִׂים וּמִן־הָעִזִּים תִּקָּחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Seh tamim zakhar ben-shanah yihyeh lakhem min-hakkvasim umin-ha'izzim tikkachu.
verses.study_note: Three qualifications for the lamb: (1) 'tamim' — without blemish, perfect, complete, whole; (2) 'zakhar' — a male; (3) 'ben-shanah' — a year old, in its prime. 'Tamim' is the same word used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and later required of all sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3,10). The lamb could be from sheep (kevasim) or goats (izzim). Peter directly applies this: 'a lamb without blemish and without spot' (1 Pet 1:19). Jesus was examined by every authority — religious and civil — and declared faultless.
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement of 'tamim' (without blemish) establishes the principle that only perfection can atone. No defective sacrifice is acceptable because the substitute must be what the sinner is not — perfect. Christ alone fulfills this requirement: 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners' (Heb 7:26). The blemishless lamb is the doctrinal foundation of substitutionary atonement.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall keep it up until the fourteenth day of the same month: and the whole assembly of the congregation of Israel shall kill it in the evening.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה לָכֶם לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת עַד אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה וְשָׁחֲטוּ אֹתוֹ כֹּל קְהַל עֲדַת־יִשְׂרָאֵל בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah lakhem lemishmeret ad arba'ah asar yom lachodesh hazzeh veshachatu oto kol kehal adat-Yisra'el bein ha'arbayim.
verses.study_note: The lamb is 'kept' (mishmeret — guarded, watched over) for four days, then slaughtered on the 14th 'between the evenings' (bein ha'arbayim) — traditionally understood as between 3:00 PM and sundown. The 'whole assembly' kills it — corporate responsibility. Every household participates simultaneously. Jesus died at the ninth hour (3:00 PM, Mark 15:34-37) — precisely when the Passover lambs were being slaughtered in the Temple. The timing is not coincidental but prophetic fulfillment of staggering precision.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'bein ha'arbayim' (between the evenings) fixes the time of slaughter at twilight — the same hour Jesus died on the cross. The entire nation kills the lamb corporately, just as 'all we like sheep have gone astray' and the LORD laid on Christ 'the iniquity of us all' (Isa 53:6). The corporate nature of both the Passover sacrifice and the crucifixion is essential to their meaning.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall take of the blood, and strike it on the two side posts and on the upper door post of the houses, wherein they shall eat it.
verses.original_text: וְלָקְחוּ מִן־הַדָּם וְנָתְנוּ עַל־שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת וְעַל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף עַל הַבָּתִּים אֲשֶׁר־יֹאכְלוּ אֹתוֹ בָּהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Velakechu min-haddam venatenu al-shetei hammezuzot ve'al-hammashkof al habbattim asher-yokhelu oto bahem.
verses.study_note: The blood is applied to three points of the doorframe: two side posts (mezuzot) and the upper lintel (mashkof). The blood is never placed on the threshold — it is never trampled underfoot (cf. Heb 10:29). The pattern of application — sides and top — forms the shape of a cross. The blood on the outside marks the house for the LORD's protection; the lamb is consumed on the inside for nourishment. Blood provides protection from judgment; the lamb's flesh provides sustenance for the journey.
verses.doctrinal_note: The blood on the doorposts is the defining image of substitutionary atonement in the Old Testament. The lamb dies so the household lives. The blood is visible — faith must be publicly applied, not merely internally held. God does not say 'when I see your good works' or 'when I see your sincerity' but 'when I see the blood, I will pass over you' (v.13). Salvation rests entirely on the shed blood of the substitute.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall eat the flesh in that night, roast with fire, and unleavened bread; and with bitter herbs they shall eat it.
verses.original_text: וְאָכְלוּ אֶת־הַבָּשָׂר בַּלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה צְלִי־אֵשׁ וּמַצּוֹת עַל־מְרֹרִים יֹאכְלֻהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'akhelu et-habbasar ballaylah hazzeh tzeli-esh umatzot al-merorim yokhluhu.
verses.study_note: Three elements of the Passover meal: (1) 'tzeli-esh' — roasted with fire, not boiled (the fire of judgment passes through the lamb); (2) 'matzot' — unleavened bread (bread of haste and sincerity, leaven representing sin); (3) 'merorim' — bitter herbs (remembrance of the bitterness of slavery). The lamb must be consumed, not merely admired — it must be personally appropriated. Jesus said, 'Except ye eat the flesh of the Son of man, and drink his blood, ye have no life in you' (John 6:53).

verses.kjv_text: Eat not of it raw, nor sodden at all with water, but roast with fire; his head with his legs, and with the purtenance thereof.
verses.original_text: אַל־תֹּאכְלוּ מִמֶּנּוּ נָא וּבָשֵׁל מְבֻשָּׁל בַּמַּיִם כִּי אִם־צְלִי־אֵשׁ רֹאשׁוֹ עַל־כְּרָעָיו וְעַל־קִרְבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Al-tokhelu mimmennu na uvashal mevushal bammayim ki im-tzeli-esh rosho al-kera'av ve'al-kirbo.
verses.study_note: Not raw (na — underdone, half-cooked), not boiled (bashal mevushal — thoroughly sodden), but roasted whole with fire. The lamb is roasted complete — head, legs, and inner parts (kirbo — entrails). Nothing is dismembered before roasting. This wholeness prefigures Christ's complete sacrifice: His entire person — mind (head), walk (legs), and inner being (entrails) — was offered to God. The insistence on fire (not water) pictures the judgment of God that the substitute endures.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall let nothing of it remain until the morning; and that which remaineth of it until the morning ye shall burn with fire.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־תוֹתִירוּ מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר וְהַנֹּתָר מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר בָּאֵשׁ תִּשְׂרֹפוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-totiru mimmennu ad-boker vehannotar mimmennu ad-boker ba'esh tisrofu.
verses.study_note: Nothing of the lamb may remain by morning — what is not eaten must be burned. This prevents any desecration of the sacred sacrifice and pictures the completeness of Christ's atoning work. There are no 'leftovers' from the cross — the sacrifice is complete, sufficient, and final. The same principle applies to the manna (Exod 16:19) — God's provision must be received in the appointed time.

verses.kjv_text: And thus shall ye eat it; with your loins girded, your shoes on your feet, and your staff in your hand; and ye shall eat it in haste: it is the LORD’s passover.
verses.original_text: וְכָכָה תֹּאכְלוּ אֹתוֹ מָתְנֵיכֶם חֲגֻרִים נַעֲלֵיכֶם בְּרַגְלֵיכֶם וּמַקֶּלְכֶם בְּיֶדְכֶם וַאֲכַלְתֶּם אֹתוֹ בְּחִפָּזוֹן פֶּסַח הוּא לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhakhah tokhelu oto motnekhem chagurim na'aleikhem beragleikhem umakkelkhem beyedkhem va'akhaltem oto bechipazon pesach hu laYHVH.
verses.study_note: The posture of eating reveals the meaning: loins girded (ready for action), sandals on (ready to march), staff in hand (ready to travel), eaten 'in haste' (bechipazon — with urgency). This is not a leisurely banquet but a warrior's meal, a pilgrim's provision. The redeemed are not settling in — they are departing. Then the defining declaration: 'pesach hu laYHVH' — 'it is the LORD's Passover.' The Passover belongs to God; He institutes it, defines it, and fulfills it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The manner of eating teaches that redemption demands readiness and response. Salvation is not passive — the redeemed must be prepared to leave the old life immediately. Girded loins, shod feet, and a staff speak of pilgrim identity: we are 'strangers and pilgrims on the earth' (Heb 11:13). The haste pictures the urgency of the gospel — 'behold, now is the day of salvation' (2 Cor 6:2).

verses.kjv_text: For I will pass through the land of Egypt this night, and will smite all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both man and beast; and against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgment: I am the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְעָבַרְתִּי בְאֶרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם בַּלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה וְהִכֵּיתִי כָל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מֵאָדָם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָה וּבְכָל־אֱלֹהֵי מִצְרַיִם אֶעֱשֶׂה שְׁפָטִים אֲנִי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'avarti ve'eretz-Mitzrayim ballaylah hazzeh vehikkeiti khol-bekhor be'eretz Mitzrayim me'adam ve'ad-behemah uvkhol-elohei Mitzrayim e'eseh shefatim ani YHVH.
verses.study_note: God Himself passes through — 'I will pass through' (ve'avarti). This is not delegated; God personally executes the final plague. Three actions: (1) smite every firstborn — human and animal; (2) execute judgment against all the gods of Egypt — each plague targeted an Egyptian deity, and this final blow strikes at the concept of divine kingship itself (Pharaoh's firstborn was considered semi-divine); (3) the self-declaration 'I am the LORD' (ani YHVH) — the covenant name that settles all authority. The Hebrew 'shefatim' (judgments) is plural — multiple sentences executed in one night.
verses.doctrinal_note: The tenth plague is simultaneously judgment on Egypt and salvation for Israel — the same night, the same event, two opposite outcomes determined solely by the blood. This is the biblical pattern: the cross is both judgment (on sin) and salvation (for the believer). 'Against all the gods of Egypt' reveals that the plagues are theological warfare — YHVH systematically dismantles every false deity. The final declaration 'I am the LORD' is the ultimate statement of divine sovereignty over all competing powers.

verses.kjv_text: And the blood shall be to you for a token upon the houses where ye are: and when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and the plague shall not be upon you to destroy you, when I smite the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה הַדָּם לָכֶם לְאֹת עַל הַבָּתִּים אֲשֶׁר אַתֶּם שָׁם וְרָאִיתִי אֶת־הַדָּם וּפָסַחְתִּי עֲלֵכֶם וְלֹא־יִהְיֶה בָכֶם נֶגֶף לְמַשְׁחִית בְּהַכֹּתִי בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah haddam lakhem le'ot al habbattim asher attem sham vera'iti et-haddam ufasachti aleikhem velo-yihyeh vakhem negef lemashchit behakkoti be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The central verse of the chapter and arguably of the entire Pentateuch. 'When I see the blood, I will pass over you' (vera'iti et-haddam ufasachti aleikhem). The blood is a 'token' (ot — sign, distinguishing mark). God does not look at the occupants' merit but at the blood. The verb 'pasachti' (I will pass over) gives the feast its name. 'Negef' (plague/blow) and 'mashchit' (the destroyer) are held back by the blood. Salvation is determined by one thing alone: the blood of the lamb on the doorpost.
verses.doctrinal_note: This is the Gospel in one verse. 'When I see the blood' — not when I see your works, your feelings, your sincerity, or your effort — 'I will pass over you.' Salvation rests entirely on the applied blood of the substitute. The blood must be applied (not merely available); faith must be exercised (not merely understood). This is the bedrock of justification by faith: the righteousness that saves is not in the sinner but in the sacrifice. 'The blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth us from all sin' (1 John 1:7).

verses.kjv_text: And this day shall be unto you for a memorial; and ye shall keep it a feast to the LORD throughout your generations; ye shall keep it a feast by an ordinance for ever.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה לָכֶם לְזִכָּרוֹן וְחַגֹּתֶם אֹתוֹ חַג לַיהוָה לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם תְּחָגֻּהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah hayyom hazzeh lakhem lezikkaron vechagotem oto chag laYHVH ledoroteikhem chukat olam techagghu.
verses.study_note: The Passover becomes a 'memorial' (zikkaron) — not mere historical memory but active, participatory remembrance. 'Throughout your generations' and 'for ever' (chukat olam — a statute of perpetuity). Each generation is to re-experience the Passover, not merely recall it. Jesus transforms this perpetual memorial at the Last Supper: 'This do in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19). The Passover memorial becomes the Lord's Supper — the same principle of remembrance, the same Lamb, now revealed.

verses.kjv_text: Seven days shall ye eat unleavened bread; even the first day ye shall put away leaven out of your houses: for whosoever eateth leavened bread from the first day until the seventh day, that soul shall be cut off from Israel.
verses.original_text: שִׁבְעַת יָמִים מַצּוֹת תֹּאכֵלוּ אַךְ בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן תַּשְׁבִּיתוּ שְּׂאֹר מִבָּתֵּיכֶם כִּי כָּל־אֹכֵל חָמֵץ וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל מִיּוֹם הָרִאשֹׁן עַד־יוֹם הַשְּׁבִעִי׃
verses.transliteration: Shiv'at yamim matzot tokhelu akh bayyom harishon tashbitu se'or mibateikhem ki khol-okhel chametz venikhretah hannefesh hahi miYisra'el miyyom harishon ad-yom hashevi'i.
verses.study_note: The Feast of Unleavened Bread extends seven days beyond Passover. 'Se'or' (leaven/yeast) must be completely removed — 'tashbitu' means to cause to cease, to annihilate. The penalty for eating leaven is being 'cut off' (nikhretah) from Israel — the most severe communal punishment, meaning excommunication or possibly death. Paul interprets this directly: 'Purge out therefore the old leaven... for even Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us' (1 Cor 5:7). After the blood saves (Passover), the leaven must go (sanctification).
verses.doctrinal_note: Passover and Unleavened Bread together picture justification and sanctification: the blood of the lamb saves (justification), and the removal of leaven represents the purging of sin from the redeemed life (sanctification). Seven days — the number of completeness — indicates that sanctification is a comprehensive, ongoing process. The severity of the penalty (being 'cut off') shows that God takes holiness in His redeemed people with utmost seriousness.

verses.kjv_text: And in the first day there shall be an holy convocation, and in the seventh day there shall be an holy convocation to you; no manner of work shall be done in them, save that which every man must eat, that only may be done of you.
verses.original_text: וּבַיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן מִקְרָא־קֹדֶשׁ וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מִקְרָא־קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם כָּל־מְלָאכָה לֹא־יֵעָשֶׂה בָהֶם אַךְ אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל לְכָל־נֶפֶשׁ הוּא לְבַדּוֹ יֵעָשֶׂה לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvayyom harishon mikra-kodesh uvayyom hashevi'i mikra-kodesh yihyeh lakhem kol-melakhah lo-ye'aseh vahem akh asher ye'akhel lekhol-nefesh hu levaddo ye'aseh lakhem.
verses.study_note: The first and seventh days are 'holy convocations' (mikra-kodesh) — sacred assemblies where no work is done except food preparation. The 'mikra' (convocation, from 'kara' — to call) indicates a calling together of God's people for worship. These bookend the week of unleavened bread, framing the entire period as sacred time. The rest from work pictures the rest of faith — salvation is God's work, not ours (Eph 2:8-9).

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall observe the feast of unleavened bread; for in this selfsame day have I brought your armies out of the land of Egypt: therefore shall ye observe this day in your generations by an ordinance for ever.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַמַּצּוֹת כִּי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה הוֹצֵאתִי אֶת־צִבְאוֹתֵיכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ushemartem et-hammatzot ki be'etzem hayyom hazzeh hotzeti et-tziv'oteikhem me'eretz Mitzrayim ushemartem et-hayyom hazzeh ledoroteikhem chukat olam.
verses.study_note: Israel is called God's 'armies' (tziv'oteikhem) — they leave Egypt not as refugees but as a marshaled host. 'In this selfsame day' (be'etzem hayyom hazzeh) — God acts with precision. The verb 'shamar' (observe/keep/guard) is used twice — emphasizing the sacredness of the ordinance. The feast of unleavened bread commemorates not merely the Passover sacrifice but the actual departure — the beginning of the new life.

verses.kjv_text: In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at even, ye shall eat unleavened bread, until the one and twentieth day of the month at even.
verses.original_text: בָּרִאשֹׁן בְּאַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ בָּעֶרֶב תֹּאכְלוּ מַצֹּת עַד יוֹם הָאֶחָד וְעֶשְׂרִים לַחֹדֶשׁ בָּעָרֶב׃
verses.transliteration: Barishon be'arba'ah asar yom lachodesh ba'erev tokhelu matzot ad yom ha'echad ve'esrim lachodesh ba'arev.
verses.study_note: The precise calendar: Nisan 14 evening through Nisan 21 evening — seven full days of unleavened bread. The exactness of the dating underscores that God works in specific times and seasons. The number seven (completeness) indicates that the purging of leaven — the removal of sin from the redeemed community — is a complete, thorough process, not partial or occasional.

verses.kjv_text: Seven days shall there be no leaven found in your houses: for whosoever eateth that which is leavened, even that soul shall be cut off from the congregation of Israel, whether he be a stranger, or born in the land.
verses.original_text: שִׁבְעַת יָמִים שְׂאֹר לֹא יִמָּצֵא בְּבָתֵּיכֶם כִּי כָּל־אֹכֵל מַחְמֶצֶת וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל בַּגֵּר וּבְאֶזְרַח הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Shiv'at yamim se'or lo yimmatze bevateikhem ki khol-okhel machmetzet venikhretah hannefesh hahi me'adat Yisra'el bagger uve'ezrach ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The prohibition extends to 'ger' (stranger/sojourner) and 'ezrach' (native-born) alike — one law for all within the covenant community. Leaven is not merely to be avoided in diet but 'not found' (lo yimmatze) anywhere in the house. The later Jewish practice of 'bedikat chametz' (searching for leaven by candlelight) grows from this command. Paul applies this: 'Know ye not that a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump?' (1 Cor 5:6).

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall eat nothing leavened; in all your habitations shall ye eat unleavened bread.
verses.original_text: כָּל־מַחְמֶצֶת לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם תֹּאכְלוּ מַצּוֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-machmetzet lo tokhelu bekhol moshevoteikhem tokhelu matzot.
verses.study_note: A summary command: the prohibition is total ('kol' — all) and universal ('bekhol moshevoteikhem' — in all your habitations/dwellings). Wherever Israel lives, this applies. The scope shows that holiness is not localized to the tabernacle or temple but extends to every home. The redeemed life is lived without leaven — without the corrupting influence of sin — in every sphere of existence.

verses.kjv_text: Then Moses called for all the elders of Israel, and said unto them, Draw out and take you a lamb according to your families, and kill the passover.
