verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְכָל־זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם מִשְׁכוּ וּקְחוּ לָכֶם צֹאן לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתֵיכֶם וְשַׁחֲטוּ הַפָּסַח׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra Mosheh lekhol-ziknei Yisra'el vayomer alehem mishkhu ukechu lakhem tzon lemishpechoteikhem veshachatu happasach.
verses.study_note: Moses relays God's instructions to the elders (ziqnei — the senior leaders). 'Draw out' (mishkhu) — literally 'pull out' or 'withdraw' — the lamb must be separated from the flock. This selection involves deliberate choice. 'Kill the Passover' (shachatu happasach) — the sacrifice itself is called 'the Passover.' The word has become both the name of the feast and the name of the lamb. Jesus at the Last Supper similarly becomes identified with the meal: 'This is my body' (Luke 22:19).

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall take a bunch of hyssop, and dip it in the blood that is in the bason, and strike the lintel and the two side posts with the blood that is in the bason; and none of you shall go out at the door of his house until the morning.
verses.original_text: וּלְקַחְתֶּם אֲגֻדַּת אֵזוֹב וּטְבַלְתֶּם בַּדָּם אֲשֶׁר־בַּסַּף וְהִגַּעְתֶּם אֶל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף וְאֶל־שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת מִן־הַדָּם אֲשֶׁר בַּסָּף וְאַתֶּם לֹא תֵצְאוּ אִישׁ מִפֶּתַח־בֵּיתוֹ עַד־בֹּקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Ulekachtem aguddat ezov utvaltem baddam asher-bassaf vehiggatem el-hammashkof ve'el-shetei hammezuzot min-haddam asher bassaf ve'attem lo tetze'u ish mippetach-beto ad-boker.
verses.study_note: Hyssop (ezov) — a small, common plant — is used to apply the blood. The lowly hyssop is accessible to everyone; salvation is not through rare or expensive means. The 'bason' (saf — basin or threshold) holds the blood. 'Strike' (higgatem — touch, cause to reach) the lintel and doorposts. David later prays, 'Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean' (Ps 51:7). The command 'none shall go out' until morning means staying under the protection of the blood. Safety is inside, behind the blood — not in the open.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hyssop principle: God uses the common and lowly to apply salvation. The blood is available to all — not through priestly mediation or costly ritual, but through a common plant anyone can gather. This foreshadows the accessibility of the Gospel. The prohibition against going out teaches that the redeemed must remain 'in Christ' — under the blood's protection. To leave the blood-covered house is to leave the place of safety.

verses.kjv_text: For the LORD will pass through to smite the Egyptians; and when he seeth the blood upon the lintel, and on the two side posts, the LORD will pass over the door, and will not suffer the destroyer to come in unto your houses to smite you.
verses.original_text: וְעָבַר יְהוָה לִנְגֹּף אֶת־מִצְרַיִם וְרָאָה אֶת־הַדָּם עַל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף וְעַל שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת וּפָסַח יְהוָה עַל־הַפֶּתַח וְלֹא יִתֵּן הַמַּשְׁחִית לָבֹא אֶל־בָּתֵּיכֶם לִנְגֹּף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'avar YHVH lingof et-Mitzrayim vera'ah et-haddam al-hammashkof ve'al shetei hammezuzot ufasach YHVH al-happetach velo yitten hammashchit lavo el-batteikhem lingof.
verses.study_note: Three distinct actions: (1) the LORD passes through (avar) to strike Egypt; (2) the LORD sees the blood and passes over (pasach) the door; (3) the LORD 'will not suffer' (lo yitten — literally 'will not give/permit') the destroyer (hammashchit) to enter. The 'mashchit' (destroyer) is a distinct agent — whether angel or force of judgment — whom God restrains at the blood-marked door. The same interplay between God's direct action and His use of an agent of destruction appears in 2 Samuel 24:16 and 1 Chronicles 21:15.
verses.doctrinal_note: Two movements of God in one night: 'avar' (pass through — judgment) and 'pasach' (pass over — mercy). The same God executes both. The only difference between judgment and mercy is the blood. This is the foundation of the doctrine of propitiation — the blood of the sacrifice satisfies divine justice and turns away divine wrath from the believer (Rom 3:25). The 'destroyer' represents the just consequences of sin, which the blood absorbs.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall observe this thing for an ordinance to thee and to thy sons for ever.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה לְחָק־לְךָ וּלְבָנֶיךָ עַד־עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ushemartem et-haddavar hazzeh lechok-lekha ulevanekha ad-olam.
verses.study_note: Again, the perpetual ordinance — 'ad-olam' (unto eternity). The Passover is intergenerational; parents must transmit it to children. 'Shamar' (observe, guard, keep) carries the weight of active, vigilant preservation — not passive remembrance. The redeemed community is entrusted with the responsibility of preserving and passing on the story of redemption.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, when ye be come to the land which the LORD will give you, according as he hath promised, that ye shall keep this service.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־תָבֹאוּ אֶל־הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר יִתֵּן יְהוָה לָכֶם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-tavo'u el-ha'aretz asher yitten YHVH lakhem ka'asher dibber ushemartem et-ha'avodah hazzo't.
verses.study_note: Before Israel has even left Egypt, God speaks of 'when' — not 'if' — they arrive in the Promised Land. Faith looks beyond the present crisis to the certain future. The 'service' (avodah — work, worship, service) shows that Passover observance is an act of worship, not mere ritual. The same word 'avodah' describes both Israel's slave labor in Egypt and their worship of God — their 'service' is being transferred from Pharaoh to YHVH.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, when your children shall say unto you, What mean ye by this service?
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־יֹאמְרוּ אֲלֵיכֶם בְּנֵיכֶם מָה הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-yomeru aleikhem beneikhem mah ha'avodah hazzo't lakhem.
verses.study_note: The Passover is designed to provoke children's questions: 'What mean ye by this service?' This question becomes one of the four questions in the later Seder liturgy. The redemption story is perpetuated through the curiosity of the next generation. God builds pedagogy into the ritual itself — the unusual actions (unleavened bread, bitter herbs, lamb blood) are meant to prompt inquiry. Faith is transmitted through question and answer, generation to generation.

verses.kjv_text: That ye shall say, It is the sacrifice of the LORD’s passover, who passed over the houses of the children of Israel in Egypt, when he smote the Egyptians, and delivered our houses. And the people bowed the head and worshipped.
verses.original_text: וַאֲמַרְתֶּם זֶבַח־פֶּסַח הוּא לַיהוָה אֲשֶׁר פָּסַח עַל־בָּתֵּי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּמִצְרַיִם בְּנָגְפּוֹ אֶת־מִצְרַיִם וְאֶת־בָּתֵּינוּ הִצִּיל וַיִּקֹּד הָעָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'amartem zevach-pesach hu laYHVH asher pasach al-battei venei-Yisra'el beMitzrayim benogfo et-Mitzrayim ve'et-batteinu hitzil vayyikkod ha'am vayyishtachavu.
verses.study_note: The answer: 'It is the sacrifice of the LORD's Passover.' The response centers on God's act — 'He passed over... He smote... He delivered.' The people respond with worship: 'bowed the head' (vayyikkod — bowing in submission) and 'worshipped' (vayyishtachavu — prostrated themselves). The proper response to the proclamation of redemption is worship, not merely intellectual assent. 'Hitzil' (delivered/rescued) — God is both judge and savior in the same event.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel went away, and did as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּלְכוּ וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן כֵּן עָשׂוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyelehu vayya'asu benei Yisra'el ka'asher tzivvah YHVH et-Mosheh ve'Aharon ken asu.
verses.study_note: Israel obeys — 'they did as the LORD had commanded.' The repetition 'so did they' (ken asu) emphasizes exact compliance. After chapters of Pharaoh's defiance, Israel's obedience stands in sharp contrast. Faith is demonstrated by obedient action: they killed the lamb, applied the blood, ate the meal, stayed inside. Hebrews 11:28 credits this obedience: 'Through faith he kept the passover.'

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that at midnight the LORD smote all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh that sat on his throne unto the firstborn of the captive that was in the dungeon; and all the firstborn of cattle.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּחֲצִי הַלַּיְלָה וַיהוָה הִכָּה כָל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכֹר פַּרְעֹה הַיֹּשֵׁב עַל־כִּסְאוֹ עַד בְּכוֹר הַשְּׁבִי אֲשֶׁר בְּבֵית הַבּוֹר וְכֹל בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi bachatzi hallaylah vaYHVH hikkah khol-bekhor be'eretz Mitzrayim mibekhor Par'oh hayyoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor hashevi asher beveit habbor vekhol bekhor behemah.
verses.study_note: Midnight (chatzi hallaylah) — the darkest hour. God strikes 'from the firstborn of Pharaoh... unto the firstborn of the captive in the dungeon' — from the highest to the lowest, no status provides exemption. Even the prisoners suffer Egypt's judgment. The universality of death spans the entire social order: palace, dungeon, and barn. 'The LORD smote' — the verb 'hikkah' (struck) is direct divine action. This is the plague that breaks Pharaoh.
verses.doctrinal_note: The death of the firstborn demonstrates that divine judgment is impartial — it touches throne and dungeon alike. No human power, rank, or institution provides shelter from God's justice. Only the blood of the lamb distinguishes between judgment and mercy. This is the biblical doctrine of universal accountability before God: 'There is no respect of persons with God' (Rom 2:11).

verses.kjv_text: And Pharaoh rose up in the night, he, and all his servants, and all the Egyptians; and there was a great cry in Egypt; for there was not a house where there was not one dead.
verses.original_text: וַיָּקָם פַּרְעֹה לַיְלָה הוּא וְכָל־עֲבָדָיו וְכָל־מִצְרַיִם וַתְּהִי צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה בְּמִצְרָיִם כִּי־אֵין בַּיִת אֲשֶׁר אֵין־שָׁם מֵת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyakam Par'oh laylah hu vekhol-avadav vekhol-Mitzrayim vattehi tze'akah gedolah beMitzrayim ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met.
verses.study_note: The 'great cry' (tze'akah gedolah) echoes and inverts the earlier 'great cry' of Israel under oppression (Exod 3:7,9). The oppressor now cries as the oppressed once cried. 'There was not a house where there was not one dead' — the judgment is absolute and universal across Egypt. Pharaoh 'rose up in the night' — he who defied God ('Who is the LORD?' 5:2) now rises in terror. The proud king is reduced to a grief-stricken father.

verses.kjv_text: And he called for Moses and Aaron by night, and said, Rise up, and get you forth from among my people, both ye and the children of Israel; and go, serve the LORD, as ye have said.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקְרָא לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן לַיְלָה וַיֹּאמֶר קוּמוּ צְּאוּ מִתּוֹךְ עַמִּי גַּם־אַתֶּם גַּם־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּלְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה כְּדַבֶּרְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyikra leMosheh ule'Aharon laylah vayomer kumu tze'u mittokh ammi gam-attem gam-benei Yisra'el ulekhu ivdu et-YHVH kedabberkhem.
verses.study_note: The total reversal. Pharaoh — who said 'Who is the LORD that I should obey his voice?' (5:2), who said 'I will not let Israel go' (5:2) — now says 'Go, serve the LORD, as ye have said.' Every word is capitulation. 'Rise up, get you forth' — he expels the people he enslaved. Moses had warned: 'thou shalt thrust us out hence altogether' (11:1). The one who defied YHVH now sends Israel to serve YHVH.

verses.kjv_text: Also take your flocks and your herds, as ye have said, and be gone; and bless me also.
verses.original_text: גַּם־צֹאנְכֶם גַּם־בְּקַרְכֶם קְחוּ כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתֶּם וָלֵכוּ וּבֵרַכְתֶּם גַּם־אֹתִי׃
verses.transliteration: Gam-tzonkhem gam-bekarkhem kechu ka'asher dibbartem valekhu uverakhtem gam-oti.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh concedes everything — flocks and herds included (earlier he demanded they stay, 10:24). Then the remarkable plea: 'and bless me also' (uverakhtem gam-oti). The proud defier of God now begs for blessing from the servants of God. This echoes the Abrahamic promise: 'I will bless them that bless thee, and curse him that curseth thee' (Gen 12:3). Pharaoh, in extremis, recognizes that blessing flows through God's people.

verses.kjv_text: And the Egyptians were urgent upon the people, that they might send them out of the land in haste; for they said, We be all dead men.
verses.original_text: וַתֶּחֱזַק מִצְרַיִם עַל־הָעָם לְמַהֵר לְשַׁלְּחָם מִן־הָאָרֶץ כִּי אָמְרוּ כֻּלָּנוּ מֵתִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vattechezak Mitzrayim al-ha'am lemaher leshallcham min-ha'aretz ki ameru kullanu metim.
verses.study_note: The Egyptians 'were urgent' (vattechezak — from 'chazak,' to be strong, to press firmly) — the same root used for Pharaoh's hardened heart! Now Egypt uses that same strength to expel Israel. 'We be all dead men' (kullanu metim) — they fear total annihilation. The tables have turned completely: once Israel served Egypt under compulsion; now Egypt serves Israel's departure with the same urgency.

verses.kjv_text: And the people took their dough before it was leavened, their kneadingtroughs being bound up in their clothes upon their shoulders.
verses.original_text: וַיִּשָּׂא הָעָם אֶת־בְּצֵקוֹ טֶרֶם יֶחְמָץ מִשְׁאֲרֹתָם צְרֻרֹת בְּשִׂמְלֹתָם עַל־שִׁכְמָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa ha'am et-betzeko terem yechmatz mish'arotam tzerurot besimletam al-shikhemam.
verses.study_note: The dough was unleavened because there was no time for it to rise (terem yechmatz — 'before it was leavened'). The kneading troughs wrapped in garments on their shoulders is a vivid detail — they literally carry the ingredients of their new life on their backs. The practical urgency of departure gives the lasting theological symbol: unleavened bread = freedom from corruption, haste of faith, break from the old life.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel did according to the word of Moses; and they borrowed of the Egyptians jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment:
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל עָשׂוּ כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּשְׂמָלֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei-Yisra'el asu khidvar Mosheh vayyish'alu miMitzrayim kelei-khesef ukhlei zahav usmalot.
verses.study_note: 'Borrowed' (vayyish'alu) is better translated 'asked' or 'requested' — they did not intend to return these items. This is the 'plundering' of Egypt, fulfilling God's promise to Abraham: 'they shall come out with great substance' (Gen 15:14). After 430 years of unpaid labor, this is back wages. The silver, gold, and raiment later become materials for the Tabernacle (Exod 25:1-7). Egypt's wealth, extracted by oppression, is redirected to worship.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD gave the people favour in the sight of the Egyptians, so that they lent unto them such things as they required. And they spoiled the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה נָתַן אֶת־חֵן הָעָם בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרַיִם וַיַּשְׁאִלוּם וַיְנַצְּלוּ אֶת־מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHVH natan et-chen ha'am be'einei Mitzrayim vayyash'ilum vayenatzelu et-Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'The LORD gave the people favour' (natan et-chen) — God sovereignly moved the Egyptians' hearts. 'They spoiled' (vayenatzelu) — literally 'stripped' or 'plundered' Egypt. The slave nation leaves as victorious conquerors, carrying the wealth of their oppressors. This is a reversal of cosmic proportions and a pattern for eschatological hope: the oppressed will be vindicated, and the spoils of the enemy will be redirected for God's purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel journeyed from Rameses to Succoth, about six hundred thousand on foot that were men, beside children.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסְעוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵרַעְמְסֵס סֻכֹּתָה כְּשֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף רַגְלִי הַגְּבָרִים לְבַד מִטָּף׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyis'u venei-Yisra'el meRa'meses Sukkotah keshesh-me'ot elef ragli haggevarim levad mittaf.
verses.study_note: The Exodus begins. Rameses (Ra'meses — 'born of Ra') was a storage city Israel helped build (Exod 1:11). Succoth (Sukkot — 'booths/shelters') is the first camp. 'About six hundred thousand men on foot' (ragli — foot soldiers) — counting only adult men. With women and children, the total population was likely 2-2.5 million. This mass departure from the world's greatest empire is unprecedented in ancient history. 'Ragli' (on foot) emphasizes they are marching, not fleeing — an organized military departure.

verses.kjv_text: And a mixed multitude went up also with them; and flocks, and herds, even very much cattle.
verses.original_text: וְגַם־עֵרֶב רַב עָלָה אִתָּם וְצֹאן וּבָקָר מִקְנֶה כָּבֵד מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam-erev rav alah ittam vetzon uvakar mikneh kaved me'od.
verses.study_note: A 'mixed multitude' (erev rav — a great mixture) — non-Israelites who joined the Exodus, likely including intermarried Egyptians, other Semitic slaves, and sympathizers. This mixed group later becomes a source of trouble (Num 11:4, 'the mixt multitude that was among them fell a lusting'). The detail shows both the universal appeal of God's deliverance and the danger of incomplete spiritual commitment. God's redemption attracts followers, but not all are genuinely transformed.

verses.kjv_text: And they baked unleavened cakes of the dough which they brought forth out of Egypt, for it was not leavened; because they were thrust out of Egypt, and could not tarry, neither had they prepared for themselves any victual.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאפוּ אֶת־הַבָּצֵק אֲשֶׁר הוֹצִיאוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם עֻגֹת מַצּוֹת כִּי לֹא חָמֵץ כִּי־גֹרְשׁוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם וְלֹא יָכְלוּ לְהִתְמַהְמֵהַּ וְגַם־צֵדָה לֹא־עָשׂוּ לָהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyofu et-habbatzek asher hotzi'u miMitzrayim ugot matzot ki lo chametz ki-goreshu miMitzrayim velo yakhelu lehitmameha vegam-tzedah lo-asu lahem.
verses.study_note: The practical origin of unleavened bread: 'they were thrust out' (goreshu — expelled, divorced, driven away) and 'could not tarry' (lehitmameha — to delay, to linger). They had prepared no provisions (tzedah — food for a journey). Faith here is stepping into the unknown without provisions — trusting that the God who delivered will also sustain. The same verb 'hitmameha' describes Lot's lingering in Genesis 19:16 — there, hesitation nearly proved fatal; here, there is no time for it.

verses.kjv_text: Now the sojourning of the children of Israel, who dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years.
verses.original_text: וּמוֹשַׁב בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יָשְׁבוּ בְּמִצְרָיִם שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Umoshav benei Yisra'el asher yashvu beMitzrayim sheloshim shanah ve'arba me'ot shanah.
verses.study_note: The 430 years of sojourning is reckoned from Abraham's entry into Canaan (Gal 3:17) or from Jacob's descent into Egypt, depending on the chronological tradition. Paul uses this number in Galatians 3:17 to argue that the Law (given 430 years after the promise to Abraham) does not annul the earlier covenant of grace. The precision of the number (not 'about' 400 but exactly 430) demonstrates God's sovereignty over historical timing.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass at the end of the four hundred and thirty years, even the selfsame day it came to pass, that all the hosts of the LORD went out from the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיְהִי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה יָצְאוּ כָּל־צִבְאוֹת יְהוָה מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi mikketz sheloshim shanah ve'arba me'ot shanah vayehi be'etzem hayyom hazzeh yatze'u kol-tziv'ot YHVH me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'The selfsame day' (be'etzem hayyom hazzeh) — God's timing is exact to the day. Not a day early, not a day late. The 430-year clock expires, and on that precise day Israel departs. They are called 'the hosts of the LORD' (tziv'ot YHVH) — God's own army. The God who counts the stars and calls them by name (Ps 147:4) also counts the days of His people's suffering with precision.

verses.kjv_text: It is a night to be much observed unto the LORD for bringing them out from the land of Egypt: this is that night of the LORD to be observed of all the children of Israel in their generations.
verses.original_text: לֵיל שִׁמֻּרִים הוּא לַיהוָה לְהוֹצִיאָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם הוּא־הַלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה לַיהוָה שִׁמֻּרִים לְכָל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְדֹרֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Leil shimmurim hu laYHVH lehotzi'am me'eretz Mitzrayim hu-hallaylah hazzeh laYHVH shimmurim lekhol-benei Yisra'el ledorotam.
verses.study_note: 'A night to be much observed' (leil shimmurim) — literally 'a night of watchings' or 'a night of guarding.' The word 'shimmurim' is an intensive plural of 'shamar' (to keep, guard, watch). God watched over Israel that night; Israel watches in commemoration. The double use of 'shimmurim' is unique in Scripture — this is the most watched-over night in history. It is a night 'unto the LORD' (laYHVH) — it belongs to God. On this same calendar date, Christ would be crucified.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'night of watchings' establishes a principle of divine vigilance: God does not sleep or slumber (Ps 121:4). He watches over His redemptive plan with meticulous care. The perpetual observance 'in their generations' connects every generation of believers to the original redemption — just as the Lord's Supper connects every generation of Christians to the cross.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses and Aaron, This is the ordinance of the passover: There shall no stranger eat thereof:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן זֹאת חֻקַּת הַפָּסַח כָּל־בֶּן־נֵכָר לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh ve'Aharon zot chukat happasach kol-ben-nekhar lo-yokhal bo.
verses.study_note: Now follows the 'ordinance' (chukat — statute, prescription) regulating who may participate. 'Ben-nekhar' (son of a foreigner) — one who has no covenant relationship with God — may not eat. The Passover is not a public feast for tourists; it is a covenant meal for the redeemed community. Participation requires commitment — belonging to the covenant people. This prefigures the New Testament principle that the Lord's Supper is for believers, not the uncommitted (1 Cor 11:27-29).

verses.kjv_text: But every man’s servant that is bought for money, when thou hast circumcised him, then shall he eat thereof.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־עֶבֶד אִישׁ מִקְנַת־כָּסֶף וּמַלְתָּה אֹתוֹ אָז יֹאכַל בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-eved ish miknat-khasef umaltah oto az yokhal bo.
verses.study_note: A purchased servant may eat the Passover — but only after circumcision. Circumcision is the sign of the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:10-14); it marks entrance into the covenant community. The servant's social status does not exclude him, but lack of covenant commitment does. This is remarkably inclusive for the ancient world — a slave, once circumcised, has equal access to the sacred meal. The entry requirement is covenant identity, not social status.

verses.kjv_text: A foreigner and an hired servant shall not eat thereof.
verses.original_text: תּוֹשָׁב וְשָׂכִיר לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Toshav vesakir lo-yokhal bo.
verses.study_note: The 'toshab' (temporary resident) and 'sakir' (hired worker) are excluded. These are transient relationships — neither has permanently committed to the community or its God. The distinction is between belonging and proximity: one can live near God's people without belonging to them. The Passover requires covenant commitment, not mere geographic proximity. This is the Old Testament expression of 'Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord' (Matt 7:22).

verses.kjv_text: In one house shall it be eaten; thou shalt not carry forth ought of the flesh abroad out of the house; neither shall ye break a bone thereof.
verses.original_text: בְּבַיִת אֶחָד יֵאָכֵל לֹא־תוֹצִיא מִן־הַבַּיִת מִן־הַבָּשָׂר חוּצָה וְעֶצֶם לֹא תִשְׁבְּרוּ־בוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Bevayit echad ye'akhel lo-totzi min-habbayit min-habbasar chutzah ve'etzem lo tishberu-vo.
verses.study_note: Three regulations: (1) eaten in one house — the community stays together; (2) no flesh carried outside — the sacred meal is not fragmented; (3) 'no bone shall be broken' (etzem lo tishberu-vo). This final detail is one of the most precise messianic types in Scripture. John explicitly cites this at the crucifixion: 'that the scripture should be fulfilled, A bone of him shall not be broken' (John 19:36). The Roman soldiers broke the legs of the two thieves but, finding Jesus already dead, did not break His bones — fulfilling this 1,500-year-old ordinance.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition against breaking a bone of the Passover lamb is prophetically fulfilled in Christ's crucifixion with extraordinary precision (John 19:33-36, Psalm 34:20). This is not allegory but typological fulfillment: the original ordinance was given with its ultimate fulfillment in view. God embedded prophetic detail in ritual law, ensuring that when Messiah came, the identification would be unmistakable. No bone broken — the sacrifice is whole, complete, undivided.

verses.kjv_text: All the congregation of Israel shall keep it.
verses.original_text: כָּל־עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל יַעֲשׂוּ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-adat Yisra'el ya'asu oto.
verses.study_note: Universal participation: 'all the congregation' (kol-adat) — no one in the covenant community is exempt. The Passover is not an elective observance or a specialized priestly function; every Israelite household participates. This communal totality pictures the universal scope of Christ's redemption: 'For God so loved the world' (John 3:16). The salvation offered in the Passover lamb is for the entire covenant community without exception.

verses.kjv_text: And when a stranger shall sojourn with thee, and will keep the passover to the LORD, let all his males be circumcised, and then let him come near and keep it; and he shall be as one that is born in the land: for no uncircumcised person shall eat thereof.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יָגוּר אִתְּךָ גֵּר וְעָשָׂה פֶסַח לַיהוָה הִמּוֹל לוֹ כָל־זָכָר וְאָז יִקְרַב לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ וְהָיָה כְּאֶזְרַח הָאָרֶץ וְכָל־עָרֵל לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yagur ittekha ger ve'asah fesach laYHVH himmol lo khol-zakhar ve'az yikrav la'asoto vehayah ke'ezrach ha'aretz vekhol-arel lo-yokhal bo.
verses.study_note: The provision for Gentile inclusion: a 'ger' (sojourner/convert) who wants to keep the Passover must be circumcised — then 'he shall be as one born in the land' (ke'ezrach ha'aretz). Once circumcised, no distinction remains between native and convert. This is a remarkable anticipation of the New Testament: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek... for ye are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The door is open to any who commit through the covenant sign. 'No uncircumcised person shall eat thereof' — the covenant sign is the non-negotiable entry requirement.
verses.doctrinal_note: Gentile inclusion in the Passover through circumcision foreshadows the universal reach of the Gospel. The condition is not ethnicity but covenant commitment. In the New Covenant, circumcision of the heart (Rom 2:29) replaces physical circumcision as the entry sign, and baptism replaces it as the public marker. The principle remains: those who enter the covenant on God's terms have full, equal access to redemption. There are no second-class believers.

verses.kjv_text: One law shall be to him that is homeborn, and unto the stranger that sojourneth among you.
verses.original_text: תּוֹרָה אַחַת יִהְיֶה לָאֶזְרָח וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכְכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Torah achat yihyeh la'ezrach velagger haggar betokhehem.
verses.study_note: 'Torah achat' — 'one Torah/law.' This is a foundational legal and theological principle: the same law applies to native Israelite and convert alike. No double standard, no partiality. The 'Torah' (instruction/law) is singular and unified for all who are in covenant. This principle of legal equality within the covenant community anticipates the New Testament declaration: 'There is one body, and one Spirit... one Lord, one faith, one baptism' (Eph 4:4-5).
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'one law' principle establishes that God does not operate a two-tier system of redemption. Those who enter the covenant — whether by birth or by choice — stand on equal footing before God. This demolishes ethnic supremacy and anticipates Paul's argument in Romans and Galatians that justification is by faith for all, without distinction. The unity of the covenant community under one Torah is the foundation for the unity of the Church under one Gospel.

verses.kjv_text: Thus did all the children of Israel; as the LORD commanded Moses and Aaron, so did they.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כָּל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶת־אַהֲרֹן כֵּן עָשׂוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu kol-benei Yisra'el ka'asher tzivvah YHVH et-Mosheh ve'et-Aharon ken asu.
verses.study_note: The chapter's second affirmation of complete obedience (cf. v.28). 'As the LORD commanded... so did they' — this formula of exact compliance is the hallmark of faithful covenant response. It echoes Noah's obedience (Gen 6:22), Abraham's obedience (Gen 22:3), and anticipates Israel's pledge at Sinai (Exod 24:7). The repetition frames the Passover narrative with obedient faith: instructions given, instructions obeyed.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass the selfsame day, that the LORD did bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt by their armies.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם עַל־צִבְאֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi be'etzem hayyom hazzeh hotzi YHVH et-benei Yisra'el me'eretz Mitzrayim al-tziv'otam.
verses.study_note: The chapter closes as it opened — with the LORD acting. 'The LORD did bring... out' (hotzi YHVH) — God is the subject, Israel the object. Deliverance is God's initiative from first to last. 'By their armies' (al-tziv'otam) — organized, marshaled, purposeful. This final verse summarizes the entire chapter: God gave the instructions (vv.1-20), Israel obeyed (vv.21-28), God judged Egypt (vv.29-30), Pharaoh submitted (vv.31-33), and God brought His people out (v.51). The redemption is complete.
verses.doctrinal_note: The final verse encapsulates the theology of Exodus 12: God is the author and finisher of redemption. He institutes the Passover, provides the lamb, judges the enemy, protects His people, and leads them out. The redeemed respond with obedience and worship, but the initiative, power, and glory belong to God alone. This is the pattern for all salvation: 'Salvation is of the LORD' (Jonah 2:9). From the first Passover to the cross to the second coming, redemption is God's work from beginning to end.

meta.overview: Exodus 12 is the theological epicenter of the Old Testament. Here God institutes the Passover — the foundational act of redemption that defines Israel's identity and foreshadows the cross of Christ with breathtaking precision. God commands each household to select an unblemished lamb on the 10th of Nisan, keep it until the 14th, slaughter it at twilight, and apply its blood to the doorposts and lintel. When the LORD passes through Egypt to strike every firstborn, He will 'pass over' (pasach) the blood-marked houses. The lamb must be roasted whole, eaten with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, in haste with sandals on and staff in hand. The Feast of Unleavened Bread is established as a perpetual ordinance. At midnight, the LORD strikes every firstborn in Egypt — from Pharaoh's heir to the prisoner's son. Pharaoh finally relents, and Israel departs after 430 years, plundering Egypt. The chapter closes with Passover regulations ensuring its perpetual observance. Every detail — the unblemished lamb, the blood on the doorposts, the unbroken bones, the haste of departure — finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, 'our Passover' (1 Cor 5:7).
meta.theme: Redemption Through the Blood of the Lamb
meta.keyWordMeaning: Passover — to pass over, to spare, to protect. From the root meaning to leap or skip over. God's judgment passes over those sheltered by the blood of the lamb.
outline: A New Beginning: The Calendar Reset (vv.1-2): The month of redemption becomes the first month
The Passover Lamb Selected (vv.3-6): Unblemished, kept four days, slaughtered at twilight
The Blood Applied (vv.7-10): Doorposts and lintel marked; the lamb roasted and consumed
The Night of the LORD (vv.11-14): Eaten in haste; the LORD passes through; a perpetual memorial
The Feast of Unleavened Bread (vv.15-20): Seven days of purging leaven — holiness in the redeemed life
Moses Instructs the Elders (vv.21-28): Hyssop, the basin, and the destroyer
The Tenth Plague: Death of the Firstborn (vv.29-30): Midnight judgment on Egypt
The Exodus Begins (vv.31-36): Pharaoh relents; Israel plunders Egypt
The Journey Begins (vv.37-42): From Rameses to Succoth; 430 years fulfilled
Passover Regulations (vv.43-51): Who may eat; no bone broken; one law for all

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The divine speech formula introduces a new section of legislation. Immediately after the death of Egypt's firstborn (ch.12), God speaks to establish the theological meaning of that event. The tenth plague is not merely a historical event but a foundation for permanent worship. What God did in history, He now encodes in law. The juxtaposition is deliberate: the same night that judgment fell on Egypt, consecration is demanded of Israel. Redemption and obligation are inseparable.

verses.kjv_text: Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb among the children of Israel, both of man and of beast: it is mine.
verses.original_text: קַדֶּשׁ־לִי כָל־בְּכוֹר פֶּטֶר כָּל־רֶחֶם בִּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָדָם וּבַבְּהֵמָה לִי הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Qaddesh-li khol-bekhor peter kol-rechem bivnei Yisra'el ba'adam uvabbehemah li hu.
verses.study_note: The chapter's foundational command: 'Sanctify unto me all the firstborn' (qaddesh-li khol-bekhor). 'Qaddesh' means to set apart, to declare holy, to consecrate — to remove from common use and dedicate to God. 'Peter kol-rechem' (whatsoever opens the womb) — the first to break open the womb belongs to God. The scope is total: 'both of man and of beast.' The reason is stated with absolute simplicity: 'it is mine' (li hu). Because God struck Egypt's firstborn and spared Israel's, every firstborn of Israel belongs to Him by right of redemption. The spared life is not the family's to keep; it belongs to the God who spared it. This principle extends through all of Scripture: what God redeems, God owns.
verses.doctrinal_note: The consecration of the firstborn establishes the foundational principle of redemption theology: the redeemed belong to the Redeemer. 'You are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19-20). Christ, the 'firstborn of every creature' (Col 1:15) and 'firstborn from the dead' (Col 1:18), fulfills this in both directions — He is both the Redeemer who claims and the Firstborn who is offered. The firstborn principle runs from Exodus 13 through the Levitical substitution (Num 3:12-13) to its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, who consecrates Himself that we might be consecrated (John 17:19).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the people, Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage; for by strength of hand the LORD brought you out from this place: there shall no leavened bread be eaten.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם זָכוֹר אֶת־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר יְצָאתֶם מִמִּצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה וְלֹא יֵאָכֵל חָמֵץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am zakhor et-hayyom hazzeh asher yetzatem miMitzrayim mibbet avadim ki bechozeq yad hotzi'a YHVH etkhem mizzeh velo ye'akhel chametz.
verses.study_note: 'Remember' (zakhor) — the imperative of memory. Not passive recollection but active, liturgical, participatory remembering. Israel is to re-enter the event in every generation. 'House of bondage' (beit avadim) — Egypt is forever defined not by its pyramids but by its slavery. 'By strength of hand' (bechozeq yad) — the strong hand of YHVH, not Israel's strength, accomplished the deliverance. The immediate consequence: 'there shall no leavened bread be eaten' (lo ye'akhel chametz). Leaven (chametz) represents the old life, corruption, and haste — they left so quickly the bread could not rise. The prohibition transforms a historical circumstance into a permanent spiritual discipline.
verses.doctrinal_note: Biblical memory is not nostalgia but liturgical re-enactment. 'Remember' (zakhor) in Hebrew is an active verb — to call to mind so as to act upon it. The same verb is used of God 'remembering' His covenant (Gen 8:1, Exod 2:24). When God remembers, He acts. When Israel remembers, they worship. Jesus transforms this at the Last Supper: 'Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19) — the Passover remembrance becomes the Eucharistic remembrance. The Exodus memory is fulfilled in the Gospel memory.

verses.kjv_text: This day came ye out in the month Abib.
verses.original_text: הַיּוֹם אַתֶּם יֹצְאִים בְּחֹדֶשׁ הָאָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Hayyom attem yotze'im bechodesh ha'Aviv.
verses.study_note: The month is identified: 'Aviv' (ha'Aviv) — meaning 'green ears,' the month of spring barley harvest. This is the original name before the Babylonian calendar name 'Nisan' was adopted. The exodus occurs in spring — the season of new life, new growth, resurrection. God's timing is not accidental: deliverance comes when creation itself is being renewed. The Passover in Aviv/Nisan later becomes the month of Christ's crucifixion and resurrection — the ultimate Exodus from the ultimate bondage.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, and the Hittites, and the Amorites, and the Hivites, and the Jebusites, which he sware unto thy fathers to give thee, a land flowing with milk and honey, that thou shalt keep this service in this month.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִי־יְבִיאֲךָ יְהוָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לַאֲבֹתֶיךָ לָתֶת לָךְ אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ וְעָבַדְתָּ אֶת־הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah khi-yevi'akha YHVH el-eretz haKena'ani vehaChitti veha'Emori vehaChivvi vehaYevusi asher nishba la'avotekha latet lakh eretz zavat chalav udevash ve'avadeta et-ha'avodah hazzo't bachodesh hazzeh.
verses.study_note: The command is future-oriented: 'when the LORD shall bring thee into the land.' Five nations are listed (Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Hivites, Jebusites) — representing the promised land's current occupants. 'Which he sware unto thy fathers' — the oath to Abraham (Gen 15:18-21), Isaac, and Jacob. The covenant promise is the guarantee. 'A land flowing with milk and honey' (eretz zavat chalav udevash) — the iconic description of abundance, first used in 3:8. 'This service' (ha'avodah hazzo't) — the same word for slavery (avodah) now describes worship. The feast is called a 'service' — the very word that defined their bondage now defines their worship. The transformation of 'avodah' from slavery to service is one of the great theological reversals of Exodus.

verses.kjv_text: Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD.
verses.original_text: שִׁבְעַת יָמִים תֹּאכַל מַצֹּת וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי חַג לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Shiv'at yamim tokhal matzot uvayyom hashevi'i chag laYHVH.
verses.study_note: Seven days of unleavened bread (matzot) — a complete period of purification. The number seven denotes completeness (cf. seven days of creation). On the seventh day, a feast (chag) to the LORD — the cycle that began with removal of leaven culminates in celebration. 'Matzot' (unleavened bread) is bread without yeast — flat, humble, quickly made. Paul interprets: 'Purge out therefore the old leaven... let us keep the feast, not with old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Cor 5:7-8). The physical removal of leaven becomes a metaphor for spiritual purification.

verses.kjv_text: Unleavened bread shall be eaten seven days; and there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters.
verses.original_text: מַצּוֹת יֵאָכֵל אֵת שִׁבְעַת הַיָּמִים וְלֹא־יֵרָאֶה לְךָ חָמֵץ וְלֹא־יֵרָאֶה לְךָ שְׂאֹר בְּכָל־גְּבֻלֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Matzot ye'akhel et shiv'at hayyamim velo-yera'eh lekha chametz velo-yera'eh lekha se'or bekhol-gevulekha.
verses.study_note: The prohibition intensifies: not merely 'do not eat' leaven but 'it shall not be seen' (lo-yera'eh) in 'all thy quarters' (bekhol-gevulekha — within all your borders). Two words are used: 'chametz' (leavened bread, the product) and 'se'or' (leaven/yeast, the agent). Both the agent of corruption and the corrupted product must be entirely removed. Jewish tradition developed the practice of 'bedikat chametz' — a thorough search of the home by candlelight before Passover to find and remove every trace of leaven. This thoroughness illustrates the comprehensive nature of sanctification: not just the obvious sins but every hidden agent of corruption.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt shew thy son in that day, saying, This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהִגַּדְתָּ לְבִנְךָ בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא לֵאמֹר בַּעֲבוּר זֶה עָשָׂה יְהוָה לִי בְּצֵאתִי מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiggadeta levinekha bayyom hahu lemor ba'avur zeh asah YHVH li betze'ti miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'Thou shalt shew thy son' (vehiggadeta levinekha) — the verb 'higgid' means to declare, to tell, to narrate. This is the origin of the 'Haggadah' — the telling, the Passover Seder narrative. Faith is transmitted generationally through telling the story. 'In that day' — every year, when the feast is observed. The remarkable phrase 'what the LORD did unto ME' (asah YHVH li) — not 'to our ancestors' but 'to me.' Every generation must experience the Exodus as their own deliverance. The Mishnah later codifies this: 'In every generation, a person must regard himself as though he personally had come out of Egypt.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The intergenerational transmission of faith is a divine command, not a suggestion. The Haggadah principle — telling the story to the next generation — is the primary means of faith formation in Scripture. Parents are the primary theologians of their children. Deuteronomy 6:6-9 expands this: teach diligently, talk of them sitting and walking, bind them, write them. The early church continued this: 'Fathers, bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord' (Eph 6:4).

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be for a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD’s law may be in thy mouth: for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה לְךָ לְאוֹת עַל־יָדְךָ וּלְזִכָּרוֹן בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ לְמַעַן תִּהְיֶה תּוֹרַת יְהוָה בְּפִיךָ כִּי בְּיָד חֲזָקָה הוֹצִאֲךָ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah lekha le'ot al-yadekha ulezikkaron bein einekha lema'an tihyeh torat YHVH befikha ki beyad chazaqah hotzi'akha YHVH miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'A sign upon thine hand' (le'ot al-yadekha) and 'a memorial between thine eyes' (ulezikkaron bein einekha) — later Judaism interpreted these literally as tefillin (phylacteries): small leather boxes containing Scripture portions bound to the arm and forehead. Whether the original intent was literal or metaphorical (the Exodus should govern what you do [hand] and how you think [between your eyes]), the point is total integration: God's deliverance must permeate all of life. 'That the LORD's law may be in thy mouth' (torat YHVH befikha) — hand, eyes, mouth: actions, thoughts, and words are all claimed by the Exodus memory.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sign on the hand and frontlets between the eyes anticipate the totality of devotion that God requires. The Shema (Deut 6:4-9) repeats this imagery. Jesus criticizes those who make phylacteries broad for show but miss the heart reality (Matt 23:5). The external signs were meant to express internal consecration. In Revelation, the mark of the beast parodies this — a counterfeit mark on hand and forehead (Rev 13:16) opposing the seal of God (Rev 7:3). What God claims through redemptive love, the beast counterfeits through coercion.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season from year to year.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁמַרְתָּ אֶת־הַחֻקָּה הַזֹּאת לְמוֹעֲדָהּ מִיָּמִים יָמִימָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veshamarta et-hachuqqah hazzo't lemo'adah miyyamim yamimah.
verses.study_note: 'Keep this ordinance' (shamarta et-hachuqqah) — 'shamar' (guard, keep, observe) combined with 'chuqqah' (statute, something engraved, a permanent decree). 'In his season' (lemo'adah) — at its appointed time. The word 'mo'ed' (appointed time) is the same word for the Tabernacle's meeting place (Ohel Mo'ed — Tent of Meeting). God's calendar has 'appointments' — and the Feast of Unleavened Bread is a non-negotiable annual appointment with God. 'From year to year' (miyyamim yamimah) — literally 'from days to days,' an idiom for annually. The rhythm is perpetual.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers, and shall give it thee,
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־יְבִאֲךָ יְהוָה אֶל־אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי כַּאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לְךָ וְלַאֲבֹתֶיךָ וּנְתָנָהּ לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-yevi'akha YHVH el-eretz haKena'ani ka'asher nishba lekha vela'avotekha unetanah lakh.
verses.study_note: The law of the firstborn (vv.11-16) is anchored in the future: 'when the LORD shall bring thee into the land.' This is prospective legislation — laws for a settled people given to a nation of wanderers. It demonstrates extraordinary faith: they have not yet crossed the Red Sea, and God is already legislating for life in Canaan. 'As he sware unto thee and to thy fathers' (nishba) — the oath to the patriarchs is the foundation. The land is not earned by conquest but given (unetanah — 'and He shall give it') by covenant faithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: That thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix, and every firstling that cometh of a beast which thou hast; the males shall be the LORD’s.
verses.original_text: וְהַעֲבַרְתָּ כָל־פֶּטֶר־רֶחֶם לַיהוָה וְכָל־פֶּטֶר שֶׁגֶר בְּהֵמָה אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה לְךָ הַזְּכָרִים לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'avarta khol-peter-rechem laYHVH vekhol-peter sheger behemah asher yihyeh lekha hazzekharim laYHVH.
verses.study_note: 'Set apart' (veha'avarta) — literally 'cause to pass over.' The same root as 'Hebrew' (ivri — one who crosses over) and echoing the Passover itself. Every firstborn that 'opens the womb' (peter rechem) must be 'passed over' to the LORD. 'Firstling' (peter sheger) — 'sheger' means the offspring of cattle, the drop of the womb. The males belong to the LORD (hazzekharim laYHVH). This applies to animals: clean firstborn animals were sacrificed; unclean firstborn (like donkeys, v.13) were redeemed or killed. The principle: life that God spared is life that God owns.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'passing over' (ha'avarta) of the firstborn to God creates a continuous, generational link to the original Passover. Every birth in Israel becomes a theological event — a reminder that life itself is a gift from the God who passed over death. The male firstborn requirement points to Christ: the 'firstborn' Son who was not redeemed but offered (Rom 8:32 — 'spared not his own Son'). Where Isaac was substituted for by a ram, Christ was the substitute Himself.

verses.kjv_text: And every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb; and if thou wilt not redeem it, then thou shalt break his neck: and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־פֶּטֶר חֲמֹר תִּפְדֶּה בְשֶׂה וְאִם־לֹא תִפְדֶּה וַעֲרַפְתּוֹ וְכֹל בְּכוֹר אָדָם בְּבָנֶיךָ תִּפְדֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-peter chamor tifdeh veseh ve'im-lo tifdeh va'arafto vekhol bekhor adam bevanekha tifdeh.
verses.study_note: A remarkable verse with three critical principles: (1) The firstborn donkey (chamor) — an unclean animal — must be redeemed with a lamb (seh). The lamb dies in the donkey's place. (2) If not redeemed, the donkey's neck is broken (va'arafto) — the unredeemed must die. (3) 'All the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem' (tifdeh) — human firstborn are redeemed, never sacrificed. The word 'padah' (redeem) means to ransom by paying a price; a substitute bears the cost. The donkey-lamb exchange is a vivid picture: an unclean creature is saved by the death of a clean substitute. This is the Gospel in miniature.
verses.doctrinal_note: The substitutionary principle is crystallized: the unclean is redeemed by the death of the clean. The donkey lives because the lamb dies. The donkey cannot save itself — it is unclean by nature (Lev 11:2-3). Only the death of an acceptable substitute preserves its life. 'If not redeemed, break its neck' — there is no third option. One is either redeemed or destroyed. This binary is the heart of biblical soteriology: 'He that hath the Son hath life; and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life' (1 John 5:12). Christ is the Lamb who redeems the unclean.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this? that thou shalt say unto him, By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage:
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה כִּי־יִשְׁאָלְךָ בִנְךָ מָחָר לֵאמֹר מַה־זֹּאת וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah ki-yish'alekha vinkha machar lemor mah-zot ve'amarta elav bechozeq yad hotzi'anu YHVH miMitzrayim mibbet avadim.
verses.study_note: The second 'when your son asks' passage (cf. v.8 and 12:26). 'In time to come' (machar — literally 'tomorrow') — the future generation. 'What is this?' (mah-zot) — the child sees the practice and asks the question. The Passover Haggadah identifies four types of sons who ask: the wise, the wicked, the simple, and the one who does not know how to ask. This is the simple son: 'What is this?' The answer is direct: 'By strength of hand the LORD brought US out' — again, 'us,' not 'them.' Every generation participates in the original deliverance.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both the firstborn of man, and the firstborn of beast: therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix, being males; but all the firstborn of my children I redeem.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כִּי־הִקְשָׁה פַרְעֹה לְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ וַיַּהֲרֹג יְהוָה כָּל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכֹר אָדָם וְעַד־בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה עַל־כֵּן אֲנִי זֹבֵחַ לַיהוָה כָּל־פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם הַזְּכָרִים וְכָל־בְּכוֹר בָּנַי אֶפְדֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi ki-hiqshah Far'oh leshallchenu vayyaharog YHVH kol-bekhor be'eretz Mitzrayim mibbkhor adam ve'ad-bekhor behemah al-ken ani zoveach laYHVH kol-peter rechem hazzekharim vekhol-bekhor banai efdeh.
verses.study_note: The full explanation connects history to practice: (1) Pharaoh was stubborn (hiqshah — hardened, from the same root as 'qashah' in 7:3); (2) therefore YHVH slew all firstborn in Egypt; (3) therefore 'I sacrifice' (zoveach) the firstborn males of livestock to the LORD; (4) but 'all the firstborn of my children I redeem' (efdeh). The logic is clear: because Egypt's firstborn died, Israel's firstborn live only by grace — and what lives by grace belongs to the one who gave grace. Animal firstborn are sacrificed; human firstborn are redeemed with a payment (later set at five shekels, Num 18:16). The distinction preserves human dignity while maintaining the consecration principle.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes: for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה לְאוֹת עַל־יָדְכָה וּלְטוֹטָפֹת בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah le'ot al-yadkhah uletotafot bein einekha ki bechozeq yad hotzi'anu YHVH miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The 'sign on the hand and frontlets between the eyes' formula repeats from v.9, now applied to the firstborn law. 'Frontlets' (totafot) is a unique word occurring only here, Deuteronomy 6:8, and 11:18 — its etymology is debated, but it refers to something bound to the forehead. The tefillin (phylacteries) of later Judaism contain four Scripture passages: Exodus 13:1-10, Exodus 13:11-16, Deuteronomy 6:4-9, and Deuteronomy 11:13-21. The phrase 'by strength of hand' (bechozeq yad) forms an inclusio with v.3, framing the entire section on unleavened bread and firstborn consecration within the reality of divine power.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when Pharaoh had let the people go, that God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near; for God said, Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt:
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּשַׁלַּח פַּרְעֹה אֶת־הָעָם וְלֹא־נָחָם אֱלֹהִים דֶּרֶךְ אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים כִּי קָרוֹב הוּא כִּי אָמַר אֱלֹהִים פֶּן־יִנָּחֵם הָעָם בִּרְאֹתָם מִלְחָמָה וְשָׁבוּ מִצְרָיְמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi beshallach Far'oh et-ha'am velo-nacham Elohim derekh eretz Pelishtim ki qarov hu ki amar Elohim pen-yinnachem ha'am bir'otam milchamah veshavu Mitzraymah.
verses.study_note: A stunning revelation of God's pastoral wisdom. The coastal route via Philistia ('the Way of Horus' in Egyptian texts) was the shortest path — a mere 10-day journey. But God chose the longer wilderness route because 'the people see war, and they return to Egypt.' God knows their weakness: fresh from slavery, they are not yet ready for battle. The Egyptian military fortresses along the coastal road would have terrified them. The word 'nacham' appears twice with different meanings: 'God led them not' (lo-nacham, from 'nachah,' to lead/guide) and 'lest the people repent/change their mind' (pen-yinnachem, from 'nacham,' to reconsider/regret). God's circuitous route is not inefficiency — it is mercy.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's route is often longer than ours would be. He factors in our weakness, our unreadiness, our tendency to retreat. The 'longer way' through the wilderness is not punishment but protection. God leads His people by what they can bear (1 Cor 10:13), not by what seems most efficient. The wilderness is God's training ground: He leads them the long way to test them, humble them, and teach them dependence (Deut 8:2). The principle applies to spiritual maturity: God does not give us battles we are not yet equipped to fight.

verses.kjv_text: But God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea: and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיַּסֵּב אֱלֹהִים אֶת־הָעָם דֶּרֶךְ הַמִּדְבָּר יַם־סוּף וַחֲמֻשִׁים עָלוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyassev Elohim et-ha'am derekh hammidbar Yam-Suf vachamushim alu venei-Yisra'el me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: 'God led the people about' (vayyassev) — 'savav' means to turn, to go around, to take a circuitous route. The detour is deliberate. 'The way of the wilderness of the Red Sea' — 'Yam Suf' literally means 'Sea of Reeds' (suf = reeds, cf. the basket of reeds that saved Moses, 2:3). The exact location is debated, but it is the body of water God will spectacularly part in chapter 14. 'Harnessed' (chamushim) — this difficult word has been interpreted as 'armed/equipped' (from 'chamesh,' five, possibly meaning organized in groups of fifty) or 'in battle array.' Despite their unreadiness for Philistine war, they march in orderly formation — a trained army beginning to form under divine command.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת־עַצְמוֹת יוֹסֵף עִמּוֹ כִּי הַשְׁבֵּעַ הִשְׁבִּיעַ אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם וְהַעֲלִתֶם אֶת־עַצְמֹתַי מִזֶּה אִתְּכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach Mosheh et-atzmot Yosef immo ki hashbea hishbi'a et-benei Yisra'el lemor paqod yifqod Elohim etkhem veha'alitem et-atzmotai mizzeh ittkhem.
verses.study_note: A deeply moving detail. Moses carries Joseph's bones (atzmot Yosef) — fulfilling the oath from Genesis 50:25. Joseph had 'straitly sworn' (hashbea hishbi'a — emphatic doubling: made them swear an oath upon an oath) that when God 'surely visits' (paqod yifqod — another emphatic doubling: He will certainly visit/attend to you), they must carry his bones to the promised land. Joseph died approximately 360 years before this moment — yet his faith-oath endures. He believed in a promise he would not live to see. Hebrews 11:22 cites this as Joseph's supreme act of faith: 'By faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones.'
verses.doctrinal_note: Joseph's bones are the physical testimony of faith in the unseen. For 360 years those bones waited in Egypt — a silent sermon on the faithfulness of God's promises. The bones finally reach Canaan in Joshua 24:32 — buried at Shechem, in the parcel of ground Jacob bought. From deathbed oath to fulfilled promise: Genesis 50:25 → Exodus 13:19 → Joshua 24:32. This arc spanning centuries demonstrates that God's promises do not expire. 'Not one word has failed of all His good promise' (1 Kings 8:56).

verses.kjv_text: And they took their journey from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסְעוּ מִסֻּכֹּת וַיַּחֲנוּ בְאֵתָם בִּקְצֵה הַמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyis'u miSukkot vayyachanu ve'Etam biqtzeh hammidbar.
verses.study_note: The second station of the journey. Succoth (Sukkot — 'booths/shelters') was the first encampment after Rameses (12:37). Now they move to Etham, 'in the edge of the wilderness' (biqtzeh hammidbar) — at the threshold between civilization and wilderness. 'Etham' possibly means 'fortress' or 'border wall' — the edge of the Egyptian frontier. They are leaving the known world behind. Each station marks a step further from Egypt and deeper into dependence on God. The wilderness is not a punishment but a classroom (Deut 8:2-5).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way; and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light; to go by day and night:
verses.original_text: וַיהוָה הֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיהֶם יוֹמָם בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן לַנְחֹתָם הַדֶּרֶךְ וְלַיְלָה בְּעַמּוּד אֵשׁ לְהָאִיר לָהֶם לָלֶכֶת יוֹמָם וָלָיְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: VaYHVH holekh lifneihem yomam be'ammud anan lanchotam hadderekh velaylah be'ammud esh leha'ir lahem lalekhet yomam valaylah.
verses.study_note: One of the most majestic verses in the Pentateuch. 'The LORD went before them' (YHVH holekh lifneihem) — God Himself leads. Not an angel, not a messenger, but YHVH personally. The form is twofold: by day, a 'pillar of cloud' (ammud anan) — providing guidance, shade from the desert sun, and the visible presence of God; by night, a 'pillar of fire' (ammud esh) — providing light, warmth, protection, and the luminous manifestation of divine glory. 'To go by day and night' — the journey never stops, and God's presence never departs. The cloud and fire are not two different manifestations but one continuous presence — cloud by day and fire by night. The same pillar serves double duty, adapted to the people's need.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pillar of cloud and fire is the visible manifestation of God's presence — the 'Shekinah' glory (from 'shakan,' to dwell). This is a theophany: God making Himself visible, tangible, and near. It embodies every dimension of God's care: guidance (leading the way), protection (shade by day, warmth by night), provision (light in darkness), and presence (God among His people). The pillar reappears at the Red Sea (14:19-20), at Sinai (19:9, 16), over the Tabernacle (40:34-38), and theologically in the New Testament as Christ — 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12) — and the Holy Spirit at Pentecost, when tongues of fire descend (Acts 2:3). The journey from Exodus 13 to Pentecost is the journey from external pillar to internal flame.

verses.kjv_text: He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people.
verses.original_text: לֹא־יָמִישׁ עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן יוֹמָם וְעַמּוּד הָאֵשׁ לָיְלָה לִפְנֵי הָעָם׃
verses.transliteration: Lo-yamish ammud he'anan yomam ve'ammud ha'esh laylah lifnei ha'am.
verses.study_note: The chapter's final verse is a declaration of unceasing divine faithfulness: 'He took not away' (lo-yamish — He did not remove, did not depart). The pillar remained continuously — day and night, without interruption, without abandonment. 'Before the people' (lifnei ha'am) — always leading, always in front, always visible. This constancy is extraordinary: Israel will grumble, rebel, worship the golden calf, reject God's provision — and the pillar will remain. God's presence is not conditioned on Israel's performance. This is covenant faithfulness in visible, tangible form. The God who promised Abraham will not depart from Abraham's children, however faithless they prove to be.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's presence does not depart based on human performance. The pillar remained through Israel's worst moments — complaint at the Red Sea (14:11-12), the golden calf (32:1-6), the ten spies' rebellion (Num 14:1-4). God's faithfulness outlasts human faithlessness: 'If we are faithless, he remains faithful, for he cannot deny himself' (2 Tim 2:13). In the New Covenant, this becomes even more personal: 'I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee' (Heb 13:5), and 'Lo, I am with you always, even unto the end of the age' (Matt 28:20). The permanent pillar of Exodus 13 is the Old Testament expression of the permanent indwelling of the Holy Spirit in the New.

meta.overview: Exodus 13 stands at the threshold between deliverance and journey — between the final plague and the wilderness march. It opens with God's command to consecrate every firstborn to Himself, establishing the theological principle that the firstborn who were spared belong to the God who spared them. Moses then instructs the people concerning the Feast of Unleavened Bread (Matzot), embedding the Exodus into Israel's annual liturgical memory through seven days of eating unleavened bread. The chapter establishes the law of the firstborn — every firstborn of man and beast must be redeemed or sacrificed — a perpetual reminder that Israel's firstborn live because Egypt's died. Then, in a remarkable strategic decision, God does NOT lead Israel by the short coastal route through Philistia but by the longer wilderness route, because the people are not yet ready for war. The chapter closes with the majestic image of God's visible, tangible presence: a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night — the LORD Himself leading His newly-born nation into the unknown.
meta.theme: Consecration, Memorial & Divine Guidance — The Firstborn, the Feast, and the Pillar
meta.keyWordMeaning: To consecrate, to set apart as holy — the opening command of the chapter. What God has redeemed, He claims as His own.
outline: Consecrate the Firstborn (vv.1-2): Every firstborn belongs to God — the theological principle of redemption
The Feast of Unleavened Bread (vv.3-10): Memorial, teaching, and the sign on the hand and frontlets between the eyes
The Law of the Firstborn (vv.11-16): Redeem the donkey, redeem the son — the perpetual reminder
God Leads by the Wilderness Way (vv.17-18): The strategic detour and Joseph's bones
The Pillar of Cloud and Fire (vv.19-22): God's visible presence leading Israel day and night

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with God speaking directly to Moses — initiating what follows. What appears to be a strategic blunder (leading Israel to the sea) is in fact God's deliberate plan. The reader is given the divine perspective before the human crisis unfolds. Everything that follows is orchestrated by YHVH, not forced by circumstance.

verses.kjv_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, that they turn and encamp before Pihahiroth, between Migdol and the sea, over against Baalzephon: before it shall ye encamp by the sea.
verses.original_text: דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיָשֻׁבוּ וְיַחֲנוּ לִפְנֵי פִּי הַחִירֹת בֵּין מִגְדֹּל וּבֵין הַיָּם לִפְנֵי בַּעַל צְפֹן נִכְחוֹ תַחֲנוּ עַל־הַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyashuvu veyachanu lifnei Pi haChirot bein Migdol uvein hayyam lifnei Ba'al Tzefon nikhcho tachanu al-hayyam.
verses.study_note: God commands Israel to 'turn' (yashuvu — to turn back) and camp in a position that appears strategically suicidal: hemmed between Migdol (a fortress), the sea, and Ba'al Tzefon (a site named after a Canaanite deity). The geography is a deliberate trap — but set by God, not Pharaoh. 'Ba'al Tzefon' (Lord of the North) was a Phoenician/Canaanite storm deity — Israel camps facing this idol's shrine. YHVH will demonstrate His power over the sea at the very doorstep of the god who supposedly controlled it.

verses.kjv_text: For Pharaoh will say of the children of Israel, They are entangled in the land, the wilderness hath shut them in.
verses.original_text: וְאָמַר פַּרְעֹה לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל נְבֻכִים הֵם בָּאָרֶץ סָגַר עֲלֵיהֶם הַמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'amar Par'oh livnei Yisra'el nevukhim hem ba'aretz sagar alehem hammidbar.
verses.study_note: God predicts Pharaoh's exact assessment: 'They are entangled' (nevukhim — confused, wandering aimlessly). 'The wilderness hath shut them in' (sagar — closed, locked). Pharaoh will read the situation as Israel's strategic incompetence. What Pharaoh interprets as confusion, God has designed as invitation — luring Egypt to its own destruction. The divine strategy uses apparent weakness as a trap.

verses.kjv_text: And I will harden Pharaoh’s heart, that he shall follow after them; and I will be honoured upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host; that the Egyptians may know that I am the LORD. And they did so.
verses.original_text: וְחִזַּקְתִּי אֶת־לֵב־פַּרְעֹה וְרָדַף אַחֲרֵיהֶם וְאִכָּבְדָה בְּפַרְעֹה וּבְכָל־חֵילוֹ וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן׃
verses.transliteration: Vechizzaqti et-lev-Par'oh veradaf achareihem ve'ikkavedah beFar'oh uvekhol-cheilo veyade'u Mitzrayim ki-ani YHVH vayya'asu-khen.
verses.study_note: God states His threefold purpose: (1) harden Pharaoh's heart, (2) be honored/glorified upon Pharaoh and his host, (3) that Egypt may know 'I am the LORD.' The word 've'ikkavedah' (I will be glorified/honored) comes from the same root 'kaved' used for Pharaoh's hardened heart. The stunning wordplay: Pharaoh's 'heavy' (kaved) heart produces God's 'glory' (kavod) upon him. God will extract glory from the very resistance that defied Him.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's sovereignty over human rebellion is one of Scripture's deepest mysteries. God hardens a heart already set on rebellion, and from that rebellion extracts His own glory. This does not make God the author of Pharaoh's evil — Pharaoh hardened himself first (8:15, 32; 9:34). God's hardening confirms and uses the direction Pharaoh has already chosen. Romans 9:17-18 and 9:22-23 explore this: God endures 'vessels of wrath fitted to destruction' in order to display the riches of His glory to 'vessels of mercy.' Pharaoh's destruction reveals the same power that saves Israel.

verses.kjv_text: And it was told the king of Egypt that the people fled: and the heart of Pharaoh and of his servants was turned against the people, and they said, Why have we done this, that we have let Israel go from serving us?
verses.original_text: וַיֻּגַּד לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם כִּי בָרַח הָעָם וַיֵּהָפֵךְ לְבַב פַּרְעֹה וַעֲבָדָיו אֶל־הָעָם וַיֹּאמְרוּ מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂינוּ כִּי־שִׁלַּחְנוּ אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵעָבְדֵנוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyuggad lemelekh Mitzrayim ki varach ha'am vayyehafekh levav Par'oh va'avadav el-ha'am vayyomeru mah-zo't asinu ki-shillachnu et-Yisra'el me'ovdenu.
verses.study_note: The report reaches Pharaoh: 'the people fled' (varach — escaped, ran away). His heart 'was turned' (vayyehafekh — from 'haphakh,' the same word for the overthrow of Sodom, Gen 19:25). The reversal is total: the man who drove Israel out (12:31-33) now regrets releasing them. 'Why have we done this?' — the plural includes his servants. The economic reality hits: Egypt has lost its slave labor force. 'From serving us' (me'ovdenu) — the same word 'avad' (to serve) that defines the Exodus contest: Israel will serve YHVH, not Pharaoh.

verses.kjv_text: And he made ready his chariot, and took his people with him:
verses.original_text: וַיֶּאְסֹר אֶת־רִכְבּוֹ וְאֶת־עַמּוֹ לָקַח עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayye'sor et-rikhbo ve'et-ammo lakach immo.
verses.study_note: Pharaoh personally 'made ready his chariot' (vayye'sor — literally 'harnessed,' from 'asar,' to bind/yoke). This is not a delegation but personal engagement. Pharaoh himself leads the pursuit. The chariot was Egypt's supreme military technology — the ancient equivalent of armored divisions. By taking 'his people' (ammo), Pharaoh commits the nation to war against the God who has already defeated him ten times.

verses.kjv_text: And he took six hundred chosen chariots, and all the chariots of Egypt, and captains over every one of them.
verses.original_text: וַיִּקַּח שֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת רֶכֶב בָּחוּר וְכֹל רֶכֶב מִצְרָיִם וְשָׁלִשִׁם עַל־כֻּלּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyiqqach shesh-me'ot rekhev bachur vekhol rekhev Mitzrayim veshalishim al-kullo.
verses.study_note: Six hundred 'chosen' (bachur — elite, picked) chariots plus 'all the chariots of Egypt' — this is the full military force of the ancient world's superpower. 'Shalishim' (captains/officers) — literally 'third men,' the elite warriors who rode as a third man in the chariot. Every chariot carries its best fighter. The army that lost its firstborn sons now sends its finest warriors to recapture the slaves who caused that loss. The repetition compulsion of sin: Pharaoh returns to the exact behavior that destroyed him.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt, and he pursued after the children of Israel: and the children of Israel went out with an high hand.
verses.original_text: וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל יֹצְאִים בְּיָד רָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vaychazzeq YHVH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitzrayim vayyirdof acharei benei Yisra'el uvenei Yisra'el yotz'im beyad ramah.
verses.study_note: God hardens (vaychazzeq — Piel of chazak, strengthens) Pharaoh's heart. This is active divine hardening, not merely permission. Israel meanwhile goes out 'with an high hand' (beyad ramah) — a raised, triumphant fist, the posture of freedom and confidence. The image is stunning: slaves walking in defiant freedom while the world's greatest army mobilizes behind them. 'Yad ramah' later becomes a negative term for presumptuous sin (Num 15:30), but here it describes holy boldness.

verses.kjv_text: But the Egyptians pursued after them, all the horses and chariots of Pharaoh, and his horsemen, and his army, and overtook them encamping by the sea, beside Pihahiroth, before Baalzephon.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְדְּפוּ מִצְרַיִם אַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיַּשִּׂיגוּ אוֹתָם חֹנִים עַל־הַיָּם כָּל־סוּס רֶכֶב פַּרְעֹה וּפָרָשָׁיו וְחֵילוֹ עַל־פִּי הַחִירֹת לִפְנֵי בַּעַל צְפֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyirdefu Mitzrayim achareihem vayyassigu otam chonim al-hayyam kol-sus rekhev Par'oh ufarashav vecheilo al-Pi haChirot lifnei Ba'al Tzefon.
verses.study_note: The trap closes: Egypt's full military — horses, chariots, horsemen, army — 'overtook them' (vayyassigu — caught up with, reached). Israel is encamped exactly where God positioned them (v.2): by the sea, before Ba'al Tzefon. The narrative tension reaches its peak. From every human perspective, Israel is doomed. But the reader already knows (v.4) that this is God's design, not Pharaoh's triumph.

verses.kjv_text: And when Pharaoh drew nigh, the children of Israel lifted up their eyes, and, behold, the Egyptians marched after them; and they were sore afraid: and the children of Israel cried out unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וּפַרְעֹה הִקְרִיב וַיִּשְׂאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־עֵינֵיהֶם וְהִנֵּה מִצְרַיִם נֹסֵעַ אַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיִּירְאוּ מְאֹד וַיִּצְעֲקוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: UFar'oh hikriv vayyis'u venei-Yisra'el et-eineihem vehineh Mitzrayim nose'a achareihem vayyire'u me'od vayyitz'aku venei-Yisra'el el-YHVH.
verses.study_note: The moment of crisis. Israel lifts their eyes and sees the Egyptian army bearing down. 'They were sore afraid' (vayyire'u me'od) — the recently triumphant slaves are now terrified. Yet their first response is right: they 'cried out unto the LORD' (vayyitz'aku el-YHVH). The same verb 'tza'ak' used for their cries under slavery (Exod 2:23) — the cry that began the Exodus. Their fear is real; their instinct to pray is correct. What follows (vv.11-12) shows the fear corrupting into complaint, but the initial cry to God is genuine.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto Moses, Because there were no graves in Egypt, hast thou taken us away to die in the wilderness? wherefore hast thou dealt thus with us, to carry us forth out of Egypt?
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הֲמִבְּלִי אֵין־קְבָרִים בְּמִצְרַיִם לְקַחְתָּנוּ לָמוּת בַּמִּדְבָּר מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ לְהוֹצִיאָנוּ מִמִּצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru el-Mosheh hamibli ein-kevarim beMitzrayim leqachtanu lamut bammidbar mah-zo't asita lanu lehotzi'anu miMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The cry to God degenerates into bitter sarcasm directed at Moses: 'Because there were no graves in Egypt?' — a darkly ironic reference to Egypt, the land of elaborate tombs and pyramids. The bitter humor barely masks their terror. They frame the Exodus not as liberation but as a death march. This is the first of many wilderness complaints and represents the pattern of Israel's spiritual immaturity: genuine faith under pressure collapses into accusation and despair.

verses.kjv_text: Is not this the word that we did tell thee in Egypt, saying, Let us alone, that we may serve the Egyptians? For it had been better for us to serve the Egyptians, than that we should die in the wilderness.
verses.original_text: הֲלֹא־זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְנוּ אֵלֶיךָ בְמִצְרַיִם לֵאמֹר חֲדַל מִמֶּנּוּ וְנַעַבְדָה אֶת־מִצְרָיִם כִּי טוֹב לָנוּ עֲבֹד אֶת־מִצְרָיִם מִמֻּתֵנוּ בַּמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Halo-zeh haddavar asher dibbarnnu eleikha veMitzrayim lemor chadal mimmennu vena'avedah et-Mitzrayim ki tov lanu avod et-Mitzrayim mimmutenu bammidbar.
verses.study_note: The complaint escalates: 'better to serve Egypt than to die.' The word 'avad' (serve) appears twice — they prefer slavery to the risk of freedom. This is the theology of the enslaved mind: safety in bondage seems preferable to danger in deliverance. It recalls the later tragedy of Numbers 14:3-4 where they propose returning to Egypt. The pattern reveals a truth about human nature: freedom is frightening, and the familiar chains of sin can feel safer than the unknown path of faith.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the people, Fear ye not, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD, which he will shew to you to day: for the Egyptians whom ye have seen to day, ye shall see them again no more for ever.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם אַל־תִּירָאוּ הִתְיַצְּבוּ וּרְאוּ אֶת־יְשׁוּעַת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂה לָכֶם הַיּוֹם כִּי אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם אֶת־מִצְרַיִם הַיּוֹם לֹא תֹסִפוּ לִרְאֹתָם עוֹד עַד־עוֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am al-tire'u hityatzvu ur'u et-yeshuat YHVH asher-ya'aseh lakhem hayyom ki asher re'item et-Mitzrayim hayyom lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam.
verses.study_note: One of the greatest declarations of faith in all Scripture. Four commands: (1) 'Fear not' (al-tire'u), (2) 'Stand still' (hityatzvu — take your position firmly), (3) 'See' (ur'u — behold, witness), (4) 'the salvation of the LORD' (yeshuat YHVH). The word 'yeshu'ah' (salvation) shares its root with the name Jesus (Yeshua). Moses declares that today's Egyptians will never be seen again 'forever' (ad-olam). The salvation is permanent, total, and irreversible. Israel's role is not to fight but to watch God fight for them.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the foundational principle of salvation: it is the LORD's work, not ours. Israel does not wield a sword; they stand and watch. 'Yeshuat YHVH' (the salvation of the LORD) is not a human achievement but a divine gift. The name 'Jesus' (Yeshua) is embedded in this declaration — He is the salvation of the LORD made flesh. Paul echoes this: 'By grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves' (Eph 2:8). Salvation has always been God's work observed by human faith.

verses.kjv_text: The LORD shall fight for you, and ye shall hold your peace.
verses.original_text: יְהוָה יִלָּחֵם לָכֶם וְאַתֶּם תַּחֲרִשׁוּן׃
verses.transliteration: YHVH yillachem lakhem ve'attem tacharishun.
verses.study_note: The shortest and most powerful summary of the Exodus theology: 'The LORD shall fight for you' (YHVH yillachem lakhem). Israel's assignment: 'hold your peace' (tacharishun — be silent, be still). The word 'charash' means to be silent, to plow, to engrave — profound stillness. This is not the silence of despair but of trust. Nine Hebrew words contain the entire gospel in seed form: God fights; His people rest. This verse echoes in Jehoshaphat's battle (2 Chr 20:15), in David's faith before Goliath, and in Christ's silence before His accusers.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Wherefore criest thou unto me? speak unto the children of Israel, that they go forward:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה מַה־תִּצְעַק אֵלָי דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיִסָּעוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh mah-titz'ak elai dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyissa'u.
verses.study_note: A remarkable divine rebuke: 'Why do you cry to me?' There is a time to pray and a time to act.
