Moses has just told Israel to 'stand still' (v.13), but God says 'go forward' (veyissa'u — break camp, march). This is not a contradiction but a sequence: faith stands still before God and then obeys when God commands movement. The command to advance — toward the sea, with no visible path — is the ultimate test of obedient faith. 'Forward' means into the water. The way out is through.

verses.kjv_text: But lift thou up thy rod, and stretch out thine hand over the sea, and divide it: and the children of Israel shall go on dry ground through the midst of the sea.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה הָרֵם אֶת־מַטְּךָ וּנְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ עַל־הַיָּם וּבְקָעֵהוּ וְיָבֹאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם בַּיַּבָּשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah harem et-mattekha unteh et-yadekha al-hayyam uveqa'ehu veyavo'u venei-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabashah.
verses.study_note: God commands Moses to lift the rod and stretch out his hand — and 'divide it' (uveqa'ehu — from 'baqa,' to split, cleave). The verb 'baqa' is used for splitting rocks (Num 20:11, Ps 78:15), for God cleaving the sea (Neh 9:11, Ps 78:13), and for the Spirit of God dividing the waters of chaos (Gen 1:6-7). 'Dry ground' (yabashah) — the seabed will become habitable land. The same word describes the dry ground that appeared at creation (Gen 1:9). God is performing a new act of creation: bringing order from chaos, land from sea, life from death.
verses.doctrinal_note: The splitting of the sea is a new creation event. The same God who separated waters from dry land in Genesis 1 now splits the sea to create a path of salvation. The rod of Moses — once a shepherd's tool, then a serpent, then the instrument of plagues — now becomes the instrument of deliverance. The same power that judged Egypt saves Israel. This typologically foreshadows baptism (1 Cor 10:1-2): passing through the waters from the old life into the new, from slavery to freedom, from death to life.

verses.kjv_text: And I, behold, I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians, and they shall follow them: and I will get me honour upon Pharaoh, and upon all his host, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen.
verses.original_text: וַאֲנִי הִנְנִי מְחַזֵּק אֶת־לֵב מִצְרַיִם וְיָבֹאוּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם וְאִכָּבְדָה בְּפַרְעֹה וּבְכָל־חֵילוֹ בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו׃
verses.transliteration: Va'ani hineni mechazzeq et-lev Mitzrayim veyavo'u achareihem ve'ikkavedah beFar'oh uvekhol-cheilo berikhbo uveparashav.
verses.study_note: God repeats His purpose from verse 4 with expanded scope: the hardening now extends to 'the hearts of the Egyptians' (plural — the entire army). They will follow Israel into the seabed. The divine strategy is breathtaking: the same path that saves Israel destroys Egypt. The cross and the Red Sea share this character — what brings salvation to believers brings judgment to those who reject God. 'I will get me honour' (ve'ikkavedah) — the kavod/kaved wordplay continues.

verses.kjv_text: And the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD, when I have gotten me honour upon Pharaoh, upon his chariots, and upon his horsemen.
verses.original_text: וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה בְּהִכָּבְדִי בְּפַרְעֹה בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו׃
verses.transliteration: Veyade'u Mitzrayim ki-ani YHVH behikkavdi beFar'oh berikhbo uveparashav.
verses.study_note: The knowledge purpose statement: 'the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD.' This is the climax of the 'knowledge' theme that runs through the plague narrative (Exod 7:5, 17; 8:10, 22; 9:14, 29; 10:2). Egypt will finally know — even in destruction — that YHVH is the sovereign God. The recognition formula 'ki-ani YHVH' (that I am the LORD) appears over 70 times in the Old Testament. God's ultimate purpose in all His acts is that He be known.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel of God, which went before the camp of Israel, removed and went behind them; and the pillar of the cloud went from before their face, and stood behind them:
verses.original_text: וַיִּסַּע מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים הַהֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵי מַחֲנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֵּלֶךְ מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיִּסַּע עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן מִפְּנֵיהֶם וַיַּעֲמֹד מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyissa mal'akh ha'Elohim haholekh lifnei machaneh Yisra'el vayyelekh me'achareihem vayyissa ammud he'anan mippneihem vayya'amod me'achareihem.
verses.study_note: The Angel of God (mal'akh ha'Elohim) and the pillar of cloud — which had been leading Israel — now move to the rear, positioning themselves between Israel and Egypt. The 'Angel of God' is identified with the pillar of cloud, suggesting a theophanic presence. This is the same 'Angel' who appeared in the burning bush (Exod 3:2) and who is repeatedly identified with YHVH Himself. God becomes Israel's rearguard — the divine shield interposes between His people and their enemies.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Angel of God taking position between Israel and Egypt is one of Scripture's most powerful images of divine protection. God does not merely send help; He personally stands between His people and danger. Isaiah 52:12 promises: 'the God of Israel will be your rearguard.' Isaiah 58:8: 'the glory of the LORD shall be thy rearguard.' The New Testament fulfillment: Christ stands between believers and condemnation — 'Who shall separate us from the love of Christ?' (Rom 8:35).

verses.kjv_text: And it came between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel; and it was a cloud and darkness to them, but it gave light by night to these: so that the one came not near the other all the night.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא בֵּין מַחֲנֵה מִצְרַיִם וּבֵין מַחֲנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיְהִי הֶעָנָן וְהַחֹשֶׁךְ וַיָּאֶר אֶת־הַלָּיְלָה וְלֹא־קָרַב זֶה אֶל־זֶה כָּל־הַלָּיְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo bein machaneh Mitzrayim uvein machaneh Yisra'el vayehi he'anan vehachoshekh vayya'er et-hallaylah velo-qarav zeh el-zeh kol-hallaylah.
verses.study_note: The pillar performs a dual function simultaneously: darkness to Egypt, light to Israel. The same cloud that blinds one group illumines the other. This is the divine distinction (hivdil) principle from chapter 8:22 made visible. It recalls creation itself: God separated light from darkness (Gen 1:4). Throughout the night, neither army could approach the other — God holds them apart until He is ready. The same Presence that guides also protects; the same glory that saves also judges.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses stretched out his hand over the sea; and the LORD caused the sea to go back by a strong east wind all that night, and made the sea dry land, and the waters were divided.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַיָּם וַיּוֹלֶךְ יְהוָה אֶת־הַיָּם בְּרוּחַ קָדִים עַזָּה כָּל־הַלַּיְלָה וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת־הַיָּם לֶחָרָבָה וַיִּבָּקְעוּ הַמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyet Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyolekh YHVH et-hayyam beruach qadim azzah kol-hallaylah vayyasem et-hayyam lecharavah vayyibaqe'u hammayim.
verses.study_note: The climactic moment. Moses stretches his hand, and YHVH acts with 'a strong east wind all that night' (ruach qadim azzah kol-hallaylah). God uses natural means (wind) for supernatural ends (dividing the sea). The 'ruach' (wind/spirit) over the waters echoes Genesis 1:2 where the Spirit of God (ruach Elohim) hovered over the waters of creation. 'The waters were divided' (vayyibaqe'u) — the same verb as v.16. Dry land (charavah) emerges from the sea, just as in creation. This is Genesis 1 replayed as redemption: God creates a new path through chaos waters for His redeemed people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The crossing of the Red Sea is the Old Testament's supreme act of salvation and the primary type of baptism. Just as God's Spirit (ruach) hovered over the waters at creation to bring forth life and order, now God's wind (ruach) divides the waters to bring forth His people in a new birth. Paul explicitly identifies this as baptism: 'all our fathers... were all baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea' (1 Cor 10:1-2). The pattern is death-burial-resurrection: Israel enters the watery grave of the sea, passes through, and emerges alive on the other side. The old life (Egypt/slavery) is drowned; the new life (freedom/worship) begins.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel went into the midst of the sea upon the dry ground: and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם בַּיַּבָּשָׁה וְהַמַּיִם לָהֶם חוֹמָה מִימִינָם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u venei-Yisra'el betokh hayyam bayyabashah vehamayim lahem chomah miminam umissemolam.
verses.study_note: Israel passes through 'the midst of the sea upon the dry ground' — a breathtaking impossibility. The 'waters were a wall' (chomah — a fortified wall, like a city wall) on both sides. This is not a receding tide but a supernatural architecture of water standing vertically. The word 'chomah' is the same used for the walls of Jericho (Josh 6:5). God builds walls of water to protect His people, just as He will tear down walls of stone to give them the Promised Land. The path through the sea is both terrifying and safe — surrounded by the instrument of death, yet walking on dry ground.

verses.kjv_text: And the Egyptians pursued, and went in after them to the midst of the sea, even all Pharaoh’s horses, his chariots, and his horsemen.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְדְּפוּ מִצְרַיִם וַיָּבֹאוּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם כֹּל סוּס פַּרְעֹה רִכְבּוֹ וּפָרָשָׁיו אֶל־תּוֹךְ הַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyirdefu Mitzrayim vayyavo'u achareihem kol sus Par'oh rikhbo ufarashav el-tokh hayyam.
verses.study_note: Egypt follows Israel into the divided sea. The hardened heart (v.17) leads them to fatal presumption: seeing the path Israel walks on, they assume it is safe for them as well. But the path of salvation for God's people is the path of destruction for His enemies. The chariots — Egypt's greatest military asset — enter the seabed. What was designed for speed and power on open ground becomes a liability in a narrow, muddy corridor between walls of water.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that in the morning watch the LORD looked unto the host of the Egyptians through the pillar of fire and of the cloud, and troubled the host of the Egyptians,
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בְּאַשְׁמֹרֶת הַבֹּקֶר וַיַּשְׁקֵף יְהוָה אֶל־מַחֲנֵה מִצְרַיִם בְּעַמּוּד אֵשׁ וְעָנָן וַיָּהָם אֵת מַחֲנֵה מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi be'ashmoret habboqer vayyashqef YHVH el-machaneh Mitzrayim be'ammud esh ve'anan vayyaham et machaneh Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: The 'morning watch' (ashmoret habboqer — the last watch, approximately 2-6 AM) — the darkest hour before dawn. 'The LORD looked' (vayyashqef — to gaze down, to peer) through the pillar of fire and cloud. When YHVH 'looks' at enemies, they are undone (cf. Psalm 104:32: 'He looketh on the earth, and it trembleth'). 'Troubled' (vayyaham) — from 'hamam,' to throw into confusion, to panic, to rout. This is the holy terror that disintegrates armies (cf. Josh 10:10, Judg 4:15, 1 Sam 7:10). The divine gaze causes supernatural panic.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'morning watch' introduces an eschatological pattern: God's decisive intervention comes in the darkness before dawn. The resurrection of Christ occurred in the early morning hours. The Second Coming is described as a thief in the night. God's salvation arrives at humanity's darkest moment. The divine 'look' that destroys Egypt is the same gaze that searches hearts (Ps 139:23) — terrifying to the guilty, comforting to the redeemed.

verses.kjv_text: And took off their chariot wheels, that they drave them heavily: so that the Egyptians said, Let us flee from the face of Israel; for the LORD fighteth for them against the Egyptians.
verses.original_text: וַיָּסַר אֵת אֹפַן מַרְכְּבֹתָיו וַיְנַהֲגֵהוּ בִּכְבֵדֻת וַיֹּאמֶר מִצְרַיִם אָנוּסָה מִפְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כִּי יְהוָה נִלְחָם לָהֶם בְּמִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyasar et ofan markevotav vayenahagehu bikhvedut vayomer Mitzrayim anusah mippenei Yisra'el ki YHVH nilcham lahem beMitzrayim.
verses.study_note: God removes 'their chariot wheels' (ofan markevotav) — Egypt's supreme military advantage becomes useless. 'They drave them heavily' (bikhvedut — from 'kaved,' heavy) — the same root word again. Pharaoh's 'heavy' heart produces 'heavy' driving. The Egyptians finally confess what Moses declared in v.14: 'the LORD fighteth for them' (YHVH nilcham lahem). Too late, Egypt recognizes the divine warrior. They want to flee, but flight from the God who controls the sea is impossible. The confession comes at the moment of destruction.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand over the sea, that the waters may come again upon the Egyptians, upon their chariots, and upon their horsemen.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ עַל־הַיָּם וְיָשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם עַל־מִצְרַיִם עַל־רִכְבּוֹ וְעַל־פָּרָשָׁיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh neteh et-yadekha al-hayyam veyashuvu hammayim al-Mitzrayim al-rikhbo ve'al-parashav.
verses.study_note: The same hand that divided the sea now commands it to return. 'Stretch out thine hand' — the gesture of division becomes the gesture of destruction. 'Let the waters come again' (veyashuvu — return) — the same word used for Pharaoh's heart 'turning' (v.5). Everything returns to its natural state: the sea returns, Pharaoh's folly returns upon his own head. The symmetry is devastating — Moses' hand opens and closes the sea like a door.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses stretched forth his hand over the sea, and the sea returned to his strength when the morning appeared; and the Egyptians fled against it; and the LORD overthrew the Egyptians in the midst of the sea.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַיָּם וַיָּשָׁב הַיָּם לִפְנוֹת בֹּקֶר לְאֵיתָנוֹ וּמִצְרַיִם נָסִים לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְנַעֵר יְהוָה אֶת־מִצְרַיִם בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyet Mosheh et-yado al-hayyam vayyashav hayyam lifnot boqer le'eitano uMitzrayim nasim likrato vayena'er YHVH et-Mitzrayim betokh hayyam.
verses.study_note: The sea returns 'to his strength' (le'eitano — to its normal force/flow) at daybreak. The Egyptians flee 'against it' (likrato — toward it, into it) — running into the very waters they should be running from. Panic has destroyed their judgment. 'The LORD overthrew' (vayena'er — literally 'shook off,' like shaking insects from a garment). The verb 'na'ar' depicts YHVH shaking Egypt off as one shakes off something insignificant. The mightiest army in the world is treated as dust to be brushed away.

verses.kjv_text: And the waters returned, and covered the chariots, and the horsemen, and all the host of Pharaoh that came into the sea after them; there remained not so much as one of them.
verses.original_text: וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם וַיְכַסּוּ אֶת־הָרֶכֶב וְאֶת־הַפָּרָשִׁים לְכֹל חֵיל פַּרְעֹה הַבָּאִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם בַּיָּם לֹא־נִשְׁאַר בָּהֶם עַד־אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyashuvu hammayim vayekhassu et-harekhev ve'et-happarashim lekhol cheil Par'oh habba'im achareihem bayyam lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad.
verses.study_note: Total annihilation: 'there remained not so much as one of them' (lo-nish'ar bahem ad-echad). The destruction is absolute. Six hundred elite chariots, the entire cavalry, the full army — every soldier who entered the sea perished. The waters 'covered' (vayekhassu) them — the same verb from v.6 where frogs 'covered' Egypt, and from Genesis 7:19-20 where Flood waters 'covered' the earth. God's judgment by water is a recurring pattern: the Flood, the Sea, and finally baptism (1 Pet 3:20-21).

verses.kjv_text: But the children of Israel walked upon dry land in the midst of the sea; and the waters were a wall unto them on their right hand, and on their left.
verses.original_text: וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָלְכוּ בַיַּבָּשָׁה בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם וְהַמַּיִם לָהֶם חֹמָה מִימִינָם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Uvenei Yisra'el halekhu vayyabashah betokh hayyam vehamayim lahem chomah miminam umissemolam.
verses.study_note: The narrator repeats verse 22 almost verbatim after the destruction account — a deliberate literary bracket (inclusio) framing the salvation. While Egypt drowns, Israel walks. Same sea, same moment, opposite outcomes. 'Dry land' (yabashah) and 'wall' (chomah) are repeated to emphasize the completeness of Israel's safety. The repetition creates a stark before-and-after: verse 22 describes them entering the sea; verse 29 reminds us they walked through it safely while Egypt perished. Hebrews 11:29 celebrates this as the supreme act of faith.

verses.kjv_text: Thus the LORD saved Israel that day out of the hand of the Egyptians; and Israel saw the Egyptians dead upon the sea shore.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹשַׁע יְהוָה בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִיַּד מִצְרָיִם וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־מִצְרַיִם מֵת עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyosha YHVH bayyom hahu et-Yisra'el miyyad Mitzrayim vayyar Yisra'el et-Mitzrayim met al-sefat hayyam.
verses.study_note: The great summary: 'The LORD saved Israel that day' (vayyosha YHVH). The verb 'yasha' (to save/deliver) is the root of 'yeshu'ah' (salvation) and 'Yeshua' (Jesus). YHVH alone is the subject of salvation. Israel sees 'the Egyptians dead upon the sea shore' — the visible, empirical evidence of complete deliverance. The oppressors who haunted their lives for over four centuries now lie lifeless on the sand. The threat is not merely defeated but annihilated. This visual confirmation transforms terror into faith (v.31).

verses.kjv_text: And Israel saw that great work which the LORD did upon the Egyptians: and the people feared the LORD, and believed the LORD, and his servant Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַיָּד הַגְּדֹלָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה בְּמִצְרַיִם וַיִּירְאוּ הָעָם אֶת־יְהוָה וַיַּאֲמִינוּ בַּיהוָה וּבְמֹשֶׁה עַבְדּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyar Yisra'el et-hayyad haggedolah asher asah YHVH beMitzrayim vayyire'u ha'am et-YHVH vayya'aminu baYHVH uveMosheh avdo.
verses.study_note: The chapter's concluding verse achieves its purpose: Israel 'saw the great work' (hayyad haggedolah — literally 'the great hand') and responded with fear and faith. 'Feared the LORD' (vayyire'u) — the terror of the Egyptians (v.10) is transformed into holy reverence for God. 'Believed the LORD' (vayya'aminu) — from 'aman,' the root of 'amen' and 'emunah' (faith). This is the first explicit statement of Israel's corporate faith. They also believe 'his servant Moses' (uveMosheh avdo) — Moses' authority is confirmed through the event. The chapter that began with Israel's terror ends with Israel's trust.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse completes the arc: from fear of man (v.10) to fear of God (v.31), from complaint (v.11-12) to belief (v.31). The Red Sea is the Old Testament's defining revelation of God's saving power. It establishes the pattern for all salvation: God acts, humans believe. The 'great hand' (yad gedolah) that judged Egypt is the same hand that saved Israel. Salvation and judgment are two sides of one divine act — a truth that finds its ultimate expression at the Cross, where Christ's death simultaneously judges sin and saves sinners. 'The people feared the LORD and believed' — this is the response God seeks in every generation.

meta.overview: Exodus 14 is the climactic chapter of the Exodus narrative — the crossing of the Red Sea. After Israel's departure from Egypt, God deliberately leads them into a geographic trap at the edge of the sea, provoking Pharaoh to pursue with the full force of his military. Israel, caught between the sea and the Egyptian army, cries out in terror. Moses speaks the defining words of faith: 'Stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD.' God commands Moses to stretch out his rod; the sea divides, and Israel passes through on dry ground with walls of water on either side. The Egyptian army follows into the seabed, but God throws them into confusion, removes their chariot wheels, and then commands Moses to stretch out his hand again — the waters return and destroy the entire Egyptian force. Not one survives. The chapter closes with Israel's fear of the LORD and belief in Moses His servant. This event becomes the foundational redemptive act of the Old Testament — referenced more than any other event in Hebrew Scripture — and serves as the supreme type of baptism into Christ and deliverance from the dominion of sin.
meta.theme: Salvation Belongs to the LORD — Deliverance Through the Waters
meta.keyWordMeaning: Salvation, deliverance — the great noun of rescue. The root of the name 'Jesus' (Yeshua). God alone saves; Israel merely watches.
outline: God Sets the Trap (vv.1-4): Israel encamped by the sea, Pharaoh provoked to pursue
Pharaoh Pursues (vv.5-9): The full military force of Egypt bears down on Israel
Israel's Fear and Moses' Faith (vv.10-14): 'Stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD'
God Commands Action (vv.15-18): 'Stretch out thy rod' — forward through the sea
The Pillar Moves (vv.19-20): The Angel of God stands between Israel and Egypt
The Sea Divides (vv.21-22): A strong east wind, dry ground, walls of water
Egypt Destroyed (vv.23-28): Chariot wheels removed, the waters return
The Great Salvation (vv.29-31): Israel believes in the LORD and His servant Moses

verses.kjv_text: Then sang Moses and the children of Israel this song unto the LORD, and spake, saying, I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously: the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea.
verses.original_text: אָז יָשִׁיר־מֹשֶׁה וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַשִּׁירָה הַזֹּאת לַיהוָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ לֵאמֹר אָשִׁירָה לַיהוָה כִּי־גָאֹה גָּאָה סוּס וְרֹכְבוֹ רָמָה בַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Az yashir-Mosheh uvenei Yisra'el et-hashirah hazzo't laYHVH vayyomeru lemor ashirah laYHVH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhevo ramah vayyam.
verses.study_note: The Song opens with a unique grammatical form: 'az yashir' — 'then sang' (or 'then will sing'), using an imperfect verb with a past-tense adverb, creating a vivid, timeless quality. Jewish tradition sees in this form a hint of eschatological singing — Israel will sing again in the world to come. 'I will sing to the LORD' (ashirah laYHVH) — the first person singular shifts to communal, establishing worship as both personal and corporate. 'He hath triumphed gloriously' (ga'oh ga'ah) — an emphatic infinitive absolute: 'He has utterly exalted Himself' or 'He is supremely exalted.' This is not mere victory but the supreme display of divine majesty. 'The horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea' (ramah vayyam) — 'ramah' means to hurl, cast down. Egypt's most feared military technology — the horse and chariot — is tossed aside like refuse.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Song of the Sea establishes the foundational pattern of biblical worship: redemption precedes praise. Israel does not sing to earn deliverance but because deliverance has already occurred. This is the pattern fulfilled in the New Testament: 'We love him, because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19). The redeemed sing because they have been redeemed. Revelation 15:3 explicitly connects this song to the eschaton, where the glorified saints sing 'the song of Moses... and the song of the Lamb' — the Exodus and Calvary united in one eternal hymn.

verses.kjv_text: The LORD is my strength and song, and he is become my salvation: he is my God, and I will prepare him an habitation; my father’s God, and I will exalt him.
verses.original_text: עָזִּי וְזִמְרָת יָהּ וַיְהִי־לִי לִישׁוּעָה זֶה אֵלִי וְאַנְוֵהוּ אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי וַאֲרֹמְמֶנְהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: 'Azzi vezimrat Yah vayehi-li lishu'ah zeh Eli ve'anvehu Elohei avi va'aromemenhu.
verses.study_note: One of the most concentrated theological statements in the Old Testament. 'My strength and song' ('azzi vezimrat Yah) — God is both the power and the melody. The shortened divine name 'Yah' (יָהּ) appears here for the first time in Scripture — the most intimate form of the divine name. 'He is become my salvation' (vayehi-li lishu'ah) — the word 'yeshu'ah' (salvation/deliverance) is the root of the name 'Yeshua' (Jesus). God does not merely give salvation; He becomes it. 'This is my God' (zeh Eli) — the demonstrative pronoun 'zeh' (this one!) expresses immediacy and intimacy. 'I will prepare him a habitation' (ve'anvehu) — this word can also mean 'I will beautify/glorify Him' or 'I will build Him a dwelling.' The rabbis debated its meaning extensively. 'My father's God' (Elohei avi) — faith is both personal and inherited; the God of the ancestors has acted again.
verses.doctrinal_note: The name 'Yah' (the short form of YHVH) appears here for the first time in the Bible, at the moment of redemption. 'Yeshu'ah' (salvation) shares its root with the name Jesus (Yeshua). The New Testament writers understood this connection: Jesus IS the yeshu'ah of God made flesh. When Simeon held the infant Jesus, he declared 'mine eyes have seen thy salvation (yeshu'ah)' (Luke 2:30) — echoing this very verse. The Exodus salvation is the type; Christ is the antitype. Isaiah 12:2 quotes this verse verbatim in a messianic context.

verses.kjv_text: The LORD is a man of war: the LORD is his name.
verses.original_text: יְהוָה אִישׁ מִלְחָמָה יְהוָה שְׁמוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: YHVH ish milchamah YHVH shemo.
verses.study_note: A stunning title: 'The LORD is a man of war' (YHVH ish milchamah) — God is portrayed as a warrior who fights personally for His people. The double naming — 'YHVH... YHVH' — frames the declaration with the covenant name. 'His name' (shemo) — the name revealed at the burning bush (Exod 3:14-15) is now demonstrated at the sea. God's name is not merely a label but a declaration of character proved in action. This is the shortest verse in the Song yet one of the most powerful: just six Hebrew words that define YHVH as the warrior-redeemer. The Divine Warrior motif, introduced here, runs through the entire Old Testament (Psalm 24:8, Isaiah 42:13, Zephaniah 3:17) and into Revelation (Rev 19:11-16).
verses.doctrinal_note: The Divine Warrior motif: YHVH fights for His people. This is not metaphor but theological reality — God personally intervenes in history against the forces that enslave His people. This motif reaches its climax in Christ, who is called 'Faithful and True' and rides forth to make war in righteousness (Rev 19:11). The spiritual battle against the enslaving powers — sin, death, Satan — requires a Divine Warrior, not merely a teacher or example. Christ on the cross is the ultimate 'man of war' conquering the powers that hold humanity captive (Col 2:15).

verses.kjv_text: Pharaoh’s chariots and his host hath he cast into the sea: his chosen captains also are drowned in the Red sea.
verses.original_text: מַרְכְּבֹת פַּרְעֹה וְחֵילוֹ יָרָה בַיָּם וּמִבְחַר שָׁלִשָׁיו טֻבְּעוּ בְיַם־סוּף׃
verses.transliteration: Markevot Par'oh vecheilo yarah vayyam umivchar shalishav tubbe'u veyam-Suf.
verses.study_note: 'Pharaoh's chariots and his host' (markevot Par'oh vecheilo) — the entire military apparatus of the world's greatest empire. 'Cast into the sea' (yarah vayyam) — 'yarah' means to throw, to shoot (as an arrow). God hurls Egypt's military machine like a projectile. 'His chosen captains' (mivchar shalishav) — 'shalish' was a high-ranking military officer, possibly the 'third man' on a chariot (driver, fighter, shield-bearer). 'Mivchar' (the choicest/best) — Egypt's elite forces. 'Drowned' (tubbe'u) — submerged, sunk. 'Red sea' is literally 'yam-Suf' — 'Sea of Reeds.' The name may refer to papyrus reeds, and the exact location is debated, but the miracle is not.

verses.kjv_text: The depths have covered them: they sank into the bottom as a stone.
verses.original_text: תְּהֹמֹת יְכַסְיֻמוּ יָרְדוּ בִמְצוֹלֹת כְּמוֹ־אָבֶן׃
verses.transliteration: Tehomot yekhasyyumu yaredu vimetzolot kemo-aven.
verses.study_note: 'The depths' (tehomot) — the primordial deeps of Genesis 1:2 (tehom). The same deep waters over which the Spirit hovered in creation now serve as the instrument of judgment. 'They sank into the bottom' (yaredu vimetzolot) — 'metzolot' means the deepest depths, the abyss. 'As a stone' (kemo-aven) — the first of three similes for the Egyptians' descent (stone here, lead in v.10, stubble in v.7). The poetic progression is deliberate: stone sinks quickly, lead even faster. Egypt's military might sinks like dead weight in the face of divine power.

verses.kjv_text: Thy right hand, O LORD, is become glorious in power: thy right hand, O LORD, hath dashed in pieces the enemy.
verses.original_text: יְמִינְךָ יְהוָה נֶאְדָּרִי בַּכֹּחַ יְמִינְךָ יְהוָה תִּרְעַץ אוֹיֵב׃
verses.transliteration: Yeminekha YHVH ne'dari bakko'ach yeminekha YHVH tir'atz oyev.
verses.study_note: The 'right hand' (yemin) of YHVH — repeated for emphasis in a poetic parallelism. The right hand symbolizes power, authority, and favor throughout Scripture. 'Is become glorious in power' (ne'dari bakko'ach) — 'ne'dari' from 'adar' means majestic, glorious, adorned. God's hand is adorned with power like a warrior with his armor. 'Hath dashed in pieces' (tir'atz) — to shatter, crush, break. The enemy is not merely defeated but pulverized. The double invocation 'Thy right hand, O LORD... thy right hand, O LORD' creates an antiphonal structure, as if the congregation responds to each clause. This is liturgical poetry designed for communal worship.

verses.kjv_text: And in the greatness of thine excellency thou hast overthrown them that rose up against thee: thou sentest forth thy wrath, which consumed them as stubble.
verses.original_text: וּבְרֹב גְּאוֹנְךָ תַּהֲרֹס קָמֶיךָ תְּשַׁלַּח חֲרֹנְךָ יֹאכְלֵמוֹ כַּקַּשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Uverov ge'onekha taharos kamekha teshalach charonekha yokhelemo kakkash.
verses.study_note: 'The greatness of thine excellency' (rov ge'onekha) — 'ga'on' means majesty, exaltation, pride. When used of God, it is supreme splendor; when used of humans, it is condemned as arrogance. 'Overthrown' (taharos) — to tear down, demolish. 'Thy wrath' (charonekha) — burning anger, from 'charah' (to burn). 'Consumed them as stubble' (yokhelemo kakkash) — fire consuming dry chaff. The similes progress: Egypt sinks like stone (v.5), burns like stubble (v.7), and sinks like lead (v.10). They are simultaneously heavy with judgment and light as worthless refuse before God's power.

verses.kjv_text: And with the blast of thy nostrils the waters were gathered together, the floods stood upright as an heap, and the depths were congealed in the heart of the sea.
verses.original_text: וּבְרוּחַ אַפֶּיךָ נֶעֶרְמוּ מַיִם נִצְּבוּ כְמוֹ־נֵד נֹזְלִים קָפְאוּ תְהֹמֹת בְּלֶב־יָם׃
verses.transliteration: Uveruach appekha ne'ermu mayim nitzevu kemo-ned nozelim kaf'u tehomot belev-yam.
verses.study_note: 'The blast of thy nostrils' (ruach appekha) — God's breath/wind (ruach) from His nostrils (af — also meaning 'anger'). The word ruach is the same as 'Spirit' — a single divine exhalation parts an ocean. 'The floods stood upright as an heap' (nitzevu kemo-ned) — 'ned' is a wall or dam. Flowing waters become a solid wall. 'The depths were congealed' (kaf'u tehomot) — 'kafa' means to thicken, congeal, freeze. Liquid becomes solid at God's command. The poetry captures the supernatural suspension of natural law: flowing water stands still, liquid depths solidify, the chaos of the sea becomes an ordered corridor. This is creation-power applied to redemption.

verses.kjv_text: The enemy said, I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide the spoil; my lust shall be satisfied upon them; I will draw my sword, my hand shall destroy them.
verses.original_text: אָמַר אוֹיֵב אֶרְדֹּף אַשִּׂיג אֲחַלֵּק שָׁלָל תִּמְלָאֵמוֹ נַפְשִׁי אָרִיק חַרְבִּי תּוֹרִישֵׁמוֹ יָדִי׃
verses.transliteration: Amar oyev erdof assig achallek shalal timla'emo nafshi arik charbi torishemo yadi.
verses.study_note: The Song quotes the enemy's arrogant boast — a staccato of six first-person verbs: 'I will pursue, I will overtake, I will divide, my lust shall be satisfied, I will draw, my hand shall destroy.' The Hebrew is rapid-fire, breathless with predatory hunger. Six 'I will' statements — the number of human incompleteness. Each verb is short, punchy, aggressive. The enemy speaks only of himself — 'I... I... I... my... I... my.' This is the voice of proud self-sufficiency that meets its end in the next verse.

verses.kjv_text: Thou didst blow with thy wind, the sea covered them: they sank as lead in the mighty waters.
verses.original_text: נָשַׁפְתָּ בְרוּחֲךָ כִּסָּמוֹ יָם צָלֲלוּ כַּעוֹפֶרֶת בְּמַיִם אַדִּירִים׃
verses.transliteration: Nashafta veruachakha kissamo yam tzalelu ka'oferet bemayim addirim.
verses.study_note: After the enemy's six boastful verbs (v.9), God responds with one: 'Thou didst blow' (nashafta). One divine breath versus six human boasts — the asymmetry is the point. God merely blows and the sea closes. 'They sank as lead' (tzalelu ka'oferet) — lead sinks instantly, irrecoverably. The 'mighty waters' (mayim addirim) — 'addirim' means majestic, noble, mighty. The waters themselves are called 'noble' — they obey their Creator while Egypt's 'nobles' (same root) perish in them.

verses.kjv_text: Who is like unto thee, O LORD, among the gods? who is like thee, glorious in holiness, fearful in praises, doing wonders?
verses.original_text: מִי־כָמֹכָה בָּאֵלִם יְהוָה מִי כָּמֹכָה נֶאְדָּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ נוֹרָא תְהִלֹּת עֹשֵׂה פֶלֶא׃
verses.transliteration: Mi-khamokha ba'elim YHVH mi kamokha ne'ddar bakkodesh nora tehillot oseh fele.
verses.study_note: The theological climax of the Song: 'Who is like thee, O LORD, among the gods?' (mi-khamokha ba'elim YHVH). This is not granting the existence of other gods but a rhetorical challenge: among all claimed powers, none compares to YHVH. The name 'Michael' (Mi-kha-El — 'Who is like God?') derives from this very question. 'Glorious in holiness' (ne'ddar bakkodesh) — 'kodesh' (holiness) appears here for the first time in reference to God's essential nature, not merely a ritual category. 'Fearful in praises' (nora tehillot) — God is so awesome that even praising Him evokes reverence. 'Doing wonders' (oseh fele) — 'fele' means things beyond human comprehension.
verses.doctrinal_note: The incomparability of God: 'mi-khamokha' becomes one of Israel's foundational confessions (cf. Psalm 35:10; 71:19; 86:8; 89:6; Micah 7:18). Holiness is here revealed as God's defining attribute — not merely moral purity (though it includes that) but utter otherness, transcendence, and perfection. Isaiah 6:3 ('Holy, holy, holy') and Revelation 4:8 extend this into the eternal worship of heaven. God's holiness is the ground of both His judgment (against Egypt) and His salvation (of Israel).

verses.kjv_text: Thou stretchedst out thy right hand, the earth swallowed them.
verses.original_text: נָטִיתָ יְמִינְךָ תִּבְלָעֵמוֹ אָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Natita yeminekha tivla'emo aretz.
verses.study_note: A verse of devastating brevity — just five Hebrew words. God stretches out His right hand and the earth (aretz) swallows the enemy. 'Swallowed' (tivla'emo) — from 'bala,' to swallow, engulf. The same word describes the earth swallowing Korah (Numbers 16:32). 'Earth' may refer to the seabed or to Sheol — the realm of the dead receiving the drowned.

verses.kjv_text: Thou in thy mercy hast led forth the people which thou hast redeemed: thou hast guided them in thy strength unto thy holy habitation.
verses.original_text: נָחִיתָ בְחַסְדְּךָ עַם־זוּ גָּאָלְתָּ נֵהַלְתָּ בְעָזְּךָ אֶל־נְוֵה קָדְשֶׁךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Nachita vechasdekha am-zu ga'alta nehalta ve'ozzekha el-neveh kodshekha.
verses.study_note: The Song transitions from past deliverance to future guidance. 'Thy mercy' (chasdekha) — 'chesed,' God's covenant lovingkindness. 'The people which thou hast redeemed' (am-zu ga'alta) — the kinsman-redeemer language (ga'al). God is not merely a warrior but a kinsman-redeemer who reclaims His own. 'Guided them' (nehalta) — like a shepherd leading his flock to water. 'Thy holy habitation' (neveh kodshekha) — this could refer to Canaan, to Sinai, or to the Temple mount. The word 'neveh' means a dwelling, a pasture — God leads His redeemed flock to His own green pasture.
verses.doctrinal_note: The two great verbs of salvation appear together: 'led' (nachita — guidance) and 'redeemed' (ga'alta — kinsman-redemption). God is both Shepherd and Kinsman-Redeemer. 'Ga'al' is the precise term for the kinsman who buys back a relative from slavery (Lev 25:48-49). Applied to God, it means He has purchased Israel from Egypt as His own family. This 'ga'al' theology runs through Ruth and Isaiah to its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, 'who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us' (Titus 2:14).

verses.kjv_text: The people shall hear, and be afraid: sorrow shall take hold on the inhabitants of Palestina.
verses.original_text: שָׁמְעוּ עַמִּים יִרְגָּזוּן חִיל אָחַז יֹשְׁבֵי פְּלָשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Shame'u ammim yirgazun chil achaz yoshevei Pelashet.
verses.study_note: The Song now looks prophetically forward to the conquest. The nations 'shall hear' (shame'u) — news of the Red Sea miracle travels across the ancient Near East. Rahab confirms this prophecy decades later: 'we have heard how the LORD dried up the water of the Red sea' (Josh 2:10). 'Be afraid' (yirgazun) — to tremble, be agitated. 'Sorrow' (chil) — travail, anguish like birth pangs. The prophetic geography traces Israel's journey: Philistia (SW), Edom (SE), Moab (E), Canaan (N).

verses.kjv_text: Then the dukes of Edom shall be amazed; the mighty men of Moab, trembling shall take hold upon them; all the inhabitants of Canaan shall melt away.
verses.original_text: אָז נִבְהֲלוּ אַלּוּפֵי אֱדוֹם אֵילֵי מוֹאָב יֹאחֲזֵמוֹ רָעַד נָמֹגוּ כֹּל יֹשְׁבֵי כְנָעַן׃
verses.transliteration: Az nivhalu allufei Edom eilei Mo'av yo'achazemo ra'ad namogu kol yoshevei Khena'an.
verses.study_note: The progression intensifies across three nations: Edom is 'amazed' (nivhalu — terrified), Moab 'trembles' (ra'ad — involuntary shaking), Canaan 'melts' (namogu — dissolves, loses all firmness). The closer to Israel's destination, the greater the fear. 'The dukes of Edom' (allufei Edom) — 'alluf' means chief, clan leader; Edom is Esau's descendants, Israel's kinsmen yet hostile. 'The mighty men of Moab' (eilei Mo'av) — 'ayil' means ram, a symbol of strength. The geography is prophetically precise — these are the actual nations Israel will encounter.

verses.kjv_text: Fear and dread shall fall upon them; by the greatness of thine arm they shall be as still as a stone; till thy people pass over, O LORD, till the people pass over, which thou hast purchased.
verses.original_text: תִּפֹּל עֲלֵיהֶם אֵימָתָה וָפַחַד בִּגְדֹל זְרוֹעֲךָ יִדְּמוּ כָּאָבֶן עַד־יַעֲבֹר עַמְּךָ יְהוָה עַד־יַעֲבֹר עַם־זוּ קָנִיתָ׃
verses.transliteration: Tippol alehem eimatah vafachad bigdol zero'akha yiddemu ka'aven ad-ya'avor ammekha YHVH ad-ya'avor am-zu kanita.
verses.study_note: 'Fear and dread' (eimatah vafachad) — two words for terror paired for maximum effect. 'By the greatness of thine arm' (bigdol zero'akha) — God's arm, the symbol of His saving power. 'Still as a stone' (yiddemu ka'aven) — the nations are paralyzed, petrified. 'Till thy people pass over' (ad-ya'avor ammekha) — the verb 'avar' (to pass over/cross) is the root of 'Hebrew' (Ivri — one who crosses over). 'Which thou hast purchased' (kanita) — 'kanah' means to acquire, buy, create. God has purchased Israel as His treasured possession (cf. Psalm 74:2).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt bring them in, and plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance, in the place, O LORD, which thou hast made for thee to dwell in, in the Sanctuary, O LORD, which thy hands have established.
verses.original_text: תְּבִאֵמוֹ וְתִטָּעֵמוֹ בְּהַר נַחֲלָתְךָ מָכוֹן לְשִׁבְתְּךָ פָּעַלְתָּ יְהוָה מִקְּדָשׁ אֲדֹנָי כּוֹנְנוּ יָדֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Tevi'emo vetitta'emo behar nachalatekha makhon leshivtekha pa'alta YHVH mikkedash Adonai konenu yadekha.
verses.study_note: The prophetic climax of the Song: God will 'bring them in and plant them' (tevi'emo vetitta'emo) — Israel is like a tree rooted in God's mountain. 'The mountain of thine inheritance' (har nachalatekha) — ultimately Mount Zion. 'The Sanctuary... which thy hands have established' (mikkedash... konenu yadekha) — the earthly sanctuary mirrors a heavenly reality that God's own hands have built. This vision leaps from the seashore to the Temple, connecting the Exodus to God's ultimate purpose: dwelling among His redeemed people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The purpose of redemption is habitation: God saves Israel in order to dwell with them. This sanctuary theology runs from Eden (Gen 2-3) through the Tabernacle and Temple to the incarnation ('the Word became flesh and dwelt/tabernacled among us,' John 1:14) and reaches its consummation in the New Jerusalem ('Behold, the tabernacle of God is with men,' Rev 21:3). The entire arc of Scripture is a sanctuary narrative: God creating, redeeming, and indwelling a people.

verses.kjv_text: The LORD shall reign for ever and ever.
verses.original_text: יְהוָה יִמְלֹךְ לְעֹלָם וָעֶד׃
verses.transliteration: YHVH yimlokh le'olam va'ed.
verses.study_note: The Song's final declaration — just five Hebrew words, yet one of the most profound theological statements in Scripture. 'The LORD shall reign' (YHVH yimlokh) — YHVH is king. Not 'will become king' but 'shall reign' — His kingship is eternal, not contingent. 'For ever and ever' (le'olam va'ed) — a doubled expression of eternity. The Song that began with 'I will sing' (ashirah — personal testimony) ends with 'The LORD shall reign' (YHVH yimlokh — cosmic proclamation). From personal experience to universal truth: the pattern of all authentic worship.
verses.doctrinal_note: The kingship of YHVH: this declaration is the seed from which all of Israel's monarchy theology grows — and ultimately the messianic hope. Every earthly king rules under YHVH's sovereignty (Psalm 2). The prophets anticipate a day when 'the LORD shall be king over all the earth' (Zech 14:9). Revelation echoes this verse: 'The Lord God omnipotent reigneth' (Rev 19:6) and 'he shall reign for ever and ever' (Rev 11:15). The Song of Moses at the sea and the Song of the Lamb in heaven declare the same truth.

verses.kjv_text: For the horse of Pharaoh went in with his chariots and with his horsemen into the sea, and the LORD brought again the waters of the sea upon them; but the children of Israel went on dry land in the midst of the sea.
verses.original_text: כִּי בָא סוּס פַּרְעֹה בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו בַּיָּם וַיָּשֶׁב יְהוָה עֲלֵהֶם אֶת־מֵי הַיָּם וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָלְכוּ בַיַּבָּשָׁה בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki va sus Par'oh berikbo uvfarashav bayyam vayyashev YHVH alehem et-mei hayyam uvenei Yisra'el halekhu vayyabbashah betokh hayyam.
verses.study_note: A prose narrative bridge between the Song of Moses and Miriam's song, summarizing the entire miracle. 'The LORD brought again the waters' (vayyashev YHVH... et-mei hayyam) — God restored the sea to its natural state, over the Egyptians. 'Dry land in the midst of the sea' (vayyabbashah betokh hayyam) — the paradox of redemption: solid ground in the middle of the ocean. The same event is simultaneously destruction and salvation, judgment and deliverance — depending entirely on which side one stands.

verses.kjv_text: And Miriam the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a timbrel in her hand; and all the women went out after her with timbrels and with dances.
verses.original_text: וַתִּקַּח מִרְיָם הַנְּבִיאָה אֲחוֹת אַהֲרֹן אֶת־הַתֹּף בְּיָדָהּ וַתֵּצֶאןָ כָל־הַנָּשִׁים אַחֲרֶיהָ בְּתֻפִּים וּבִמְחֹלֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Vattikkach Miryam hanevi'ah achot Aharon et-hattof beyadah vattetze'nah khol-hannashim achareha betuppim uvimecholot.
verses.study_note: Miriam is called 'the prophetess' (hanevi'ah) — the first woman to receive this title in Scripture. She is 'the sister of Aaron' — this identification may reflect Miriam's role in Aaron's early life or a separate literary tradition. She takes a 'timbrel' (tof — a hand drum) and leads all the women in music and dance. This is corporate worship with instruments, physical movement, and female leadership — all established at the foundational moment of Israel's worship.
verses.doctrinal_note: Miriam as the first named female worship leader in Scripture establishes that women participate in the prophetic and liturgical life of God's people from the very beginning. Joel 2:28-29 anticipates a day when 'your sons and your daughters shall prophesy' — Miriam prefigures the universal outpouring of the Spirit upon all flesh.

verses.kjv_text: And Miriam answered them, Sing ye to the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously; the horse and his rider hath he thrown into the sea.
verses.original_text: וַתַּעַן לָהֶם מִרְיָם שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה כִּי־גָאֹה גָּאָה סוּס וְרֹכְבוֹ רָמָה בַיָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vatta'an lahem Miryam shiru laYHVH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhevo ramah vayyam.
verses.study_note: 'Miriam answered them' (vatta'an lahem) — the verb 'anah' (to answer/respond) indicates antiphonal singing. Miriam's group responds to Moses' group, creating the first recorded antiphonal worship in Scripture. Her refrain repeats v.1 but shifts from 'I will sing' (ashirah — singular) to 'Sing ye' (shiru — plural imperative). She commands communal praise. Some scholars believe Miriam's couplet may be the oldest fragment of the Song, with Moses' expanded version being the full development.

verses.kjv_text: So Moses brought Israel from the Red sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur; and they went three days in the wilderness, and found no water.
verses.original_text: וַיַּסַּע מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִיַּם־סוּף וַיֵּצְאוּ אֶל־מִדְבַּר־שׁוּר וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁלֹשֶׁת־יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר וְלֹא־מָצְאוּ מָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyassa Mosheh et-Yisra'el miyyam-Suf vayyetze'u el-midbar-Shur vayyelekhu sheloshet-yamim bammidbar velo-matze'u mayim.
verses.study_note: An abrupt transition: from the highest worship to the harshest testing. 'Moses brought Israel' (vayyassa Mosheh) — 'nasa' means to pull up stakes, to march. The wilderness of Shur ('wall') lies east of Egypt. 'Three days... no water' — after the greatest miracle, the most basic provision is withheld. Three days — the biblical period of testing and transformation (Gen 22:4, Jonah 1:17, Matt 12:40). The immediate juxtaposition of miracle and trial is one of Scripture's most honest patterns.

verses.kjv_text: And when they came to Marah, they could not drink of the waters of Marah, for they were bitter: therefore the name of it was called Marah.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ מָרָתָה וְלֹא יָכְלוּ לִשְׁתֹּת מַיִם מִמָּרָה כִּי מָרִים הֵם עַל־כֵּן קָרָא־שְׁמָהּ מָרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u Maratah velo yakhelu lishtot mayim miMarah ki marim hem al-ken kara-shemah Marah.
verses.study_note: After three days without water, they find water — but it is bitter. 'Marah' (מָרָה) means 'bitter.' The threefold repetition of the root 'mar' (bitter) is emphatic: they came to Maratah (Bitter-place), they could not drink miMarah (from Bitter), because marim (bitter) they were. The place, the water, and the experience are all 'mar.' This is the first testing of the redeemed community. They sang in v.1; they will grumble in v.24. Naomi uses this same word: 'Call me Mara, for the Almighty hath dealt very bitterly with me' (Ruth 1:20).

verses.kjv_text: And the people murmured against Moses, saying, What shall we drink?
verses.original_text: וַיִּלֹּנוּ הָעָם עַל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר מַה־נִּשְׁתֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyillonu ha'am al-Mosheh lemor mah-nishteh.
verses.study_note: 'Murmured' (vayyillonu) — from 'lun,' to lodge a complaint, to grumble persistently. This is the first occurrence of the murmuring motif that will dominate the wilderness narrative (Exod 16:2, 17:3; Num 14:2). The complaint is directed 'against Moses' (al-Mosheh), not against God — yet Moses is God's representative. The question 'What shall we drink?' is a legitimate need expressed through illegitimate means — grumbling rather than prayer. The distance from 'I will sing to the LORD' (v.1) to 'What shall we drink?' (v.24) is the distance between faith and sight.

verses.kjv_text: And he cried unto the LORD; and the LORD shewed him a tree, which when he had cast into the waters, the waters were made sweet: there he made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there he proved them,
verses.original_text: וַיִּצְעַק אֶל־יְהוָה וַיּוֹרֵהוּ יְהוָה עֵץ וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶל־הַמַּיִם וַיִּמְתְּקוּ הַמָּיִם שָׁם שָׂם לוֹ חֹק וּמִשְׁפָּט וְשָׁם נִסָּהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyitz'ak el-YHVH vayyorehu YHVH etz vayyashlek el-hammayim vayyimteku hammayim sham sam lo chok umishpat vesham nissahu.
verses.study_note: Moses does what the people should have done: 'he cried unto the LORD' (vayyitz'ak). 'The LORD shewed him a tree' (vayyorehu... etz) — the verb 'yarah' means to teach, to direct (the same root as 'Torah'). God teaches Moses by showing him a tree. When cast into the water, the bitter becomes sweet (vayyimteku). The tree transforms the water — a powerful type of the cross, where the 'tree' of Calvary transforms the bitterness of human existence into the sweetness of redemption. 'There he made for them a statute and an ordinance' (chok umishpat) — at the site of testing, God establishes law. 'There he proved them' (nissahu) — from 'nasah,' to test.
verses.doctrinal_note: The tree cast into bitter waters is one of the richest types of the cross in the Old Testament. Peter declares: 'who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24). Galatians 3:13: 'Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree.' The cross transforms what is bitter and death-dealing into what is sweet and life-giving. The word 'yarah' (showed/taught) connecting to 'Torah' suggests that God's instruction itself is the remedy for life's bitterness.

verses.kjv_text: And said, If thou wilt diligently hearken to the voice of the LORD thy God, and wilt do that which is right in his sight, and wilt give ear to his commandments, and keep all his statutes, I will put none of these diseases upon thee, which I have brought upon the Egyptians: for I am the LORD that healeth thee.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אִם־שָׁמוֹעַ תִּשְׁמַע לְקוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ וְהַיָּשָׁר בְּעֵינָיו תַּעֲשֶׂה וְהַאֲזַנְתָּ לְמִצְוֺתָיו וְשָׁמַרְתָּ כָּל־חֻקָּיו כָּל־הַמַּחֲלָה אֲשֶׁר־שַׂמְתִּי בְמִצְרַיִם לֹא־אָשִׂים עָלֶיךָ כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה רֹפְאֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer im-shamo'a tishma lekol YHVH Elohekha vehayashar be'einav ta'aseh veha'azanta lemitzvotav veshamarta kol-chukkav kol-hammachalah asher-samti veMitzrayim lo-asim alekha ki ani YHVH rofe'ekha.
verses.study_note: A conditional covenant: 'If thou wilt diligently hearken' (im-shamo'a tishma — emphatic infinitive absolute: 'if hearing you will hear'). Four conditions: (1) hearken to YHVH's voice, (2) do what is right in His sight, (3) give ear to His commandments, (4) keep all His statutes. The promise: 'I will put none of these diseases upon thee which I have brought upon the Egyptians.' The climactic revelation: 'I am the LORD that healeth thee' (ani YHVH rofe'ekha) — the divine name 'YHVH Rofekha' (the LORD your Healer), revealed at the place of bitterness, not abundance.
verses.doctrinal_note: YHVH Rofekha — 'The LORD who heals you' — is the first compound divine name revealed after the Exodus. Significantly, this name is given at Marah (Bitterness), not at the Red Sea (Triumph). God reveals Himself as Healer precisely where His people experience suffering. This name finds its ultimate expression in Christ, who 'healed all that were sick, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, Himself took our infirmities and bare our sicknesses' (Matt 8:16-17). Jesus is YHVH Rofekha incarnate.

verses.kjv_text: And they came to Elim, where were twelve wells of water, and threescore and ten palm trees: and they encamped there by the waters.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹאוּ אֵילִמָה וְשָׁם שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה עֵינֹת מַיִם וְשִׁבְעִים תְּמָרִים וַיַּחֲנוּ־שָׁם עַל־הַמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo'u Elimah vesham shtem esreh einot mayim veshiv'im temarim vayyachanu-sham al-hammayim.
verses.study_note: After Marah's bitterness, God brings Israel to Elim — an oasis of abundance. 'Twelve wells' (shtem esreh einot) — one for each tribe. 'Seventy palm trees' (shiv'im temarim) — seventy corresponds to Jacob's family who entered Egypt (Gen 46:27) and later to the seventy elders (Exod 24:1, Num 11:16). The numbers are covenantal: God provides with exact precision. After the test comes the provision. The chapter that began with a song, moved through bitterness, and ends at an oasis traces the believer's journey: worship, testing, provision — all under God's sovereign direction. 'They encamped there by the waters' — rest beside living water, a foretaste of Psalm 23:2.

meta.overview: Exodus 15 is the great song of redemption — Israel's response to the miracle at the Red Sea. It is widely regarded as one of the oldest poems in the Hebrew Bible, composed at the moment of deliverance and preserved with its archaic Hebrew forms. The chapter divides into three movements: (1) The Song of Moses (vv.1-18), a triumphant hymn celebrating YHVH as the Divine Warrior who destroyed Pharaoh's army in the sea; (2) Miriam's antiphonal response (vv.20-21), the first worship-leading by a named woman in Scripture; and (3) The journey to Marah (vv.22-27), where the bitter waters are healed — a test of faith immediately following the greatest deliverance. The Song itself moves from personal testimony ('I will sing,' v.1) through a recounting of the sea event (vv.4-12) to a prophetic vision of God leading Israel to His holy dwelling (vv.13-17), culminating in the declaration 'The LORD shall reign for ever and ever' (v.18). Theologically, the chapter establishes the paradigm of salvation-then-worship that runs through all of Scripture. Israel is saved AT the sea and sings AFTER the sea — redemption precedes praise. The Song contains some of the most exalted titles for God in the Old Testament: 'my strength and song' (v.2), 'a man of war' (v.3), 'glorious in holiness' (v.11). It also anticipates the conquest of Canaan (vv.14-16) and the establishment of God's sanctuary (v.17) — connecting the Exodus to the entire redemptive program. In Revelation 15:3, the redeemed in heaven sing 'the song of Moses... and the song of the Lamb,' uniting this chapter with the ultimate consummation of salvation.
meta.theme: Redemption Celebrated — The Song of Deliverance & the God Who Heals
meta.keyWordMeaning: Song — a feminine noun indicating a complete, composed song of praise. The Song of the Sea is the first major hymn in Scripture, establishing the pattern of singing as the redeemed community's response to God's saving acts.
outline: The Song of Moses — Personal Praise (vv.1-5): 'I will sing to the LORD' — the God who triumphs gloriously
The Song of Moses — The Right Hand of God (vv.6-10): YHVH the Divine Warrior destroys the enemy
The Song of Moses — Incomparable Holiness (vv.11-13): 'Who is like thee?' — God's steadfast love leads His people
The Song of Moses — Prophetic Vision (vv.14-18): The nations tremble; God plants Israel at His holy mountain
The Narrative Bridge & Miriam's Song (vv.19-21): Miriam leads the women in antiphonal worship
The Waters of Marah (vv.22-27): Bitter water made sweet — 'I am the LORD that healeth thee'

verses.kjv_text: And they took their journey from Elim, and all the congregation of the children of Israel came unto the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after their departing out of the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וַיִּסְעוּ מֵאֵילִם וַיָּבֹאוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־מִדְבַּר־סִין אֲשֶׁר בֵּין־אֵילִם וּבֵין סִינָי בַּחֲמִשָּׁה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי לְצֵאתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyis'u me'Elim vayyavo'u kol-adat benei-Yisra'el el-midbar-Sin asher bein-Elim uvein Sinai bachamishah asar yom lachodesh hasheni letzetam me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Precisely one month after the Exodus (the 15th of the second month — they departed on the 15th of the first month). The journey moves from Elim ('palms' — the oasis of 12 springs and 70 palm trees, 15:27) into the 'wilderness of Sin' — not related to the English word 'sin' but a geographic name. The move from oasis to barren wilderness tests Israel's memory: will they remember God's provision at Elim, or will they define reality by present circumstances? The entire congregation (kol-adat) travels together — community and complaint are both corporate.

verses.kjv_text: And the whole congregation of the children of Israel murmured against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness:
verses.original_text: וַיִּלּוֹנוּ כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל עַל־מֹשֶׁה וְעַל־אַהֲרֹן בַּמִּדְבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyillonu kol-adat benei-Yisra'el al-Mosheh ve'al-Aharon bammidbar.
verses.study_note: The 'whole congregation murmured' (vayyillonu — from 'lun,' to murmur, to lodge a complaint). This is the second major grumbling episode (after Marah, 15:24). The verb 'lun' becomes a defining word for Israel's wilderness behavior — repeated throughout Numbers. They murmur against Moses and Aaron, but God hears it as directed against Himself (v.8). The wilderness (midbar) is the testing ground — where Israel's faith, gratitude, and trust are revealed under pressure.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel said unto them, Would to God we had died by the hand of the LORD in the land of Egypt, when we sat by the flesh pots, and when we did eat bread to the full; for ye have brought us forth into this wilderness, to kill this whole assembly with hunger.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵהֶם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִי־יִתֵּן מוּתֵנוּ בְיַד־יְהוָה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּשִׁבְתֵּנוּ עַל־סִיר הַבָּשָׂר בְּאָכְלֵנוּ לֶחֶם לָשֹׂבַע כִּי־הוֹצֵאתֶם אֹתָנוּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר הַזֶּה לְהָמִית אֶת־כָּל־הַקָּהָל הַזֶּה בָּרָעָב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru alehem benei Yisra'el mi-yitten mutenu veyad-YHVH be'eretz Mitzrayim beshivtenu al-sir habbasar be'okhlenu lechem lasova ki-hotzatem otanu el-hammidbar hazzeh lehamit et-kol-haqqahal hazzeh bara'av.
verses.study_note: A stunning complaint: 'Would to God we had died... in Egypt' (mi-yitten mutenu — 'who would give our dying' — wishing for death). They romanticize slavery: 'when we sat by the flesh pots' (sir habbasar — pots of meat) and 'ate bread to the full' (lechem lasova). Memory is selective — they recall Egypt's food but not Egypt's whips. They accuse Moses and Aaron of deliberate murder: 'to kill this whole assembly with hunger.' The complaint contains a theological reversal: they would rather have died under Pharaoh's hand than live under God's provision. The 'flesh pots' of bondage are preferred to the freedom of faith.
verses.doctrinal_note: The longing for Egypt is the perennial temptation of the redeemed: to prefer the known comforts of bondage to the unknown provisions of faith. Israel's romanticized memory of slavery reveals a heart that has not yet been transformed by the experience of deliverance. Physical liberation precedes spiritual transformation — God brought Israel out of Egypt, but Egypt had not yet come out of Israel. The same pattern applies to believers: salvation is instantaneous, but sanctification is a wilderness journey.

verses.kjv_text: Then said the LORD unto Moses, Behold, I will rain bread from heaven for you; and the people shall go out and gather a certain rate every day, that I may prove them, whether they will walk in my law, or no.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הִנְנִי מַמְטִיר לָכֶם לֶחֶם מִן־הַשָּׁמָיִם וְיָצָא הָעָם וְלָקְטוּ דְּבַר־יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ לְמַעַן אֲנַסֶּנּוּ הֲיֵלֵךְ בְּתוֹרָתִי אִם־לֹא׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHVH el-Mosheh hineni mamtir lakhem lechem min-hashamayim veyatza ha'am velaketu devar-yom beyomo lema'an anassenu hayelekh betorati im-lo.
verses.study_note: God responds to grumbling not with wrath but with grace: 'I will rain bread from heaven' (mamtir... lechem min-hashamayim). The verb 'mamtir' (to cause to rain) is the same used for God raining judgment on Sodom (Gen 19:24) — but here God rains provision, not destruction. 'A certain rate every day' (devar-yom beyomo — literally 'the matter of a day in its day') — daily portion, daily dependence. Then the purpose is revealed: 'that I may prove them' (anassenu — test, try). The manna is not merely food — it is a test of obedience. 'Whether they will walk in my law, or no' (hayelekh betorati) — the Torah is mentioned before Sinai, confirming that God's instruction existed before the formal covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The manna serves a dual purpose: provision and probation. God feeds and tests simultaneously. The daily gathering requirement creates enforced dependence — Israel cannot stockpile grace. This is the Old Testament foundation for 'Give us this day our daily bread' (Matt 6:11). The manna tests whether Israel will trust God's sufficiency day by day or attempt to secure their own future through hoarding. Faith is daily, not once-for-all.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall come to pass, that on the sixth day they shall prepare that which they bring in; and it shall be twice as much as they gather daily.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה בַּיּוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי וְהֵכִינוּ אֵת אֲשֶׁר־יָבִיאוּ וְהָיָה מִשְׁנֶה עַל אֲשֶׁר־יִלְקְטוּ יוֹם יוֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah bayyom hashishi vehekinu et asher-yavi'u vehayah mishneh al asher-yilketu yom yom.
verses.study_note: The Sabbath principle is introduced through the manna before it is formally given at Sinai (ch.20). On the sixth day, a double portion (mishneh — 'double, second') falls. This pre-Sinai Sabbath provision proves that the Sabbath rest is not merely Mosaic legislation but a creation principle embedded in God's providence. God adjusts His supernatural provision to teach the rhythm of work and rest. 'Prepare' (hekinu — from 'kun,' to establish, to prepare) — the sixth day requires extra work to honor the seventh day's rest.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses and Aaron said unto all the children of Israel, At even, then ye shall know that the LORD hath brought you out from the land of Egypt:
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן אֶל־כָּל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל עֶרֶב וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי יְהוָה הוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh ve'Aharon el-kol-benei Yisra'el erev vidatem ki YHVH hotzi etkhem me'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: Moses and Aaron announce a two-part revelation: 'at even' (erev — evening) and 'in the morning' (boker — v.7). The evening brings quail — 'ye shall know that the LORD brought you out.' The morning brings manna — 'ye shall see the glory of the LORD' (v.7). Knowledge (yada) comes through God's acts, not abstract teaching. God proves Himself by providing exactly what was complained about: meat (evening) and bread (morning). He answers the complaint with generosity rather than condemnation.

verses.kjv_text: And in the morning, then ye shall see the glory of the LORD; for that he heareth your murmurings against the LORD: and what are we, that ye murmur against us?
verses.original_text: וּבֹקֶר וּרְאִיתֶם אֶת־כְּבוֹד יְהוָה בְּשָׁמְעוֹ אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם עַל־יְהוָה וְנַחְנוּ מָה כִּי תַלִּינוּ עָלֵינוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Uvoker ure'item et-kevod YHVH beshom'o et-telunnoteikhem al-YHVH venachnu mah ki tallinu aleinu.
verses.study_note: The 'glory of the LORD' (kevod YHVH) will be seen in the morning — the manna itself is a manifestation of divine glory. God's provision is His glory made visible. Moses and Aaron redirect the complaint: 'What are we?' (venachnu mah) — the leaders are not the target; the murmuring is ultimately 'against the LORD' (al-YHVH). Every complaint against God's appointed leaders is, at root, a complaint against God Himself. The word 'telunnoteikhem' (your murmurings) — from the same root as v.2 — is repeated to emphasize the seriousness of the sin.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's glory (kavod) is revealed not only in spectacular theophanies but in daily provision. The manna on the ground is 'the glory of the LORD' — God's weight, His substance, His reality made tangible in bread. This redefines glory: it is not distant and overwhelming but near and nourishing. Jesus embodies this: 'The Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory' (John 1:14). God's glory comes down to feed His people.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, This shall be, when the LORD shall give you in the evening flesh to eat, and in the morning bread to the full; for that the LORD heareth your murmurings which ye murmur against him: and what are we? your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה בְּתֵת יְהוָה לָכֶם בָּעֶרֶב בָּשָׂר לֶאֱכֹל וְלֶחֶם בַּבֹּקֶר לִשְׂבֹּעַ בִּשְׁמֹעַ יְהוָה אֶת־תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר־אַתֶּם מַלִּינִם עָלָיו וְנַחְנוּ מָה לֹא עָלֵינוּ תְלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם כִּי עַל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh betet YHVH lakhem ba'erev basar le'ekhol velechem babboker lisboa bishmo'a YHVH et-telunnoteikhem asher-attem mallinim alav venachnu mah lo aleinu telunnoteikhem ki al-YHVH.
verses.study_note: Moses clarifies with precision: evening brings flesh (basar), morning brings bread (lechem) 'to the full' (lisboa — to satisfaction). Then the rebuke: 'your murmurings are not against us, but against the LORD' (ki al-YHVH). The repetition of 'murmurings' (telunnoteikhem — three times in vv.7-8) drives the point home. Moses and Aaron are mere instruments; the real relationship is between Israel and YHVH. To reject God's provision through His agents is to reject God Himself.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses spake unto Aaron, Say unto all the congregation of the children of Israel, Come near before the LORD: for he hath heard your murmurings.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־אַהֲרֹן אֱמֹר אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל קִרְבוּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה כִּי שָׁמַע אֵת תְּלֻנֹּתֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh el-Aharon emor el-kol-adat benei Yisra'el kirvu lifnei YHVH ki shama et telunnoteikhem.
verses.study_note: 'Come near before the LORD' (kirvu lifnei YHVH) — rather than driving the murmurers away, God invites them closer. The appropriate response to divine hearing is not fear but approach. 'He hath heard' (shama) — God hears complaint the same way He hears prayer. Aaron speaks (his priestly role as spokesman) while Moses listens to God. The congregation is summoned to a corporate encounter with God — their grumbling will be answered by His presence, not merely His provisions.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as Aaron spake unto the whole congregation of the children of Israel, that they looked toward the wilderness, and, behold, the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי כְּדַבֵּר אַהֲרֹן אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּפְנוּ אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר וְהִנֵּה כְּבוֹד יְהוָה נִרְאָה בֶּעָנָן׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi kedabber Aharon el-kol-adat benei Yisra'el vayyifnu el-hammidbar vehineh kevod YHVH nir'ah be'anan.
verses.study_note: While Aaron speaks, the people turn and see: 'the glory of the LORD appeared in the cloud' (kevod YHVH nir'ah be'anan). The cloud — the shekinah presence that guides Israel (13:21-22) — now blazes with visible glory. God's response to grumbling is theophany: He shows up. The direction is significant — they 'looked toward the wilderness' (el-hammidbar) — the very place they feared becomes the location of divine revelation. God appears not in the places of comfort but in the places of need.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber YHVH el-Mosheh lemor.
verses.study_note: The standard revelation formula: 'And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the divine speech act that initiates covenant instruction. After the theophany of v.10, God now gives specific instructions. The pattern is consistent: God appears, then God speaks. Presence precedes precept.

verses.kjv_text: I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel: speak unto them, saying, At even ye shall eat flesh, and in the morning ye shall be filled with bread; and ye shall know that I am the LORD your God.
verses.original_text: שָׁמַעְתִּי אֶת־תְּלוּנֹּת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל דַּבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם לֵאמֹר בֵּין הָעַרְבַּיִם תֹּאכְלוּ בָשָׂר וּבַבֹּקֶר תִּשְׂבְּעוּ־לָחֶם וִידַעְתֶּם כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Shamati et-telunnot benei Yisra'el dabber alehem lemor bein ha'arbayim tokhelu vasar uvabboker tisbe'u-lachem vidatem ki ani YHVH Eloheikhem.
verses.study_note: God's response is remarkable: 'I have heard' (shamati) — He acknowledges the complaint without dismissing it. Then pure grace: flesh in the evening, bread to fullness in the morning. The purpose: 'ye shall know that I am the LORD your God' (vidatem ki ani YHVH Eloheikhem) — the recognition formula central to Exodus theology (cf. 6:7, 7:5, 14:4). God's provision is not merely charitable — it is revelatory. Through manna, Israel will know God's character: faithful, generous, sovereign, personal ('your God').
verses.doctrinal_note: God answers grumbling with grace — not because the complaint is justified but because His character is generous. The manna is designed to produce knowledge of God ('ye shall know that I am the LORD'). Every act of divine provision is simultaneously an act of divine revelation. God feeds the body to teach the soul. Jesus embodies this principle: He fed 5,000 with bread and then declared Himself the Bread of Life (John 6). Physical provision points to spiritual reality.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that at even the quails came up, and covered the camp: and in the morning the dew lay round about the host.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בָעֶרֶב וַתַּעַל הַשְּׂלָו וַתְּכַס אֶת־הַמַּחֲנֶה וּבַבֹּקֶר הָיְתָה שִׁכְבַת הַטַּל סָבִיב לַמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi va'erev vatta'al hasselav vattekhas et-hammachaneh uvabboker hayetah shikhvat hattal saviv lammachaneh.
verses.study_note: The fulfillment: quail in the evening, dew around the camp in the morning. The 'quail' (selav) — migratory birds that cross the Sinai, flying low and exhausted. God uses natural phenomena supernaturally — the timing and quantity are miraculous. The dew 'lay round about' (shikhvat hattal saviv) — the manna comes hidden within the dew, like a gift wrapped in moisture. The camp is surrounded — provision covers them completely. God's grace is not sparse but encompassing.

verses.kjv_text: And when the dew that lay was gone up, behold, upon the face of the wilderness there lay a small round thing, as small as the hoar frost on the ground.
verses.original_text: וַתַּעַל שִׁכְבַת הַטָּל וְהִנֵּה עַל־פְּנֵי הַמִּדְבָּר דַּק מְחֻסְפָּס דַּק כַּכְּפֹר עַל־הָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Vatta'al shikhvat hattal vehineh al-penei hammidbar daq mechuspas daq kakkefor al-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: When the dew evaporates, the manna is revealed: 'a small round thing' (daq mechuspas) — 'daq' means thin, fine, small; 'mechuspas' is a rare word appearing only here, meaning flaky, scale-like, or granular. It resembles 'hoar frost' (kefor — white frost). The description emphasizes its unfamiliarity — nothing like this existed before. It appears on 'the face of the wilderness' — the barren desert surface becomes a table spread by God. Where there was nothing, God provides everything.

verses.kjv_text: And when the children of Israel saw it, they said one to another, It is manna: for they wist not what it was. And Moses said unto them, This is the bread which the LORD hath given you to eat.
verses.original_text: וַיִּרְאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֹּאמְרוּ אִישׁ אֶל־אָחִיו מָן הוּא כִּי לֹא יָדְעוּ מַה־הוּא וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֲלֵהֶם הוּא הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר נָתַן יְהוָה לָכֶם לְאָכְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyir'u venei-Yisra'el vayyomeru ish el-achiv man hu ki lo yade'u mah-hu vayomer Mosheh alehem hu hallechem asher natan YHVH lakhem le'okhlah.
verses.study_note: The naming moment: 'man hu' — 'What is it?' (or 'It is manna'). The word 'man' is likely an Aramaic/archaic interrogative meaning 'what?' Israel names the substance by their confusion — they literally don't know what it is. The name preserves the mystery: God's provision is beyond human categorization. Moses provides the interpretation: 'This is the bread which the LORD hath given you.' The people asked 'What is it?' — Moses answers 'It is God's gift.' Understanding follows faith; they must eat it before they fully comprehend it. Jesus makes the same demand: 'I am the bread of life' — eat, then understand (John 6:35,51).
verses.doctrinal_note: The manna is named by a question: 'What is it?' (man hu). This preserves a crucial theological principle: God's provision often exceeds our comprehension. Israel must consume what they cannot classify. Faith feeds on what reason cannot fully explain. The name 'manna' becomes a permanent witness to the mystery of grace — God gives what we cannot produce, understand, or replicate. Jesus identifies Himself as the fulfillment: 'I am the living bread which came down from heaven' (John 6:51).

verses.kjv_text: This is the thing which the LORD hath commanded, Gather of it every man according to his eating, an omer for every man, according to the number of your persons; take ye every man for them which are in his tents.
verses.original_text: זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה לִקְטוּ מִמֶּנּוּ אִישׁ לְפִי אָכְלוֹ עֹמֶר לַגֻּלְגֹּלֶת מִסְפַּר נַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אִישׁ לַאֲשֶׁר בְּאָהֳלוֹ תִּקָּחוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Zeh haddavar asher tzivvah YHVH liktu mimmennu ish lefi okhlo omer laggulgolet mispar nafshotekhem ish la'asher be'oholo tikkachu.
verses.study_note: The gathering rule: an 'omer per head' (omer laggulgolet — literally 'an omer per skull'). An omer is approximately 2.3 liters — enough for a day's sustenance. The measure is per person ('according to the number of your souls'), not per household — individual need, not collective accumulation, determines the portion. Each person gathers for those 'in his tent' — heads of household provide for their dependents. The system enforces equality: no one gets more than they need, no one gets less.

verses.kjv_text: And the children of Israel did so, and gathered, some more, some less.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיִּלְקְטוּ הַמַּרְבֶּה וְהַמַּמְעִיט׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'asu-khen benei Yisra'el vayyilketu hammarbeh vehammam'it.
verses.study_note: Israel obeys — they go out and gather. Some gather more (hammarbeh), some less (hammam'it). The variation is natural — different energy, different ability, different family sizes. But as v.18 will reveal, the result is miraculously equalized. Human effort varies; divine provision is constant. Paul quotes this passage directly in 2 Corinthians 8:15 to teach the principle of economic equality in the early church.

verses.kjv_text: And when they did mete it with an omer, he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack; they gathered every man according to his eating.
verses.original_text: וַיָּמֹדּוּ בָעֹמֶר וְלֹא הֶעְדִּיף הַמַּרְבֶּה וְהַמַּמְעִיט לֹא הֶחְסִיר אִישׁ לְפִי־אָכְלוֹ לָקָטוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyamoddu va'omer velo he'edif hammarbeh vehammam'it lo hechsir ish lefi-okhlo lakatu.
verses.study_note: The miracle of equalization: when measured, those who gathered much (hammarbeh) had 'nothing over' (lo he'edif), and those who gathered little (hammam'it) had 'no lack' (lo hechsir). The omer standard normalized everything. This is divine economics: sufficiency for all, surplus for none. Paul cites this verse verbatim in 2 Corinthians 8:15 as the basis for Christian generosity — the abundance of one should supply the lack of another.
verses.doctrinal_note: The manna teaches a radical economics of grace: God provides sufficiency, not surplus. The person who gathers much cannot hoard; the person who gathers little does not starve. This is not enforced redistribution but supernatural equalization — God's provision adjusts to need. Paul applies this principle to the church: 'that there may be equality' (2 Cor 8:14). In the economy of grace, God provides enough for all when His people trust and share.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said, Let no man leave of it till the morning.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֲלֵהֶם אִישׁ אַל־יוֹתֵר מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh alehem ish al-yoter mimmennu ad-boker.
verses.study_note: The anti-hoarding command: 'Let no man leave of it till the morning' (al-yoter — do not leave over, do not keep surplus). This is the daily faith requirement — today's provision is for today. Keeping it overnight demonstrates distrust in tomorrow's provision. The command creates a rhythm of daily dependence that mirrors the Lord's Prayer: 'Give us this day our daily bread' (Matt 6:11). Security is in the Giver, not in the gift.

verses.kjv_text: Notwithstanding they hearkened not unto Moses; but some of them left of it until the morning, and it bred worms, and stank: and Moses was wroth with them.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא־שָׁמְעוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וַיּוֹתִרוּ אֲנָשִׁים מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר וַיָּרֻם תּוֹלָעִים וַיִּבְאַשׁ וַיִּקְצֹף עֲלֵהֶם מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Velo-shame'u el-Mosheh vayyotiru anashim mimmennu ad-boker vayyarum tola'im vayyiv'ash vayyiktzof alehem Mosheh.
verses.study_note: Disobedience: some hoard overnight. The result is graphic — 'it bred worms and stank' (vayyarum tola'im vayyiv'ash). The manna that was pure provision becomes corruption when hoarded. Grace cannot be stored apart from its daily renewal. 'Moses was wroth' (vayyiktzof) — angry, because hoarding reveals distrust in God's faithfulness. The worms and stench are the physical manifestation of a spiritual reality: trying to secure yourself apart from daily dependence on God produces decay.

verses.kjv_text: And they gathered it every morning, every man according to his eating: and when the sun waxed hot, it melted.
verses.original_text: וַיִּלְקְטוּ אֹתוֹ בַּבֹּקֶר בַּבֹּקֶר אִישׁ כְּפִי אָכְלוֹ וְחַם הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְנָמָס׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyilketu oto babboker babboker ish kefi okhlo vecham hashemesh venamas.
verses.study_note: 'Morning by morning' (babboker babboker — emphatic repetition) — the manna requires daily early rising. When the sun grows hot, 'it melted' (venamas). Grace has a window — there is an appointed time to receive God's provision. Those who sleep in or delay miss it. The daily rhythm creates discipline: early rising, immediate gathering, prompt obedience. Spiritual provision, like manna, requires attentiveness and timeliness. Lamentations 3:22-23 echoes this: 'His mercies are new every morning.'

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that on the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, two omers for one man: and all the rulers of the congregation came and told Moses.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשִּׁשִּׁי לָקְטוּ לֶחֶם מִשְׁנֶה שְׁנֵי הָעֹמֶר לָאֶחָד וַיָּבֹאוּ כָּל־נְשִׂיאֵי הָעֵדָה וַיַּגִּידוּ לְמֹשֶׁה׃
