verses.transliteration: Ve'attah im-shamoa tishme'u beqoli ushemartem et-beriti viheyitem li segullah mikkol-ha'ammim ki-li kol-ha'aretz.
verses.study_note: The conditional clause: 'if ye will obey my voice indeed' (im shamoa tishme'u) — the emphatic infinitive absolute intensifies the demand for genuine, total obedience. Two requirements: obey God's voice and keep His covenant. The reward is extraordinary: Israel will be God's 'peculiar treasure' (segullah). The segullah was a king's personal treasure — his private, cherished wealth set apart from the common treasury. Israel is not merely one nation among many but God's personal possession, His crown jewel among all peoples. The universal qualifier 'for all the earth is mine' prevents any misunderstanding: God's choosing Israel does not mean He has abandoned the nations. He owns everything; but Israel holds a unique, treasured position in His economy of redemption.
verses.doctrinal_note: The concept of 'segullah' (peculiar treasure / special possession) becomes a golden thread through Scripture. Moses reaffirms it in Deuteronomy 7:6 and 14:2; Malachi 3:17 promises a future 'segullah' of the faithful remnant; and Peter applies it directly to the Church in 1 Peter 2:9 — 'a peculiar people' (laos eis peripoiesin). The New Testament community inherits the identity first offered to Israel at Sinai. Election is always for mission: Israel is treasured not for hoarding but for displaying God's glory to all nations.

verses.kjv_text: And ye shall be unto me a kingdom of priests, and an holy nation. These are the words which thou shalt speak unto the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: וְאַתֶּם תִּהְיוּ־לִי מַמְלֶכֶת כֹּהֲנִים וְגוֹי קָדוֹשׁ אֵלֶּה הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר תְּדַבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attem tihyu-li mamlechet kohanim vegoy qadosh elleh haddevarim asher tedabber el-benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: Two astonishing titles: 'a kingdom of priests' (mamlechet kohanim) and 'a holy nation' (goy qadosh). The first means either a kingdom composed entirely of priests or a royal priesthood — every Israelite has direct access to God and mediates His presence to the world. The second means a nation set apart, consecrated, distinct from all others by its relationship to YHWH. Together, these titles define Israel's vocation: priestly mediation (representing God to the world and the world to God) and holy distinction (moral and spiritual separation for divine purposes). The phrase 'these are the words' (elleh haddevarim) is a solemn formula — this is direct divine speech, not Moses's paraphrase. God is defining Israel's very identity and mission.
verses.doctrinal_note: Peter directly quotes this verse in 1 Peter 2:9: 'But ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people.' The identity first offered to national Israel at Sinai is now applied to the Church — Jew and Gentile united in Christ. Revelation 1:6 and 5:10 extend this further: Christ 'hath made us kings and priests unto God.' The trajectory is clear: what Sinai proposed, the Cross accomplished. Every believer is now a priest with direct access to God through Christ (Heb 4:16), and the Church is the 'holy nation' scattered among all nations to declare God's excellencies.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses came and called for the elders of the people, and laid before their faces all these words which the LORD commanded him.
verses.original_text: וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וַיִּקְרָא לְזִקְנֵי הָעָם וַיָּשֶׂם לִפְנֵיהֶם אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה אֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyavo Mosheh vayyikra leziqnei ha'am vayyasem lifneihem et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh asher tzivvahu YHWH.
verses.study_note: Moses descends and assembles the elders — the representative leaders of the twelve tribes. He 'laid before their faces' (vayyasem lifneihem) — the verb 'sam' (to place, set) suggests careful, deliberate presentation. Moses does not embellish or interpret; he places God's exact words before the people. The phrase 'all these words' (kol haddevarim ha'elleh) confirms complete, faithful transmission. This establishes the prophetic principle: the messenger delivers the message without alteration. Moses is the prototype of prophetic faithfulness — he speaks only what God commands.

verses.kjv_text: And all the people answered together, and said, All that the LORD hath spoken we will do. And Moses returned the words of the people unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲנוּ כָל־הָעָם יַחְדָּו וַיֹּאמְרוּ כֹּל אֲשֶׁר־דִּבֶּר יְהוָה נַעֲשֶׂה וַיָּשֶׁב מֹשֶׁה אֶת־דִּבְרֵי הָעָם אֶל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyaanu khol-ha'am yachdav vayomeru kol asher-dibber YHWH na'aseh vayyashev Mosheh et-divrei ha'am el-YHWH.
verses.study_note: The people respond 'together' (yachdav) — unanimously, as one voice. Their confession 'all that the LORD hath spoken we will do' (kol asher dibber YHWH na'aseh) is repeated three times in the Sinai narrative (here, 24:3, and 24:7). The verb 'na'aseh' (we will do) is a solemn commitment to covenant obedience. Moses then 'returned' (vayyashev) the people's words to God — acting as mediator in both directions, carrying God's word down and the people's answer up. This bilateral exchange defines covenant: God proposes, the people consent, and the mediator facilitates. The whole scene is a formal covenant ratification ceremony.
verses.doctrinal_note: Israel's confident declaration 'we will do' (na'aseh) reveals both the nobility and the tragedy of human covenant response. The willingness is sincere but the capacity is lacking — within forty days they will worship the golden calf (Exod 32). This exposes the fundamental problem that the Old Covenant cannot solve: the law is holy, but human flesh is weak (Rom 8:3). The New Covenant addresses this by writing the law on the heart (Jer 31:33) and empowering obedience through the indwelling Spirit (Ezek 36:26-27).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Lo, I come unto thee in a thick cloud, that the people may hear when I speak with thee, and believe thee for ever. And Moses told the words of the people unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי בָּא אֵלֶיךָ בְּעַב הֶעָנָן בַּעֲבוּר יִשְׁמַע הָעָם בְּדַבְּרִי עִמָּךְ וְגַם־בְּךָ יַאֲמִינוּ לְעוֹלָם וַיַּגֵּד מֹשֶׁה אֶת־דִּבְרֵי הָעָם אֶל־יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh hinneh anokhi ba elekha be'av he'anan ba'avur yishma ha'am bedabberi immakh vegam-bekha ya'aminu le'olam vayyagged Mosheh et-divrei ha'am el-YHWH.
verses.study_note: God announces He will come in a 'thick cloud' (av he'anan) — the cloud that simultaneously reveals and conceals. The purpose is twofold: the people will 'hear' God speaking with Moses, and this will establish Moses's prophetic authority 'for ever' (le'olam). The thick cloud protects the people from the full weight of God's glory (no man can see God and live — Exod 33:20) while allowing them to witness the divine communication. This is revelation through mediation — God accommodates His infinite glory to finite human capacity. The cloud becomes a permanent symbol of divine presence: it leads Israel by day (Exod 13:21), fills the tabernacle (Exod 40:34), and descends on Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10-11).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go unto the people, and sanctify them to day and to morrow, and let them wash their clothes,
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵךְ אֶל־הָעָם וְקִדַּשְׁתָּם הַיּוֹם וּמָחָר וְכִבְּסוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh lekh el-ha'am veqiddashtam hayyom umachar vekhibbesu simlotam.
verses.study_note: God commands consecration — 'sanctify them' (veqiddashtam). The verb 'qadash' means to set apart, to make holy, to consecrate for sacred use. This two-day preparation period involves outward and inward purification. Washing garments (vekhibbesu simlotam) is the visible, physical symbol of spiritual cleansing. The concept is clear: encountering the holy God requires preparation. One does not stumble casually into the divine presence. This principle runs through all of Scripture: the priests wash before service (Lev 8), the worshiper purifies before approaching the sanctuary, and the New Testament calls believers to 'cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit' (2 Cor 7:1).

verses.kjv_text: And be ready against the third day: for the third day the LORD will come down in the sight of all the people upon mount Sinai.
verses.original_text: וְהָיוּ נְכֹנִים לַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי כִּי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי יֵרֵד יְהוָה לְעֵינֵי כָל־הָעָם עַל־הַר סִינָי׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayu nekhonim layyom hashelishi ki bayyom hashelishi yered YHWH le'einei khol-ha'am al-har Sinai.
verses.study_note: The 'third day' (yom hashelishi) becomes a loaded theological marker. After two days of consecration, God descends on the third day. The verb 'come down' (yered) is extraordinary — the transcendent God of heaven voluntarily descends to earth. 'In the sight of all the people' (le'einei khol ha'am) — this is a public, national revelation, not a private mystical experience. Two to three million people witness the theophany. The entire nation sees, hears, and trembles. This is the most public divine manifestation in the Old Testament, establishing beyond all doubt that YHWH is the living God who acts in history before witnesses.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'third day' pattern carries deep typological significance throughout Scripture. Abraham finds the place of sacrifice on the third day (Gen 22:4). Joseph releases his brothers on the third day (Gen 42:18). Esther approaches the king on the third day (Esth 5:1). Jonah is delivered on the third day (Jonah 1:17). Hosea prophesies 'after two days... in the third day he will raise us up' (Hos 6:2). And ultimately, Christ rises from the dead on the third day (1 Cor 15:4). At Sinai, God descends to meet His people on the third day; in the resurrection, God raises His Son to meet all peoples on the third day.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt set bounds unto the people round about, saying, Take heed to yourselves, that ye go not up into the mount, or touch the border of it: whosoever toucheth the mount shall be surely put to death:
verses.original_text: וְהִגְבַּלְתָּ אֶת־הָעָם סָבִיב לֵאמֹר הִשָּׁמְרוּ לָכֶם עֲלוֹת בָּהָר וּנְגֹעַ בְּקָצֵהוּ כָּל־הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּהָר מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehigbalta et-ha'am saviv lemor hishamru lakhem alot bahar unegoah beqatzehu kol-hannogeah bahar mot yumat.
verses.study_note: God commands Moses to 'set bounds' (higbalta) — physical barriers marking the sacred perimeter around the mountain. The boundary is absolute: no person or animal may touch even the border (qatzehu) of the mountain, on penalty of death. This is not arbitrary harshness but holy necessity — God's unmediated presence is lethal to sinful humanity. The boundary teaches a fundamental theological lesson: holiness creates distinction. There is a qualitative difference between the sacred and the common, between God's space and human space. The tabernacle and temple will formalize this same principle with their graduated zones of holiness.

verses.kjv_text: There shall not an hand touch it, but he shall surely be stoned, or shot through; whether it be beast or man, it shall not live: when the trumpet soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount.
verses.original_text: לֹא־תִגַּע בּוֹ יָד כִּי־סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל אוֹ־יָרֹה יִיָּרֶה אִם־בְּהֵמָה אִם־אִישׁ לֹא יִחְיֶה בִּמְשֹׁךְ הַיֹּבֵל הֵמָּה יַעֲלוּ בָהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Lo-tigga bo yad ki-saqol yissaqel o-yaroh yiyareh im-behemah im-ish lo yichyeh bimshokh hayyovel hemmah ya'alu vahar.
verses.study_note: The execution method is specified: stoning or shooting (with arrows) — not touching the violator, since even the executioner must not enter the sacred zone. The prohibition covers both beast and man — the holiness is not a moral category (animals have no moral guilt) but an ontological reality; the raw presence of God is incompatible with unredeemed flesh. The final clause is remarkable: 'when the trumpet (yovel) soundeth long, they shall come up to the mount.' The yovel (jubilee horn / ram's horn) signals the eventual invitation to approach. The same God who sets boundaries also opens access — but on His terms and in His timing. This foreshadows the tearing of the temple veil (Matt 27:51), when the barrier between God and humanity was permanently removed through Christ's death.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses went down from the mount unto the people, and sanctified the people; and they washed their clothes.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה מִן־הָהָר אֶל־הָעָם וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֶת־הָעָם וַיְכַבְּסוּ שִׂמְלֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyered Mosheh min-hahar el-ha'am vayeqaddesh et-ha'am vayekhabbesu simlotam.
verses.study_note: Moses descends and executes the consecration. He 'sanctified the people' (vayeqaddesh et-ha'am) — the Piel intensive form indicates Moses actively consecrated them through ritual and instruction. They respond by washing their garments. The up-and-down movement of Moses on the mountain is constant throughout chapters 19-24 — he is the shuttle between heaven and earth, the mediator who traverses the boundary that the people cannot cross. This physical pattern of ascent and descent mirrors the spiritual reality of mediation.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto the people, Be ready against the third day: come not at your wives.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־הָעָם הֱיוּ נְכֹנִים לִשְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים אַל־תִּגְּשׁוּ אֶל־אִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer el-ha'am heyu nekhonim lishloshet yamim al-tiggeshu el-ishah.
verses.study_note: Moses adds a specific instruction from God: sexual abstinence for the three-day preparation period. 'Come not at your wives' (al-tiggeshu el-ishah) is not a statement about the impurity of sexuality but about the total consecration required for the divine encounter. Just as the Levitical priests abstained before sacred service (1 Sam 21:4-5), so the entire 'kingdom of priests' (v.6) must consecrate themselves completely. The body participates in holiness alongside the soul. This instruction underscores the awesome gravity of what is about to happen — God Himself will descend, and every dimension of human life must be oriented toward that encounter.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on the third day in the morning, that there were thunders and lightnings, and a thick cloud upon the mount, and the voice of the trumpet exceeding loud; so that all the people that was in the camp trembled.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בִּהְיֹת הַבֹּקֶר וַיְהִי קֹלֹת וּבְרָקִים וְעָנָן כָּבֵד עַל־הָהָר וְקֹל שֹׁפָר חָזָק מְאֹד וַיֶּחֱרַד כָּל־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּחֲנֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi vayyom hashelishi biheyot habboqer vayehi qolot uveraqim ve'anan kaved al-hahar veqol shofar chazaq me'od vayyecherad kol-ha'am asher bamachaneh.
verses.study_note: The theophany begins. The narrative explodes with sensory detail: thunders (qolot — literally 'voices'), lightnings (veraqim), a thick/heavy cloud (anan kaved), and the voice of a trumpet (qol shofar) 'exceeding loud' (chazaq me'od). Every sense is overwhelmed. The 'voices' (qolot) may be thunder or the sound of God's own voice reverberating — the ambiguity is intentional. The trumpet is not blown by human hands; it is a supernatural shofar blast that grows louder and louder (v.19). The result: 'all the people trembled' (vayyecherad kol ha'am). The verb 'charad' means to tremble with terror, to quake with dread. This is not gentle awe but bone-deep, existential terror before the living God. Hebrews 12:18-21 recalls this moment: 'so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The Sinai theophany establishes the paradigm for every subsequent divine manifestation: the prophetic visions of Isaiah 6, Ezekiel 1, and Daniel 7 all echo Sinai's thunder, fire, cloud, and terror. More significantly, Pentecost (Acts 2) deliberately parallels Sinai: wind (like thunder), tongues of fire (like Sinai's fire), and divine speech in languages (like the 'voices' at Sinai). Jewish tradition held that at Sinai, God's voice divided into seventy languages for all nations. At Pentecost, the Spirit speaks in the languages of every nation under heaven. Sinai gave the Law written on stone; Pentecost gave the Spirit writing on hearts.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses brought forth the people out of the camp to meet with God; and they stood at the nether part of the mount.
verses.original_text: וַיּוֹצֵא מֹשֶׁה אֶת־הָעָם לִקְרַאת הָאֱלֹהִים מִן־הַמַּחֲנֶה וַיִּתְיַצְּבוּ בְּתַחְתִּית הָהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyotze Mosheh et-ha'am liqrat haElohim min-hammachaneh vayyityatzvu betachtit hahar.
verses.study_note: Moses leads the people 'to meet God' (liqrat haElohim) — the language of a formal appointment, a arranged encounter. They 'stood' (vayyityatzvu) — the Hithpael form of 'yatzav' implies positioning themselves deliberately, taking a firm stance. They stand at the 'nether part' (tachtit — the lowest part, the base, the foot) of the mountain. The spatial arrangement is deeply symbolic: God is at the summit, the people are at the base, and Moses moves between them. The entire scene is a cosmic throne room — God enthroned above, the nation assembled below as His covenant subjects, the mediator ascending and descending.

verses.kjv_text: And mount Sinai was altogether on a smoke, because the LORD descended upon it in fire: and the smoke thereof ascended as the smoke of a furnace, and the whole mount quaked greatly.
verses.original_text: וְהַר סִינַי עָשַׁן כֻּלּוֹ מִפְּנֵי אֲשֶׁר יָרַד עָלָיו יְהוָה בָּאֵשׁ וַיַּעַל עֲשָׁנוֹ כְּעֶשֶׁן הַכִּבְשָׁן וַיֶּחֱרַד כָּל־הָהָר מְאֹד׃
verses.transliteration: VeHar Sinai ashan kullo mippenei asher yarad alav YHWH ba'esh vayya'al ashano ke'eshen hakkivshan vayyecherad kol-hahar me'od.
verses.study_note: The centerpiece of the theophany. Sinai is 'altogether on a smoke' — the entire mountain is engulfed. The cause: 'the LORD descended upon it in fire' (yarad alav YHWH ba'esh). YHWH literally comes down. The smoke is compared to 'the smoke of a furnace' (eshen hakkivshan) — the same word used for the smoking furnace in Abraham's covenant vision (Gen 15:17) and the destruction of Sodom (Gen 19:28). And 'the whole mount quaked greatly' (vayyecherad kol hahar me'od) — even inanimate creation trembles at God's presence. The mountain, the people, and the very earth convulse before the Almighty. Fire is the primary medium of God's self-revelation: the burning bush (Exod 3), the pillar of fire (Exod 13), Sinai, the tabernacle glory, Elijah's altar (1 Kings 18), and the tongues of fire at Pentecost (Acts 2).
verses.doctrinal_note: The descent of YHWH in fire reveals the essential nature of God: 'our God is a consuming fire' (Heb 12:29, quoting Deut 4:24). Fire purifies, illuminates, and consumes. It represents both holiness (which purifies the righteous) and judgment (which consumes the wicked). The same fire that descended on Sinai descended at Pentecost, but with a profound difference: at Sinai, the fire came on the mountain and the people were kept away; at Pentecost, the fire came on the people themselves (Acts 2:3). What Sinai kept external, Pentecost made internal. The law was written on stone by fire; the Spirit writes on hearts by fire.

verses.kjv_text: And when the voice of the trumpet sounded long, and waxed louder and louder, Moses spake, and God answered him by a voice.
verses.original_text: וַיְהִי קוֹל הַשֹּׁפָר הוֹלֵךְ וְחָזֵק מְאֹד מֹשֶׁה יְדַבֵּר וְהָאֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶנּוּ בְקוֹל׃
verses.transliteration: Vayehi qol hashofar holekh vechazaq me'od Mosheh yedabber vehaElohim ya'annennu veqol.
verses.study_note: A stunning detail: the shofar blast 'waxed louder and louder' (holekh vechazaq me'od) — the participial construction indicates continuous, escalating intensity. Unlike natural sound that fades with time, this supernatural trumpet grows ever stronger. In this overwhelming sonic environment, Moses speaks and God answers 'by a voice' (veqol — literally 'in/with a voice'). God responds audibly to Moses in the hearing of the people. This is the only occasion in recorded history where God carries on a public, audible conversation with a human being before an entire nation. The people hear not only the theophanic phenomena but the actual divine voice speaking words to Moses.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount: and the LORD called Moses up to the top of the mount; and Moses went up.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה עַל־הַר סִינַי אֶל־רֹאשׁ הָהָר וַיִּקְרָא יְהוָה לְמֹשֶׁה אֶל־רֹאשׁ הָהָר וַיַּעַל מֹשֶׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyered YHWH al-Har Sinai el-rosh hahar vayyikra YHWH leMosheh el-rosh hahar vayya'al Mosheh.
verses.study_note: The narrative reaches its apex: 'the LORD came down upon mount Sinai, on the top of the mount' — the transcendent God of heaven is now localized on the summit of a specific mountain in a specific desert. Heaven has touched earth. Then the LORD 'called Moses up' (vayyikra YHWH leMosheh) to the summit — and 'Moses went up' (vayya'al Mosheh). This is the supreme meeting: YHWH at the top, Moses ascending to meet Him. Only Moses has this access; even Aaron cannot come yet (v.24). The singular privilege of Moses as the covenant mediator is established here and never surpassed until Christ, who does not merely ascend the mountain but ascends into heaven itself (Heb 9:24).

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go down, charge the people, lest they break through unto the LORD to gaze, and many of them perish.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה רֵד הָעֵד בָּעָם פֶּן־יֶהֶרְסוּ אֶל־יְהוָה לִרְאוֹת וְנָפַל מִמֶּנּוּ רָב׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh red ha'ed ba'am pen-yehersu el-YHWH lir'ot venafal mimmennu rav.
verses.study_note: God immediately sends Moses back down with an urgent warning. The verb 'break through' (yehersu) suggests the people might be tempted to rush past the boundaries in curiosity or religious fervor. The motive God identifies is 'to gaze' (lir'ot) — to look, to see, to satisfy curiosity about the divine. But gazing upon God's unmediated glory means death: 'many of them perish' (venafal mimmennu rav). God's concern is protective, not restrictive. The boundary exists because God loves His people and does not want them destroyed by His holiness. This anticipates the consistent biblical pattern: Nadab and Abihu die for unauthorized approach (Lev 10:1-2), Uzzah dies for touching the ark (2 Sam 6:6-7), and Uzziah is struck with leprosy for entering the temple unlawfully (2 Chr 26:19-21).

verses.kjv_text: And let the priests also, which come near to the LORD, sanctify themselves, lest the LORD break forth upon them.
verses.original_text: וְגַם הַכֹּהֲנִים הַנִּגָּשִׁים אֶל־יְהוָה יִתְקַדָּשׁוּ פֶּן־יִפְרֹץ בָּהֶם יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vegam hakkohanim hannigashim el-YHWH yitqaddash pen-yifrotz bahem YHWH.
verses.study_note: Even the priests — those who have authorized access — must sanctify themselves additionally. This is remarkable because the formal Levitical priesthood has not yet been established (that comes in Exodus 28-29). These 'priests' may be the firstborn sons who served priestly functions before the Levitical appointment, or the heads of families who offered sacrifices (as in the patriarchal period). The warning is stark: 'lest the LORD break forth upon them' (pen-yifrotz bahem YHWH). The verb 'paratz' means to burst out, to break through violently — the same word used of breaching a wall or a flood breaking its banks. God's holiness is like a dam under enormous pressure; unauthorized contact releases devastating force.

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the LORD, The people cannot come up to mount Sinai: for thou chargedst us, saying, Set bounds about the mount, and sanctify it.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־יְהוָה לֹא־יוּכַל הָעָם לַעֲלֹת אֶל־הַר סִינָי כִּי־אַתָּה הַעֵדֹתָה בָּנוּ לֵאמֹר הַגְבֵּל אֶת־הָהָר וְקִדַּשְׁתּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh el-YHWH lo-yukhal ha'am la'alot el-Har Sinai ki-attah ha'edotah banu lemor hagbel et-hahar veqiddaschto.
verses.study_note: Moses respectfully reminds God that the boundaries have already been set. His response reveals a deeply human moment: Moses essentially says, 'Lord, we already did what you asked — the people know they cannot approach.' Some commentators see this as faithful obedience ensuring clarity; others detect a hint of Moses's human reasoning with God. God does not rebuke Moses but gives a further instruction (v.24). The phrase 'sanctify it' (veqiddaschto) — the mountain itself is sanctified, not only the people. The ground where God stands becomes holy ground, as Moses learned at the burning bush (Exod 3:5). Holiness radiates outward from God's presence.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto him, Away, get thee down, and thou shalt come up, thou, and Aaron with thee: but let not the priests and the people break through to come up unto the LORD, lest he break forth upon them.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוָה לֶךְ־רֵד וְעָלִיתָ אַתָּה וְאַהֲרֹן עִמָּךְ וְהַכֹּהֲנִים וְהָעָם אַל־יֶהֶרְסוּ לַעֲלֹת אֶל־יְהוָה פֶּן־יִפְרֹץ־בָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer elav YHWH lekh-red ve'alita attah veAharon immakh vehakkohanim veha'am al-yehersu la'alot el-YHWH pen-yifrotz-bam.
verses.study_note: God grants permission for Aaron to accompany Moses — the first mention of Aaron being granted any access to the mountain. But the restriction is emphatic: the priests and people must not break through. The phrase 'lest he break forth upon them' (pen-yifrotz bam) repeats the warning of v.22 with even greater urgency. The graduated access is clear: Moses has the highest access (summit), Aaron has partial access (lower heights), the priests remain at the boundary, and the people stay at the base. This hierarchy foreshadows the tabernacle/temple structure: Most Holy Place (high priest alone), Holy Place (priests), court (Israelites), and beyond the court (Gentiles). Access to God is real but graded by calling and consecration.
verses.doctrinal_note: The graduated levels of access at Sinai — Moses, Aaron, priests, people — anticipate the entire sacrificial system. Only through mediation can sinful humanity approach the holy God. The New Testament declares this entire structure fulfilled and superseded in Christ: 'Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way' (Heb 10:19-20). What required layers of mediation at Sinai is now available to every believer through the torn veil and the shed blood. The mountain that could not be touched (Heb 12:18) gives way to Mount Zion that welcomes all who come by faith (Heb 12:22-24).

verses.kjv_text: So Moses went down unto the people, and spake unto them.
verses.original_text: וַיֵּרֶד מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyered Mosheh el-ha'am vayomer alehem.
verses.study_note: The chapter ends with deliberate abruptness. Moses descends and speaks to the people — but the content of his speech is not recorded. The narrative breaks off mid-scene, creating a cliffhanger that flows directly into chapter 20 and the Ten Commandments. The effect is literary genius: the reader, like the people, is left standing at the base of the smoking mountain, trembling, waiting for God to speak. What follows in chapter 20 — 'And God spake all these words, saying' — delivers the most consequential utterance in the Old Testament. This brief verse completes the chapter's pattern of Moses ascending and descending — he has made at least three round trips in this single chapter, faithfully mediating between the transcendent God above and the trembling people below.
verses.doctrinal_note: Exodus 19 closes as it began — with Moses mediating between heaven and earth. The entire chapter is structured as covenant preparation: God proposes (vv.3-6), the people accept (vv.7-8), God prescribes consecration (vv.10-15), God descends in terrifying glory (vv.16-20), and the boundaries are enforced (vv.21-25). Everything points forward to the words that follow in chapter 20. The theological trajectory is clear: (1) God saves first, then gives law (grace before commandment); (2) Approaching God requires consecration and mediation; (3) God's holiness is both beautiful and terrible; (4) The covenant is bilateral — God proposes, the people consent. This framework governs the entire biblical theology of covenant and reaches its consummation in the New Covenant, where Christ fulfills every element: He is the mediator (v.3), the eagles' wings of deliverance (v.4), the maker of the priestly kingdom (v.6), and the one who opens access through the torn veil (Heb 10:19-20).

meta.overview: Exodus 19 is the theological hinge of the entire Pentateuch — the moment when Israel arrives at Mount Sinai and YHWH descends to meet His people. After three months of wilderness travel since leaving Egypt, Israel encamps at the base of the mountain where Moses first encountered God at the burning bush (Exod 3). God initiates the Mosaic Covenant with a breathtaking proposal: if Israel will obey His voice, they will be His 'peculiar treasure' (segullah), a 'kingdom of priests,' and a 'holy nation' — language that redefines Israel's identity from a rescued slave people to a consecrated covenant community. The people unanimously consent: 'All that the LORD hath spoken we will do.' God then commands three days of consecration — washing garments, setting boundaries around the mountain, and abstaining from physical contact — to prepare them for the divine encounter. On the third day, Sinai erupts in thunders, lightnings, thick cloud, trumpet blasts, fire, smoke, and violent earthquake as YHWH descends in fire. The mountain quakes exceedingly. The people tremble at the base. Moses alone is called to ascend. This chapter establishes the fundamental pattern of covenant theology: grace precedes law ('I bare you on eagles' wings' comes before the commandments), holiness requires separation, and the holy God can only be approached on His terms. The Sinai theophany becomes the defining revelation of God's nature in the Old Testament, echoed in every subsequent prophetic vision and fulfilled in the incarnation of Christ and the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost.
meta.theme: Covenant Proposal, Consecration & the Terrifying Theophany of YHWH
meta.keyWordMeaning: To be holy, to consecrate, to set apart — the central verb of the chapter. God commands Moses to 'sanctify' (qadash) the people, and the mountain itself is 'sanctified' by God's presence. Holiness is the prerequisite for encountering the living God.
outline: Arrival at Sinai — The Third Month (vv.1-2): Israel encamps before the mountain of God
The Covenant Proposal — Eagles' Wings and a Peculiar Treasure (vv.3-6): YHWH offers Israel covenant identity as kingdom of priests and holy nation
The People's Consent — All That the LORD Hath Spoken (vv.7-8): Israel unanimously accepts the covenant terms
Three Days of Consecration (vv.9-15): Washing, boundaries, and preparation for the divine encounter
The Third Day — Theophany on Sinai (vv.16-20): Thunder, lightning, trumpet, fire, smoke, earthquake, and the descent of YHWH
Final Warning — Touch Not the Mountain (vv.21-25): Moses and Aaron ascend; the people are kept back from the holy ground

verses.kjv_text: And God spake all these words, saying,
verses.original_text: וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים אֵת כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה לֵאמֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vaydabber Elohim et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh lemor.
verses.study_note: This single verse establishes the most extraordinary event in redemptive history: God Himself speaks audibly and directly to an entire nation. 'Elohim' is the subject — not Moses, not an angel, but God. 'All these words' (kol-haddevarim) — the Ten Commandments are literally 'the Ten Words' (aseret haddevarim, cf. Deut 4:13; 10:4). The verb 'dabar' (to speak) indicates direct, personal, intelligible speech. Deuteronomy 5:22 adds that God 'added no more' — the Decalogue was a complete, self-contained divine utterance. This is the supreme Old Testament theophany: God reveals not merely His presence or power but His moral will. The 'words' parallel Genesis 1 — the same God who spoke creation into being now speaks His law into existence.
verses.doctrinal_note: That God 'spake' the commandments directly establishes their unique authority. No other portion of Scripture was delivered by the unmediated voice of God to an assembled people. The Decalogue stands as the constitution of God's covenant nation. Jesus affirms their abiding moral authority (Matt 5:17-19; 19:17-19), and Paul identifies the law as 'holy, and just, and good' (Rom 7:12). The law reveals God's character and exposes human sin (Rom 3:20; 7:7), driving sinners to Christ as their righteousness (Gal 3:24).

verses.kjv_text: I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.
verses.original_text: אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Anokhi YHWH Elohekha asher hotzeitikha me'eretz Mitzrayim mibbeit avadim.
verses.study_note: The prologue to the Decalogue is not a commandment but a declaration of identity and grace. God does not begin with 'Thou shalt' but with 'I AM' (Anokhi). He identifies Himself as YHWH — the covenant name revealed at the burning bush (Exod 3:14). 'Thy God' (Elohekha) — the singular possessive is intimate and personal; God addresses each individual Israelite, not merely the collective. Before any command is given, God establishes the relationship: He is their redeemer. 'Which have brought thee out' (asher hotzeitikha) — the Hiphil causative of 'yatza' (to go out) — God actively, powerfully extracted them from slavery. 'House of bondage' (beit avadim) literally 'house of slaves' — Egypt is defined not by its pyramids or civilization but by its enslavement of God's people. The theological structure is revolutionary: grace precedes law. God saves first, then commands. Obedience flows from gratitude, not from merit-seeking.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the foundational principle of biblical ethics: grace before law, redemption before obedience. God does not say 'Obey these commands and I will save you' but 'I have saved you; now live as My people.' This pattern — indicative before imperative — pervades the entire Bible and reaches its fullest expression in the New Testament: 'We love him, because he first loved us' (1 John 4:19). Paul's epistles consistently place the doctrinal indicative (Romans 1-11) before the ethical imperative (Romans 12-16). The Decalogue is not a ladder to earn God's favor but the family rules for those already redeemed.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt have no other gods before me.
verses.original_text: לֹא יִהְיֶה־לְךָ אֱלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים עַל־פָּנָיַ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo yihyeh-lekha elohim acherim al-panaya.
verses.study_note: The First Commandment: absolute, exclusive covenant allegiance. 'No other gods' (elohim acherim) — the plural 'elohim' when applied to false gods means 'powers, authorities, so-called deities.' The phrase 'before me' (al-panaya) literally means 'before my face' or 'in my presence' — since God is omnipresent, there is no place where other gods could exist outside His awareness. This is not merely a ranking ('put Me first among many') but an absolute prohibition: no other god may exist in your loyalty alongside YHWH. The commandment presupposes the suzerainty treaty structure of the ancient Near East, where a vassal king owed exclusive loyalty to his sovereign lord. YHWH claims total sovereignty over Israel's worship, trust, and obedience.
verses.doctrinal_note: The First Commandment establishes monotheistic exclusivity as the foundation of all morality. Every subsequent commandment flows from this one: if God alone is God, then He alone defines right worship (2nd), the sanctity of His Name (3rd), holy time (4th), and all social ethics (5th-10th). Jesus identifies this as the 'first and great commandment': 'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind' (Matt 22:37-38, citing Deut 6:5). Idolatry — giving ultimate allegiance to anything other than God — remains the root sin from which all others grow (Rom 1:21-25).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth:
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲשֶׂה־לְּךָ פֶסֶל וְכָל־תְּמוּנָה אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׁמַיִם מִמַּעַל וַאֲשֶׁר בָּאָרֶץ מִתַּחַת וַאֲשֶׁר בַּמַּיִם מִתַּחַת לָאָרֶץ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'aseh-lekha fesel vekhol-temunah asher bashamayim mimma'al va'asher ba'aretz mittachat va'asher bammayim mittachat la'aretz.
verses.study_note: The Second Commandment: God governs not only whom we worship (1st) but how we worship (2nd). 'Graven image' (fesel) — a carved idol, from 'pasal' (to hew, carve). 'Any likeness' (kol-temunah) — any representation, form, or visual depiction. The three-tier cosmology — heaven, earth, water under the earth — is comprehensive: nothing in the entire created order may serve as a representation of the Creator. This commandment strikes at the universal ancient Near Eastern practice of representing deities through images. It affirms God's radical transcendence: He cannot be captured, controlled, or reduced to any created form. The prohibition is not against art per se (God later commands the cherubim on the Ark, Exod 25:18-20) but against making images as objects of worship that claim to represent or mediate God's presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Second Commandment protects the Creator-creature distinction: God is wholly other and cannot be represented by anything He has made. Idolatry domesticates God, reducing the infinite to the manageable. Paul identifies this exchange of glory for images as the root of human rebellion (Rom 1:22-23): 'Changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts.' The only authorized 'image' of God is humanity itself (Gen 1:26-27) — and ultimately Christ, 'who is the image of the invisible God' (Col 1:15). John's final word echoes this: 'Little children, keep yourselves from idols' (1 John 5:21).

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me;
verses.original_text: לֹא־תִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לָהֶם וְלֹא תָעָבְדֵם כִּי אָנֹכִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ אֵל קַנָּא פֹּקֵד עֲוֹן אָבֹת עַל־בָּנִים עַל־שִׁלֵּשִׁים וְעַל־רִבֵּעִים לְשֹׂנְאָי׃
verses.transliteration: Lo-tishtachaveh lahem velo ta'avdem ki anokhi YHWH Elohekha El qanna poked avon avot al-banim al-shilleshim ve'al-ribbe'im leson'ai.
verses.study_note: Two actions prohibited: 'bow down' (hishtachaveh — to prostrate in worship) and 'serve' (ta'avdem — to render religious service). God reveals His character as 'El qanna' — 'a jealous God.' Divine jealousy is not petty insecurity but the fierce, righteous love of a covenant husband who will not share His bride with rivals (cf. Hos 2). 'Visiting the iniquity' (poked avon) — 'paqad' means to attend to, reckon, hold to account. The generational consequences (to the third and fourth generation) describe the natural, cascading effects of idolatry within family systems — not arbitrary punishment of innocent descendants. The qualification 'of them that hate me' (leson'ai) is crucial: the punishment falls on those who continue in their fathers' rebellion, not on those who repent (cf. Ezek 18:20).
verses.doctrinal_note: God's jealousy (qin'ah) is a central divine attribute that protects the covenant relationship. It is not a flaw but the holy intensity of love that will not tolerate betrayal. The prophets develop this theme extensively: God as the jealous husband of Israel (Hos 2; Jer 2:2; Ezek 16). The generational visiting of iniquity demonstrates that sin has social consequences — idolatry corrupts families and cultures over time. Yet Ezekiel 18:20 clarifies individual accountability: 'The soul that sinneth, it shall die.' The tension is resolved in understanding that ongoing rebellion ('them that hate me') sustains the generational pattern, while repentance breaks it.

verses.kjv_text: And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments.
verses.original_text: וְעֹשֶׂה חֶסֶד לַאֲלָפִים לְאֹהֲבַי וּלְשֹׁמְרֵי מִצְוֹתָי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'oseh chesed la'alafim le'ohavai uleshomrei mitzvotai.
verses.study_note: The contrast is stunning and deliberate: judgment extends to three or four generations, but mercy (chesed) extends to 'thousands' (la'alafim) — an exponential disproportion of grace over judgment. 'Chesed' is the great covenant word of the Old Testament — loyal love, steadfast kindness, faithful mercy that flows from God's covenant commitment. Those who receive this mercy are described by two parallel terms: 'them that love me' (le'ohavai) and 'keep my commandments' (uleshomrei mitzvotai). Love and obedience are inseparable — the same pairing Jesus makes: 'If ye love me, keep my commandments' (John 14:15). This verse reveals God's heart: He is far more inclined to mercy than to judgment. The ratio is roughly 3-4 versus thousands — grace overwhelms wrath.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse provides the Old Testament foundation for the principle that God's mercy is His primary disposition: 'His mercy endureth for ever' (Psalm 136). The linkage of love and obedience anticipates Jesus' teaching in John 14:15,21,23 — obedience is not the condition for earning love but the evidence of a loving relationship. The 'thousands' of mercy versus three-four generations of judgment reveals what Exodus 34:6-7 will later expand: God is 'merciful and gracious, longsuffering, and abundant in goodness and truth.' Grace always exceeds judgment in divine proportion.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain; for the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh his name in vain.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִשָּׂא אֶת־שֵׁם־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לַשָּׁוְא כִּי לֹא יְנַקֶּה יְהוָה אֵת אֲשֶׁר יִשָּׂא אֶת־שְׁמוֹ לַשָּׁוְא׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tissa et-shem-YHWH Elohekha lashshav ki lo yenaqqeh YHWH et asher yissa et-shemo lashshav.
verses.study_note: The Third Commandment: the Hebrew 'lo tissa' literally means 'you shall not lift up / carry / bear' the Name. 'Take' is too weak; this is about carrying God's Name as His representative. 'In vain' (lashshav) means 'for emptiness, falsehood, worthlessness, nothingness.' The commandment encompasses far more than profanity — it prohibits: false oaths in God's Name (Lev 19:12), invoking God to validate lies, using His Name in magical incantation, empty or hypocritical worship, and claiming to represent God falsely. Israel 'bore' (nasa) the Name of YHWH as His covenant people — to bear it 'in vain' was to misrepresent God's character to the watching world. The penalty clause is severe: 'the LORD will not hold him guiltless' (lo yenaqqeh) — literally 'will not declare clean/innocent.' This is the only commandment with its own explicit penalty attached.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Third Commandment guards the holiness of God's revealed character. The 'Name' in Hebrew thought represents the full person, character, and reputation of the one named. To bear YHWH's Name in vain is to empty it of meaning — to claim God while living in contradiction to His nature. Jesus expands this principle: 'Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven' (Matt 7:21). Christians bear Christ's Name — to live in hypocrisy is a violation of the same principle. The emphatic penalty ('will NOT hold guiltless') underscores God's fierce protection of His Name's integrity.

verses.kjv_text: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy.
verses.original_text: זָכוֹר אֶת־יוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת לְקַדְּשׁוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Zakhor et-yom hashabbat leqaddesho.
verses.study_note: The Fourth Commandment begins with 'Remember' (zakhor) — the only commandment using this verb (Deuteronomy 5:12 uses 'shamor' / 'observe,' and the Rabbis teach both were spoken simultaneously). 'Sabbath' (shabbat) derives from 'shavat' (to cease, rest, desist). 'To keep it holy' (leqaddesho) — to sanctify, set apart, make sacred. The Sabbath is not merely rest from work but active consecration of time to God. This is the longest commandment, receiving four full verses of elaboration (vv.8-11), underscoring its importance. The Sabbath is the sign of the Mosaic Covenant (Exod 31:13-17), just as the rainbow is the sign of the Noahic Covenant and circumcision is the sign of the Abrahamic Covenant.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Sabbath principle embeds creation theology into the rhythm of daily life. By resting on the seventh day, Israel re-enacts and affirms God's creative order: work is meaningful, but God — not labor — is ultimate. The Sabbath also has eschatological dimensions: Hebrews 4:9-10 speaks of a 'sabbatismos' (Sabbath rest) remaining for God's people — the eternal rest in Christ. Jesus declared Himself 'Lord even of the sabbath' (Mark 2:28), and the early church moved corporate worship to the first day (the day of resurrection, Acts 20:7; 1 Cor 16:2), celebrating the new creation inaugurated by Christ's rising.

verses.kjv_text: Six days shalt thou labour, and do all thy work:
verses.original_text: שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים תַּעֲבֹד וְעָשִׂיתָ כָּל־מְלַאכְתֶּךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Sheshet yamim ta'avod ve'asita kol-melakhtekha.
verses.study_note: Before commanding rest, God commands work. 'Labour' (ta'avod) — from 'avad' (to work, serve, till). This is the same root as 'eved' (servant) and connects back to Eden where Adam was placed to 'avad' (till/serve) the garden (Gen 2:15). Work is not a curse but a pre-Fall divine mandate; the Sabbath presupposes six days of meaningful labor. 'All thy work' (kol-melakhtekha) — 'melakhah' is purposeful, skilled work (the same word used for the construction of the Tabernacle, Exod 35-40). The six-day work week is itself a divine institution — neither endless toil nor perpetual leisure, but rhythmic, purposeful labor punctuated by sacred rest.

verses.kjv_text: But the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy cattle, nor thy stranger that is within thy gates:
verses.original_text: וְיוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שַׁבָּת לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לֹא תַעֲשֶׂה כָל־מְלָאכָה אַתָּה וּבִנְךָ וּבִתְּךָ עַבְדְּךָ וַאֲמָתְךָ וּבְהֶמְתֶּךָ וְגֵרְךָ אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׁעָרֶיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Veyom hashevi'i shabbat laYHWH Elohekha lo ta'aseh khol-melakhah attah uvinkha uvittekha avdekha va'amatekha uvehemtekha vegerekha asher bish'arekha.
verses.study_note: The Sabbath rest is radically inclusive. Seven categories of beneficiaries are listed: you, your son, your daughter, your manservant, your maidservant, your cattle, and your stranger (ger — the resident alien). This is revolutionary social legislation: even slaves and animals receive mandated rest. In the ancient Near East, only the elite rested; servants worked ceaselessly. The Sabbath dismantles social hierarchy before God — master and slave, citizen and immigrant, human and animal all rest together. 'Within thy gates' (bish'arekha) means within the community — the Sabbath shapes the entire social order, not just private devotion. The Sabbath is 'to YHWH' (laYHWH) — it belongs to God, not to human productivity.

verses.kjv_text: For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is, and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.original_text: כִּי שֵׁשֶׁת־יָמִים עָשָׂה יְהוָה אֶת־הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֶת־הָאָרֶץ אֶת־הַיָּם וְאֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁר־בָּם וַיָּנַח בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי עַל־כֵּן בֵּרַךְ יְהוָה אֶת־יוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת וַיְקַדְּשֵׁהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ki sheshet-yamim asah YHWH et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'aretz et-hayyam ve'et-kol-asher-bam vayyanach bayyom hashevi'i al-ken berakh YHWH et-yom hashabbat vayeqaddesheh.
verses.study_note: The Sabbath is grounded in creation, not in cultural convention. God's rest on the seventh day (Gen 2:2-3) is the pattern Israel is to imitate — 'imitatio Dei' (imitation of God). 'Rested' (vayyanach) — from 'nuach' (to rest, settle, be at peace) — God's rest was not from exhaustion but from completion and satisfaction: His work was 'very good' (Gen 1:31). 'Blessed' (berakh) and 'hallowed' (vayeqaddesheh) — the same two verbs from Genesis 2:3. God did three things to the Sabbath: He rested on it, blessed it, and sanctified it. Deuteronomy 5:15 gives a second ground — the Exodus deliverance — showing the Sabbath is both a creation ordinance and a redemption memorial. The Sabbath thus looks backward to creation, outward to social justice, and forward to eschatological rest.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Sabbath's creation foundation means it transcends Israelite national life — it reflects the structure of reality itself. The Sabbath declares that human worth is not defined by productivity but by being made in God's image. The creation-rest connection is developed in Hebrews 4:1-11 where the author argues that a 'sabbatismos' remains — an eschatological rest that Joshua's conquest did not fully provide. Christ fulfills this in His invitation: 'Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28). The Sabbath principle — that life has rhythm, rest, and sacred purpose — remains morally binding even if its specific day and ceremonies have been fulfilled in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: Honour thy father and thy mother: that thy days may be long upon the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee.
verses.original_text: כַּבֵּד אֶת־אָבִיךָ וְאֶת־אִמֶּךָ לְמַעַן יַאֲרִכוּן יָמֶיךָ עַל הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ נֹתֵן לָךְ׃
verses.transliteration: Kabbed et-avikha ve'et-immekha lema'an ya'arikhun yamekha al ha'adamah asher-YHWH Elohekha noten lakh.
verses.study_note: The Fifth Commandment bridges the two tables of the law: it is the first commandment with a promise (Eph 6:2) and the hinge between duties to God and duties to neighbor. 'Honour' (kabbed) — from 'kaved' (to be heavy, weighty, glorious) — the same root as 'kavod' (glory). To honor parents is to give them weight, gravity, significance. This is not childish obedience but lifelong respect, including caring for aged parents. Both father and mother are named equally — revolutionary in patriarchal societies. The promise — 'that thy days may be long upon the land' — links family integrity to national stability. A society that dishonors its elders destroys its own foundations. The 'land' (ha'adamah) is the Promised Land, but the principle extends to all civilizations.
verses.doctrinal_note: Paul identifies this as 'the first commandment with promise' (Eph 6:2-3), indicating that the family is God's primary institution for transmitting faith and maintaining social order. The Fifth Commandment establishes the principle of delegated authority: parents represent God's authority to children, and honoring parents teaches reverence for all legitimate authority (government, church, elders). Jesus rebuked the Pharisees for using 'Corban' to circumvent the duty to care for parents (Mark 7:9-13), showing that even religious devotion cannot nullify this commandment. The connection between honoring parents and longevity in the land suggests that family faithfulness is the bedrock of covenant community.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not kill.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִרְצָח׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tirtzach.
verses.study_note: The Sixth Commandment — the shortest in Hebrew (two words). 'Kill' in the KJV translates 'ratzach,' which specifically means unlawful killing — murder, manslaughter, or blood-vengeance killing. Hebrew has multiple words for killing: 'harag' (general killing), 'mut/hemit' (to put to death, used for judicial execution), 'nakah' (to strike/slay in battle). 'Ratzach' is the most restricted term, used almost exclusively for unauthorized, personal killing of another human being. This commandment does not prohibit judicial execution (which God Himself prescribes in Gen 9:6; Exod 21:12-14) nor warfare conducted under divine or governmental authority. What it absolutely prohibits is the unauthorized taking of human life — murder born of hatred, malice, vengeance, or negligence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sanctity of human life is grounded in the 'imago Dei': 'Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man' (Gen 9:6). Every human being, regardless of age, status, or condition, bears God's image and is therefore inviolable. Jesus radicalizes this commandment in the Sermon on the Mount: 'whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment' (Matt 5:21-22). The prohibition extends from the external act to the internal disposition — hatred is murder in embryo. John confirms: 'Whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer' (1 John 3:15). The commandment thus protects not only physical life but the dignity of every person created in God's likeness.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not commit adultery.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִנְאָף׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tin'af.
verses.study_note: The Seventh Commandment: 'na'af' specifically denotes sexual intercourse with another man's wife (or betrothed). In the broader context of Mosaic law, sexual ethics extend to incest (Lev 18), prostitution, and all sexual relations outside the one-man-one-woman marriage covenant (Gen 2:24). Marriage is the foundational human covenant — a reflection of God's covenant faithfulness to Israel (Hos 2; Ezek 16). Adultery shatters the most intimate human bond and, typologically, represents spiritual unfaithfulness to God. The prophets consistently use adultery as the metaphor for idolatry: Israel 'committing adultery' with foreign gods (Jer 3:8-9; Ezek 23). The placement after 'Thou shalt not kill' connects the sanctity of life to the sanctity of the covenant bond from which life springs.
verses.doctrinal_note: Marriage as covenant is established in Genesis 2:24 — 'Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh.' Jesus affirms the creation order of marriage (Matt 19:4-6) and extends the commandment to the heart: 'whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her already in his heart' (Matt 5:27-28). The marriage covenant images the relationship between Christ and His Church (Eph 5:25-32). Adultery therefore violates not only a human covenant but the divine archetype it represents. Hebrews 13:4 declares: 'Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge.'

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not steal.
verses.original_text: לֹא תִגְנֹב׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tignov.
verses.study_note: The Eighth Commandment: 'ganav' means to steal, take secretly, carry away another's property. The prohibition protects not merely possessions but the labor and stewardship they represent. Since work is divinely ordained (Gen 2:15; Exod 20:9), the fruit of that work is sacred. Some scholars note that in its original context, 'ganav' may have primarily referred to kidnapping — stealing a person (cf. Exod 21:16; Deut 24:7) — which was a capital offense. If so, this commandment protects both persons and property, with kidnapping as the ultimate theft. The commandment assumes the legitimacy of private property: you cannot 'steal' what no one rightfully owns. The positive counterpart is generosity: 'Let him that stole steal no more: but rather let him labour... that he may have to give to him that needeth' (Eph 4:28).
verses.doctrinal_note: The Eighth Commandment establishes the right of property as grounded in the creation mandate to work and steward the earth (Gen 1:28; 2:15). God is the ultimate owner of all things (Ps 24:1), but He delegates stewardship to humans who bear responsibility for what is entrusted to them (the parable of the talents, Matt 25:14-30). Theft violates both the victim's God-given stewardship and the thief's own calling to productive labor. The commandment applies not only to overt stealing but to fraud, deceptive business practices, unjust wages (Jer 22:13; James 5:4), and any form of taking what does not rightfully belong to you.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲנֶה בְרֵעֲךָ עֵד שָׁקֶר׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'aneh vere'akha ed shaqer.
verses.study_note: The Ninth Commandment: 'bear false witness' (ta'aneh ed shaqer) literally means 'you shall not answer/testify against your neighbor as a lying witness.' The primary setting is judicial — courtroom testimony in Israel's legal proceedings, where cases were decided by witnesses (Deut 19:15). False testimony could result in the wrongful execution of an innocent person (as with Naboth, 1 Kings 21:10-13). The principle extends beyond the courtroom to all forms of dishonesty: slander, gossip, deception, misrepresentation, and character assassination. 'Neighbor' (re'a) encompasses all fellow human beings in covenant community. Truth is the foundation of justice, and justice is the foundation of social order. When truth collapses, society collapses with it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Ninth Commandment reflects God's own nature as 'the God of truth' (Isa 65:16) who 'cannot lie' (Titus 1:2; Heb 6:18). Satan is 'a liar, and the father of it' (John 8:44). Every act of deception aligns with the satanic rather than the divine. The commandment protects not only individual reputation but the entire system of justice upon which covenant community depends. Proverbs devotes extensive attention to the destructive power of false speech (Prov 6:16-19; 12:22; 19:5,9; 25:18). James 3 develops the theology of the tongue as a 'fire' and a 'world of iniquity.' Truth-telling is not merely ethical but ontological — it reflects the character of the God who defines reality by His Word.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s house, thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that is thy neighbour’s.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַחְמֹד בֵּית רֵעֶךָ לֹא תַחְמֹד אֵשֶׁת רֵעֶךָ וְעַבְדּוֹ וַאֲמָתוֹ וְשׁוֹרוֹ וַחֲמֹרוֹ וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר לְרֵעֶךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Lo tachmod beit re'ekha lo tachmod eshet re'ekha ve'avdo va'amato veshoro vachamoro vekhol asher lere'ekha.
verses.study_note: The Tenth Commandment is the most revolutionary of all: it legislates the interior life. Every previous commandment addresses actions; this one addresses desire. 'Covet' (tachmod) — from 'chamad' (to desire, delight in, take pleasure in). 'Chamad' is not merely noticing attractiveness but the inward grasping, the acquisitive desire that leads to action. The same verb is used for Eve's response to the forbidden fruit: 'a tree to be desired' (nechmad, Gen 3:6). The list moves from house to wife to servants to animals to 'any thing' — an expanding circle that culminates in the absolute 'vekhol asher lere'ekha' (everything that belongs to your neighbor). Paul identifies this commandment as the one that exposed his sin: 'I had not known lust, except the law had said, Thou shalt not covet' (Rom 7:7). The commandment reveals that sin is not merely behavioral but dispositional — rooted in the heart.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Tenth Commandment is the law's deepest probe into human nature. It demonstrates that God's moral standard extends beyond outward conformity to inward reality. Jesus makes this explicit: 'out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, false witness, blasphemies' (Matt 15:19) — essentially tracing every commandment back to the heart. Paul's autobiography of conviction (Rom 7:7-12) shows that coveting revealed the depth of his sinfulness and drove him to Christ. The commandment thus serves the law's ultimate purpose: not to make us righteous by our own effort but to reveal our desperate need for grace. James traces the progression: 'When lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death' (James 1:15). Coveting is the seed from which all external sin grows.

verses.kjv_text: And all the people saw the thunderings, and the lightnings, and the noise of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking: and when the people saw it, they removed, and stood afar off.
verses.original_text: וְכָל־הָעָם רֹאִים אֶת־הַקּוֹלֹת וְאֶת־הַלַּפִּידִם וְאֵת קוֹל הַשֹּׁפָר וְאֶת־הָהָר עָשֵׁן וַיַּרְא הָעָם וַיָּנֻעוּ וַיַּעַמְדוּ מֵרָחֹק׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhol-ha'am ro'im et-haqqolot ve'et-hallappidim ve'et qol hashofar ve'et-hahar ashen vayyar ha'am vayyanu'u vayya'amdu merachoq.
verses.study_note: The people 'saw the thunderings' (ro'im et-haqqolot) — they 'saw' sounds, a synesthetic experience suggesting a theophany so overwhelming it transcended normal sensory categories. Jewish tradition (based on the Mekhilta) teaches that Israel 'saw what is normally heard and heard what is normally seen' — a total collapse of ordinary perception before the divine presence. Four phenomena are named: thunderings (qolot — literally 'voices'), lightnings (lappidim — torch-flames), the trumpet sound (qol hashofar), and the smoking mountain. The response is primal terror: they 'trembled' (vayyanu'u — literally shook, wavered) and 'stood afar off' (vayya'amdu merachoq). Sinai is simultaneously the greatest revelation and the greatest alienation — God draws near, and the people recoil.
verses.doctrinal_note: The terror of Sinai demonstrates the fundamental human problem: sinful humanity cannot endure the unmediated presence of a holy God. The writer of Hebrews develops this theologically: 'For ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched, and that burned with fire, nor unto blackness, and darkness, and tempest... but ye are come unto mount Sion' (Heb 12:18-22). Sinai reveals the terror of the law; Zion reveals the grace of the gospel. The people's retreat to 'afar off' anticipates the need for a mediator — one who can stand between God and humanity, which Moses partially fulfills and Christ fully accomplishes (1 Tim 2:5).

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto Moses, Speak thou with us, and we will hear: but let not God speak with us, lest we die.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה דַּבֵּר־אַתָּה עִמָּנוּ וְנִשְׁמָעָה וְאַל־יְדַבֵּר עִמָּנוּ אֱלֹהִים פֶּן־נָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vayyomeru el-Mosheh dabber-attah immanu venishma'ah ve'al-yedabber immanu Elohim pen-namut.
verses.study_note: The people request prophetic mediation: 'Speak thou with us' — let Moses be the intermediary between God and Israel. 'But let not God speak with us, lest we die' (pen-namut) — a theologically accurate instinct: sinful humanity cannot survive direct encounter with divine holiness (cf. Exod 33:20). Their fear is not irrational but reflects a genuine understanding of the gap between the holy and the profane. This request effectively establishes the prophetic office: from this point forward, God speaks to Israel through Moses and his prophetic successors. Deuteronomy 18:15-18 explicitly connects this moment to the promise of a future prophet 'like unto Moses' — fulfilled in Christ (Acts 3:22-23; 7:37).
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse establishes the necessity and office of the mediator. Israel's request for Moses to stand between them and God anticipates the entire mediatorial structure of the Old Covenant: priests, prophets, and kings who bridge the gap between God and His people. Yet all these mediators are partial and temporary. The request for a human mediator points ultimately to Christ, the perfect Mediator: 'For there is one God, and one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Tim 2:5). What Israel feared at Sinai — the unmediated presence of God — believers receive in Christ: 'we have boldness and access with confidence by the faith of him' (Eph 3:12).

verses.kjv_text: And Moses said unto the people, Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם אַל־תִּירָאוּ כִּי לְבַעֲבוּר נַסּוֹת אֶתְכֶם בָּא הָאֱלֹהִים וּבַעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה יִרְאָתוֹ עַל־פְּנֵיכֶם לְבִלְתִּי תֶחֱטָאוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer Mosheh el-ha'am al-tira'u ki leva'avur nassot etkhem ba ha'Elohim uva'avur tihyeh yir'ato al-peneikhem levilti techeta'u.
verses.study_note: Moses resolves a profound paradox: 'Fear not' (al-tira'u) yet 'that his fear may be before your faces' (tihyeh yir'ato al-peneikhem). Two different 'fears' are distinguished. The first is paralyzing terror that drives people away from God; Moses says do not have this. The second is reverential awe that keeps people from sin; Moses says this is the purpose of the theophany. God's terrifying self-revelation at Sinai serves a pedagogical purpose: 'to prove you' (nassot etkhem) — to test, refine, and demonstrate their character. The fear of God, properly understood, is not the enemy of faith but its guardian: 'The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom' (Prov 9:10). The goal is 'that ye sin not' (levilti techeta'u) — holy fear produces holy living.
verses.doctrinal_note: Moses' distinction between slavish fear and holy fear is one of the great theological insights of the Pentateuch. Slavish fear drives people from God; holy fear draws them toward Him in reverent obedience. This tension runs through the entire Bible: God is simultaneously the one to be feared and the one to be trusted. The resolution is the gospel: 'perfect love casteth out fear' (1 John 4:18) — not the reverential fear of God, but the servile fear of punishment. In Christ, believers are not slaves trembling before a master but children approaching a Father — 'ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear; but ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father' (Rom 8:15).

verses.kjv_text: And the people stood afar off, and Moses drew near unto the thick darkness where God was.
verses.original_text: וַיַּעֲמֹד הָעָם מֵרָחֹק וּמֹשֶׁה נִגַּשׁ אֶל־הָעֲרָפֶל אֲשֶׁר־שָׁם הָאֱלֹהִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vayya'amod ha'am merachoq uMosheh niggash el-ha'arafel asher-sham ha'Elohim.
verses.study_note: A stunning visual contrast: the people stand 'afar off' (merachoq) while Moses 'drew near' (niggash) 'unto the thick darkness' (el-ha'arafel) where God was. The 'arafel' — thick darkness, dense cloud — is paradoxical: God dwells in unapproachable light (1 Tim 6:16) yet meets Moses in impenetrable darkness. This is the 'divine darkness' of classical theology: God is known precisely in the recognition that He transcends all human comprehension. Solomon later echoes this at the Temple dedication: 'The LORD said that he would dwell in the thick darkness' (1 Kings 8:12). Moses' ability to approach where the people cannot is a function of his unique mediatorial calling (Num 12:6-8). He enters the darkness that others cannot bear — a type of Christ who enters the ultimate darkness of the cross on behalf of His people.

verses.kjv_text: And the LORD said unto Moses, Thus thou shalt say unto the children of Israel, Ye have seen that I have talked with you from heaven.
verses.original_text: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כֹּה תֹאמַר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אַתֶּם רְאִיתֶם כִּי מִן־הַשָּׁמַיִם דִּבַּרְתִּי עִמָּכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh koh tomar el-benei Yisra'el attem re'item ki min-hashamayim dibbarti immakhem.
verses.study_note: God now speaks through Moses (the mediation the people requested). He emphasizes what they witnessed: 'Ye have seen' (attem re'item) — the experience was empirical, not secondhand. 'From heaven' (min-hashamayim) — though God manifested on Sinai, His speech came from heaven, emphasizing His transcendence even in immanent encounter. This distinction is theologically important: God did not 'descend' to become a local mountain deity but spoke from His heavenly throne through the earthly theophany. The verse transitions from the Decalogue proper to supplementary worship instructions, grounding these additional commands in the same divine authority.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not make with me gods of silver, neither shall ye make unto you gods of gold.
verses.original_text: לֹא תַעֲשׂוּן אִתִּי אֱלֹהֵי כֶסֶף וֵאלֹהֵי זָהָב לֹא תַעֲשׂוּ לָכֶם׃
verses.transliteration: Lo ta'asun itti elohei khesef velohei zahav lo ta'asu lakhem.
verses.study_note: God reiterates and specifies the Second Commandment: no gods of silver or gold 'with me' (itti). The phrase 'with me' is striking — the prohibition is not merely against replacing God but against supplementing Him. Israel is forbidden to worship YHWH alongside other representations, even those intended to represent Him. This directly anticipates the golden calf crisis (Exod 32), where Aaron fashions a golden calf and declares 'These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' (Exod 32:4) — the calf was likely intended to represent YHWH, not to replace Him. Silver and gold — the most precious metals — cannot capture the infinite God. The costliest materials are worthless for representing the Creator.
verses.doctrinal_note: The specific mention of silver and gold idols connects to the broader biblical principle that human craftsmanship cannot contain or represent God. Isaiah mocks the idol-maker who fashions a god from the same wood he uses for fuel (Isa 44:9-20). Acts 17:29 states: 'We ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device.' God alone determines how He will be worshipped — the 'regulative principle' of worship that flows from the Second Commandment.

verses.kjv_text: An altar of earth thou shalt make unto me, and shalt sacrifice thereon thy burnt offerings, and thy peace offerings, thy sheep, and thine oxen: in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee.
verses.original_text: מִזְבַּח אֲדָמָה תַּעֲשֶׂה־לִּי וְזָבַחְתָּ עָלָיו אֶת־עֹלֹתֶיךָ וְאֶת־שְׁלָמֶיךָ אֶת־צֹאנְךָ וְאֶת־בְּקָרֶךָ בְּכָל־הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַזְכִּיר אֶת־שְׁמִי אָבוֹא אֵלֶיךָ וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ׃
verses.transliteration: Mizbach adamah ta'aseh-lli vezavachta alav et-olotekha ve'et-shelamekha et-tzonekha ve'et-beqarekha bekhol-hamaqom asher azkir et-shemi avo elekha uverakhttikha.
verses.study_note: God prescribes the simplest possible altar: 'an altar of earth' (mizbach adamah) — raw, unhewn, natural material. No elaborate temple architecture, no ornamental art, no human craftsmanship to impress. The contrast with pagan worship is stark: Canaanite and Egyptian temples were architectural marvels; YHWH requires dirt. Two types of offerings are named: burnt offerings (olot — entirely consumed, representing total consecration) and peace offerings (shelamim — shared between God and the worshiper, representing fellowship and communion). The closing promise is extraordinary: 'in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee' — God promises to be present wherever He chooses to reveal Himself. 'And I will bless thee' (uverakhttikha) — simple, obedient worship receives divine blessing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The earthen altar establishes a fundamental principle: worship is about God's prescribed means, not human innovation. The simplicity of the altar contrasts with the magnificence of the God who meets there — it is God's presence, not the altar's beauty, that makes worship sacred. This principle anticipates Jesus' teaching: 'God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth' (John 4:24). The promise 'I will come unto thee' is the essence of all biblical worship: God initiates His presence among His people. From Sinai to Tabernacle to Temple to Incarnation to the indwelling Spirit, God comes to His people.

verses.kjv_text: And if thou wilt make me an altar of stone, thou shalt not build it of hewn stone: for if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־מִזְבַּח אֲבָנִים תַּעֲשֶׂה־לִּי לֹא תִבְנֶה אֶתְהֶן גָּזִית כִּי חַרְבְּךָ הֵנַפְתָּ עָלֶיהָ וַתְּחַלְלֶהָ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-mizbach avanim ta'aseh-lli lo tivneh ethen gazit ki charberkha henafta aleiha vatechalelha.
verses.study_note: If stone is used instead of earth, it must be unhewn (lo gazit) — natural, uncut stone. The reason is remarkable: 'if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it' (vatechalelha — literally 'you have profaned it'). The word for 'tool' is 'cherev' — which is also the word for 'sword.' The instrument of human violence and craftsmanship desecrates what is sacred. This principle appears again at the Temple construction: 'the house, when it was in building, was built of stone made ready before it was brought thither: so that there was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house' (1 Kings 6:7). Worship must not bear the marks of human manipulation or violence. God sanctifies what He has made; human tools can only profane it.
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition against hewn stone expresses the principle that salvation is entirely God's work, not human achievement. An altar shaped by human tools would suggest human contribution to the means of atonement. Isaiah captures this principle: 'All our righteousnesses are as filthy rags' (Isa 64:6). The unhewn altar anticipates the 'stone cut out without hands' of Daniel's vision (Dan 2:34-35) — the kingdom of God established not by human effort but by divine power. Christ Himself is the 'living stone, disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God' (1 Pet 2:4) — the altar and the sacrifice in one.

verses.kjv_text: Neither shalt thou go up by steps unto mine altar, that thy nakedness be not discovered thereon.
verses.original_text: וְלֹא תַעֲלֶה בְמַעֲלֹת עַל־מִזְבְּחִי אֲשֶׁר לֹא תִגָּלֶה עֶרְוָתְךָ עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Velo ta'aleh vema'alot al-mizbichi asher lo tiggaleh ervatekha alav.
verses.study_note: The final instruction: no steps leading up to the altar, lest the priest's 'nakedness' (ervah) be exposed. This practical concern carries deep theological significance. 'Nakedness' (ervah) in Scripture is connected to shame since the Fall (Gen 3:7,10,21) — exposed nakedness symbolizes human vulnerability, shame, and the exposure of sin. Worship must not create conditions for shame or indignity. The priests later receive linen breeches (Exod 28:42) to address this concern. More broadly, the prohibition against elevation teaches that no one 'ascends' to God by their own steps — God comes down to meet humanity at ground level. The altar is low because grace stoops; human religion builds upward, but divine worship brings God near.
verses.doctrinal_note: This closing verse completes the chapter's theology of worship. From the Decalogue's 'I AM the LORD thy God' to this final instruction about the altar, the entire chapter declares: worship is God-defined and grace-grounded. No steps upward — because God descended to Sinai. No human tools — because God provides the means of worship. No images — because God defines His own representation. No other gods — because YHWH alone is God. The chapter that began with the Ten Commandments ends with the altar — law and sacrifice, commandment and atonement, holiness and grace. The same God who reveals the standard of righteousness also provides the means of approaching Him when that standard is broken. This is the gospel in seed form.

meta.overview: Exodus 20 is the theological summit of the Pentateuch and one of the most consequential chapters in all of Scripture. God Himself speaks audibly to an entire nation — the only time in recorded history when a deity addresses a people directly without prophetic mediation. The chapter opens with God's self-identification as YHWH, the covenant redeemer who brought Israel out of Egypt, establishing grace as the foundation of law. The Ten Commandments ('Ten Words,' aseret haddevarim) then follow in a carefully structured sequence: the first four governing humanity's vertical relationship with God (no other gods, no images, no vain use of the Name, Sabbath observance), and the final six governing the horizontal relationships among people (honor parents, do not murder, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not bear false witness, do not covet). The commandments move from internal allegiance (vv.2-6) to external worship (vv.7-11) to social ethics (vv.12-17), demonstrating that true religion encompasses belief, worship, and conduct. After the Decalogue, the people tremble at the theophany of thunder, lightning, smoke, and trumpet, and beg Moses to mediate — a scene that Hebrews 12:18-21 later contrasts with the grace of the New Covenant. The chapter concludes with God's instructions about proper worship: no graven images, and an earthen altar without hewn stones — worship must be God-defined, not humanly crafted. Jesus summarizes the entire Decalogue in two commandments: love God (Deut 6:5) and love your neighbor (Lev 19:18), declaring that on these two hang all the Law and the Prophets (Matt 22:37-40). Paul declares that Christ is 'the end [telos — goal, fulfillment] of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth' (Rom 10:4).
meta.theme: The Decalogue: Covenant Law Spoken by God — Grace Before Commandment
meta.keyWordMeaning: Word, speech, command, matter — the Ten Commandments are literally the 'Ten Words' (aseret haddevarim, Deut 4:13). God's 'davar' is both creative and legislative: the same God who spoke the world into existence now speaks His moral will to His covenant people. Every commandment is a 'davar' — a living word with creative and binding force.
outline: God's Self-Identification and the Prologue of Grace (vv.1-2): YHWH as Redeemer before Lawgiver
The First Commandment: No Other Gods (v.3): Exclusive covenant allegiance
The Second Commandment: No Graven Images (vv.4-6): God defines how He is to be worshipped
The Third Commandment: The Name of YHWH (v.7): Protecting the sacred Name
The Fourth Commandment: Remember the Sabbath (vv.8-11): Creation rest as covenant sign
The Fifth Commandment: Honor Father and Mother (v.12): The bridge between the two tables
The Sixth Commandment: Thou Shalt Not Kill (v.13): The sanctity of human life
The Seventh Commandment: Thou Shalt Not Commit Adultery (v.14): Covenant faithfulness in marriage
The Eighth Commandment: Thou Shalt Not Steal (v.15): The sanctity of property and labor
The Ninth Commandment: No False Witness (v.16): Truth as the foundation of justice
The Tenth Commandment: Thou Shalt Not Covet (v.17): The internal root of all external sin
The People Tremble at the Theophany (vv.18-21): Fear, distance, and the need for a mediator
God's Instructions on Worship and Altars (vv.22-26): Worship must be God-defined, not humanly crafted

verses.kjv_text: Now these are the judgments which thou shalt set before them.
verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה הַמִּשְׁפָּטִים אֲשֶׁר תָּשִׂים לִפְנֵיהֶם׃
