verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh hammishpatim asher tasim lifneihem.
verses.study_note: This verse is the heading for the entire "Book of the Covenant" (mishpatim section) extending through Exodus 23:33. The conjunction "and" (ve) connects these laws directly to the Ten Commandments just given — they are not separate legislation but the practical application of the Decalogue. "Judgments" (mishpatim) are case laws, the casuistic "if...then" rulings that translate absolute moral principles into concrete situations. "Thou shalt set before them" (tasim lifneihem) — God commands Moses to lay these laws out clearly, like setting food on a table (the same verb is used in Deut 4:44). The Torah is not hidden but presented for understanding. Jewish tradition counts this as the beginning of the Oral Law tradition — not merely written statutes but laws requiring interpretation and application by judges.
verses.doctrinal_note: The transition from the Ten Commandments (apodictic law: "Thou shalt / shalt not") to the mishpatim (casuistic law: "if a man...then") demonstrates that God's moral absolutes must be worked out in the complexities of daily life. Theology that does not descend into ethics is incomplete. The God who thundered from Sinai now legislates about servants, injuries, and property — because holiness permeates every dimension of human existence. Jesus affirmed this principle: "the weightier matters of the law — justice, mercy, and faithfulness" (Matt 23:23).

verses.kjv_text: If thou buy an Hebrew servant, six years he shall serve: and in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing.
verses.original_text: כִּי תִקְנֶה עֶבֶד עִבְרִי שֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים יַעֲבֹד וּבַשְּׁבִעִת יֵצֵא לַחָפְשִׁי חִנָּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki tikneh eved Ivri shesh shanim ya'avod uvashshevi'it yetze lachofshi chinnam.
verses.study_note: The very first case law after the Ten Commandments concerns the rights of servants — a revolutionary theological statement about God's priorities. In a world where slavery was absolute and lifelong, God legislates a maximum six-year term for Hebrew debt-servants, with automatic release in the seventh year. The "seventh year" (shevi'it) echoes the sabbatical principle of creation (Gen 2:2-3) and the Sabbath commandment (Exod 20:8-11) — just as God rested on the seventh day, servants must be released in the seventh year. "Free for nothing" (lachofshi chinnam) — without any payment or obligation. The servant owes nothing for his freedom. Israel, freshly delivered from Egyptian bondage, must never create a permanent underclass. The God who said "I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage" (20:2) now ensures that His redeemed people extend that same liberation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The seventh-year release is profoundly typological. As Israel was enslaved and set free by God's sovereign act, so every Hebrew servant must experience a sabbatical deliverance. The principle of "free for nothing" (chinnam — graciously, without cost) prefigures the gospel: "Being justified freely (dorean) by his grace" (Rom 3:24). Christ's redemption grants spiritual freedom that the sabbatical year could only shadow. Paul applies this directly: "Ye are bought with a price; be not ye the servants of men" (1 Cor 7:23).

verses.kjv_text: If he came in by himself, he shall go out by himself: if he were married, then his wife shall go out with him.
verses.original_text: אִם־בְּגַפּוֹ יָבֹא בְּגַפּוֹ יֵצֵא אִם־בַּעַל אִשָּׁה הוּא וְיָצְאָה אִשְׁתּוֹ עִמּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-begappo yavo begappo yetze im-ba'al ishshah hu veyatze'ah ishto immo.
verses.study_note: The principle of status preservation: the servant exits with whatever status he entered. "By himself" (begappo) — literally "with his body/back," meaning alone, unmarried. If he entered married, his wife leaves with him. The marriage bond is recognized as existing prior to and independent of the servitude. The master has no claim on the servant's spouse. This protects family integrity even within the institution of servitude — a radical concept in the ancient Near East, where a master typically owned the servant's entire household. The repetition of begappo ("with his body... with his body") creates a legal formula of exact reciprocity.

verses.kjv_text: If his master have given him a wife, and she have born him sons or daughters; the wife and her children shall be her master’s, and he shall go out by himself.
verses.original_text: אִם־אֲדֹנָיו יִתֶּן־לוֹ אִשָּׁה וְיָלְדָה־לוֹ בָנִים אוֹ בָנוֹת הָאִשָּׁה וִילָדֶיהָ תִּהְיֶה לַאדֹנֶיהָ וְהוּא יֵצֵא בְגַפּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-adonav yitten-lo ishshah veyaldah-lo vanim o vanot ha'ishshah viyladeha tihyeh la'adoneha vehu yetze vegappo.
verses.study_note: This is the most difficult verse in the servant legislation and the setup for the voluntary bondslave ceremony in vv.5-6. If the master provided a wife during service, the wife and children remain with the master's household at release. This seems harsh by modern standards but must be understood within the legal framework: the master had economic obligations for the wife and children, and the servant's debt service did not cover their maintenance. The verse creates a deliberate tension — the servant faces a choice between freedom and family — which drives the profound theology of the next two verses. It is precisely this painful dilemma that makes the bondslave's choice so powerful as a type of Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The tension between freedom and love created here is theologically intentional. The servant who has come to love his wife and children faces the ultimate choice: personal liberty or covenant love. This impossible dilemma is resolved not by legal mechanism but by voluntary self-surrender (vv.5-6) — the servant freely chooses permanent service out of love. This is the most profound Old Testament type of Christ's incarnation: "He took upon him the form of a servant" (Phil 2:7) — not by compulsion but by love.

verses.kjv_text: And if the servant shall plainly say, I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free:
verses.original_text: וְאִם־אָמֹר יֹאמַר הָעֶבֶד אָהַבְתִּי אֶת־אֲדֹנִי אֶת־אִשְׁתִּי וְאֶת־בָּנָי לֹא אֵצֵא חָפְשִׁי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-amor yomar ha'eved ahavti et-adoni et-ishti ve'et-banai lo etze chofshi.
verses.study_note: One of the most theologically loaded verses in the Torah. "Shall plainly say" (amor yomar) — the emphatic infinitive absolute. This is no mumbled resignation but a clear, public, deliberate declaration. The servant says three things: (1) "I love my master" (ahavti et-adoni), (2) "my wife," (3) "and my children." Love is the motivation — not obligation, not economic calculation, not coercion. The word ahav (love) is the same verb used in the Shema: "Thou shalt love (ahav) the LORD thy God" (Deut 6:5). And then the climactic declaration: "I will not go out free" (lo etze chofshi) — a voluntary renunciation of liberty for the sake of love. The servant chooses permanent service because his love for those he serves is greater than his love of freedom.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the theological heart of the bondslave passage and one of the richest Christological types in the Old Testament. Christ, who existed in the "form of God" (Phil 2:6), voluntarily chose not to exercise His divine prerogatives but took "the form of a servant" (Phil 2:7). His declaration mirrors the bondslave's: "I love my Master [the Father], my wife [the Church], and my children [believers]" — and therefore "I will not go out free." Jesus said, "No man taketh my life from me... I lay it down of myself" (John 10:18). The incarnation, like the bondslave ceremony, is a voluntary act of love that chooses permanent service over rightful freedom.

verses.kjv_text: Then his master shall bring him unto the judges; he shall also bring him to the door, or unto the door post; and his master shall bore his ear through with an aul; and he shall serve him for ever.
verses.original_text: וְהִגִּישׁוֹ אֲדֹנָיו אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים וְהִגִּישׁוֹ אֶל־הַדֶּלֶת אוֹ אֶל־הַמְּזוּזָה וְרָצַע אֲדֹנָיו אֶת־אָזְנוֹ בַּמַּרְצֵעַ וַעֲבָדוֹ לְעֹלָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiggisho adonav el-ha'Elohim vehiggisho el-haddelet o el-hammezuzah veratza adonav et-ozno bammartzea va'avado le'olam.
verses.study_note: The bondslave ceremony — one of the most symbol-laden rituals in the Torah. Three elements: (1) Brought "unto the judges" (el-ha'Elohim) — literally "unto God" or "unto the gods [judges]," indicating this is a sacred, public, legally binding act witnessed by divine authority. (2) Brought "to the door, or unto the door post" (el-haddelet o el-hammezuzah) — the doorpost is the same location where Passover blood was applied (Exod 12:7,22). The door is the threshold between slavery and freedom, and the servant chooses to remain. (3) The ear is pierced with an awl (martzea) — the ear that heard "I am the LORD thy God who brought thee out" now bears the mark of voluntary servitude. "For ever" (le'olam) — permanently, until the Jubilee year (Lev 25:40). The pierced ear against the doorpost combines Passover imagery with covenant commitment.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Christological typology is extraordinary: (a) The doorpost (mezuzah) connects to Passover — Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us (1 Cor 5:7). (b) The piercing of the body at the doorpost prefigures Christ's crucifixion — "they pierced my hands and my feet" (Ps 22:16). (c) Psalm 40:6 (quoted in Heb 10:5) reads: "Mine ears hast thou opened" — literally "ears thou hast dug/pierced for me" — directly referencing this ceremony and applying it to the Messiah. (d) The servant's declaration "I will not go out free" becomes Christ's "Lo, I come to do thy will, O God" (Heb 10:7). The bondslave of Exodus 21 is perhaps the single most complete Old Testament type of Christ's voluntary, love-motivated, permanent self-offering.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man sell his daughter to be a maidservant, she shall not go out as the menservants do.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִמְכֹּר אִישׁ אֶת־בִּתּוֹ לְאָמָה לֹא תֵצֵא כְּצֵאת הָעֲבָדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yimkor ish et-bitto le'amah lo tetze ketze't ha'avadim.
verses.study_note: This verse introduces protections for female servants that were unprecedented in the ancient Near East. "She shall not go out as the menservants do" — she is not simply released after six years to fend for herself. The following verses (8-11) reveal that her arrangement is essentially a marriage contract: the master either marries her, gives her to his son, or must allow her full redemption. The higher protections reflect the greater vulnerability of women in ancient society. Far from condoning exploitation, the law creates a legal framework ensuring that a daughter sold into service receives the status of a wife, not a disposable laborer. The verb "sell" (makar) in this context means to arrange a marriage alliance through service — similar to Jacob's service for Rachel (Gen 29:18-20).
verses.doctrinal_note: God's law provides greater protection for the more vulnerable — a principle that runs throughout Scripture and finds its fullest expression in Christ's ministry to the marginalized. The law does not merely tolerate the existing social order but reforms it, creating rights and protections where none existed. This principle of advocating for the powerless is the foundation of biblical social ethics (Prov 31:8-9; Isa 1:17; James 1:27).

verses.kjv_text: If she please not her master, who hath betrothed her to himself, then shall he let her be redeemed: to sell her unto a strange nation he shall have no power, seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her.
verses.original_text: אִם־רָעָה בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנֶיהָ אֲשֶׁר־לוֹ יְעָדָהּ וְהֶפְדָּהּ לְעַם נָכְרִי לֹא־יִמְשֹׁל לְמָכְרָהּ בְּבִגְדוֹ־בָהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-ra'ah be'einei adoneha asher-lo ye'adah vehefddah le'am nokhri lo-yimshol lemokhrah bevigdo-vah.
verses.study_note: Three critical protections: (1) If the master finds her displeasing, he must allow her redemption (hefddah) — her family may buy her back. (2) He is absolutely forbidden from selling her to foreigners (am nokhri). (3) The legal basis: "seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her" (bevigdo-vah) — if he fails to fulfill his obligations, he is the covenant breaker, and she retains her rights. The verb "betrothed" (ye'adah) confirms this arrangement has marital dimensions. The word "redeemed" (hefddah, from padah) introduces the redemption concept — the same vocabulary later applied to God redeeming Israel (Exod 13:13; Ps 130:7-8). God protects those who cannot protect themselves.

verses.kjv_text: And if he have betrothed her unto his son, he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־לִבְנוֹ יִיעָדֶנָּה כְּמִשְׁפַּט הַבָּנוֹת יַעֲשֶׂה־לָּהּ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-livno yi'adenah kemishpat habbanot ya'aseh-lah.
verses.study_note: If the master designates the servant-woman for his son, she must be treated "after the manner of daughters" (kemishpat habbanot) — with full daughter-in-law rights, not as a servant. Her status is elevated to that of a freeborn woman of the household. The word mishpat (manner/right) is the same word used in verse 1 for "judgments" — she receives the full legal standing that family membership entails. This provision transforms a potentially exploitative arrangement into one of full familial incorporation.

verses.kjv_text: If he take him another wife; her food, her raiment, and her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish.
verses.original_text: אִם־אַחֶרֶת יִקַּח־לוֹ שְׁאֵרָהּ כְּסוּתָהּ וְעֹנָתָהּ לֹא יִגְרָע׃
verses.transliteration: Im-acheret yiqqach-lo she'erah kesutah ve'onatah lo yigra.
verses.study_note: Even if the master takes another wife, three rights of the first wife are inviolable: (1) she'erah — her food/flesh-provisions (sustenance), (2) kesutah — her clothing (covering), and (3) onatah — her conjugal rights (duty of marriage). These three constitute the minimum legal obligations of a husband in Israelite law. "Shall he not diminish" (lo yigra) — these rights cannot be reduced. This is the earliest known legal codification of a wife's conjugal rights in the ancient world. The Talmud later built extensively on this verse for marriage law. Notably, the Torah protects even the sexual rights of a woman in a patriarchal society — an assertion of her dignity as a person, not merely property.

verses.kjv_text: And if he do not these three unto her, then shall she go out free without money.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־שְׁלָשׁ־אֵלֶּה לֹא יַעֲשֶׂה לָהּ וְיָצְאָה חִנָּם אֵין כָּסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-shelosh-elleh lo ya'aseh lah veyatze'ah chinnam ein kasef.
verses.study_note: The enforcement provision — the teeth of the law. If the master fails to provide food, clothing, or conjugal rights, the woman goes free automatically, without any redemption payment. "Without money" (chinnam ein kasef) — she owes nothing. The failure is entirely his. This gives a servant-wife the legal right to unilateral divorce based on neglect — a right that freeborn women in surrounding cultures often did not possess. The Torah thus gives the most vulnerable woman in society a legal exit from a broken covenant. "Chinnam" (freely) echoes verse 2 — the same gracious freedom extended to male servants is here extended to female servants on even stronger terms.

verses.kjv_text: He that smiteth a man, so that he die, shall be surely put to death.
verses.original_text: מַכֵּה אִישׁ וָמֵת מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Makkeh ish vamet mot yumat.
verses.study_note: The transition from servant law to capital law. "Smiteth a man so that he die" — intentional, fatal violence. "Shall be surely put to death" (mot yumat) — the emphatic infinitive absolute expressing absolute legal certainty. This is the Sixth Commandment ("Thou shalt not kill") applied as enforceable law. The brevity of the Hebrew — just five words — conveys the gravity and finality. Human life bears the image of God (Gen 9:6); to murder is to assault the divine image. No ransom or compensation can substitute for the death penalty in cases of willful murder (Num 35:31). The law applies universally — "a man" (ish) without social qualification — servant or free, rich or poor.
verses.doctrinal_note: The sanctity of human life is grounded in the imago Dei (Gen 1:26-27; 9:6). Capital punishment for murder is the strongest legal expression of the value God places on human life. It is not that life is cheap but that it is so precious that its deliberate destruction demands the ultimate penalty. This principle undergirds all biblical ethics: every person, regardless of status, bears God's image and is precious in His sight.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man lie not in wait, but God deliver him into his hand; then I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee.
verses.original_text: וַאֲשֶׁר לֹא צָדָה וְהָאֱלֹהִים אִנָּה לְיָדוֹ וְשַׂמְתִּי לְךָ מָקוֹם אֲשֶׁר יָנוּס שָׁמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Va'asher lo tzadah veha'Elohim innah leyado vesamti lekha maqom asher yanus shammah.
verses.study_note: The critical distinction between murder and manslaughter. "Lie not in wait" (lo tzadah) — no premeditation, no ambush. "But God deliver him into his hand" (veha'Elohim innah leyado) — an extraordinary theological statement: even accidental death falls within God's sovereign providence. The verb innah means to cause to happen, to bring about by divine appointment. God is sovereign even over apparent accidents. The remedy: "I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee" — the cities of refuge, later established in Numbers 35:9-34 and Joshua 20. God provides sanctuary for the unintentional killer, protecting him from the blood avenger (go'el haddam) while maintaining justice. This is the seed of the entire cities-of-refuge system.
verses.doctrinal_note: The cities of refuge are a profound type of Christ. The manslayer flees to a designated place of protection to escape the avenger of blood. Christ is the believer's refuge: "We who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us" (Heb 6:18). The distinction between willful sin and sins of ignorance runs throughout Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution at the cross, where Christ prayed "Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do" (Luke 23:34).

verses.kjv_text: But if a man come presumptuously upon his neighbour, to slay him with guile; thou shalt take him from mine altar, that he may die.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יָזִד אִישׁ עַל־רֵעֵהוּ לְהָרְגוֹ בְעָרְמָה מֵעִם מִזְבְּחִי תִּקָּחֶנּוּ לָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yazid ish al-re'ehu lehorgo ve'ormah me'im mizbchi tiqqachennu lamut.
verses.study_note: The contrast with v.13 — willful, premeditated murder. "Presumptuously" (yazid) — with deliberate malice and arrogance. "With guile" (ve'ormah) — using cunning and deception. For such a murderer, even the altar provides no sanctuary. "Thou shalt take him from mine altar" — even sacred asylum is denied. Joab later attempted to escape execution by grasping the horns of the altar (1 Kings 2:28-34), but Solomon ordered him removed, following this very principle. The altar is a place of mercy, not a shield for injustice. God's holiness will not be co-opted to protect the wicked.

verses.kjv_text: And he that smiteth his father, or his mother, shall be surely put to death.
verses.original_text: וּמַכֵּה אָבִיו וְאִמּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Umakkeh aviv ve'immo mot yumat.
verses.study_note: The Fifth Commandment ("Honour thy father and thy mother") is given legal enforcement. Striking (makkeh) a parent is a capital offense — not merely a family dispute but an assault on the divinely ordained order of authority. Both "father and mother" are named, giving the mother equal legal standing with the father — unusual in ancient Near Eastern law codes. In Hammurabi's Code, striking a social superior was punished, but Israelite law uniquely extends this to the parent-child relationship as a reflection of one's relationship to God. The parent represents divine authority in the family structure.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gravity of this law reflects the theology that parental authority is delegated divine authority. To strike a parent is to rebel against the God-appointed structure of human community. Jesus condemned the Pharisees for using Corban to circumvent this commandment (Mark 7:9-13), showing that the principle of honoring parents extends beyond mere physical non-violence to active, sacrificial care.

verses.kjv_text: And he that stealeth a man, and selleth him, or if he be found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death.
verses.original_text: וְגֹנֵב אִישׁ וּמְכָרוֹ וְנִמְצָא בְיָדוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Vegoenv ish umkharo venimtza veyado mot yumat.
verses.study_note: Kidnapping — the stealing of a person for sale — is a capital offense. The Eighth Commandment ("Thou shalt not steal") reaches its most extreme application: the theft of a human being. "A man" (ish) is generic — any person, regardless of status, nationality, or age. Both the act of stealing and the state of possession ("found in his hand") are criminalized. In the ancient world, the slave trade was a primary economic institution. God categorically condemns it with the death penalty. This law, had it been applied, would have abolished the entire international slave trade. Paul echoes this in 1 Timothy 1:10, listing "menstealers" (andrapodistai) among the worst sinners.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the definitive biblical condemnation of human trafficking and chattel slavery. The Eighth Commandment protects not only property but personhood — to steal a human being is the supreme theft because it steals the image of God. The distinction between Israelite debt-servitude (vv.2-6) and kidnapping-slavery (v.16) is absolute: one is a temporary economic arrangement with rights; the other is a capital crime. All forms of human trafficking stand condemned by this verse.

verses.kjv_text: And he that curseth his father, or his mother, shall surely be put to death.
verses.original_text: וּמְקַלֵּל אָבִיו וְאִמּוֹ מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Umqallel aviv ve'immo mot yumat.
verses.study_note: Cursing (meqallel) a parent is a capital offense paralleling v.15 (striking a parent). The verb qalal means to make light of, to treat with contempt, to curse — the opposite of kaved (to honor/make heavy), used in the Fifth Commandment. While v.15 addresses physical violence, v.17 addresses verbal violence — showing that both the hand and the tongue can commit capital offenses against the parent-child covenant. Jesus cited this law (Matt 15:4; Mark 7:10) as still morally binding, condemning the Pharisees who found legal loopholes to avoid supporting their parents. The parallel between striking (v.15) and cursing (v.17) demonstrates that words carry the same moral weight as actions.

verses.kjv_text: And if men strive together, and one smite another with a stone, or with his fist, and he die not, but keepeth his bed:
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יְרִיבֻן אֲנָשִׁים וְהִכָּה־אִישׁ אֶת־רֵעֵהוּ בְּאֶבֶן אוֹ בְאֶגְרֹף וְלֹא יָמוּת וְנָפַל לְמִשְׁכָּב׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yerivun anashim vehikkah-ish et-re'ehu be'even o ve'egrof velo yamut venafal lemishkav.
verses.study_note: The transition to personal injury law — assault that is serious but non-fatal. "If men strive together" (yerivun) — a mutual altercation, not a one-sided attack. The weapons specify the severity: a stone (even) or a fist (egrof) — objects capable of serious harm. The victim does not die but "keepeth his bed" (nafal lemishkav — literally "falls to his lying-down"). This is the first recorded tort law — civil liability for personal injury. The following verse establishes the remedy: the assailant pays medical expenses and lost wages. This casuistic "if...then" structure is characteristic of ancient Near Eastern law, but Israel's version uniquely combines civil remedy with theological accountability.

verses.kjv_text: If he rise again, and walk abroad upon his staff, then shall he that smote him be quit: only he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall cause him to be thoroughly healed.
verses.original_text: אִם־יָקוּם וְהִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּחוּץ עַל־מִשְׁעַנְתּוֹ וְנִקָּה הַמַּכֶּה רַק שִׁבְתּוֹ יִתֵּן וְרַפֹּא יְרַפֵּא׃
verses.transliteration: Im-yakum vehithallekh bachutz al-mish'anto venikkah hammakkeh raq shivto yitten verappo yerappei.
verses.study_note: The remedy for non-fatal assault — the earliest known tort-law formula for personal injury compensation. Two obligations: (1) "Pay for the loss of his time" (shivto yitten) — compensation for lost wages during recovery. The word sheveth means cessation/sitting — the economic loss from inability to work. (2) "Cause him to be thoroughly healed" (rappo yerappei — emphatic infinitive absolute) — pay all medical expenses until full recovery. The assailant is "quit" (nikkah — acquitted of the capital charge) only when the victim recovers and walks with his staff. This is remarkably sophisticated tort law establishing both compensatory damages and the duty to pay for medical care.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man smite his servant, or his maid, with a rod, and he die under his hand; he shall be surely punished.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יַכֶּה אִישׁ אֶת־עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ אֶת־אֲמָתוֹ בַּשֵּׁבֶט וּמֵת תַּחַת יָדוֹ נָקֹם יִנָּקֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yakkeh ish et-avdo o et-amato bashshevet umet tachat yado naqom yinnaqem.
verses.study_note: A groundbreaking law: a master who beats a servant to death is "surely punished" (naqom yinnaqem — emphatic infinitive absolute of vengeance/punishment). In every other ancient Near Eastern legal code — Hammurabi, Hittite, Sumerian — a master could kill his slave with impunity, as slaves were property. Israelite law declares that a servant is a person protected by law. "Under his hand" (tachat yado) — immediately, as a direct result of the beating. The rod (shevet) was the instrument of discipline, not death. The punishment for killing a servant is debated — some scholars understand naqom yinnaqem as the death penalty (as in v.12), others as severe judicial penalty. Either way, the servant has legal personhood.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is revolutionary in the ancient world — the first legal code in history to penalize a master for killing a servant. It establishes the principle that every human being, regardless of social status, possesses legal personhood and the right to life. Servants are not property to be disposed of but persons made in God's image. This principle finds its ultimate expression in Galatians 3:28: "There is neither bond nor free... for ye are all one in Christ Jesus."

verses.kjv_text: Notwithstanding, if he continue a day or two, he shall not be punished: for he is his money.
verses.original_text: אַךְ אִם־יוֹם אוֹ יוֹמַיִם יַעֲמֹד לֹא יֻקַּם כִּי כַסְפּוֹ הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Akh im-yom o yomayim ya'amod lo yuqqam ki khaspo hu.
verses.study_note: The most debated verse in the servant legislation. If the servant survives a day or two after discipline, the master is not punished, "for he is his money" (ki khaspo hu). The reasoning: (1) survival beyond a day or two indicates the master did not intend to kill; (2) the master's economic loss in the servant's death serves as its own penalty — he has destroyed his own investment. "He is his money" does not mean the servant is merely property — v.20 has already established that killing a servant is punishable. Rather, it acknowledges the economic relationship while limiting criminal liability to cases of clear lethal intent. The tension between vv.20-21 reflects the Torah's realistic engagement with an imperfect social institution while progressively humanizing it.

verses.kjv_text: If men strive, and hurt a woman with child, so that her fruit depart from her, and yet no mischief follow: he shall be surely punished, according as the woman’s husband will lay upon him; and he shall pay as the judges determine.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִנָּצוּ אֲנָשִׁים וְנָגְפוּ אִשָּׁה הָרָה וְיָצְאוּ יְלָדֶיהָ וְלֹא יִהְיֶה אָסוֹן עָנוֹשׁ יֵעָנֵשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר יָשִׁית עָלָיו בַּעַל הָאִשָּׁה וְנָתַן בִּפְלִלִים׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yinnatzu anashim venagefu ishshah harah veyatze'u yeladeha velo yihyeh ason anosh ye'anesh ka'asher yashit alav ba'al ha'ishshah venatan biflilim.
verses.study_note: One of the most significant verses for the theology of unborn life. "Hurt a woman with child" — the injury is to a pregnant woman caught in a fight between men. "Her fruit depart" (veyatze'u yeladeha — literally "and her children come out") — premature birth. The critical phrase is "and yet no mischief follow" (velo yihyeh ason) — if no serious harm occurs to either mother or child. "Mischief" (ason) means serious injury or death — the same word used of Jacob's fear for Benjamin (Gen 42:4; 44:29). In this case (no ason), a fine is imposed, determined by the husband and approved by judges. But if ason (serious harm) does follow — the next verse applies the lex talionis, "life for life." This structure treats the unborn child as a person: harm to the child triggers the same penalties as harm to any other person.
verses.doctrinal_note: This passage is foundational for the biblical theology of the sanctity of unborn life. The Hebrew text uses yeladeha ("her children") — not "fetus" or "embryo" but "children." The law treats injury to the unborn child with the same categories applied to injury of any person — fine for non-serious harm, lex talionis for serious harm, death for death. The unborn child is a person under God's law, not a mere appendage of the mother. This undergirds the biblical principle that human life begins at conception and is precious in God's sight (Ps 139:13-16; Jer 1:5; Luke 1:41-44).

verses.kjv_text: And if any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life,
verses.original_text: וְאִם־אָסוֹן יִהְיֶה וְנָתַתָּה נֶפֶשׁ תַּחַת נָפֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-ason yihyeh venatatah nefesh tachat nafesh.
verses.study_note: If "mischief" (ason — serious harm or death) does occur to either the mother or the unborn child, the penalty escalates to "life for life" (nefesh tachat nafesh). This is the beginning of the lex talionis — the law of retribution. "Nefesh" means soul, life, person — the deepest word for human life in Hebrew. "Tachat" means "in place of, under, instead of" — exact equivalence. The principle is not vengeance but proportionality: the punishment must match the crime, no more and no less. This was a limitation on blood vengeance, which in the ancient world often escalated geometrically (cf. Lamech's boast in Gen 4:24). The lex talionis is justice tempered by restraint.
verses.doctrinal_note: The lex talionis operates on the principle of exact justice — proportionality, not excess. In the ancient Near East, vengeance was unlimited; the strong could extract any penalty from the weak. God's law introduces measured justice: the punishment fits the crime. Jesus does not abolish this principle but transcends it with the ethic of grace: "But I say unto you, resist not evil" (Matt 5:39). The cross is where both principles meet — perfect justice (life for life) and perfect grace (Christ's life for ours).

verses.kjv_text: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
verses.original_text: עַיִן תַּחַת עַיִן שֵׁן תַּחַת שֵׁן יָד תַּחַת יָד רֶגֶל תַּחַת רָגֶל׃
verses.transliteration: Ayin tachat ayin shen tachat shen yad tachat yad regel tachat ragel.
verses.study_note: The full articulation of the lex talionis — arguably the most misunderstood verse in the Bible. Four pairs of exact equivalence: eye-eye, tooth-tooth, hand-hand, foot-foot. This is not barbarism but civilization — it replaced the ancient custom of unlimited vengeance with measured, proportional justice. In practice, Jewish tradition (Talmud, Bava Kamma 83b-84a) understood this as requiring monetary compensation equivalent to the injury, not literal mutilation. The point is that the penalty must be exactly equivalent to the harm — neither excessive (vengeance) nor insufficient (injustice). Each pair moves from more severe (eye) to less severe (foot), creating a descending scale of injuries, all governed by the same principle of proportionality.

verses.kjv_text: Burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.
verses.original_text: כְּוִיָּה תַּחַת כְּוִיָּה פֶּצַע תַּחַת פָּצַע חַבּוּרָה תַּחַת חַבּוּרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Keviyyah tachat keviyyah petza tachat patza chabbburah tachat chabburah.
verses.study_note: The lex talionis continues with three more pairs: burning-burning, wound-wound, stripe-stripe. These address injuries from different causes: burning (keviyyah — from fire or hot objects), wound (petza — a cutting injury, laceration), and stripe (chabburah — a bruise, welt from beating). Together with v.24, the seven pairs create a comprehensive scale covering every category of bodily harm. The word chabburah (stripe/bruise) is the same word used prophetically of the Messiah in Isaiah 53:5 — "and with his stripes (chabburato) we are healed." The very vocabulary of the lex talionis is absorbed into Messianic prophecy: Christ bears the chabbburah that we deserved, satisfying divine justice by receiving in His body the punishment proportional to our sin.
verses.doctrinal_note: Isaiah 53:5 uses the word chabburah (stripe/wound) from this very passage to describe the Messiah's atoning suffering. The lex talionis — which demands exact proportional justice — finds its ultimate fulfillment at the cross, where Christ receives the precise punishment that human sin deserves. His stripes (chabburato) heal because they satisfy the justice that the lex talionis embodies. Grace does not bypass justice but fulfills it.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man smite the eye of his servant, or the eye of his maid, that it perish; he shall let him go free for his eye’s sake.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יַכֶּה אִישׁ אֶת־עֵין עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ־אֶת־עֵין אֲמָתוֹ וְשִׁחֲתָהּ לַחָפְשִׁי יְשַׁלְּחֶנּוּ תַּחַת עֵינוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yakkeh ish et-ein avdo o-et-ein amato veshichatah lachofshi yeshallechennu tachat eino.
verses.study_note: A stunning application of the lex talionis to master-servant relations. If a master destroys a servant's eye, the servant goes free — the permanent loss of the eye is compensated by permanent freedom. "That it perish" (veshichatah — to destroy, ruin) — the damage must be permanent. The freedom granted "for his eye's sake" (tachat eino) uses the same word tachat (in place of) from the lex talionis formula. The servant's freedom replaces the lost eye. This is the lex talionis applied as liberation: the master cannot pay a fine and retain the servant. The only adequate compensation for bodily harm to a servant is the servant's freedom. This law made abuse economically devastating for masters.
verses.doctrinal_note: Servants are full persons under the lex talionis. The law does not merely fine the master but liberates the servant — the most costly and most just penalty possible. This demonstrates that in God's law, freedom is the natural right of every person, and bondage is the exception that abuse terminates. The gospel fulfills this principle: Christ's wounds secure our freedom. "Where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty" (2 Cor 3:17).

verses.kjv_text: And if he smite out his manservant’s tooth, or his maidservant’s tooth; he shall let him go free for his tooth’s sake.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־שֵׁן עַבְדּוֹ אוֹ־שֵׁן אֲמָתוֹ יַפִּיל לַחָפְשִׁי יְשַׁלְּחֶנּוּ תַּחַת שִׁנּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-shen avdo o-shen amato yappil lachofshi yeshallechennu tachat shinno.
verses.study_note: The same principle applied to a lesser injury — a knocked-out tooth. If even a tooth (shen) — a relatively minor permanent injury compared to an eye — grants the servant freedom, then how much more any greater injury. The tooth is mentioned because it is the smallest permanent bodily part whose loss can be clearly verified. Rabbinical interpretation extended this law to all permanent physical damage: any irreversible bodily harm by a master freed the servant. Both "manservant" and "maidservant" are named — male and female servants have equal protection. The law uses a fortiori (qal va-chomer) reasoning: if a tooth grants freedom, every greater injury certainly does.

verses.kjv_text: If an ox gore a man or a woman, that they die: then the ox shall be surely stoned, and his flesh shall not be eaten; but the owner of the ox shall be quit.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִגַּח שׁוֹר אֶת־אִישׁ אוֹ אֶת־אִשָּׁה וָמֵת סָקוֹל יִסָּקֵל הַשּׁוֹר וְלֹא יֵאָכֵל אֶת־בְּשָׂרוֹ וּבַעַל הַשּׁוֹר נָקִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yiggach shor et-ish o et-ishshah vamet saqol yissaqel hashor velo ye'akhel et-besaro uva'al hashor naqi.
verses.study_note: The goring ox laws — ancient liability law for dangerous animals. If an ox kills a person, three consequences: (1) the ox is "surely stoned" (saqol yissaqel — emphatic infinitive absolute) — the animal receives the death penalty, echoing Genesis 9:5 ("at the hand of every beast will I require it"). (2) Its flesh shall not be eaten — the animal is treated as accursed, not as food. The prohibition against eating makes it economically total: the owner loses everything. (3) The owner is "quit" (naqi — innocent) — because the first offense was unforeseeable. Note that both "man or woman" are named as potential victims — equal protection regardless of gender. This is the principle of strict liability for animal behavior without owner negligence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The stoning of the ox reflects Genesis 9:5-6: even animal life that takes human life is subject to divine justice, because the victim bears God's image. The prohibition against eating the flesh signifies that the animal has become an instrument of death — it is under a kind of curse, excluded from the life-sustaining cycle of food. This principle of accountability extends even to the non-human realm and underscores the absolute sanctity of human life.

verses.kjv_text: But if the ox were wont to push with his horn in time past, and it hath been testified to his owner, and he hath not kept him in, but that he hath killed a man or a woman; the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death.
verses.original_text: וְאִם שׁוֹר נַגָּח הוּא מִתְּמֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם וְהוּעַד בִּבְעָלָיו וְלֹא יִשְׁמְרֶנּוּ וְהֵמִית אִישׁ אוֹ אִשָּׁה הַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל וְגַם־בְּעָלָיו יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im shor naggach hu mittemol shilshom vehu'ad bive'alav velo yishmerennu vehemit ish o ishshah hashor yissaqel vegam-be'alav yumat.
verses.study_note: The critical escalation: if the ox had a known history of goring ("wont to push" — naggach, a habitual gorer), and the owner was formally warned (vehu'ad — testified to, legally notified), and he still failed to restrain it — then both the ox is stoned and the owner is put to death. "In time past" (mittemol shilshom — literally "from yesterday, from three days ago") is a legal formula for established pattern. This is the earliest articulation of the legal principle of criminal negligence — knowing a danger exists and failing to prevent it makes one personally liable for the consequences. The owner's knowledge transforms an accident into culpable homicide.
verses.doctrinal_note: This law establishes the profound moral principle that knowledge creates responsibility. Ignorance may mitigate guilt (v.28), but willful negligence after warning compounds it to the point of capital liability. The spiritual application is direct: "To him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin" (James 4:17). Greater knowledge brings greater accountability (Luke 12:47-48; Heb 10:26-27).

verses.kjv_text: If there be laid on him a sum of money, then he shall give for the ransom of his life whatsoever is laid upon him.
verses.original_text: אִם־כֹּפֶר יוּשַׁת עָלָיו וְנָתַן פִּדְיֹן נַפְשׁוֹ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־יוּשַׁת עָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Im-kofer yushat alav venatan pidyon nafsho kekhol asher-yushat alav.
verses.study_note: The ransom exception — in cases of negligent homicide by a goring ox (not willful murder, which permits no ransom per Num 35:31), the owner may pay a "ransom" (kofer) to redeem his own life from the death penalty. Two key terms: (1) kofer — ransom, atonement price. The same word used for the atonement money of Exodus 30:12 and the covering/propitiation concept throughout Leviticus. (2) pidyon — redemption price, from padah (to redeem). The owner pays the redemption price for his own life (nafsho — his soul/person). This is the legal-economic expression of the substitutionary principle: a price paid in place of a life. The family of the deceased sets the amount, establishing the victim's value.
verses.doctrinal_note: The vocabulary of kofer (ransom) and pidyon (redemption) in a legal context demonstrates how Israel's civil law and theological vocabulary interpenetrate. The ransom paid to redeem a negligent owner's life prefigures the ultimate ransom: "The Son of man came... to give his life a ransom (lytron) for many" (Mark 10:45). Christ's death is the kofer — the atonement price paid to redeem those under the sentence of death.

verses.kjv_text: Whether he have gored a son, or have gored a daughter, according to this judgment shall it be done unto him.
verses.original_text: אוֹ־בֵן יִגָּח אוֹ־בַת יִגָּח כַּמִּשְׁפָּט הַזֶּה יֵעָשֶׂה לּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: O-ven yiggach o-vat yiggach kammishpat hazzeh ye'aseh lo.
verses.study_note: Equal justice for children — if the goring ox kills a son or daughter (minor children), the same law applies. The life of a child is valued equally with that of an adult. In other ancient Near Eastern codes, the penalty for killing a child was often less severe, or the owner's child was killed as retaliatory justice. God's law rejects vicarious punishment (the owner is liable, not his children) and affirms equal value (children and adults receive the same protection). "According to this judgment" (kammishpat hazzeh) — the same standard, no exceptions, no diminishment.

verses.kjv_text: If the ox shall push a manservant or a maidservant; he shall give unto their master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned.
verses.original_text: אִם־עֶבֶד יִגַּח הַשּׁוֹר אוֹ אָמָה כֶּסֶף שְׁלֹשִׁים שְׁקָלִים יִתֵּן לַאדֹנָיו וְהַשּׁוֹר יִסָּקֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Im-eved yiggach hashor o amah kesef sheloshim sheqalim yitten la'adonav vehashor yissaqel.
verses.study_note: If a goring ox kills a servant, the owner pays thirty shekels of silver to the servant's master, and the ox is stoned. Thirty shekels was the standard valuation of a servant (Lev 27:4) — roughly the price of two years' wages. While modern readers note the apparent inequality (a servant's death receives a fixed compensation rather than the ransom process of vv.29-30), the law still treats the servant's death as requiring both payment and the destruction of the animal. The ox is still stoned — the servant's life still invokes the Genesis 9:5-6 principle of the image of God. The thirty shekels of silver become the most prophetically loaded price in Scripture.
verses.doctrinal_note: Thirty pieces of silver — the price of a servant — becomes the price Judas receives for betraying Jesus (Matt 26:15; 27:3-9; Zech 11:12-13). Zechariah prophesied: "They weighed for my price thirty pieces of silver... cast them to the potter in the house of the LORD." The Messiah, the ultimate Servant of the LORD (Isa 42:1; 52:13), is valued at the price of a slave — the ultimate insult to His divine person. Yet this very price becomes the means of redemption. God takes the world's lowest valuation of His Son and transforms it into the purchase price of salvation.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man shall open a pit, or if a man shall dig a pit, and not cover it, and an ox or an ass fall therein;
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִפְתַּח אִישׁ בּוֹר אוֹ כִּי־יִכְרֶה אִישׁ בֹּר וְלֹא יְכַסֶּנּוּ וְנָפַל־שָׁמָּה שּׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yiftach ish bor o khi-yikhreh ish bor velo yekhessennu venafal-shammah shor o chamor.
verses.study_note: Property damage law: the open pit. Two scenarios are covered: opening an existing pit (yiftach — uncovers a cistern) or digging a new one (yikhreh — excavates). In either case, failure to cover it (velo yekhessennu) creates liability. The negligence principle from vv.29-30 continues: one who creates a hazard is responsible for its consequences. Cisterns and water storage pits were common in Israelite agriculture; leaving one uncovered was a foreseeable danger. The law extends negligence liability from persons (the goring ox) to property (the uncovered pit). This is foundational tort law — the duty of care to prevent foreseeable harm.

verses.kjv_text: The owner of the pit shall make it good, and give money unto the owner of them; and the dead beast shall be his.
verses.original_text: בַּעַל הַבּוֹר יְשַׁלֵּם כֶּסֶף יָשִׁיב לִבְעָלָיו וְהַמֵּת יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ba'al habbor yeshallem kesef yashiv liv'alav vehammet yihyeh-lo.
verses.study_note: The remedy: full restitution. The pit owner pays the full value of the animal and keeps the carcass (which has salvage value for hide and possibly meat, if the animal was clean). "Make it good" (yeshallem) — from shalam, the root of shalom (peace/wholeness). To make restitution is literally to "make whole" — to restore the injured party to their prior state. This is the heart of biblical justice: not merely punishing the wrongdoer but restoring the victim to completeness. The dead animal goes to the pit owner — he absorbs the loss and the salvage, maintaining the principle that the negligent party bears all consequences.

verses.kjv_text: And if one man’s ox hurt another’s, that he die; then they shall sell the live ox, and divide the money of it; and the dead ox also they shall divide.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִגֹּף שׁוֹר־אִישׁ אֶת־שׁוֹר רֵעֵהוּ וָמֵת וּמָכְרוּ אֶת־הַשּׁוֹר הַחַי וְחָצוּ אֶת־כַּסְפּוֹ וְגַם אֶת־הַמֵּת יֶחֱצוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yiggof shor-ish et-shor re'ehu vamet umakheru et-hashor hachai vechatzu et-kaspo vegam et-hammet yechetzun.
verses.study_note: When one man's ox kills another's: the solution is remarkably equitable. Both the living ox and the dead one are divided equally between the two owners. The living ox is sold and the proceeds split; the dead ox is likewise divided. Both parties share the loss equally because neither owner was negligent — the event was unforeseeable. The verb "divide" (chatzu — from chatzah, to split in half) emphasizes exact equity. This contrasts with v.36, where prior knowledge changes the outcome. Without negligence, the loss is shared; with negligence, the loss falls entirely on the negligent party.

verses.kjv_text: Or if it be known that the ox hath used to push in time past, and his owner hath not kept him in; he shall surely pay ox for ox; and the dead shall be his own.
verses.original_text: אוֹ נוֹדַע כִּי שׁוֹר נַגָּח הוּא מִתְּמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם וְלֹא יִשְׁמְרֶנּוּ בְּעָלָיו שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם שׁוֹר תַּחַת הַשּׁוֹר וְהַמֵּת יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: O noda ki shor naggach hu mittemol shilshom velo yishmerennu be'alav shallem yeshallem shor tachat hashor vehammet yihyeh-lo.
verses.study_note: The chapter's final verse returns to the negligence principle: if the ox was a known gorer (naggach — same word as v.29) with an established pattern ("in time past" — mittemol shilshom), and the owner failed to restrain it, the penalty is full restitution: "ox for ox" (shor tachat hashor) — exact replacement, not shared loss. The negligent owner pays for the entire replacement animal and keeps the dead carcass (absorbing both the loss and the salvage). "Surely pay" (shallem yeshallem — emphatic infinitive absolute) — no negotiation, no shared responsibility. Knowledge of the danger combined with failure to act equals full liability. The chapter thus ends as it began — with the principle that knowledge and responsibility are inseparable under God's law.
verses.doctrinal_note: Exodus 21 concludes where it began — with the integration of knowledge and responsibility. From the servant who chooses service out of love (v.5-6) to the owner who must bear the consequences of known negligence (v.36), the chapter weaves a consistent theology: God's law governs every relationship — master and servant, parent and child, neighbor and neighbor, even human and animal. Justice is not abstract but concrete, not theoretical but practical, not harsh but proportional. The mishpatim reveal a God who is equally concerned with the cosmic theophany of Sinai and the uncovered pit in a field — because holiness touches everything.

meta.overview: Exodus 21 begins the "Book of the Covenant" (Sefer HaBerit, cf. 24:7) — the first detailed expansion of the Ten Commandments into practical civil and criminal law. Immediately after the theophany at Sinai (ch. 19-20), God descends from cosmic revelation to case-by-case jurisprudence, demonstrating that the holy God of the mountain is equally concerned with the daily realities of human justice. The chapter opens with laws governing Hebrew servitude (vv.1-11), establishing radical protections unparalleled in the ancient Near East: mandatory release in the seventh year, rights for female servants, and the voluntary bondslave ceremony of ear-piercing at the doorpost. It then moves to capital offenses — murder, kidnapping, striking or cursing parents (vv.12-17) — before addressing personal injury law (vv.18-27), including the famous lex talionis ("eye for eye, tooth for tooth") which was not a license for revenge but a principle of proportional justice limiting excessive punishment. The chapter concludes with laws about dangerous animals (vv.28-36). Throughout, a revolutionary principle emerges: every person — servant, woman, foreigner — possesses inherent dignity before God's law. The mishpatim (ordinances) of Exodus 21 reveal that Sinai's holiness is not abstract but concrete, shaping how Israel treats the weakest and most vulnerable.
meta.theme: Covenant Justice: The Sanctity of Persons Under God's Law
meta.keyWordMeaning: Judgments, ordinances, case laws — from shaphat (to judge). The practical legal rulings that apply the moral principles of the Ten Commandments to real-life situations. These are not arbitrary statutes but expressions of God's righteous character applied to human community.
outline: The Ordinances Introduced (v.1)
Laws of Hebrew Servitude: The Seventh-Year Release (vv.2-6)
Rights of Female Servants: Protection of the Vulnerable (vv.7-11)
Capital Offenses: Murder, Manslaughter & the Cities of Refuge (vv.12-14)
Capital Offenses: Kidnapping and Dishonoring Parents (vv.15-17)
Personal Injury: Assault, Liability & Restitution (vv.18-19)
The Law of the Servant: Striking and Death (vv.20-21)
Injury to a Pregnant Woman: The Unborn in God's Law (vv.22-25)
The Lex Talionis: Eye for Eye — Proportional Justice (vv.23-25)
Liberation of Injured Servants (vv.26-27)
The Goring Ox: Animal Liability and Negligence (vv.28-32)
Property Damage: Open Pits and Restitution (vv.33-36)

verses.kjv_text: If a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep.
verses.original_text: כִּי יִגְנֹב־אִישׁ שׁוֹר אוֹ־שֶׂה וּטְבָחוֹ אוֹ מְכָרוֹ חֲמִשָּׁה בָקָר יְשַׁלֵּם תַּחַת הַשּׁוֹר וְאַרְבַּע־צֹאן תַּחַת הַשֶּׂה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki yignov-ish shor o-seh utevaho o mekharo chamishah vakar yeshallem tachat hashor ve'arba-tzon tachat hasseh.
verses.study_note: The chapter opens with graduated restitution for livestock theft — the most valuable movable property in an agrarian society. An ox (shor) required fivefold restitution because it was a work animal whose theft deprived the owner of productive capacity (plowing, threshing). A sheep (seh) required fourfold because its economic value, while significant, was less than an ox. The higher penalty for the ox reflects the principle that restitution must account for consequential damages, not just the item's market value. David unknowingly pronounced this very penalty upon himself in Nathan's parable (2 Sam 12:6). Zacchaeus voluntarily applied fourfold restitution as evidence of genuine repentance (Luke 19:8). The verb 'yeshallem' (he shall restore) is from the root 'shalam' — to make whole, the same root as 'shalom.' True peace requires making things right.
verses.doctrinal_note: The graduated restitution principle reveals a theology of justice that is restorative rather than merely punitive. Unlike Mesopotamian codes (Hammurabi prescribed death for theft), God's law aims to restore wholeness. This principle undergirds the New Testament understanding of Christ's atonement: He does not merely cancel debt but makes superabundant restitution — restoring far more than was lost (Rom 5:15-17). The fivefold and fourfold pattern also shows that sin's consequences exceed the original act.

verses.kjv_text: If a thief be found breaking up, and be smitten that he die, there shall no blood be shed for him.
verses.original_text: אִם־בַּמַּחְתֶּרֶת יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב וְהֻכָּה וָמֵת אֵין לוֹ דָּמִים׃
verses.transliteration: Im-bammachteret yimmatze haggannav vehukah vamet ein lo damim.
verses.study_note: This verse addresses the right of self-defense during a nighttime burglary. 'Breaking up' (bammachteret) literally means 'in the act of tunneling' — digging through the mud-brick wall of a home (ancient Near Eastern houses had thick earthen walls). If the homeowner strikes and kills the thief in the darkness, there is no bloodguilt ('ein lo damim' — literally 'there is no blood for him'). The rationale is that in darkness, the homeowner cannot determine the intruder's intentions and must assume the worst — a threat to life, not just property. This establishes the principle that defense of life takes precedence, and the burden of risk falls on the criminal, not the victim.

verses.kjv_text: If the sun be risen upon him, there shall be blood shed for him; for he should make full restitution; if he have nothing, then he shall be sold for his theft.
verses.original_text: אִם־זָרְחָה הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ עָלָיו דָּמִים לוֹ שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם אִם־אֵין לוֹ וְנִמְכַּר בִּגְנֵבָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-zarechah hashemesh alav damim lo shallem yeshallem im-ein lo venimkar bignevato.
verses.study_note: The daylight changes everything. If the sun has risen and the homeowner can see that the intruder is a thief and not a murderer, killing him incurs bloodguilt. In daylight, the homeowner can call for help, identify the intruder, and use proportionate response. The thief must make full restitution (shallem yeshallem — emphatic infinitive absolute, stressing completeness). If he cannot pay, he is sold into servitude — not as punishment but as a mechanism for repayment. This connects directly to the servant laws of chapter 21. The law thus protects both the victim's property rights and the thief's right to life — a remarkable balance of justice and mercy.

verses.kjv_text: If the theft be certainly found in his hand alive, whether it be ox, or ass, or sheep; he shall restore double.
verses.original_text: אִם־הִמָּצֵא תִמָּצֵא בְיָדוֹ הַגְּנֵבָה מִשּׁוֹר עַד־חֲמוֹר עַד־שֶׂה חַיִּים שְׁנַיִם יְשַׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Im-himmatze timmatze veyado haggnevah mishshor ad-chamor ad-seh chayyim shenayim yeshallem.
verses.study_note: If the stolen animal is found alive and still in the thief's possession ('in his hand'), the penalty is only double restitution rather than four or fivefold. The reduced penalty reflects that the owner's loss was temporary and recoverable — the animal can be returned along with one additional as compensation. 'Certainly found' (himmatze timmatze) is another emphatic infinitive absolute — 'found, it is found' — suggesting evidence beyond doubt. This graduated system of penalties (double for recoverable theft, fourfold/fivefold for consumed or sold theft) demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of proportional justice.

verses.kjv_text: If a man shall cause a field or vineyard to be eaten, and shall put in his beast, and shall feed in another man’s field; of the best of his own field, and of the best of his own vineyard, shall he make restitution.
verses.original_text: כִּי יַבְעֶר־אִישׁ שָׂדֶה אוֹ־כֶרֶם וְשִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּעִירֹה וּבִעֵר בִּשְׂדֵה אַחֵר מֵיטַב שָׂדֵהוּ וּמֵיטַב כַּרְמוֹ יְשַׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki yav'er-ish sadeh o-kerem veshillach et-be'iro uvi'er bisdeh acher metav sadehu umetav karmo yeshallem.
verses.study_note: This addresses agricultural negligence — allowing livestock to graze in another's field or vineyard. The penalty is elegant: restitution must come from 'the best' (metav) of the offender's own field and vineyard. Not the average produce, but the finest. This principle of 'best-quality restitution' elevates justice beyond mere calculation to generosity. The verb 'yav'er' (cause to be eaten/consumed) has a wordplay connection to 'bi'er' (to burn/consume) in the next verse — grazing and fire both consume another's livelihood. Property boundaries in ancient Israel were sacred markers of each family's inheritance from God (Deut 19:14).

verses.kjv_text: If fire break out, and catch in thorns, so that the stacks of corn, or the standing corn, or the field, be consumed therewith; he that kindled the fire shall surely make restitution.
verses.original_text: כִּי־תֵצֵא אֵשׁ וּמָצְאָה קֹצִים וְנֶאֱכַל גָּדִישׁ אוֹ הַקָּמָה אוֹ הַשָּׂדֶה שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם הַמַּבְעִר אֶת־הַבְּעֵרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-tetze esh umatze'ah kotzim vene'ekhal gadish o hakkamah o hassadeh shallem yeshallem hammav'ir et-habba'erah.
verses.study_note: Fire damage law — if someone starts a fire (perhaps for clearing land) and it spreads through thorns (kotzim) to another's harvested stacks, standing grain, or field, the fire-starter bears full liability. 'Shall surely make restitution' (shallem yeshallem) — the emphatic form again. The law holds people responsible for the foreseeable consequences of their actions, not just their intentions. In the dry climate of the Near East, fire spread was a constant danger. Thorns served as natural field boundaries and were highly flammable. Samson later exploits this principle destructively (Judg 15:4-5). The theological principle: we are accountable for the consequences of what we set in motion.

verses.kjv_text: If a man shall deliver unto his neighbour money or stuff to keep, and it be stolen out of the man’s house; if the thief be found, let him pay double.
verses.original_text: כִּי־יִתֵּן אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ כֶּסֶף אוֹ־כֵלִים לִשְׁמֹר וְגֻנַּב מִבֵּית הָאִישׁ אִם־יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב יְשַׁלֵּם שְׁנָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-yitten ish el-re'ehu kesef o-khelim lishmor vegunnav mibbeit ha'ish im-yimmatze haggannav yeshallem shenayim.
verses.study_note: This begins the bailment laws — the law of entrusted property. When someone entrusts valuables (money or 'stuff' — khelim, meaning vessels, tools, goods) to a neighbor for safekeeping, and those goods are stolen, the thief (if found) pays double to the owner — not to the custodian. In a society without banks, vaults, or insurance, entrusting property to a trusted neighbor was the primary means of securing valuables. These laws thus regulate one of the most fundamental economic relationships in ancient life. The word 're'ehu' (his neighbor/friend) indicates that these transactions occurred within networks of trust and community relationship.
verses.doctrinal_note: The bailment laws reveal that community trust is a sacred obligation under God's covenant. Entrusting property to another creates a fiduciary relationship that God Himself oversees. When trust is violated, God is invoked as judge (v.8). This principle undergirds the New Testament concept of stewardship — we are custodians of what God entrusts to us, and we will give account (Matt 25:14-30; 1 Cor 4:2).

verses.kjv_text: If the thief be not found, then the master of the house shall be brought unto the judges, to see whether he have put his hand unto his neighbour’s goods.
verses.original_text: אִם־לֹא יִמָּצֵא הַגַּנָּב וְנִקְרַב בַּעַל־הַבַּיִת אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים אִם־לֹא שָׁלַח יָדוֹ בִּמְלֶאכֶת רֵעֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-lo yimmatze haggannav venikrav ba'al-habbayit el-ha'Elohim im-lo shalach yado bimle'khet re'ehu.
verses.study_note: If the thief is not found, suspicion may fall on the custodian himself. He must come 'unto the judges' — but the Hebrew is remarkable: 'el-ha'Elohim' — literally 'unto God' or 'unto the gods.' The KJV translates 'judges' because the judges acted as God's representatives in rendering verdicts. The custodian must swear a sacred oath before the divine tribunal that he did not 'put his hand' (shalach yado — a Hebrew idiom for embezzlement or misappropriation) upon his neighbor's property. This judicial oath invokes God as the all-seeing witness who knows what happened in secret. The phrase 'ba'al-habbayit' (master of the house) becomes 'baal habayit' — the householder, later used frequently in rabbinic literature and by Jesus Himself (Matt 10:25).
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'el-ha'Elohim' establishes a foundational principle: human courts derive their authority from God. Judges stand in God's place to adjudicate. When evidence is insufficient, the oath before God becomes the court of final appeal — because God sees what human eyes cannot. This is the basis of all biblical jurisprudence: the courtroom is sacred space, and perjury is not merely a civil offense but a sin against the divine Judge. Psalm 82:1 echoes this: 'God standeth in the congregation of the mighty; he judgeth among the gods [judges].'

verses.kjv_text: For all manner of trespass, whether it be for ox, for ass, for sheep, for raiment, or for any manner of lost thing, which another challengeth to be his, the cause of both parties shall come before the judges; and whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour.
verses.original_text: עַל־כָּל־דְּבַר־פֶּשַׁע עַל־שׁוֹר עַל־חֲמוֹר עַל־שֶׂה עַל־שַׂלְמָה עַל־כָּל־אֲבֵדָה אֲשֶׁר יֹאמַר כִּי־הוּא זֶה עַד הָאֱלֹהִים יָבֹא דְּבַר־שְׁנֵיהֶם אֲשֶׁר יַרְשִׁיעֻן אֱלֹהִים יְשַׁלֵּם שְׁנַיִם לְרֵעֵהוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Al-kol-devar-pesha al-shor al-chamor al-seh al-salmah al-kol-avedah asher yomar ki-hu zeh ad ha'Elohim yavo devar-sheneihem asher yarshi'un Elohim yeshallem shenayim lere'ehu.
verses.study_note: This is a comprehensive jurisdictional verse covering 'all manner of trespass' (kol-devar-pesha). The list covers the range of disputed property: livestock (ox, ass, sheep), clothing (salmah), and 'any manner of lost thing' (kol-avedah). The phrase 'which another challengeth to be his' (ki-hu zeh — 'for this is it') captures the moment of accusation: 'That's mine!' Both parties must present their case before 'the judges' (Elohim) — again, the divine tribunal. The one whom 'the judges shall condemn' (yarshi'un Elohim — 'whom God declares guilty') pays double. This establishes a formal adversarial legal process with divine oversight, protecting both accuser and accused.

verses.kjv_text: If a man deliver unto his neighbour an ass, or an ox, or a sheep, or any beast, to keep; and it die, or be hurt, or driven away, no man seeing it:
verses.original_text: כִּי־יִתֵּן אִישׁ אֶל־רֵעֵהוּ חֲמוֹר אוֹ־שׁוֹר אוֹ־שֶׂה וְכָל־בְּהֵמָה לִשְׁמֹר וּמֵת אוֹ־נִשְׁבַּר אוֹ־נִשְׁבָּה אֵין רֹאֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Ki-yitten ish el-re'ehu chamor o-shor o-seh vekhol-behemah lishmor umet o-nishbar o-nishbah ein ro'eh.
verses.study_note: This verse shifts to bailment of live animals — a more complex scenario than money or goods. Three possible misfortunes are listed: death (met), injury (nishbar — literally 'broken'), or being driven away (nishbah — carried off by raiders or predators). The critical phrase is 'no man seeing it' (ein ro'eh) — there are no witnesses. Without evidence, the case becomes one person's word against another's. The law must address this evidentiary gap, and the next verse provides the remarkable solution: the oath before YHWH.

verses.kjv_text: Then shall an oath of the LORD be between them both, that he hath not put his hand unto his neighbour’s goods; and the owner of it shall accept thereof, and he shall not make it good.
verses.original_text: שְׁבֻעַת יְהוָה תִּהְיֶה בֵּין שְׁנֵיהֶם אִם־לֹא שָׁלַח יָדוֹ בִּמְלֶאכֶת רֵעֵהוּ וְלָקַח בְּעָלָיו וְלֹא יְשַׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Shevu'at YHWH tihyeh bein sheneihem im-lo shalach yado bimle'khet re'ehu velakach be'alav velo yeshallem.
verses.study_note: The solution for cases without witnesses: 'an oath of the LORD' (shevu'at YHWH). This is one of the rare places where the divine name YHWH is explicitly invoked in legal procedure. The custodian swears by YHWH that he did not embezzle the property, and the owner must accept this oath — 'the owner of it shall accept thereof.' The oath settles the matter; no further restitution is required. This is extraordinary: the covenant Name of God becomes the guarantor of justice when human evidence fails. The oath invokes God's omniscience — He who sees in secret (Matt 6:4) becomes the witness. Hebrews 6:16 reflects on this principle: 'an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'oath of YHWH' reveals that God stands as the ultimate guarantor of justice in human affairs. When evidence fails and no witness exists, YHWH Himself fills the gap. This has profound implications: all human transactions ultimately occur before God's eyes. Every oath invokes His name as witness and judge. This sanctifies all economic life — there is no 'secular' sphere where God is absent. The swearing of false oaths thus becomes not merely perjury but blasphemy against the divine Name (Lev 19:12; Exod 20:7).

verses.kjv_text: And if it be stolen from him, he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof.
verses.original_text: וְאִם־גָּנֹב יִגָּנֵב מֵעִמּוֹ יְשַׁלֵּם לִבְעָלָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'im-ganov yigganev me'immo yeshallem liv'alav.
verses.study_note: If the entrusted animal is stolen (not killed by a wild beast or lost to accident), the custodian must make restitution. The distinction is crucial: theft implies a failure of watchfulness that the custodian could have prevented, whereas death by predator or accident (vv.10-11) is beyond human control. The emphatic 'ganov yigganev' (stolen, it is stolen) stresses that this is confirmed theft, not mere disappearance. The custodian accepted the duty to guard (lishmor) the animal and is liable for preventable losses. This establishes the legal principle of reasonable care — the custodian is not an insurer against all loss, but is liable for negligence.

verses.kjv_text: If it be torn in pieces, then let him bring it for witness, and he shall not make good that which was torn.
verses.original_text: אִם־טָרֹף יִטָּרֵף יְבִאֵהוּ עֵד הַטְּרֵפָה לֹא יְשַׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Im-tarof yittaref yevi'ehu ed hattrefah lo yeshallem.
verses.study_note: If the animal is killed by a predator ('torn in pieces' — tarof yittaref, another emphatic infinitive absolute), the custodian must bring evidence: 'let him bring it for witness' (yevi'ehu ed). He must produce the torn remains — a mangled carcass, bloody wool, bones — as physical proof of predator attack. With this evidence, he is not liable. This is the same principle Jacob's sons violated when they brought Joseph's blood-soaked coat to their father (Gen 37:31-33) — fabricating false evidence of an animal attack. Amos 3:12 describes a shepherd rescuing 'two legs, or a piece of an ear' from a lion — the remnant serves as proof. The word 'trefah' (torn/treif) later becomes the basis for Jewish dietary laws regarding non-kosher meat.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man borrow ought of his neighbour, and it be hurt, or die, the owner thereof being not with it, he shall surely make it good.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יִשְׁאַל אִישׁ מֵעִם רֵעֵהוּ וְנִשְׁבַּר אוֹ־מֵת בְּעָלָיו אֵין־עִמּוֹ שַׁלֵּם יְשַׁלֵּם׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yish'al ish me'im re'ehu venishbar o-met be'alav ein-immo shallem yeshallem.
verses.study_note: This shifts from bailment (safekeeping for another's benefit) to borrowing (use for one's own benefit). The borrower assumes greater liability because he benefits from using the animal. If the borrowed animal is injured or dies while the owner is absent, the borrower must make full restitution (shallem yeshallem). The key condition is 'the owner thereof being not with it' — the owner's absence shifts full responsibility to the borrower. The verb 'yish'al' (borrow/ask) is the same root as 'Saul' (sha'ul — 'asked for'), and becomes significant in the narrative of Israel requesting a king.

verses.kjv_text: But if the owner thereof be with it, he shall not make it good: if it be an hired thing, it came for his hire.
verses.original_text: אִם־בְּעָלָיו עִמּוֹ לֹא יְשַׁלֵּם אִם־שָׂכִיר הוּא בָּא בִּשְׂכָרוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Im-be'alav immo lo yeshallem im-sakhir hu ba viskharo.
verses.study_note: Two exceptions reduce the borrower's liability: (1) If the owner is present ('with it'), the borrower is not liable — the owner retains supervisory responsibility and presumably could have intervened. (2) If the animal was hired (sakhir — rented), the rental fee includes risk coverage — 'it came for his hire' means the hire price already accounts for normal wear, injury, and risk. This verse creates a sophisticated three-tier liability system: highest for borrowers (full restitution), moderate for hired goods (risk included in price), and lowest when the owner is present (owner bears own risk). This legal framework demonstrates remarkable economic sophistication in the Mosaic code.

verses.kjv_text: And if a man entice a maid that is not betrothed, and lie with her, he shall surely endow her to be his wife.
verses.original_text: וְכִי־יְפַתֶּה אִישׁ בְּתוּלָה אֲשֶׁר לֹא־אֹרָשָׂה וְשָׁכַב עִמָּהּ מָהֹר יִמְהָרֶנָּה לּוֹ לְאִשָּׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhi-yefatteh ish betulah asher lo-orasah veshakhav immah mahor yimharennah lo le'ishah.
verses.study_note: The law shifts from property to persons — specifically the protection of unbetrothed women. 'Entice' (yefatteh) means to persuade, seduce — not forcible rape (which is addressed in Deut 22:25-27 with the death penalty). The man who seduces an unbetrothed virgin must 'surely endow her' (mahor yimharennah) — pay the bride-price (mohar) and marry her. The emphatic form stresses the obligation is non-negotiable. This law protects the woman from social and economic ruin: in the ancient world, loss of virginity without marriage severely diminished a woman's prospects. The man cannot enjoy the benefits of sexual intimacy without accepting the full responsibilities of marriage and provision.
verses.doctrinal_note: This law establishes the principle that sexual intimacy creates binding obligation. The man cannot separate physical union from covenantal commitment. This principle is carried forward in Paul's theology of sexual ethics (1 Cor 6:15-20): 'he which is joined to an harlot is one body.' God's design links sexual union to marital covenant — separating them violates the created order and injures the vulnerable party.

verses.kjv_text: If her father utterly refuse to give her unto him, he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins.
verses.original_text: אִם־מָאֵן יְמָאֵן אָבִיהָ לְתִתָּהּ לוֹ כֶּסֶף יִשְׁקֹל כְּמֹהַר הַבְּתוּלֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Im-ma'en yema'en aviha letittah lo kesef yishkol kemohar habetulot.
verses.study_note: The father retains authority to refuse the marriage (ma'en yema'en — 'utterly refuse,' emphatic). Even so, the seducer must still pay the full bride-price (mohar habetulot — 'the dowry of virgins'). This means the woman is compensated regardless of whether the marriage occurs. The father's veto protects the daughter from being locked into a marriage with a man who has already demonstrated dishonorable character. Later tradition set the mohar at fifty shekels of silver (Deut 22:29). The verb 'yishkol' (shall weigh out) reflects the ancient practice of weighing silver rather than counting coins — pre-coinage economy.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live.
verses.original_text: מְכַשֵּׁפָה לֹא תְחַיֶּה׃
verses.transliteration: Mekhashefah lo techayeh.
verses.study_note: One of the shortest and most absolute commands in the Torah. 'Mekhashefah' is a feminine participle — a woman who practices sorcery (keshef). The masculine form 'mekhashef' would apply equally to men (cf. Deut 18:10-12 which condemns all practitioners regardless of gender). Sorcery in the ancient Near East was not harmless folk magic but the invocation of spiritual powers outside YHWH — an attempt to manipulate the supernatural realm through incantation, divination, and necromancy. It represented a fundamental rejection of YHWH's sovereignty and an alliance with rival spiritual powers. The severity of the penalty reflects the severity of the offense: it strikes at the first commandment — 'thou shalt have no other gods before me.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The prohibition of sorcery is rooted in the exclusivity of YHWH's sovereignty. All attempts to access spiritual power outside of God — whether through divination, necromancy, or occult practice — constitute covenant treason. The spiritual powers behind sorcery are real but hostile to God (Deut 32:17; 1 Cor 10:20). Paul lists 'witchcraft' (pharmakeia) among the works of the flesh (Gal 5:20), and Revelation places sorcerers outside the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:8; 22:15). The prohibition endures because the spiritual reality it addresses endures.

verses.kjv_text: Whosoever lieth with a beast shall surely be put to death.
verses.original_text: כָּל־שֹׁכֵב עִם־בְּהֵמָה מוֹת יוּמָת׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-shokhev im-behemah mot yumat.
verses.study_note: Another absolute, apodictic command with the emphatic death formula: 'mot yumat' (dying, he shall die). Bestiality violated the created order of Genesis 1-2 where God established distinct kinds and designated human sexuality within the marriage covenant. In the surrounding Canaanite, Hittite, and Egyptian cultures, ritual bestiality was sometimes practiced in fertility cult worship. Leviticus 18:23 calls it 'tevel' — confusion/perversion — a mixing of what God has separated. Leviticus 20:15-16 extends the penalty to include the destruction of the animal, indicating the depth of the defilement. These laws set Israel apart from the sexual practices of the nations they would encounter in Canaan.

verses.kjv_text: He that sacrificeth unto any god, save unto the LORD only, he shall be utterly destroyed.
verses.original_text: זֹבֵחַ לָאֱלֹהִים יָחֳרָם בִּלְתִּי לַיהוָה לְבַדּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Zoveach la'Elohim yochoram bilti laYHWH levaddo.
verses.study_note: The climax of the apodictic capital offenses: sacrifice to any god other than YHWH alone. The verb 'yochoram' (shall be utterly destroyed) is from 'charam' — the ban of total destruction, the same word used for the devotion of Jericho to destruction (Josh 6:17). This is the strongest possible penalty — not merely death but total annihilation as something accursed. 'Bilti laYHWH levaddo' — 'except to the LORD only' — the word 'levaddo' (only, alone) is emphatic. YHWH's exclusive claim on Israel's worship is absolute. This law stands as the legal enforcement of the first commandment and the theological center of Israel's covenant identity. Idolatry is not merely wrong worship — it is high treason against the covenant King.
verses.doctrinal_note: The 'cherem' (ban/devotion to destruction) applied to idolatry reveals that false worship is the most destructive force in covenant life. Every other sin in this chapter has a remedy — restitution, compensation, marriage obligation. But idolatry admits no remedy because it strikes at the very foundation of the covenant relationship. Just as adultery destroys a marriage at its core, idolatry destroys the divine-human covenant. This explains why Scripture consistently uses adultery as a metaphor for idolatry (Hosea 1-3; Ezek 16; James 4:4) and why the first commandment must be the first commandment.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt neither vex a stranger, nor oppress him: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt.
verses.original_text: וְגֵר לֹא־תוֹנֶה וְלֹא תִלְחָצֶנּוּ כִּי־גֵרִים הֱיִיתֶם בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Veger lo-toneh velo tilchatzenu ki-gerim heyitem be'eretz Mitzrayim.
verses.study_note: A dramatic shift in tone marks the most theologically significant section of the chapter. Two prohibitions protect the 'ger' (stranger/sojourner/resident alien): 'lo-toneh' (do not wrong/vex) and 'lo tilchatzenu' (do not oppress/crush). 'Toneh' refers to verbal and economic exploitation; 'lachatz' means to press, squeeze, oppress — the same word used for Egypt's oppression of Israel (Exod 3:9). The grounding is revolutionary: 'for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt.' Israel's ethical obligation arises from their own experience of suffering. Having known the pain of being aliens and oppressed, they must never inflict that pain on others. This appeal to empathy through experience is unique in ancient law — no other Near Eastern code grounds ethics in communal memory of suffering.
verses.doctrinal_note: The stranger-protection law is grounded in Israel's exodus experience — God's redemptive act creates ethical obligation. 'Because I delivered you, you must not oppress.' This principle transforms historical memory into moral imperative. Jesus extends this radically in the parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:25-37) and in His identification with the stranger: 'I was a stranger, and ye took me in' (Matt 25:35). The early church understood that all believers are 'strangers and pilgrims' (1 Pet 2:11), making hospitality to outsiders a core Christian virtue.

verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child.
verses.original_text: כָּל־אַלְמָנָה וְיָתוֹם לֹא תְעַנּוּן׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-almanah veyatom lo te'annun.
verses.study_note: The protection extends to widows and orphans — the most vulnerable members of ancient society. 'Kol' (every/any) is emphatic: not a single widow or orphan may be afflicted. The verb 'te'annun' (afflict) means to humble, put down, cause to suffer — the same root ('anah') used for Israel's affliction in Egypt (Gen 15:13; Exod 1:11-12). The triad of stranger, widow, and orphan becomes the standard biblical measure of a society's justice (Deut 24:17-22; Isa 1:17; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10; James 1:27). In a patriarchal society, women without husbands and children without fathers had no legal advocate, no inheritance rights, and no economic security. God places Himself in that role.
verses.doctrinal_note: God's protection of widows and orphans reveals His character as 'Father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows' (Ps 68:5). James 1:27 defines 'pure religion' as visiting 'the fatherless and widows in their affliction.' This is not peripheral to faith but its very definition. When Israel exploited the vulnerable, the prophets declared their worship invalid (Isa 1:11-17; Amos 5:21-24). Social justice and genuine worship are inseparable in biblical theology.
