And again: 'one measure' (middah achat) — every curtain identical. Thirty is the age of priestly maturity (Num 4:3) and the age at which Jesus began His ministry (Luke 3:23). The protective outer layer is larger than the precious inner layer — grace covers more than glory demands.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt couple five curtains by themselves, and six curtains by themselves, and shalt double the sixth curtain in the forefront of the tabernacle.
verses.original_text: וְחִבַּרְתָּ אֶת־חֲמֵשׁ הַיְרִיעֹת לְבָד וְאֶת־שֵׁשׁ הַיְרִיעֹת לְבָד וְכָפַלְתָּ אֶת־הַיְרִיעָה הַשִּׁשִּׁית אֶל־מוּל פְּנֵי הָאֹהֶל׃
verses.transliteration: Vechibbarta et-chamesh hayeri'ot levad ve'et-shesh hayeri'ot levad vekhafalta et-hayeri'ah hashshishit el-mul penei ha'ohel.
verses.study_note: Unlike the linen curtains (divided 5+5), the goats' hair curtains are divided 5+6 — an asymmetric arrangement. The sixth curtain of the front set is 'doubled' (vekhafalta) at the entrance, creating a reinforced, doubled layer at the Tabernacle's front face. This practical feature protects the most exposed area. The doubling at the 'forefront' (penei — literally 'face') means the Tabernacle presents a doubled, strengthened facade. The asymmetry (5+6 versus 5+5) means the eleventh curtain provides extra coverage at the rear, ensuring no gap in protection. Every structural detail serves both function and symbolism: God's dwelling is fully covered, fully protected, with extra reinforcement where exposure is greatest.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make fifty loops on the edge of the one curtain that is outmost in the coupling, and fifty loops in the edge of the curtain which coupleth the second.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ חֲמִשִּׁים לֻלָאֹת עַל שְׂפַת הַיְרִיעָה הָאֶחָת הַקִּיצֹנָה בַּחֹבָרֶת וַחֲמִשִּׁים לֻלָאֹת עַל שְׂפַת הַיְרִיעָה הַחֹבֶרֶת הַשֵּׁנִית׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita chamishim lula'ot al sefat hayeri'ah ha'achat hakkitzonah bacchovaret vachamishim lula'ot al sefat hayeri'ah hachoveret hashenit.
verses.study_note: The goats' hair curtains follow the same joining pattern as the linen curtains: fifty loops on each joining edge, one hundred total. The parallel construction between the inner and outer layers reinforces their unity of design. What differs is the material of the clasps (v.11) — bronze rather than gold. The repetition of fifty loops mirrors the inner layer exactly, indicating that the outer tent has the same divine precision as the inner curtains, even though it is made of coarser material. In God's economy, the protective and the beautiful receive equal attention to detail.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make fifty taches of brass, and put the taches into the loops, and couple the tent together, that it may be one.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ קַרְסֵי נְחֹשֶׁת חֲמִשִּׁים וְהֵבֵאתָ אֶת־הַקְּרָסִים בַּלֻּלָאֹת וְחִבַּרְתָּ אֶת־הָאֹהֶל וְהָיָה אֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita qarsei nechoshet chamishim veheveta et-haqqrasim ballula'ot vechibbarta et-ha'ohel vehayah echad.
verses.study_note: The outer tent's clasps are bronze (nechoshet), not gold. This is the first instance of a crucial Tabernacle principle: materials decrease in value from inside to outside. The innermost layer uses gold clasps; the outer layer uses bronze. This gradient — gold (nearest God), silver (redemption/structure), bronze (judgment/outer courts) — runs throughout the entire Tabernacle design. Bronze is associated with judgment and endurance (the bronze altar of burnt offering, the bronze serpent). Yet the result is the same: 'vehayah echad' — 'it shall be one.' Whether united by gold or bronze, the goal is always oneness. The tent (ohel) achieves the same unity as the tabernacle (mishkan).

verses.kjv_text: And the remnant that remaineth of the curtains of the tent, the half curtain that remaineth, shall hang over the backside of the tabernacle.
verses.original_text: וְסֶרַח הָעֹדֵף בִּירִיעֹת הָאֹהֶל חֲצִי הַיְרִיעָה הָעֹדֶפֶת תִּסְרַח עַל אֲחֹרֵי הַמִּשְׁכָּן׃
verses.transliteration: Veserach ha'odef biyri'ot ha'ohel chatzi hayeri'ah ha'odefet tisrach al achorei hammishkan.
verses.study_note: The excess (serach — overhang, surplus) of the goats' hair curtains hangs over the back (achorei — 'behind') of the tabernacle. Since the outer curtains are both longer and more numerous (11 vs. 10), they extend beyond the inner curtains on all sides. The half-curtain surplus at the rear provides extra protection at the back wall. The word 'serach' (remnant/overhang) suggests something that exceeds the boundary — the outer tent is deliberately oversized to ensure complete coverage. Grace always exceeds the minimum requirement.

verses.kjv_text: And a cubit on the one side, and a cubit on the other side of that which remaineth in the length of the curtains of the tent, it shall hang over the sides of the tabernacle on this side and on that side, to cover it.
verses.original_text: וְהָאַמָּה מִזֶּה וְהָאַמָּה מִזֶּה בָּעֹדֵף בְּאֹרֶךְ יְרִיעֹת הָאֹהֶל יִהְיֶה סָרוּחַ עַל־צִדֵּי הַמִּשְׁכָּן מִזֶּה וּמִזֶּה לְכַסֹּתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'ammah mizzeh veha'ammah mizzeh ba'odef be'orekh yeri'ot ha'ohel yihyeh saruach al-tziddei hammishkan mizzeh umizzeh lekhassoto.
verses.study_note: The extra cubit on each side — one cubit (about 18 inches) hanging down past the inner curtains on the north and south — ensures the linen curtains are never exposed to the elements. The purpose is explicit: 'to cover it' (lekhassoto) — from 'kasah,' to cover, conceal, protect. The inner beauty of the linen curtains is completely hidden beneath the goats' hair covering. This covering (kasah) is the same verb used for the atonement concept: God 'covers' sin. The outer, coarser layer protects and conceals the inner glory — just as Christ's humble human appearance concealed His divine nature, and just as the atonement covers the sinner's guilt.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make a covering for the tent of rams’ skins dyed red, and a covering above of badgers’ skins.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ מִכְסֶה לָאֹהֶל עֹרֹת אֵילִם מְאָדָּמִים וּמִכְסֵה עֹרֹת תְּחָשִׁים מִלְמָעְלָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita mikhseh la'ohel orot eilim me'oddamim umikhseh orot techashim milma'lah.
verses.study_note: Two additional outer coverings: rams' skins dyed red (orot eilim me'oddamim) and 'badgers' skins' (orot techashim) on top. The rams' skins are specifically dyed red (me'oddamim — from 'adom,' red, the same root as 'adam' — man, and 'dam' — blood). Rams were the quintessential substitutionary sacrifice animal — Abraham found a ram caught in a thicket as Isaac's substitute (Gen 22:13), and the ram of consecration was central to priestly ordination (Exod 29:15-22). The red dye intensifies the sacrificial symbolism: blood-colored skins covering God's dwelling. The 'techashim' (traditionally 'badgers') are debated — possibly dugong (sea cow), porpoise, or fine leather. Whatever the identification, these outermost skins were durable, weather-resistant, and plain. The Tabernacle's exterior was deliberately unattractive — rough animal skins giving no hint of the gold, blue, purple, and scarlet glory within.
verses.doctrinal_note: The four layers of the Tabernacle covering present a complete typology of Christ viewed from different perspectives. From outside (the world's view): plain, unattractive skins — 'no beauty that we should desire him' (Isa 53:2). The rams' skins dyed red: substitutionary sacrifice, blood atonement — 'the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). The goats' hair: the sin offering (the goat was the primary sin offering animal) — 'he hath made him to be sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21). The inner linen with blue, purple, scarlet and gold: the full divine glory of Christ — 'we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father' (John 1:14). One must enter in to see the glory.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make boards for the tabernacle of shittim wood standing up.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַקְּרָשִׁים לַמִּשְׁכָּן עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים עֹמְדִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita et-haqqerashim lammishkan atzei shittim omedim.
verses.study_note: The structural framework begins: boards (qerashim — frames or planks) of acacia wood (atzei shittim) standing upright (omedim). Acacia (shittah/shittim) is the only timber that grows abundantly in the Sinai wilderness — a thorny, hard, virtually indestructible desert wood. Its Hebrew name 'shittim' is related to 'shoteh' (folly/deviation), giving an interpretive tradition that the acacia represents humanity in its fallen, 'thorny' condition, which God takes and overlays with gold (v.29) — making the worthless precious. The boards stand (omedim — 'standing') — they are upright, firmly established. The word 'omedim' carries connotations of service and readiness ('standing before the LORD' is a phrase for priestly service).
verses.doctrinal_note: Acacia wood overlaid with gold is one of the most theologically rich material combinations in the Tabernacle. Wood represents humanity (organic, from the earth, perishable); gold represents deity (incorruptible, glorious, precious). Wood covered in gold pictures the union of human and divine natures — the central mystery of the Incarnation. Christ is the 'branch' (netzer) of David — human wood from human roots — yet clothed in divine glory. The boards 'standing upright' prefigure believers who 'stand' in grace (Rom 5:2) and are 'pillars in the temple of my God' (Rev 3:12).

verses.kjv_text: Ten cubits shall be the length of a board, and a cubit and a half shall be the breadth of one board.
verses.original_text: עֶשֶׂר אַמּוֹת אֹרֶךְ הַקָּרֶשׁ וְאַמָּה וַחֲצִי הָאַמָּה רֹחַב הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Eser ammot orekh haqqaresh ve'ammah vachatzi ha'ammah rochav haqqeresh ha'echad.
verses.study_note: Each board is ten cubits tall (approximately 15 feet / 4.5 meters) and a cubit and a half wide (about 27 inches / 68 cm). Ten cubits establishes the Tabernacle's interior height — the same as the width of the Most Holy Place, creating a perfect cube for the inner sanctum (ten cubits on each side). The cubit and a half (1.5 cubits) means twenty boards side by side on the south and north walls create a thirty-cubit length — the full length of the Tabernacle. Every dimension interlocks. The measurements are not arbitrary but produce precise proportions: the Holy Place is a double square (20 × 10 cubits) and the Most Holy Place is a perfect cube (10 × 10 × 10) — the same proportional pattern later magnified in Solomon's Temple and perfected in the heavenly city (Rev 21:16).

verses.kjv_text: Two tenons shall there be in one board, set in order one against another: thus shalt thou make for all the boards of the tabernacle.
verses.original_text: שְׁתֵּי יָדוֹת לַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד מְשֻׁלָּבֹת אִשָּׁה אֶל־אֲחֹתָהּ כֵּן תַּעֲשֶׂה לְכֹל קַרְשֵׁי הַמִּשְׁכָּן׃
verses.transliteration: Shtei yadot laqqeresh ha'echad meshullavot ishah el-achotah ken ta'aseh lekhol qarshei hammishkan.
verses.study_note: Each board has two tenons (yadot — literally 'hands') that fit into the silver sockets below. The tenons are 'set in order one against another' — again the sisterly language: 'ishah el-achotah' (a woman to her sister). The 'hands' (yadot) of each board reach down into the silver foundation, locked in place. The image is powerful: each board stands by virtue of being held in silver (redemption, v.19). Without the silver sockets, the boards cannot stand. The uniformity — 'thus shalt thou make for all the boards' — means every component shares the same design, the same connection method, the same foundation. There are no exceptional boards, no special cases. Unity of design ensures structural integrity.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make the boards for the tabernacle, twenty boards on the south side southward.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַקְּרָשִׁים לַמִּשְׁכָּן עֶשְׂרִים קֶרֶשׁ לִפְאַת נֶגְבָּה תֵימָנָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita et-haqqerashim lammishkan esrim qeresh lif'at negbah teimanah.
verses.study_note: Twenty boards form the south wall (negbah teimanah — 'the south side southward'). The Tabernacle was always oriented east-west, with the entrance facing east — toward the sunrise, toward the coming of light. The south side (negev/teman) receives the most sunlight. Twenty boards at 1.5 cubits each yields exactly 30 cubits for the Tabernacle's length. This precision demonstrates that every board is accounted for — none can be added or removed without altering the whole structure. Twenty is 2 × 10 — a doubled completeness.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make forty sockets of silver under the twenty boards; two sockets under one board for his two tenons, and two sockets under another board for his two tenons.
verses.original_text: וְאַרְבָּעִים אַדְנֵי־כֶסֶף תַּעֲשֶׂה תַּחַת עֶשְׂרִים הַקָּרֶשׁ שְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד לִשְׁתֵּי יְדֹתָיו וּשְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד לִשְׁתֵּי יְדֹתָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'arba'im adnei-khesef ta'aseh tachat esrim haqqaresh shenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad lishtei yedotav ushenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad lishtei yedotav.
verses.study_note: Forty silver sockets (adnei-khesef) form the foundation of the south wall — two under each of twenty boards. The word for 'socket' is 'eden' (adon/aden) — from the same root as 'adon' (lord/master) and possibly related to 'Eden' (foundation/delight). The foundation of God's dwelling is silver. In Exodus 30:11-16, each Israelite male paid a half-shekel of silver as 'atonement money' (kopher) — and this silver was specifically designated for casting the sockets (Exod 38:25-27). Each socket weighed one talent (about 75 pounds / 34 kg). The total: 100 sockets for the Tabernacle (96 for boards + 4 for the veil posts) required 100 talents of silver — every shekel from the atonement census. The entire Tabernacle literally stands on atonement money. Every Israelite's contribution supports God's dwelling.
verses.doctrinal_note: The silver sockets are one of the most profound types in the Tabernacle. Silver in Scripture represents redemption/atonement — Joseph was sold for silver (Gen 37:28), the atonement money was silver (Exod 30:12-16), and Christ was betrayed for silver (Matt 26:15). The Tabernacle's foundation is literally made from the ransom price of God's people. This teaches that God's dwelling among His people rests on the foundation of redemption. Without atonement, there is no habitation of God. The church is 'built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets' (Eph 2:20), and that foundation is the gospel of redemption through Christ's blood.

verses.kjv_text: And for the second side of the tabernacle on the north side there shall be twenty boards:
verses.original_text: וּלְצֶלַע הַמִּשְׁכָּן הַשֵּׁנִית לִפְאַת צָפוֹן עֶשְׂרִים קָרֶשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Uletzela hammishkan hashenit lif'at tzafon esrim qaresh.
verses.study_note: The north side mirrors the south exactly — twenty boards. The word for 'side' is 'tzela' — the same word used for the 'rib' taken from Adam to form Eve (Gen 2:21-22). The Tabernacle has two 'ribs' — north and south — like a body. The north (tzafon — from 'tzafan,' to hide/treasure up) was associated with darkness and mystery in ancient Near Eastern cosmology. The symmetry of the Tabernacle — identical north and south walls — expresses balance, order, and divine harmony. God's dwelling is perfectly proportioned, with no side favored over another.

verses.kjv_text: And their forty sockets of silver; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board.
verses.original_text: וְאַרְבָּעִים אַדְנֵיהֶם כָּסֶף שְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד וּשְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'arba'im adneihem kasef shenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad ushenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad.
verses.study_note: Identical to the south wall: forty silver sockets, two per board. The total for north and south walls alone is eighty sockets — eighty talents of silver. The repetitive, methodical listing reinforces the point: every board, every socket, every measurement on the north side exactly matches the south. God's dwelling is symmetrical — not chaotic, not lopsided, not improvised. The repetition itself is instructive: biblical architecture teaches through pattern and correspondence. What is true on one side is equally true on the other. This structural integrity reflects the character of the God who dwells within — consistent, orderly, dependable.

verses.kjv_text: And for the sides of the tabernacle westward thou shalt make six boards.
verses.original_text: וּלְיַרְכְּתֵי הַמִּשְׁכָּן יָמָּה תַּעֲשֶׂה שִׁשָּׁה קְרָשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Uleyarketei hammishkan yammah ta'aseh shishah qerashim.
verses.study_note: The western wall (yammah — 'seaward,' since the Mediterranean lies to the west of Israel) — the back wall, farthest from the entrance, closest to the Most Holy Place — receives six boards. Six is the number of man (created on the sixth day) and of incompleteness (one short of seven). The western wall is where the Ark of the Covenant sits behind the veil. The word 'yarketei' (sides/rear) carries connotations of the 'innermost parts' — the deepest recesses. This is the most sacred wall of the most sacred structure, and yet it uses the fewest boards — six plus two corner boards (v.23). God's most intimate dwelling requires the least human material.

verses.kjv_text: And two boards shalt thou make for the corners of the tabernacle in the two sides.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁנֵי קְרָשִׁים תַּעֲשֶׂה לִמְקֻצְעֹת הַמִּשְׁכָּן בַּיַּרְכָתָיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ushenei qerashim ta'aseh limkutzo't hammishkan bayyarkatayim.
verses.study_note: Two additional corner boards (limkutzo't — 'for the corners') complete the western wall. The corners are the most structurally critical points — where walls meet and forces converge. The corner boards must handle stress from two directions simultaneously. Corners in biblical imagery represent strength and stability: 'the chief corner stone' (Ps 118:22; Isa 28:16; Eph 2:20) is the most important stone in a building. These two corner boards bind the north-south walls to the western wall, creating structural integrity. The total western wall is thus eight boards (6 + 2 corners) with sixteen silver sockets.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall be coupled together beneath, and they shall be coupled together above the head of it unto one ring: thus shall it be for them both; they shall be for the two corners.
verses.original_text: וְיִהְיוּ תוֹאֲמִם מִלְּמַטָּה וְיַחְדָּו יִהְיוּ תַמִּים עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ אֶל־הַטַּבַּעַת הָאֶחָת כֵּן יִהְיֶה לִשְׁנֵיהֶם לִשְׁנֵי הַמִּקְצֹעֹת יִהְיוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Veyihyu to'amim millemattah veyachdav yihyu tammim al-rosho el-hattaba'at ha'achat ken yihyeh lishneihem lishnei hammiktzoot yihyu.
verses.study_note: The corner boards are joined at both bottom and top — 'coupled together beneath' and 'coupled together above the head of it unto one ring.' The word 'to'amim' (coupled/twinned) is from 'ta'am' — twins, like Jacob and Esau. They are twinned below and unified (tammim — complete, whole) above into a single ring at the top. This creates a structural bond that transfers load from the roof through the walls into the silver foundations. The ring at the top (taba'at) becomes the point of convergence where two walls become one corner. The engineering is sophisticated; the theology more so: what is separated below converges into unity above.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall be eight boards, and their sockets of silver, sixteen sockets; two sockets under one board, and two sockets under another board.
verses.original_text: וְהָיוּ שְׁמֹנָה קְרָשִׁים וְאַדְנֵיהֶם כֶּסֶף שִׁשָּׁה עָשָׂר אֲדָנִים שְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד וּשְׁנֵי אֲדָנִים תַּחַת הַקֶּרֶשׁ הָאֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayu shemonah qerashim ve'adneihem kesef shishah asar adanim shenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad ushenei adanim tachat haqqeresh ha'echad.
verses.study_note: Summary of the western wall: eight boards, sixteen silver sockets. The total for the entire Tabernacle structure is now calculable: twenty boards south (40 sockets) + twenty boards north (40 sockets) + eight boards west (16 sockets) = forty-eight boards standing in ninety-six silver sockets. The four veil pillars add four more sockets (v.32), bringing the total to one hundred silver sockets — exactly one hundred talents of silver from the atonement census (Exod 38:27). Eight is the number of new beginning (circumcision on the eighth day, resurrection on the first day of the new week). The western wall, closest to God's presence, stands on the number of new creation.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make bars of shittim wood; five for the boards of the one side of the tabernacle,
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ בְרִיחִם עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים חֲמִשָּׁה לְקַרְשֵׁי צֶלַע־הַמִּשְׁכָּן הָאֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita verichim atzei shittim chamishah leqarshei tzela-hammishkan ha'echad.
verses.study_note: Horizontal crossbars (verichim — bars, bolts) of acacia wood bind the standing boards together laterally. Five bars per side — an arrangement that prevents the individual boards from separating under stress. The bars run through gold rings attached to the boards (v.29), threading through them like a spine. The word 'beriach' (bar/bolt) is related to 'barach' (to flee) — a bar that 'flees' or runs from one end to the other. Without these crossbars, the boards would be individual, disconnected panels; with them, the boards become a wall — individual members united into a single, rigid structure. Five bars per side maintain the 'grace' number (five offerings, five books of Moses).

verses.kjv_text: And five bars for the boards of the other side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the boards of the side of the tabernacle, for the two sides westward.
verses.original_text: וַחֲמִשָּׁה בְרִיחִם לְקַרְשֵׁי צֶלַע הַמִּשְׁכָּן הַשֵּׁנִית וַחֲמִשָּׁה בְרִיחִם לְקַרְשֵׁי צֶלַע הַמִּשְׁכָּן לַיַּרְכָתַיִם יָמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Vachamishah verichim leqarshei tzela hammishkan hashenit vachamishah verichim leqarshei tzela hammishkan layyarkatayim yammah.
verses.study_note: Five bars for the north side and five bars for the western wall — fifteen crossbars total (5 north + 5 south + 5 west). The entrance (east) has no bars because it has no boards — only the curtain/screen (v.36). The crossbars unite three walls into a U-shaped structure open at the east. The eastern opening faces the sunrise and the approaching worshiper — God's dwelling is open in one direction: toward those who come. It is closed and fortified on three sides (protection) but open at the front (invitation). This architectural fact embodies the dual nature of God's holiness: He is unapproachable in His majesty yet inviting to those who approach through the prescribed way.

verses.kjv_text: And the middle bar in the midst of the boards shall reach from end to end.
verses.original_text: וְהַבְּרִיחַ הַתִּיכֹן בְּתוֹךְ הַקְּרָשִׁים מַבְרִחַ מִן־הַקָּצֶה אֶל־הַקָּצֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehaberiach hattikhon betokh haqqerashim mavriach min-haqqatzeh el-haqqatzeh.
verses.study_note: The middle bar — singular and special — runs the entire length of the wall from end to end (min-haqqatzeh el-haqqatzeh). While the other four bars are arranged two above and two below (each covering half the wall length), the middle bar spans the full distance. This central bar passes 'in the midst of the boards' (betokh haqqerashim) — not on the surface but through the center, hidden within the structure. It is invisible from both inside and outside the Tabernacle, yet it is the primary structural element holding everything together. Jewish tradition (Midrash Tanchuma, Terumah 11) states this bar was made from the tree Abraham planted at Beer-sheba (Gen 21:33) — continuity from the patriarchs to the Tabernacle.
verses.doctrinal_note: The hidden middle bar that runs from end to end is a powerful type of Christ's unifying presence within His church. He is the invisible cohesive force that holds all the members together — 'in him all things consist [hold together]' (Col 1:17). The bar is in the 'midst' (betokh) — the same word for God walking 'in the midst' of the camp (Deut 23:14) and Christ being 'in the midst' of the seven churches (Rev 1:13; 2:1). What holds the dwelling together is not visible from outside; it is the hidden, internal presence of God Himself.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt overlay the boards with gold, and make their rings of gold for places for the bars: and thou shalt overlay the bars with gold.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הַקְּרָשִׁים תְּצַפֶּה זָהָב וְאֶת־טַבְּעֹתֵיהֶם תַּעֲשֶׂה זָהָב בָּתִּים לַבְּרִיחִם וְצִפִּיתָ אֶת־הַבְּרִיחִם זָהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-haqqerashim tetzappeh zahav ve'et-tabb'oteihem ta'aseh zahav battim labberichim vetzippita et-habberichim zahav.
verses.study_note: Everything is overlaid with gold (tetzappeh zahav) — the boards, the rings (tabb'ot), and the bars. The verb 'tzafah' (to overlay/plate) means to cover completely — every surface of acacia wood disappears under gold. From inside the Tabernacle, no wood is visible — only gold. From this interior perspective, the walls are solid gold reflecting lamplight from the menorah in a warm, shimmering glow. The gold rings (tabb'ot) serve as channels through which the bars pass, creating the union between horizontal and vertical elements. The word 'battim' (places/houses) for the bar channels means literally 'houses' — even the fastening points are 'dwelling places.' In God's house, even the joints have homes.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt rear up the tabernacle according to the fashion thereof which was shewed thee in the mount.
verses.original_text: וַהֲקֵמֹתָ אֶת־הַמִּשְׁכָּן כְּמִשְׁפָּטוֹ אֲשֶׁר הָרְאֵיתָ בָּהָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vahaqemota et-hammishkan kemishpato asher hor'eita bahar.
verses.study_note: A pivotal command: build it 'according to the fashion thereof which was shewed thee in the mount' (kemishpato asher hor'eita bahar). The word 'mishpato' means its 'judgment/ordinance/pattern' — the Tabernacle has a divinely ordained design that Moses saw on Mount Sinai (cf. Exod 25:9,40). This is not human architecture but a heavenly pattern. Hebrews 8:5 quotes this directly: the earthly tabernacle was made 'according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount.' The implication is staggering: there is a heavenly reality of which the earthly Tabernacle is a copy and shadow. Moses did not invent the design; he received it by revelation. The earthly structure corresponds to a heavenly original — the true tabernacle 'which the Lord pitched, and not man' (Heb 8:2).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make a vail of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen of cunning work: with cherubims shall it be made:
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ פָרֹכֶת תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב יַעֲשֶׂה אֹתָהּ כְּרֻבִים׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita farokhet tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh moshzar ma'aseh choshev ya'aseh otah keruvim.
verses.study_note: The theological centerpiece of the chapter — the veil (parokhet). Made of the same four materials as the inner curtains (blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen) with cherubim woven in by skilled artisans (ma'aseh choshev). The 'parokhet' separated the Holy Place (where priests ministered daily) from the Most Holy Place (where only the high priest entered once a year on Yom Kippur). The word 'parokhet' is related to 'perek' (separation, breaking) — it is the barrier that divides. The cherubim on the veil correspond exactly to the cherubim on the Ark's mercy seat behind it — angelic guardians on both sides of the boundary between God and man. This is the veil that was 'rent in twain from the top to the bottom' at Christ's crucifixion (Matt 27:51) — torn from heaven downward, not from earth upward. God tore the veil; man could not.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hebrews 10:20 identifies the veil explicitly as 'his flesh' — the body of Christ. The four colors represent four aspects of Christ presented in the four Gospels: blue (heavenly Son of God — John), purple (the King — Matthew), scarlet (the suffering Servant — Mark), white linen (the perfect Man — Luke). The cherubim woven into the veil recall Eden's guardians (Gen 3:24): since the Fall, cherubim have barred the way to God's presence. The veil says: 'You cannot enter.' The tearing of the veil at Calvary says: 'Now you can.' The way into the holiest is opened 'through the veil, that is to say, his flesh' (Heb 10:20). Christ's broken body is the torn veil — the barrier between God and man removed forever.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt hang it upon four pillars of shittim wood overlaid with gold: their hooks shall be of gold, upon the four sockets of silver.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּה אֹתָהּ עַל־אַרְבָּעָה עַמּוּדֵי שִׁטִּים מְצֻפִּים זָהָב וָוֵיהֶם זָהָב עַל־אַרְבָּעָה אַדְנֵי־כָסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Venatattah otah al-arba'ah ammudei shittim metzuppim zahav vaveihem zahav al-arba'ah adnei-khasef.
verses.study_note: The veil hangs on four pillars (ammudei) of gold-overlaid acacia wood, with golden hooks (vaveihem — literally 'their vavs,' the Hebrew letter vav which is shaped like a hook). The pillars stand in four silver sockets. Four is the number of the earth and creation (four corners, four winds, four seasons). The veil's support structure uses all three Tabernacle metals: gold (pillars and hooks), silver (sockets), and implicitly bronze (in the broader courtyard). The silver sockets here use the same atonement silver as all other sockets — the veil, like the walls, stands on redemption. The Hebrew letter vav (ו) is the conjunction 'and' — it connects. The hooks that hold the veil literally connect heaven (the Most Holy Place) to the approach of man (the Holy Place).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt hang up the vail under the taches, that thou mayest bring in thither within the vail the ark of the testimony: and the vail shall divide unto you between the holy place and the most holy.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּה אֶת־הַפָּרֹכֶת תַּחַת הַקְּרָסִים וְהֵבֵאתָ שָׁמָּה מִבֵּית לַפָּרֹכֶת אֵת אֲרוֹן הָעֵדוּת וְהִבְדִּילָה הַפָּרֹכֶת לָכֶם בֵּין הַקֹּדֶשׁ וּבֵין קֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Venatattah et-happarokhet tachat haqqrasim veheveta shammah mibbeit lapparokhet et aron ha'edut vehivdilah happarokhet lakhem bein haqqodesh uvein kodesh haqqodashim.
verses.study_note: The veil is positioned 'under the taches' (clasps) — at the junction where the two sets of inner curtains meet. Behind the veil goes the 'ark of the testimony' (aron ha'edut). The veil 'shall divide' (vehivdilah — from 'badal,' to separate, distinguish) 'between the holy place and the most holy' (bein haqqodesh uvein kodesh haqqodashim). The verb 'hivdilah' is the same used in creation: God 'divided' light from darkness (Gen 1:4), waters above from waters below (Gen 1:6-7). The veil is a creation-act of separation — the same divine principle that orders cosmos from chaos now orders sacred space. 'Kodesh haqqodashim' (Holy of Holies) is a Hebrew superlative: the holiest of all holy places, the most sacred space on earth.
verses.doctrinal_note: The veil's function is separation — and specifically, separation that protects. The Most Holy Place is not hidden to exclude but to protect: sinful humanity cannot survive unmediated exposure to divine holiness. The veil thus represents both the problem (sin separates us from God) and God's mercy (the veil protects us from being consumed). Only the high priest, once a year, with blood, could pass beyond the veil (Lev 16) — prefiguring Christ who 'by his own blood' entered 'once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us' (Heb 9:12).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put the mercy seat upon the ark of the testimony in the most holy place.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּ אֶת־הַכַּפֹּרֶת עַל אֲרוֹן הָעֵדוּת בְּקֹדֶשׁ הַקֳּדָשִׁים׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatta et-hakkapporet al aron ha'edut bekodesh haqqodashim.
verses.study_note: The mercy seat (kapporet) is placed upon the Ark in the Most Holy Place. The 'kapporet' is the gold lid of the Ark with two cherubim whose wings overshadow it — the specific location where God's presence dwells between the cherubim (Exod 25:22). The word 'kapporet' comes from 'kafar' — to cover, to atone. It is literally 'the place of atonement' — the place where the high priest sprinkles the blood of the sin offering on Yom Kippur (Lev 16:14-15). Above the kapporet, between the cherubim, God speaks to Moses (Num 7:89). The mercy seat is thus the intersection of three realities: God's presence (above), God's law (inside the Ark), and God's atonement (the blood on the cover). Where law condemns, blood atones, and God is present — that is the mercy seat.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt set the table without the vail, and the candlestick over against the table on the side of the tabernacle toward the south: and thou shalt put the table on the north side.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתָּ אֶת־הַשֻּׁלְחָן מִחוּץ לַפָּרֹכֶת וְאֶת־הַמְּנֹרָה נֹכַח הַשֻּׁלְחָן עַל צֶלַע הַמִּשְׁכָּן תֵּימָנָה וְהַשֻּׁלְחָן תִּתֵּן עַל צֶלַע צָפוֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamta et-hashulchan michutz lapparokhet ve'et-hammenorah nokhach hashulchan al tzela hammishkan teimanah vehashulchan titten al tzela tzafon.
verses.study_note: The arrangement of the Holy Place: the table of showbread (shulchan) on the north side, the menorah (menorah) on the south side, facing each other across the sacred space. 'Without the vail' (michutz lapparokhet) — in the outer sanctum where priests minister daily. The menorah on the south provides the only light source in the windowless structure, illuminating the twelve loaves on the table across from it. The table represents God's provision (bread), and the menorah represents God's illumination (light). They face each other: provision and revelation stand in perpetual dialogue. The altar of incense (not mentioned here but described in chapter 30) stands between them before the veil — prayer mediates between provision and light.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make an hanging for the door of the tent, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ מָסָךְ לְפֶתַח הָאֹהֶל תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מַעֲשֵׂה רֹקֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita masakh lefetach ha'ohel tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh moshzar ma'aseh rokem.
verses.study_note: The screen (masakh) for the entrance — the 'door of the tent' (petach ha'ohel). The same four colors as the veil: blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen. However, there is a crucial difference: the veil was 'ma'aseh choshev' (cunning/designer work, with cherubim woven in), while this screen is 'ma'aseh rokem' (needlework/embroidery, without cherubim). The entrance screen invites approach but does not depict the angelic guardians. There are no cherubim barring the way at the door — only at the veil. The worshiper may enter the Holy Place without confronting the angelic sentinels; only at the innermost boundary do the cherubim stand guard. The 'petach' (opening/door) is the same word used for the 'door' of the tent of meeting where God met with individuals (Exod 33:9-10).
verses.doctrinal_note: The three entrances to the Tabernacle — the gate of the outer court, the screen of the Holy Place, and the veil of the Most Holy Place — present a progressive theology of access to God. Each uses the same four colors (blue, purple, scarlet, white) representing the same Christ, but the craftsmanship intensifies as one approaches God's presence. Christ is 'the door' (John 10:9) — the same Person at every level of approach. The absence of cherubim on the entrance screen shows that the way in is open; the presence of cherubim on the veil shows that the final barrier required blood. Christ is both the open door and the torn veil.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make for the hanging five pillars of shittim wood, and overlay them with gold, and their hooks shall be of gold: and thou shalt cast five sockets of brass for them.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ לַמָּסָךְ חֲמִשָּׁה עַמּוּדֵי שִׁטִּים וְצִפִּיתָ אֹתָם זָהָב וָוֵיהֶם זָהָב וְיָצַקְתָּ לָהֶם חֲמִשָּׁה אַדְנֵי נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita lammasakh chamishah ammudei shittim vetzippita otam zahav vaveihem zahav veyatzakta lahem chamishah adnei nechoshet.
verses.study_note: The entrance screen hangs on five pillars (chamishah ammudei) of gold-overlaid acacia, but their sockets are bronze (nechoshet), not silver. This is a critical distinction: the veil's four pillars stand in silver (atonement) sockets; the entrance screen's five pillars stand in bronze (judgment) sockets. The progression from outside to inside is bronze → silver → gold: judgment → atonement → divine glory. The worshiper approaches God by first passing through the realm of judgment (bronze), then standing on the ground of atonement (silver), then entering the fullness of divine presence (gold). Five pillars for the entrance (five being the number of grace) versus four for the veil — grace at the entrance, creation/earth at the final boundary. The chapter that began with the innermost glory (v.1) ends at the outer door — God's instruction moves from His presence outward to meet His people where they stand.
verses.doctrinal_note: The bronze sockets at the entrance versus silver sockets at the veil complete the Tabernacle's metal theology. Bronze (outer court, entrance) represents judgment — the first reality a sinner encounters. The brazen altar of burnt offering stands in the outer court; one must pass through judgment to enter. Silver (veil, walls) represents redemption — atonement money paid for every Israelite. Gold (everything inside) represents divine glory — the final reality for those who have passed through judgment and redemption. This is the gospel pattern: conviction of sin (bronze/judgment), justification through blood atonement (silver/redemption), and glorification in God's presence (gold/glory). Romans 8:30 follows the same trajectory: 'whom he justified, them he also glorified.'

meta.overview: Exodus 26 is the architectural heart of the Torah — God's detailed blueprint for the Tabernacle (mishkan), the portable sanctuary where YHWH would dwell among His people in the wilderness. The chapter moves from the innermost layer outward: first the ten curtains of fine-twined linen with cherubim woven in blue, purple, and scarlet (vv.1-6), then the eleven curtains of goats' hair forming the outer tent (vv.7-13), then the protective coverings of rams' skins dyed red and badgers' skins (v.14). The structure itself follows: the acacia wood boards (qerashim) set in silver sockets forming the walls (vv.15-25), the crossbars that bind them together (vv.26-30), and finally the crowning theological feature — the veil (parokhet) separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, and the screen (masakh) at the entrance (vv.31-37). Every measurement, material, and color carries typological significance. The fine linen speaks of righteousness, the blue of heaven, the purple of royalty, the scarlet of blood sacrifice. The acacia wood overlaid with gold pictures humanity joined to deity. The veil, woven with cherubim, guards the way to God's presence — the same cherubim that guarded Eden (Gen 3:24). This chapter is not merely architectural instruction but a theology of divine presence: how an infinitely holy God can dwell among a sinful people. The Tabernacle is God's answer to the problem of Genesis 3 — the restoration of divine-human communion through mediated, sacrificial approach. Hebrews 9-10 identifies the veil as Christ's flesh, torn at the cross to open the way into God's presence forever.
meta.theme: The Dwelling Place of God: Architecture of Divine Presence
meta.keyWordMeaning: Dwelling place, tabernacle — from 'shakhan,' to dwell, to settle, to abide. The mishkan is where God 'tabernacles' among His people. The root gives us 'Shekinah' — the dwelling glory of God. John 1:14 uses the same concept: the Word 'dwelt' (literally 'tabernacled') among us.
outline: The Inner Curtains of Fine Linen (vv.1-6): Ten curtains with cherubim in blue, purple, and scarlet, joined by gold clasps
The Outer Curtains of Goats' Hair (vv.7-13): Eleven curtains forming the tent over the tabernacle, joined by bronze clasps
The Protective Coverings (v.14): Rams' skins dyed red and badgers' skins — the outermost layers
The Boards and Silver Sockets (vv.15-25): Acacia wood frames standing in silver foundations — the structure of the dwelling
The Crossbars (vv.26-30): Five bars per side binding the boards together — unity and strength
The Veil: Separating Holy from Most Holy (vv.31-35): The parokhet with cherubim — guarding the way to God's presence
The Screen for the Door (vv.36-37): The entrance to the Holy Place — the way in

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make an altar of shittim wood, five cubits long, and five cubits broad; the altar shall be foursquare: and the height thereof shall be three cubits.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים חָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת אֹרֶךְ וְחָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת רֹחַב רָבוּעַ יִהְיֶה הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְשָׁלֹשׁ אַמּוֹת קֹמָתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita et-hammizbeach atzei shittim chamesh ammot orekh vechamesh ammot rochav ravua yihyeh hammizbeach veshalosh ammot qomato.
verses.study_note: The altar of burnt offering (mizbeach ha'olah) is the first piece of tabernacle furniture described after the inner sanctuary items (ark, table, lampstand, curtains). Its placement in the courtyard means it is encountered first — before the laver, before the Holy Place, before the Most Holy Place. The dimensions are significant: five cubits long by five cubits broad (approximately 7.5 x 7.5 feet) makes it 'foursquare' (ravua) — a perfect square symbolizing completeness and stability. At three cubits high (approximately 4.5 feet), it stands at chest height. This is by far the largest piece of furniture in the entire tabernacle complex. The material is acacia wood (atzei shittim) — the same incorruptible desert hardwood used for the ark, the table, and the boards of the tabernacle. That wood can survive the altar's fire is itself a theological statement about the nature of the sacrifice it represents.
verses.doctrinal_note: The bronze altar is the supreme type of the cross of Christ in the tabernacle system. Just as no one could approach God's presence in the tabernacle without first passing the altar of sacrifice, no one comes to the Father except through the substitutionary death of Christ (John 14:6). The altar's position — first in the worshiper's path — declares the non-negotiable priority of atonement. Hebrews 13:10 affirms: 'We have an altar, whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle.' The bronze altar was the place of death; Calvary is the place where the Lamb of God took away the sin of the world (John 1:29). The 'foursquare' design anticipates the universal scope of Christ's atonement — reaching to all four corners of the earth.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make the horns of it upon the four corners thereof: his horns shall be of the same: and thou shalt overlay it with brass.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ קַרְנֹתָיו עַל אַרְבַּע פִּנֹּתָיו מִמֶּנּוּ תִּהְיֶיןָ קַרְנֹתָיו וְצִפִּיתָ אֹתוֹ נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita qarnotav al arba pinnotav mimmennu tihyeyna qarnotav vetzippita oto nechoshet.
verses.study_note: Four horns (qarnotav) project from the four corners — 'of the same' (mimmennu — literally 'from it'), meaning the horns are not attached additions but integral extensions of the altar structure itself. The horns serve multiple functions: sacrificial blood was applied to them on the Day of Atonement and at sin offerings (Lev 4:7,18,25,30), the horns could be grasped by those seeking asylum (1 Kings 1:50; 2:28), and they symbolize power and authority (the 'horn' throughout Scripture represents strength). The entire altar is overlaid with 'brass' (nechoshet — more accurately 'bronze' or 'copper'). Bronze is the metal of judgment throughout the tabernacle system — the bronze serpent (Num 21:9), the bronze laver, and this bronze altar all represent divine judgment. Gold appears in the Holy Place and Most Holy Place (divine glory); bronze appears in the courtyard (divine judgment). The worshiper's first encounter is not with glory but with judgment met by sacrifice.
verses.doctrinal_note: The horns of the altar are one of the richest typological elements in the tabernacle. Blood applied to the horns represented atonement reaching to the full extent of the altar's authority — to all four corners, all directions, all people. When the fugitive grasped the altar's horns (1 Kings 1:50), he found sanctuary in the very place of sacrifice — a profound picture of sinners finding refuge in the cross. The horns also represent the power (horn = strength) of the sacrifice itself. Psalm 118:27 declares: 'Bind the sacrifice with cords, even unto the horns of the altar.' Christ was 'bound' to His cross as the final sacrifice, and the power of that sacrifice extends to the four corners of the earth.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make his pans to receive his ashes, and his shovels, and his basons, and his fleshhooks, and his firepans: all the vessels thereof thou shalt make of brass.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ סִירֹתָיו לְדַשְּׁנוֹ וְיָעָיו וּמִזְרְקֹתָיו וּמִזְלְגֹתָיו וּמַחְתֹּתָיו לְכָל־כֵּלָיו תַּעֲשֶׂה נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita sirotav ledaschno veya'av umizrqotav umizlegotav umachtotav lekhol-kelav ta'aseh nechoshet.
verses.study_note: Five specialized vessels for the altar's service, all made of bronze: (1) 'pans' (sirotav) for collecting and removing the fatty ashes (deshen — the residue of burnt offerings); (2) 'shovels' (ya'av) for clearing ashes; (3) 'basons' (mizrqotav — sprinkling bowls) for catching and dashing sacrificial blood against the altar; (4) 'fleshhooks' (mizlegotav — three-pronged forks) for arranging the pieces of meat on the fire; and (5) 'firepans' (machtotav — censers/fire-holders) for carrying live coals. Each vessel serves the mechanics of sacrifice. The specification that 'all the vessels' must be bronze reinforces the judgment theme: everything associated with the altar of sacrifice is bronze, the metal of testing by fire. Even the tools of worship operate under the sign of divine judgment.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make for it a grate of network of brass; and upon the net shalt thou make four brasen rings in the four corners thereof.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ לוֹ מִכְבָּר מַעֲשֵׂה רֶשֶׁת נְחֹשֶׁת וְעָשִׂיתָ עַל־הָרֶשֶׁת אַרְבַּע טַבְּעֹת נְחֹשֶׁת עַל אַרְבַּע קְצוֹתָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita lo mikhbar ma'aseh reshet nechoshet ve'asita al-hareshet arba tabb'ot nechoshet al arba qetzotav.
verses.study_note: A grate of bronze network (mikhbar ma'aseh reshet) — essentially a mesh or lattice that sits within the altar. The exact placement is debated: some place it halfway up inside the altar as a grill on which the sacrificial wood and animal pieces rested, allowing ash to fall below; others place it around the outside as a decorative or functional band. Four bronze rings at the four corners serve as attachment points for the carrying staves. The 'network' (reshet) design allows air circulation to feed the fire — a practical necessity for continuous burning. The engineering detail reflects divine care: God designs not only the theology of worship but its mechanics.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put it under the compass of the altar beneath, that the net may be even to the midst of the altar.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּה אֹתָהּ תַּחַת כַּרְכֹּב הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִלְּמָטָּה וְהָיְתָה הָרֶשֶׁת עַד חֲצִי הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Venatattah otah tachat karkov hammizbeach millemattah vehaytah hareshet ad chatzi hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: The grate is positioned 'under the compass' (tachat karkov) of the altar — 'karkov' likely refers to a ledge, rim, or surrounding border halfway up the altar's exterior. The net extends to 'the midst' (chatzi — half) of the altar, meaning it sits at the midpoint. This positions the grate at approximately 1.5 cubits (about 2.25 feet) above the ground. The precise positioning matters: the sacrificial fire must be elevated enough for proper combustion, yet the altar must be accessible for the priests to place offerings. Every measurement is specified by God — nothing in the tabernacle is left to human design preference.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make staves for the altar, staves of shittim wood, and overlay them with brass.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ בַדִּים לַמִּזְבֵּחַ בַּדֵּי עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים וְצִפִּיתָ אֹתָם נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita vaddim lammizbeach baddei atzei shittim vetzippita otam nechoshet.
verses.study_note: Carrying staves (vaddim) of acacia wood overlaid with bronze — the same construction pattern used for the ark's staves (gold-overlaid, Exod 25:13-14) and the table's staves (gold-overlaid, Exod 25:28). The altar's staves are bronze-overlaid, consistent with the courtyard's bronze theme. The staves make the altar portable — it must travel with Israel through the wilderness. This is a pilgrim altar for a pilgrim people. The place of sacrifice is not fixed to one location but moves with God's people wherever they go. Unlike pagan temples permanently anchored to sacred sites, Israel's worship center is mobile because God is present with His people in motion, not restricted to geography.

verses.kjv_text: And the staves shall be put into the rings, and the staves shall be upon the two sides of the altar, to bear it.
verses.original_text: וְהוּבָא אֶת־בַּדָּיו בַּטַּבָּעֹת וְהָיוּ הַבַּדִּים עַל־שְׁתֵּי צַלְעֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ בִּשְׂאֵת אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehuva et-baddav battabb'ot vehayu havaddim al-shetei tzal'ot hammizbeach bis'et oto.
verses.study_note: The staves are inserted through the rings and positioned on the 'two sides' (shetei tzal'ot) of the altar for carrying. The word 'tzal'ot' (sides/ribs) is the same word used for Eve being fashioned from Adam's 'rib' (tzela, Gen 2:21-22) and for the 'sides' of the tabernacle boards (Exod 26:20). The phrase 'to bear it' (bis'et oto) uses the verb 'nasa' — to lift up, to carry, to bear. The same verb is used for bearing sin (Isa 53:12) and bearing the ark. The entire worship system is designed for movement — Israel carries God's altar through the wilderness just as Christ bears the weight of divine judgment through human history.

verses.kjv_text: Hollow with boards shalt thou make it: as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it.
verses.original_text: נְבוּב לֻחֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר הֶרְאָה אֹתְךָ בָּהָר כֵּן יַעֲשׂוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Nevuv luchot ta'aseh oto ka'asher her'ah otekha bahar ken ya'asu.
verses.study_note: The altar is 'hollow with boards' (nevuv luchot) — not a solid block but a framework of planks forming an open box. Tradition suggests it was filled with earth when set up (cf. Exod 20:24: 'an altar of earth thou shalt make unto me'), combining the earthen altar principle with the portable board construction. The concluding formula is critical: 'as it was shewed thee in the mount, so shall they make it' (ka'asher her'ah otekha bahar ken ya'asu). God showed Moses a pattern — a heavenly blueprint — and the earthly construction must conform precisely to it. This formula appears repeatedly (25:9,40; 26:30; 27:8) and Hebrews 8:5 identifies its theological significance: the tabernacle is a 'shadow' (skia) and 'example' (hypodeigma) of 'heavenly things.'
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'as it was shewed thee in the mount' is cited directly in Hebrews 8:5 to establish one of the most important principles in biblical theology: the earthly tabernacle is patterned after a heavenly reality. Moses saw the original; he built the copy. The earthly altar, priesthood, and sacrifices are 'shadows' (Col 2:17; Heb 10:1) of the true altar, true Priest, and true Sacrifice in heaven. Christ did not enter a man-made sanctuary but 'into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God for us' (Heb 9:24). Every specification in Exodus 27 is not mere religious architecture but a three-dimensional portrait of heavenly redemption realities.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make the court of the tabernacle: for the south side southward there shall be hangings for the court of fine twined linen of an hundred cubits long for one side:
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ אֵת חֲצַר הַמִּשְׁכָּן לִפְאַת נֶגֶב־תֵּימָנָה קְלָעִים לֶחָצֵר שֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מֵאָה בָאַמָּה אֹרֶךְ לַפֵּאָה הָאֶחָת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita et chatzer hammishkan lif'at negev-teimanah qela'im lechatzer shesh mashzar me'ah va'ammah orekh lape'ah ha'echat.
verses.study_note: The court (chatzer) of the tabernacle is now described. The southern wall consists of 'hangings' (qela'im — curtain screens) of 'fine twined linen' (shesh mashzar) one hundred cubits long (approximately 150 feet). Fine linen (shesh) represents righteousness and purity throughout Scripture (Rev 19:8: 'fine linen is the righteousness of saints'). The court walls create the sacred enclosure — the defined boundary separating the tabernacle precinct from the common camp. The worshiper approaching the tabernacle would see a wall of white linen approximately 7.5 feet high, glistening in the desert sun. It simultaneously conceals what is inside and announces that something holy is within.

verses.kjv_text: And the twenty pillars thereof and their twenty sockets shall be of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets shall be of silver.
verses.original_text: וְעַמֻּדָיו עֶשְׂרִים וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֶשְׂרִים נְחֹשֶׁת וָוֵי הָעַמֻּדִים וַחֲשֻׁקֵיהֶם כָּסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'ammudav esrim ve'adneihem esrim nechoshet vavei ha'ammudim vachashukeihem kasef.
verses.study_note: Twenty pillars (ammudim) stand in twenty bronze sockets (adneihem — bases/foundations) along the south side. The pillars support the linen curtains. The hooks (vavei — literally 'hooks' or 'pegs,' also the name of the Hebrew letter vav) and 'fillets' (chashukeihem — connecting bands/rods) are silver. The metal combination is significant: bronze at the base (foundations — judgment), silver connecting and fastening (silver represents redemption throughout the tabernacle — the atonement money was silver, Exod 30:11-16). The court structure thus declares: judgment (bronze) at the foundation, redemption (silver) binding everything together, and righteousness (white linen) displayed for all to see.

verses.kjv_text: And likewise for the north side in length there shall be hangings of an hundred cubits long, and his twenty pillars and their twenty sockets of brass; the hooks of the pillars and their fillets of silver.
verses.original_text: וְכֵן לִפְאַת צָפוֹן בָּאֹרֶךְ קְלָעִים מֵאָה אֹרֶךְ וְעַמֻּדָיו עֶשְׂרִים וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֶשְׂרִים נְחֹשֶׁת וָוֵי הָעַמֻּדִים וַחֲשֻׁקֵיהֶם כָּסֶף׃
verses.transliteration: Vekhen lif'at tzafon ba'orekh qela'im me'ah orekh ve'ammudav esrim ve'adneihem esrim nechoshet vavei ha'ammudim vachashukeihem kasef.
verses.study_note: The north side exactly mirrors the south: 100 cubits of fine linen curtains, twenty pillars, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and fillets. The symmetry is deliberate and theological: the court is balanced, orderly, and proportioned. God's dwelling reflects His character — perfect order, harmony, and design. The repetition is not redundant but emphatic: every side of the sacred enclosure is given equal care and specificity. There is no preferred side or neglected corner in God's design. The total perimeter of curtain wall is 300 cubits (south 100 + north 100 + west 50 + east approaches 50), enclosing a space of 100 by 50 cubits — a 2:1 ratio that mirrors the tabernacle structure itself.

verses.kjv_text: And for the breadth of the court on the west side shall be hangings of fifty cubits: their pillars ten, and their sockets ten.
verses.original_text: וְרֹחַב הֶחָצֵר לִפְאַת־יָם קְלָעִים חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה עַמֻּדֵיהֶם עֲשָׂרָה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֲשָׂרָה׃
verses.transliteration: Verochav hechatzer lif'at-yam qela'im chamishim ammah ammudeihem asarah ve'adneihem asarah.
verses.study_note: The west side (lif'at yam — literally 'toward the sea,' since the Mediterranean is west of Israel) is 50 cubits wide with ten pillars and ten bronze sockets. This is the rear of the tabernacle enclosure — the end farthest from the entrance gate. The Most Holy Place, containing the Ark of the Covenant, sits at the western end of the tabernacle within this rear wall. Worshipers entered from the east and moved westward toward the presence of God — a directional symbolism: one moves toward the setting sun, away from the world, into the depth of divine mystery. The pillar spacing averages approximately five cubits apart, creating a uniform and dignified appearance.

verses.kjv_text: And the breadth of the court on the east side eastward shall be fifty cubits.
verses.original_text: וְרֹחַב הֶחָצֵר לִפְאַת קֵדְמָה מִזְרָחָה חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה׃
verses.transliteration: Verochav hechatzer lif'at qedmah mizrachah chamishim ammah.
verses.study_note: The east side — the entrance side — is also 50 cubits wide. 'East' is expressed with two Hebrew words: 'qedmah' (forward/eastward — from 'qedem,' meaning 'front, ancient, east') and 'mizrachah' (toward the sunrise — from 'zarach,' to rise, to shine). In Hebrew spatial orientation, east is 'forward' — the direction one faces. The tabernacle entrance always faces east, toward the rising sun. This is likely deliberate: the light enters from the east, the worshiper enters from the east, and God's glory fills the space from within. Adam was expelled eastward from Eden (Gen 3:24); Israel approaches God from the east, reversing the trajectory of the Fall.

verses.kjv_text: The hangings of one side of the gate shall be fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three.
verses.original_text: וַחֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה קְלָעִים לַכָּתֵף עַמֻּדֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Vachamesh esreh ammah qela'im lakkatef ammudeihem sheloshah ve'adneihem sheloshah.
verses.study_note: The east side is divided into three sections: 15 cubits of curtain on one side (the 'shoulder' — katef) of the gate, the gate itself in the center, and 15 cubits on the other side (v.15). The gate occupies the remaining 20 cubits (50 total minus 15 minus 15 = 20). Each curtain section has three pillars on three bronze sockets. The 'shoulder' (katef) metaphor is vivid — the curtain walls flanking the gate are like the shoulders flanking the entrance. The gate is proportionally significant: at 20 cubits wide in a 50-cubit wall, it occupies nearly half the eastern face, making it generous and welcoming rather than narrow and forbidding.

verses.kjv_text: And on the other side shall be hangings fifteen cubits: their pillars three, and their sockets three.
verses.original_text: וְלַכָּתֵף הַשֵּׁנִית חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה קְלָעִים עַמֻּדֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה׃
verses.transliteration: Velakkatef hashenit chamesh esreh qela'im ammudeihem sheloshah ve'adneihem sheloshah.
verses.study_note: The matching 'shoulder' on the other side of the gate — identical in every dimension: fifteen cubits of curtains, three pillars, three bronze sockets. The symmetry frames the gate perfectly. The total east-side configuration: 15 cubits of plain white linen curtain, then 20 cubits of the colorful gate screen, then 15 cubits of plain white linen curtain. The gate is both centered and distinguished — the only place of color and access on the entire eastern face. There is no second entrance, no side door, no back gate. One way in, through the gate. This singular access point is a powerful theological statement developed further in verse 16.

verses.kjv_text: And for the gate of the court shall be an hanging of twenty cubits, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen, wrought with needlework: and their pillars shall be four, and their sockets four.
verses.original_text: וּלְשַׁעַר הֶחָצֵר מָסָךְ עֶשְׂרִים אַמָּה תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מַעֲשֵׂה רֹקֵם עַמֻּדֵיהֶם אַרְבָּעָה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם אַרְבָּעָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ulesha'ar hechatzer masakh esrim ammah tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh mashzar ma'aseh roqem ammudeihem arba'ah ve'adneihem arba'ah.
verses.study_note: The gate screen (masakh) is the only entrance to the tabernacle court — 20 cubits wide, supported by four pillars on four bronze sockets. Its fabric is the same four-color combination used throughout the tabernacle: blue (tekhelet), purple (argaman), scarlet (tola'at shani), and fine white linen (shesh mashzar), 'wrought with needlework' (ma'aseh roqem — embroidered artistry). Blue represents heaven; purple, royalty; scarlet, blood/sacrifice; and white linen, righteousness. Together they compose a portrait of the Redeemer: the heavenly King (blue/purple) who shed His blood (scarlet) to provide righteousness (white) for His people. The gate is the single point of access — conspicuously visible, generously wide, and beautiful with color in contrast to the plain white walls on either side.
verses.doctrinal_note: The gate of the court is the first of three entrances in the tabernacle complex: the court gate (27:16), the door of the tabernacle (26:36), and the veil before the Most Holy Place (26:31-33). All three feature the same four colors, but with increasing artistry: the gate is 'needlework' (embroidered on one side), the tabernacle door is also needlework, and the veil is 'cunning work' (woven through, visible on both sides) with cherubim. Each threshold brings the worshiper closer to God — and each requires its own condition of access. Christ identifies Himself with this singular entrance: 'I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved' (John 10:9). There is one way to the Father — not through many gates but through one: Jesus Christ (John 14:6; Acts 4:12).

verses.kjv_text: All the pillars round about the court shall be filleted with silver; their hooks shall be of silver, and their sockets of brass.
verses.original_text: כָּל־עַמּוּדֵי הֶחָצֵר סָבִיב מְחֻשָּׁקִים כֶּסֶף וָוֵיהֶם כָּסֶף וְאַדְנֵיהֶם נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Kol-ammudei hechatzer saviv mechusshaqim kesef vaveihem kasef ve'adneihem nechoshet.
verses.study_note: A summary statement: all the pillars around the entire court are connected with silver bands (mechusshaqim kesef), have silver hooks, and stand on bronze sockets. The metal arrangement is consistently symbolic: bronze at the base touching the earth (judgment), silver connecting the structure (redemption — from the atonement money), and white linen displayed outward (righteousness). The pillar count around the entire court totals sixty (20 south + 20 north + 10 west + 6 east flanking + 4 gate = 60), though some calculations differ. The combination of metals and fabric creates a unified statement: redemption (silver) rises from judgment (bronze) and displays righteousness (linen) — the very gospel in architectural form.

verses.kjv_text: The length of the court shall be an hundred cubits, and the breadth fifty every where, and the height five cubits of fine twined linen, and their sockets of brass.
verses.original_text: אֹרֶךְ הֶחָצֵר מֵאָה בָאַמָּה וְרֹחַב חֲמִשִּׁים בַּחֲמִשִּׁים וְקֹמָה חָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת שֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר וְאַדְנֵיהֶם נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Orekh hechatzer me'ah va'ammah verochav chamishim bachamishim veqomah chamesh ammot shesh mashzar ve'adneihem nechoshet.
verses.study_note: The summary dimensions: 100 cubits long (approximately 150 feet), 50 cubits wide (approximately 75 feet), and 5 cubits high (approximately 7.5 feet). The 2:1 ratio (100 x 50) creates a harmonious, proportioned rectangle. At five cubits high, the curtain wall is tall enough to prevent casual viewing into the court — a person of normal height cannot see over it. The sacred space is truly enclosed, truly set apart. The total area of approximately 11,250 square feet is modest by temple standards but sufficient for the wilderness community. The entire area of the court, with its altar, laver, and tabernacle, is approximately the size of half a football field — intimate enough for priestly service yet spacious enough for national worship.

verses.kjv_text: All the vessels of the tabernacle in all the service thereof, and all the pins thereof, and all the pins of the court, shall be of brass.
verses.original_text: לְכֹל כְּלֵי הַמִּשְׁכָּן בְּכֹל עֲבֹדָתוֹ וְכָל־יְתֵדֹתָיו וְכָל־יְתֵדֹת הֶחָצֵר נְחֹשֶׁת׃
verses.transliteration: Lekhol kelei hammishkan bekhol avodato vekhol-yetedotav vekhol-yetedot hechatzer nechoshet.
verses.study_note: The 'pins' (yetedot — tent pegs/stakes) anchor the entire structure to the ground. Every tent peg for both the tabernacle and the court is bronze. These unsung elements are essential — without tent pegs, the entire structure collapses in the desert wind. The 'vessels' (kelei) for all the 'service' (avodah — work, labor, ministry) of the tabernacle are also bronze where they belong to the courtyard service. Avodah is the same word used for Israel's slave 'service' in Egypt (Exod 1:14) — the term is now redeemed and consecrated. What was once forced labor under Pharaoh is now willing worship under YHWH. Even tent pegs serve the glory of God.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt command the children of Israel, that they bring thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamp to burn always.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה תְּצַוֶּה אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיִקְחוּ אֵלֶיךָ שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זָךְ כָּתִית לַמָּאוֹר לְהַעֲלֹת נֵר תָּמִיד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah tetzavveh et-benei Yisra'el veyiqchu elekha shemen zayit zakh katit lamma'or leha'alot ner tamid.
verses.study_note: A shift from architecture to worship practice. The command concerns oil for the lampstand (menorah, described in 25:31-40). 'Pure oil olive beaten' (shemen zayit zakh katit) specifies the highest quality: 'zakh' means clear, pure, transparent — no sediment or impurity; 'katit' means beaten or crushed, not pressed in a mill. The first drops of oil from hand-beaten olives were the purest and clearest, producing a flame with minimal smoke. This oil causes the lamp 'to burn always' — literally 'to cause the lamp to ascend continually' (leha'alot ner tamid). The verb 'alah' (to go up, to ascend) is used: the light 'ascends' perpetually. The 'ner tamid' (perpetual lamp) becomes a central symbol in Jewish worship to this day — the eternal light that burns in every synagogue.
verses.doctrinal_note: The perpetual lamp (ner tamid) carries profound Christological and pneumatological significance. Christ declared 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12; 9:5) — He is the true menorah, the light that never goes out. The oil beaten from crushed olives represents the Holy Spirit (1 Sam 16:13; Zech 4:2-6), and the process of crushing (katit) foreshadows Gethsemane ('oil press'), where Christ was 'crushed' for our iniquities (Isa 53:5,10). The light must never go out because God's truth, presence, and illumination are perpetual. When Eli's negligence nearly let the lamp go out (1 Sam 3:3), it symbolized the near-extinction of divine revelation in Israel. The Church is called to maintain the perpetual lamp — to keep the light of the gospel burning in every generation.

verses.kjv_text: In the tabernacle of the congregation without the vail, which is before the testimony, Aaron and his sons shall order it from evening to morning before the LORD: it shall be a statute for ever unto their generations on the behalf of the children of Israel.
verses.original_text: בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד מִחוּץ לַפָּרֹכֶת אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָעֵדֻת יַעֲרֹךְ אֹתוֹ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו מֵעֶרֶב עַד־בֹּקֶר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Be'Ohel Mo'ed michutz lapparokhet asher al-ha'edut ya'arokh oto Aharon uvanav me'erev ad-boqer lifnei YHWH chuqqat olam ledorotam me'et benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: The location is precisely specified: in the 'tabernacle of the congregation' (Ohel Mo'ed — the Tent of Meeting), 'without the vail' (michutz lapparokhet — outside the inner veil), 'before the testimony' (al-ha'edut — before the Ark containing the tablets). This places the menorah in the Holy Place, the room between the entrance and the Most Holy Place, alongside the table of showbread and the altar of incense. Aaron and his sons tend the lamp 'from evening to morning' (me'erev ad-boqer) — through the night hours. While Israel sleeps, the priestly family keeps the light burning before YHWH. The chapter closes with a powerful formula: 'a statute for ever unto their generations' (chuqqat olam ledorotam) — this is not a temporary arrangement but a perpetual ordinance. The phrase 'on the behalf of' (me'et — literally 'from with') the children of Israel makes the priestly service representative: Aaron tends the light for and on behalf of the entire nation. The chapter that began with the altar of sacrifice closes with the perpetual light — from atonement to illumination, from the cross to the glory.
verses.doctrinal_note: Exodus 27 closes with a comprehensive picture of the gospel in architectural form. The worshiper's journey through the tabernacle court mirrors the believer's journey to God: (1) approach through the single gate (Christ, the only way — John 14:6); (2) encounter the bronze altar (the cross, where sin is dealt with — Heb 9:22); (3) proceed inward toward the Holy Place where the perpetual lamp burns (the light of Christ and the Spirit illuminating the believer's path — John 8:12; Ps 119:105). The priestly charge to keep the light burning 'from evening to morning' anticipates the Church's mission to maintain the gospel light through the dark night of this present age until the morning of Christ's return — 'the night is far spent, the day is at hand' (Rom 13:12). The 'statute for ever' finds its fulfillment in the New Jerusalem, where 'they need no candle, neither light of the sun; for the Lord God giveth them light: and they shall reign for ever and ever' (Rev 22:5).

meta.overview: Exodus 27 continues the divine blueprint for the tabernacle, moving from the inner sanctuary outward to the most public elements of Israel's worship complex. The chapter contains three major sections: the altar of burnt offering (vv.1-8), the court of the tabernacle (vv.9-19), and the commandment concerning the oil for the lampstand (vv.20-21). The bronze altar (mizbeach ha'olah) is the first object encountered upon entering the tabernacle courtyard — no one approaches the presence of God without first passing the place of sacrifice. It is the largest piece of furniture in the entire complex, five cubits square and three cubits high, overlaid with bronze (nechoshet), equipped with horns, pans, shovels, basins, fleshhooks, and firepans. Every morning and evening, the whole burnt offering ascends from this altar as a perpetual testimony that the way to God is through substitutionary blood. The court itself is defined by fine linen curtains hung on pillars of bronze with silver hooks and bands — creating a sacred enclosure of 100 by 50 cubits that separates the holy precinct from the common camp. The chapter closes with the perpetual lamp: pure beaten olive oil must burn continually in the tabernacle of the congregation, tended by Aaron and his sons from evening to morning before the LORD. Theologically, Exodus 27 establishes the architecture of atonement and access. The bronze altar declares that sin must be dealt with before God can be approached; the court curtains declare that holiness requires defined boundaries; and the perpetual lamp declares that God's light must never go out among His people. Every element points forward to Christ: the altar to His cross, the court to His body (the true temple), and the lamp to Him who is 'the light of the world' (John 8:12).
meta.theme: The Altar of Sacrifice, the Court of Holiness & the Perpetual Light
meta.keyWordMeaning: Altar — from the root 'zavach' (to slaughter, to sacrifice). The mizbeach is the place of slaughter, the location where the sacrificial animal meets its death in substitution for the sinner. It is the central object of Israelite worship and the first thing encountered in the tabernacle court.
outline: The Altar of Burnt Offering — Construction (vv.1-2): Five cubits square, three cubits high, with four horns
The Altar's Vessels and Bronze Overlay (vv.3-5): Pans, shovels, basins, fleshhooks, firepans, and the grate of network
The Altar's Staves for Transport (vv.6-8): Acacia wood and bronze rings — the portable altar for a pilgrim people
The Court of the Tabernacle — South Side (vv.9-10): Fine linen hangings, twenty pillars, twenty sockets of bronze
The Court — North and West Sides (vv.11-13): Matching dimensions with the south; the east side fifty cubits
The Court — The Gate (vv.14-16): A screen of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen — the single entrance
Summary of the Court and Its Furnishings (vv.17-19): Silver hooks, bronze sockets, and the pins and cords
The Oil for the Lampstand — Perpetual Light (vv.20-21): Pure beaten olive oil tended by Aaron and his sons from evening to morning

verses.kjv_text: And take thou unto thee Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him, from among the children of Israel, that he may minister unto me in the priest’s office, even Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar, Aaron’s sons.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה הַקְרֵב אֵלֶיךָ אֶת־אַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ וְאֶת־בָּנָיו אִתּוֹ מִתּוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְכַהֲנוֹ־לִי אַהֲרֹן נָדָב וַאֲבִיהוּא אֶלְעָזָר וְאִיתָמָר בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah haqrev elekha et-Aharon achikha ve'et-banav itto mittokh benei Yisra'el lekhahano-li Aharon Nadav va'Avihu El'azar ve'Itamar benei Aharon.
verses.study_note: God formally establishes the Aaronic priesthood. 'Take thou unto thee' (haqrev elekha) — literally 'bring near to yourself.' The verb 'qarav' (to draw near, bring near) is the same root used for 'qorban' (offering/sacrifice) — the priest is himself a living offering, brought near to God for service. Aaron is named first, then his four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar. Nadab and Abihu will die offering strange fire (Lev 10:1-2); the priestly line will continue through Eleazar (the Zadokite line) and Ithamar. 'From among the children of Israel' (mittokh benei Yisra'el) — the priest is taken from among the people, not from outside. Hebrews 5:1 echoes this: 'every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God.' The priest must be one of the people to represent them.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Aaronic priesthood is established by divine calling, not human appointment: 'No man taketh this honour unto himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron' (Heb 5:4). Christ Himself did not self-appoint but was 'called of God an high priest after the order of Melchisedec' (Heb 5:10). Yet Christ's priesthood surpasses Aaron's: Aaron was taken from sinful humanity and had to offer sacrifices for his own sins first (Heb 7:27); Christ is 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners' (Heb 7:26). The naming of all four sons — two who will die in judgment and two who will continue the line — foreshadows the seriousness of priestly ministry: access to God's presence is both supreme privilege and mortal danger.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make holy garments for Aaron thy brother for glory and for beauty.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ בִגְדֵי־קֹדֶשׁ לְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ לְכָבוֹד וּלְתִפְאָרֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita vigdei-qodesh le'Aharon achikha lekhavod uletif'aret.
verses.study_note: The programmatic verse for the entire chapter: 'holy garments' (vigdei-qodesh) made 'for glory and for beauty' (lekhavod uletif'aret). Two magnificent words define the purpose: 'kavod' (glory — weight, honor, splendor, the visible radiance of God's character) and 'tif'eret' (beauty — splendor, adornment, magnificence). These are not merely decorative qualities but theological statements: the high priest's garments make visible what is spiritually true about his office. He is clothed in glory because he represents the glorious God; he is clothed in beauty because holiness is beautiful. The same word pair describes God Himself: 'Honour and majesty are before him: strength and beauty are in his sanctuary' (Ps 96:6). Adam and Eve were originally clothed in God's glory; the Fall left them naked and ashamed (Gen 3:7). The priestly garments are a partial restoration of that lost glory — pointing forward to the ultimate restoration in Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: The phrase 'for glory and for beauty' reveals that worship is aesthetic as well as moral — God cares about beauty in His service. The priestly garments are the most detailed divine instructions about clothing in the Bible, demonstrating that the material world can express spiritual truth. The garments also function as a theology of investiture: Aaron is not naturally glorious but must be 'clothed' in glory — it comes from outside himself. This is the biblical principle of imputed righteousness: 'I will greatly rejoice in the LORD... for he hath clothed me with the garments of salvation, he hath covered me with the robe of righteousness' (Isa 61:10). Christ needs no external garments of glory — He is 'the brightness of his glory' (Heb 1:3) — but believers are 'clothed with Christ' (Gal 3:27).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt speak unto all that are wise hearted, whom I have filled with the spirit of wisdom, that they may make Aaron’s garments to consecrate him, that he may minister unto me in the priest’s office.
verses.original_text: וְאַתָּה תְּדַבֵּר אֶל־כָּל־חַכְמֵי־לֵב אֲשֶׁר מִלֵּאתִיו רוּחַ חָכְמָה וְעָשׂוּ אֶת־בִּגְדֵי אַהֲרֹן לְקַדְּשׁוֹ לְכַהֲנוֹ־לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'attah tedabber el-kol-chakhmei-lev asher mille'tiv ruach chokhmah ve'asu et-bigdei Aharon leqaddesho lekhahano-li.
verses.study_note: The garments are made by 'wise hearted' (chakhmei-lev) craftsmen whom God has 'filled with the spirit of wisdom' (ruach chokhmah). Sacred craftsmanship is a Spirit-empowered gift, not merely human skill. The 'spirit of wisdom' here is the same divine empowerment later given to Bezalel (Exod 31:3). In Hebrew thought, 'wisdom' (chokhmah) encompasses practical skill, artistic ability, and technical mastery — all directed by the Holy Spirit. The purpose is 'to consecrate him' (leqaddesho) — the garments themselves participate in the sanctification of the priest. Clothing transforms identity: Aaron becomes the high priest not by mere appointment but by investiture — the act of being clothed in sacred garments. Without the garments, he cannot minister.

verses.kjv_text: And these are the garments which they shall make; a breastplate, and an ephod, and a robe, and a broidered coat, a mitre, and a girdle: and they shall make holy garments for Aaron thy brother, and his sons, that he may minister unto me in the priest’s office.
