verses.original_text: וְאֵלֶּה הַבְּגָדִים אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשׂוּ חֹשֶׁן וְאֵפוֹד וּמְעִיל וּכְתֹנֶת תַּשְׁבֵּץ מִצְנֶפֶת וְאַבְנֵט וְעָשׂוּ בִגְדֵי־קֹדֶשׁ לְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ וּלְבָנָיו לְכַהֲנוֹ־לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'elleh habegadim asher ya'asu choshen ve'efod ume'il ukhtonet tashbetz mitznefet ve'avnet ve'asu vigdei-qodesh le'Aharon achikha ulevanav lekhahano-li.
verses.study_note: Six garments are named for the high priest: (1) the breastplate (choshen — the judgment breastplate with twelve stones), (2) the ephod (efod — the richly woven shoulder garment), (3) the robe (me'il — the all-blue robe worn beneath the ephod), (4) the broidered coat (khtonet tashbetz — a checkered or woven linen tunic), (5) the mitre (mitznefet — the priestly turban), and (6) the girdle (avnet — the sash or waistband). These are listed roughly from outermost/most visible to innermost. Each garment has a specific function and symbolic meaning. Together they transform Aaron from an ordinary Levite into the visible representative of God's holiness and Israel's mediator. The garments function as a second skin of holiness — a sacred identity layered over natural humanity.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall take gold, and blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine linen.
verses.original_text: וְהֵם יִקְחוּ אֶת־הַזָּהָב וְאֶת־הַתְּכֵלֶת וְאֶת־הָאַרְגָּמָן וְאֶת־תּוֹלַעַת הַשָּׁנִי וְאֶת־הַשֵּׁשׁ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehem yikchu et-hazzahav ve'et-hattkhelet ve'et-ha'argaman ve'et-tola'at hashshani ve'et-hashshesh.
verses.study_note: Five materials for the priestly garments — each carrying symbolic weight: (1) Gold (zahav) — the metal of divinity, royalty, and incorruptibility. (2) Blue (tekhelet) — a rare blue-purple dye from the murex snail, representing heaven, the divine realm. (3) Purple (argaman) — a reddish-purple dye of royalty and sovereignty, worn by kings. (4) Scarlet (tola'at hashshani) — literally 'the worm of scarlet,' a crimson dye from crushed insects (the coccus ilicis), representing blood, sacrifice, and atonement. (5) Fine linen (shesh) — white byssus, representing purity and righteousness: 'fine linen is the righteousness of saints' (Rev 19:8). These same five materials appear throughout the Tabernacle construction. Together they proclaim the priest as divine representative (gold), heavenly mediator (blue), royal sovereign (purple), sacrificial servant (scarlet), and righteous intercessor (white linen).

verses.kjv_text: And they shall make the ephod of gold, of blue, and of purple, of scarlet, and fine twined linen, with cunning work.
verses.original_text: וְעָשׂוּ אֶת־הָאֵפֹד זָהָב תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן תּוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asu et-ha'efod zahav tekhelet ve'argaman tola'at shani veshesh moshzar ma'aseh choshev.
verses.study_note: The ephod is the signature garment of the high priest — a richly woven vest or apron worn over the chest and back, held by shoulder straps. It is made of all five materials: gold thread woven with blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twined linen. 'Cunning work' (ma'aseh choshev) — literally 'the work of a skilled designer/thinker.' The word 'choshev' (from 'chashav' — to think, design, plan) implies that this is not mere weaving but artful, intentional design work — sacred art. The ephod is unique in Scripture: only the high priest wears it, and it becomes so identified with the priestly office that 'ephod' sometimes stands as shorthand for the entire priestly function (1 Sam 23:9; 30:7). The five materials woven together speak of a single unified garment expressing multiple realities — humanity (linen), royalty (purple), heavenly origin (blue), sacrificial blood (scarlet), and divine nature (gold).

verses.kjv_text: It shall have the two shoulderpieces thereof joined at the two edges thereof; and so it shall be joined together.
verses.original_text: שְׁתֵּי כְתֵפֹת חֹבְרֹת יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ אֶל־שְׁנֵי קְצוֹתָיו וְחֻבָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Shtei khetfot chovrot yihyeh-lo el-shnei qetzotav vechubar.
verses.study_note: The ephod has two shoulder pieces (khetfot) that join front and back panels. The shoulder is the place of bearing weight and responsibility: 'the government shall be upon his shoulder' (Isa 9:6). The onyx stones engraved with Israel's names (vv.9-12) will be set on these shoulder pieces — the priest literally carries the weight of the nation on his shoulders as he enters God's presence. The joining (chubar) of front and back into a unified garment speaks of the priest as the unifying link between God and His people. The garment's construction mirrors its theological function: the priest holds together what sin has separated.

verses.kjv_text: And the curious girdle of the ephod, which is upon it, shall be of the same, according to the work thereof; even of gold, of blue, and purple, and scarlet, and fine twined linen.
verses.original_text: וְחֵשֶׁב אֲפֻדָּתוֹ אֲשֶׁר עָלָיו כְּמַעֲשֵׂהוּ מִמֶּנּוּ יִהְיֶה זָהָב תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר׃
verses.transliteration: Vecheishev afuddato asher alav kema'asehu mimmennu yihyeh zahav tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh moshzar.
verses.study_note: The 'curious girdle' (cheishev) — a skillfully woven waistband — is an integral part of the ephod, not a separate piece: 'of the same' (mimmennu — from it, of the same piece). It is woven of the same five materials as the ephod, binding it around the priest's waist. The girdle symbolizes readiness for service (cf. 'gird up thy loins,' 1 Pet 1:13) and secures the entire priestly ensemble in place. In Scripture, the girdle/belt is associated with strength, truth, and preparedness: 'the girdle of his loins shall be righteousness, and the girdle of his reins faithfulness' (Isa 11:5). The five Tabernacle colors woven together in a single band express the unified purpose of priestly mediation.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take two onyx stones, and grave on them the names of the children of Israel:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ אֶת־שְׁתֵּי אַבְנֵי־שֹׁהַם וּפִתַּחְתָּ עֲלֵיהֶם שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta et-shtei avnei-shoham ufittachta aleihem shemot benei Yisra'el.
verses.study_note: Two onyx stones (avnei-shoham) are to be engraved with the names of the twelve tribes. 'Shoham' is likely onyx or sardonyx — a banded stone of layered colors. The engraving (pittachta — to engrave, open, carve) of names on stone parallels God's writing on the stone tablets of the Decalogue. The names are carved into the stones permanently — not painted or applied superficially but cut into the substance of the gem. This images how God knows His people: their names are engraved, not merely noted. 'I have graven thee upon the palms of my hands' (Isa 49:16). The onyx stones will rest on the high priest's shoulders (v.12), meaning the priest carries Israel's identity on his person as he approaches God.

verses.kjv_text: Six of their names on one stone, and the other six names of the rest on the other stone, according to their birth.
verses.original_text: שִׁשָּׁה מִשְּׁמֹתָם עַל הָאֶבֶן הָאֶחָת וְאֶת־שְׁמוֹת הַשִּׁשָּׁה הַנּוֹתָרִים עַל־הָאֶבֶן הַשֵּׁנִית כְּתוֹלְדֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Shishah mishshemotam al ha'even ha'achat ve'et-shemot hashshishah hannotarim al-ha'even hashenit ketoldotam.
verses.study_note: Six names on one shoulder stone, six on the other — arranged 'according to their birth' (ketoldotam). The birth order connects the names to the patriarchal narrative of Genesis: the history of God's promises and Israel's formation. Each name carries a story — Reuben's forfeited birthright, Judah's royal destiny, Joseph's suffering and exaltation. The balanced division (6 and 6) creates symmetry on the priest's shoulders: the full weight of Israel distributed evenly. Every tribe is equally represented in God's presence — no tribe omitted, none more prominently placed than another. This principle of equal access before God through the mediator anticipates the New Testament truth: 'there is neither Jew nor Greek... for ye are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28).

verses.kjv_text: With the work of an engraver in stone, like the engravings of a signet, shalt thou engrave the two stones with the names of the children of Israel: thou shalt make them to be set in ouches of gold.
verses.original_text: מַעֲשֵׂה חָרַשׁ אֶבֶן פִּתּוּחֵי חֹתָם תְּפַתַּח אֶת־שְׁתֵּי הָאֲבָנִים עַל־שְׁמֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֻסַבֹּת מִשְׁבְּצוֹת זָהָב תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Ma'aseh charash even pittuchei chotam tefattach et-shtei ha'avanim al-shemot benei Yisra'el musambot mishbetzot zahav ta'aseh otam.
verses.study_note: The engraving is compared to the work of a signet ring (pittuchei chotam — engravings of a seal). In the ancient world, a signet was a mark of authority, identity, and ownership. Signet-quality engraving is the finest work possible — each name cut with the precision and permanence of a royal seal. The stones are set in 'ouches of gold' (mishbetzot zahav) — filigree settings or frames of gold that secure and display the gems. Gold framing the names of Israel's tribes speaks of divine protection surrounding God's people: they are held in settings of glory. The signet imagery connects to Haggai 2:23 where God calls Zerubbabel 'my signet' — God's chosen representative, sealed and authorized.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put the two stones upon the shoulders of the ephod for stones of memorial unto the children of Israel: and Aaron shall bear their names before the LORD upon his two shoulders for a memorial.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתָּ אֶת־שְׁתֵּי הָאֲבָנִים עַל כִּתְפֹת הָאֵפֹד אַבְנֵי זִכָּרֹן לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְנָשָׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת־שְׁמוֹתָם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה עַל־שְׁתֵּי כְתֵפָיו לְזִכָּרֹן׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamta et-shtei ha'avanim al kitfot ha'efod avnei zikkaron livnei Yisra'el venasa Aharon et-shemotam lifnei YHWH al-shtei khetefav lezikkaron.
verses.study_note: The shoulder stones are 'stones of memorial' (avnei zikkaron) — they cause God to 'remember' His people. 'Aaron shall bear their names before the LORD' (venasa Aharon et-shemotam lifnei YHWH) — the verb 'nasa' (to bear, carry, lift up) is the great priestly verb. The priest bears the people's names, bears their burdens, bears their sins (Lev 16:21-22). He carries the nation on his shoulders and later on his heart (v.29). The shoulder represents strength; the heart represents love. Together they image the complete priestly care: the high priest carries his people with both power and compassion. 'Before the LORD' (lifnei YHWH) — directly before God's face. This is the essence of priestly intercession: bringing the names of God's people into the divine presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: Aaron bearing Israel's names on his shoulders is a profound type of Christ, the true High Priest who carries His people by His power: 'the government shall be upon his shoulder' (Isa 9:6). The Good Shepherd 'layeth it on his shoulders, rejoicing' (Luke 15:5). Christ bears the weight of His people's sins, their needs, and their identity before the Father. The 'memorial' (zikkaron) does not imply God might forget but is a covenant term for the ongoing, active attention God gives to His people. Just as the Passover is a zikkaron (Exod 12:14), the shoulder stones perpetually present Israel before God. Christ's intercession is the reality: 'he ever liveth to make intercession' (Heb 7:25).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make ouches of gold;
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ מִשְׁבְּצֹת זָהָב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita mishbetzot zahav.
verses.study_note: The gold settings (mishbetzot) are given their own separate command, emphasizing their importance. These filigree frames serve a dual function: they secure the precious stones so they cannot fall, and they display them beautifully. The gold itself is the most precious metal — incorruptible, untarnishable, beautiful. In the typological framework, the gold settings represent God's sovereign protection of His people: the names of the tribes are held securely in divine care. 'I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The brevity of this verse highlights the gold settings as worthy of distinct attention.

verses.kjv_text: And two chains of pure gold at the ends; of wreathen work shalt thou make them, and fasten the wreathen chains to the ouches.
verses.original_text: וּשְׁתֵּי שַׁרְשְׁרֹת זָהָב טָהוֹר מִגְבָּלֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם מַעֲשֵׂה עֲבֹת וְנָתַתָּה אֶת־שַׁרְשְׁרֹת הָעֲבֹתֹת עַל־הַמִּשְׁבְּצֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Ushtei sharshrot zahav tahor migbalot ta'aseh otam ma'aseh avot venatatah et-sharshrot ha'avotot al-hammishbetzot.
verses.study_note: Two chains of pure gold (zahav tahor — gold without alloy or impurity), wreathed or braided like cords. The chains connect the shoulder settings to the breastplate (v.22-25), linking the stones of strength (shoulders) to the stones of love (heart). 'Wreathen work' (ma'aseh avot) — literally 'rope work,' braided gold chains of extraordinary craftsmanship. The chains are not merely functional connectors but beautiful in their own right — twisted gold that catches and refracts light. The gold chains ensure that shoulders and heart are always connected: the priest cannot bear Israel in strength without also bearing them in love, and vice versa. This unity of strength and compassion is the mark of true intercession.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make the breastplate of judgment with cunning work; after the work of the ephod thou shalt make it; of gold, of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, and of fine twined linen, shalt thou make it.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ חֹשֶׁן מִשְׁפָּט מַעֲשֵׂה חֹשֵׁב כְּמַעֲשֵׂה אֵפֹד תַּעֲשֶׂנּוּ זָהָב תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita choshen mishpat ma'aseh choshev kema'aseh efod ta'asenu zahav tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh moshzar ta'aseh oto.
verses.study_note: The 'breastplate of judgment' (choshen mishpat) — perhaps the most theologically significant single garment in the Old Testament. 'Choshen' may derive from a root meaning 'beauty' or 'ornament'; 'mishpat' means judgment, justice, decision. It is called the breastplate 'of judgment' because it bears the Urim and Thummim (v.30) — the instruments of divine decision-making. Made of the same five materials as the ephod, it is worn directly over the priest's heart. The breastplate will hold twelve precious stones (vv.17-20), each engraved with a tribal name, ensuring that when the priest stands before God, every tribe is represented over his heart. The heart in Hebrew thought is the seat of intellect, will, and emotion — the priest's heart beats for his people.
verses.doctrinal_note: The breastplate of judgment unites two themes: justice (mishpat) and love (worn over the heart). God's character holds justice and mercy together — 'mercy and truth are met together; righteousness and peace have kissed each other' (Ps 85:10). Christ, the true High Priest, bears His people on His heart in love while executing perfect justice. The breastplate's dual function — displaying the tribes' names and holding the Urim and Thummim — shows that the priest both represents the people to God and communicates God's will to the people. Christ fulfills both: He intercedes for believers (Rom 8:34) and reveals the Father's will (John 1:18).

verses.kjv_text: Foursquare it shall be being doubled; a span shall be the length thereof, and a span shall be the breadth thereof.
verses.original_text: רָבוּעַ יִהְיֶה כָּפוּל זֶרֶת אָרְכּוֹ וְזֶרֶת רָחְבּוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ravua yihyeh kaful zeret orkho vezeret rochbo.
verses.study_note: The breastplate is square (ravua) and doubled (kaful) — folded over to form a pouch, which will contain the Urim and Thummim (v.30). A 'span' (zeret) is the distance from thumb to little finger of an outstretched hand — approximately nine inches. The square shape symbolizes completeness and stability; the doubling creates the pocket for God's instruments of judgment. The dimensions — a perfect square — echo the Holy of Holies which is also a perfect square/cube (1 Kings 6:20), and the New Jerusalem which descends as a perfect cube (Rev 21:16). Symmetry and proportion in sacred objects reflect the orderliness and perfection of God's character.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt set in it settings of stones, even four rows of stones: the first row shall be a sardius, a topaz, and a carbuncle: this shall be the first row.
verses.original_text: וּמִלֵּאתָ בוֹ מִלֻּאַת אֶבֶן אַרְבָּעָה טוּרִים אָבֶן טוּר אֹדֶם פִּטְדָה וּבָרֶקֶת הַטּוּר הָאֶחָד׃
verses.transliteration: Umille'ta vo millu'at even arba'ah turim aven tur odem pitdah uvareket hattur ha'echad.
verses.study_note: Four rows of three stones each — twelve in total, one for each tribe. The first row: sardius (odem — a red stone, likely carnelian), topaz (pitdah — a golden-yellow stone), and carbuncle (vareket — an emerald or green stone). The exact identification of several stones remains debated, but the theological principle is clear: each tribe has its own unique stone, its own distinct beauty, its own particular glory. No two stones are alike, just as no two tribes are interchangeable. The twelve together form a unified display of diverse beauty — a theology of unity in diversity that anticipates Paul's body metaphor: 'there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit' (1 Cor 12:4). These same twelve stones reappear in the foundations of the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:19-20).
verses.doctrinal_note: The twelve stones on the breastplate connect Genesis to Revelation. The patriarchal promises (twelve sons) become the priestly reality (twelve stones over the heart) and culminate in the eschatological vision (twelve foundation stones of the New Jerusalem, Rev 21:19-20). Each stone is distinct — ruby, topaz, emerald — reflecting the unique identity and calling of each tribe. God does not create uniformity but harmonious diversity. The New Testament church embodies this same principle: diverse gifts, one body (1 Cor 12:12-27). Every believer is a unique stone in the temple God is building (1 Pet 2:5).

verses.kjv_text: And the second row shall be an emerald, a sapphire, and a diamond.
verses.original_text: וְהַטּוּר הַשֵּׁנִי נֹפֶךְ סַפִּיר וְיָהֲלֹם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehattur hasheni nofekh sappir veyahalom.
verses.study_note: The second row: emerald (nofekh — possibly turquoise or garnet), sapphire (sappir — lapis lazuli, the deep blue stone associated with God's throne in Exod 24:10 and Ezek 1:26), and diamond (yahalom — possibly jasper or another clear, hard stone). The sapphire is particularly significant: the same word describes the pavement beneath God's feet when the elders saw God on Sinai (Exod 24:10). By wearing the sapphire on his breast, the priest carries a fragment of heaven's throne-room imagery over his heart. The breastplate becomes a portable theophany — a wearable reminder that the God of glory dwells with His people.

verses.kjv_text: And the third row a ligure, an agate, and an amethyst.
verses.original_text: וְהַטּוּר הַשְּׁלִישִׁי לֶשֶׁם שְׁבוֹ וְאַחְלָמָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehattur hashlishi leshem shevo ve'achlamah.
verses.study_note: The third row: ligure (leshem — possibly jacinth or amber), agate (shevo — a banded stone), and amethyst (achlamah — a purple stone, traditionally associated with sobriety and clarity of mind). Each stone displays a different color, texture, and quality of light — the breastplate is a dazzling mosaic of color. The progressive arrangement across four rows creates a visual symphony: reds, greens, blues, purples, and yellows all represented. The beauty is not accidental but designed by God — each stone chosen with intentionality for a specific tribe, a specific position, a specific place over the priest's heart.

verses.kjv_text: And the fourth row a beryl, and an onyx, and a jasper: they shall be set in gold in their inclosings.
verses.original_text: וְהַטּוּר הָרְבִיעִי תַּרְשִׁישׁ וְשֹׁהַם וְיָשְׁפֵה מְשֻׁבָּצִים זָהָב יִהְיוּ בְּמִלּוּאֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Vehattur harevi'i tarshish veshoham veyashpeh meshubatzim zahav yihyu bemillu'otam.
verses.study_note: The fourth and final row: beryl (tarshish — a sea-green or aquamarine stone, named after the city Tarshish), onyx (shoham — the same stone as the shoulder pieces, v.9), and jasper (yashpeh — a variegated opaque stone). All twelve stones are set in gold (meshubatzim zahav) — each unique gem framed in the same precious metal. The gold unifies what the stones diversify. The completion of the four rows completes the twelve — representing the totality of God's people. The twelve tribes, the twelve apostles (Rev 21:14), the twelve gates of the New Jerusalem — the number of covenant completeness runs from Genesis to Revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And the stones shall be with the names of the children of Israel, twelve, according to their names, like the engravings of a signet; every one with his name shall they be according to the twelve tribes.
verses.original_text: וְהָאֲבָנִים תִּהְיֶיןָ עַל־שְׁמֹת בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה עַל־שְׁמֹתָם פִּתּוּחֵי חוֹתָם אִישׁ עַל־שְׁמוֹ תִּהְיֶיןָ לִשְׁנֵי עָשָׂר שָׁבֶט׃
verses.transliteration: Veha'avanim tihyeina al-shemot benei-Yisra'el shtem esreh al-shemotam pittuchei chotam ish al-shemo tihyeina lishnei asar shavet.
verses.study_note: Each of the twelve stones bears one tribal name, engraved with signet-quality craftsmanship (pittuchei chotam). 'Every one with his name' (ish al-shemo) — literally 'a man according to his name.' Each stone is individually identified with a specific tribe; each tribe has its own unique gem. The repeated emphasis on names is theologically rich: in the biblical world, a name represents the full identity and character of the person. To bear someone's name is to bear their entire being. The high priest does not carry abstract representations but personal identities into God's presence. Jesus assures: 'I know my sheep, and am known of mine' (John 10:14). God knows His people by name, not merely by number.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make upon the breastplate chains at the ends of wreathen work of pure gold.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ עַל־הַחֹשֶׁן שַׁרְשְׁרֹת גַּבְלֻת מַעֲשֵׂה עֲבֹת זָהָב טָהוֹר׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita al-hachoshen sharshrot gavlut ma'aseh avot zahav tahor.
verses.study_note: Braided chains of pure gold attach the breastplate to the ephod — the same chains described in v.14 now specifically connected to the breastplate. These golden chains ensure the breastplate never separates from the ephod: the heart-garment is permanently linked to the shoulder-garment. The priest's intercession is a single, integrated act — strength and love, bearing and caring, supporting and cherishing are never divided. The chains are 'pure gold' (zahav tahor) — the connective tissue of priestly mediation is as precious as the garments themselves.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make upon the breastplate two rings of gold, and shalt put the two rings on the two ends of the breastplate.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ עַל־הַחֹשֶׁן שְׁתֵּי טַבְּעוֹת זָהָב וְנָתַתָּ אֶת־שְׁתֵּי הַטַּבָּעוֹת עַל־שְׁנֵי קְצוֹת הַחֹשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita al-hachoshen shtei tabb'ot zahav venatatah et-shtei hattabb'ot al-shnei qetzot hachoshen.
verses.study_note: Gold rings (tabb'ot) at the upper corners of the breastplate serve as attachment points for the golden chains. The rings are functional hardware — they bear the weight of the breastplate and keep it secured to the ephod. In Scripture, rings frequently symbolize authority and covenant (the signet ring, Gen 41:42; Esth 3:10). These structural details demonstrate God's attention to both beauty and engineering in sacred design: every element must be both functional and beautiful, practical and symbolic.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put the two wreathen chains of gold in the two rings which are on the ends of the breastplate.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּה אֶת־שְׁתֵּי עֲבֹתֹת הַזָּהָב עַל־שְׁתֵּי הַטַּבָּעֹת אֶל־קְצוֹת הַחֹשֶׁן׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatah et-shtei avotot hazzahav al-shtei hattabb'ot el-qetzot hachoshen.
verses.study_note: The chains thread through the rings — connecting breastplate to shoulder pieces. The detail is precise and repeated because the connection must be secure: the breastplate bearing Israel's names must never separate from the priest's body. The engineering of sacred garments reflects the reliability of divine care: God's hold on His people is not casual but carefully, permanently secured.

verses.kjv_text: And the other two ends of the two wreathen chains thou shalt fasten in the two ouches, and put them on the shoulderpieces of the ephod before it.
verses.original_text: וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי קְצוֹת שְׁתֵּי הָעֲבֹתֹת תִּתֵּן עַל־שְׁתֵּי הַמִּשְׁבְּצוֹת וְנָתַתָּה עַל־כִּתְפוֹת הָאֵפֹד אֶל־מוּל פָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et shtei qetzot shtei ha'avotot titten al-shtei hammishbetzot venatatah al-kitfot ha'efod el-mul panav.
verses.study_note: The golden chains attach at both ends: one end to the breastplate rings, the other to the gold settings (mishbetzot) on the ephod's shoulder pieces. 'Before it' (el-mul panav) — on the front side. The complete chain of connection runs: shoulder stone → gold setting → gold chain → ring → breastplate. The circuit is complete: Israel's names are on the shoulders (strength) connected by gold (divinity/incorruptibility) to the breastplate over the heart (love). The entire assembly is a physical theology of intercession.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make two rings of gold, and thou shalt put them upon the two ends of the breastplate in the border thereof, which is in the side of the ephod inward.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ שְׁתֵּי טַבְּעוֹת זָהָב וְשַׂמְתָּ אֹתָם עַל־שְׁנֵי קְצוֹת הַחֹשֶׁן עַל־שְׂפָתוֹ אֲשֶׁר אֶל־עֵבֶר הָאֵפוֹד בָּיְתָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita shtei tabb'ot zahav vesamta otam al-shnei qetzot hachoshen al-sefato asher el-ever ha'efod baytah.
verses.study_note: Two additional gold rings at the lower corners of the breastplate, on the inner side facing the ephod. These lower rings work with the upper attachment to keep the breastplate flat and secure against the priest's chest. The doubling of attachment points — upper chains and lower rings — ensures redundant security. The breastplate bearing Israel's names is held in place by multiple connections, just as God's care for His people operates through multiple channels of grace.

verses.kjv_text: And two other rings of gold thou shalt make, and shalt put them on the two sides of the ephod underneath, toward the forepart thereof, over against the other coupling thereof, above the curious girdle of the ephod.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ שְׁתֵּי טַבְּעוֹת זָהָב וְנָתַתָּה אֹתָם עַל־שְׁתֵּי כִתְפוֹת הָאֵפוֹד מִלְּמַטָּה מִמּוּל פָּנָיו לְעֻמַּת מַחְבַּרְתּוֹ מִמַּעַל לְחֵשֶׁב הָאֵפוֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita shtei tabb'ot zahav venatatah otam al-shtei kitfot ha'efod millemattah mimmul panav le'ummat machbarto mimma'al lecheishev ha'efod.
verses.study_note: Corresponding rings on the ephod itself, positioned below the shoulder pieces, above the girdle. These rings will be tied to the breastplate's lower rings with a blue cord (v.28), creating a secure lower attachment. The meticulous engineering continues: every connection point is specified with precision. God's instructions leave nothing to human improvisation — sacred worship is God-designed in every detail.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall bind the breastplate by the rings thereof unto the rings of the ephod with a lace of blue, that it may be above the curious girdle of the ephod, and that the breastplate be not loosed from the ephod.
verses.original_text: וְיִרְכְּסוּ אֶת־הַחֹשֶׁן מִטַּבְּעֹתָיו אֶל־טַבְּעֹת הָאֵפוֹד בִּפְתִיל תְּכֵלֶת לִהְיוֹת עַל־חֵשֶׁב הָאֵפוֹד וְלֹא־יִזַּח הַחֹשֶׁן מֵעַל הָאֵפוֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Veyirkesu et-hachoshen mittabb'otav el-tabb'ot ha'efod biftil tekhelet lihyot al-cheishev ha'efod velo-yizzach hachoshen me'al ha'efod.
verses.study_note: A blue cord (petil tekhelet) binds the lower rings together, and the purpose is stated explicitly: 'that the breastplate be not loosed from the ephod' (velo-yizzach hachoshen me'al ha'efod). The verb 'yizzach' means to be removed, displaced, or dislodged. The binding is permanent — the breastplate must never separate. The blue cord speaks of heavenly connection: blue is the color of heaven, of the divine realm. The heart that carries God's people is tied to the shoulders that bear them by a heavenly bond. Nothing can separate the priest from his people — a type of Christ's unbreakable bond with His own: 'Who shall separate us from the love of Christ?' (Rom 8:35).
verses.doctrinal_note: The inseparability of the breastplate from the ephod is a powerful type of Christ's inseparable union with His people. The blue cord — heavenly in color — binds the two together permanently. Romans 8:35-39 is the theological fulfillment: neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor any other creature 'shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.' The breastplate must never be 'loosed' (yizzach) — God's people are permanently secured over the heart of their High Priest.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron shall bear the names of the children of Israel in the breastplate of judgment upon his heart, when he goeth in unto the holy place, for a memorial before the LORD continually.
verses.original_text: וְנָשָׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת־שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּחֹשֶׁן הַמִּשְׁפָּט עַל־לִבּוֹ בְּבֹאוֹ אֶל־הַקֹּדֶשׁ לְזִכָּרֹן לִפְנֵי יְהוָה תָּמִיד׃
verses.transliteration: Venasa Aharon et-shemot benei Yisra'el bechoshen hammishpat al-libbo bevo'o el-haqqodesh lezikkaron lifnei YHWH tamid.
verses.study_note: The summary purpose statement for the breastplate — one of the most beautiful verses in Exodus. Aaron bears (nasa) the names upon his heart (al-libbo) when he enters the holy place (bevo'o el-haqqodesh), for a memorial before the LORD continually (lezikkaron lifnei YHWH tamid). Every key word carries weight: 'bear' (the priest's fundamental task), 'names' (personal identities, not abstractions), 'heart' (the center of love, intellect, and will), 'holy place' (the inner sanctuary of God's presence), 'memorial' (ongoing, active divine attention), 'before the LORD' (directly in God's face), 'continually' (tamid — perpetually, without ceasing). This verse is the supreme Old Testament description of priestly intercession and its most complete typological anticipation of Christ.
verses.doctrinal_note: Aaron bears Israel on his heart 'continually' (tamid) — this is the word used for the perpetual fire on the altar (Lev 6:13) and the continual showbread (Exod 25:30). Christ's intercession is likewise perpetual: 'he ever liveth to make intercession for them' (Heb 7:25). The names are not on a scroll in a box but over the living, beating heart of the priest. Christ does not intercede abstractly but personally, knowing each of His own by name (John 10:3). The movement from shoulders (v.12 — strength) to heart (v.29 — love) reveals the fullness of priestly care: Christ carries His people by power and cherishes them with love, presenting their names before the Father unceasingly.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put in the breastplate of judgment the Urim and the Thummim; and they shall be upon Aaron’s heart, when he goeth in before the LORD: and Aaron shall bear the judgment of the children of Israel upon his heart before the LORD continually.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּ אֶל־חֹשֶׁן הַמִּשְׁפָּט אֶת־הָאוּרִים וְאֶת־הַתֻּמִּים וְהָיוּ עַל־לֵב אַהֲרֹן בְּבֹאוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וְנָשָׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת־מִשְׁפַּט בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל עַל־לִבּוֹ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה תָּמִיד׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatah el-choshen hammishpat et-ha'urim ve'et-hattummim vehayu al-lev Aharon bevo'o lifnei YHWH venasa Aharon et-mishpat benei Yisra'el al-libbo lifnei YHWH tamid.
verses.study_note: The Urim and Thummim (ha'urim veha'tummim) — the most mysterious objects in the Old Testament. 'Urim' likely derives from 'or' (light); 'Thummim' from 'tom' (completeness, perfection, truth). Together: 'Lights and Perfections' or 'Light and Truth.' They are placed inside the breastplate pouch (the doubled, square garment of v.16) and are used to discern God's will in matters of national importance (Num 27:21; 1 Sam 28:6). Their exact form and method of operation are never described — a deliberate mystery. They disappear from use after the early monarchy and are not mentioned as present in the Second Temple. Aaron 'bears the judgment' (mishpat) of Israel on his heart — he carries the responsibility of discerning God's will for the entire nation.
verses.doctrinal_note: The Urim and Thummim represent God's provision for divine guidance — the principle that God's people need not navigate by human wisdom alone. The priest carries both the people's names (intercession) and God's judgment instruments (revelation) on the same garment, over the same heart. Christ fulfills both functions perfectly: He is the 'wisdom of God' (1 Cor 1:24) and the one in whom 'are hid all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col 2:3). Believers no longer need external instruments of guidance because the Spirit indwells them: 'ye have an unction from the Holy One, and ye know all things' (1 John 2:20). What was external and mysterious under the Old Covenant is internal and personal under the New.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make the robe of the ephod all of blue.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ אֶת־מְעִיל הָאֵפוֹד כְּלִיל תְּכֵלֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita et-me'il ha'efod kelil tekhelet.
verses.study_note: The robe of the ephod (me'il ha'efod) is 'all of blue' (kelil tekhelet) — entirely, completely blue. Unlike the ephod with its five colors, the robe is monochromatic: pure heaven-blue. 'Kelil' means whole, complete, entire — no other color intrudes. This is the garment worn beneath the ephod, visible at the hem. Blue (tekhelet) is the color of heaven, the sky, the divine realm — the robe envelops the priest in the color of God's domain. He is clothed in heaven itself. The robe is also associated with royalty and authority: Samuel wore a me'il (1 Sam 2:19), and the me'il that Saul tore became a prophetic sign (1 Sam 15:27-28). Christ as heavenly High Priest wears the ultimate robe of divine origin.
verses.doctrinal_note: The all-blue robe speaks of Christ's heavenly origin and nature. While the ephod's five colors represent the complexity of the mediatorial office, the robe's single blue color represents the priest's fundamental identity as heaven's representative on earth. Christ 'came down from heaven' (John 6:38) and is 'from above' (John 8:23). The robe worn closest to the body (beneath the ephod) suggests that the deepest identity of the true High Priest is heavenly, divine, and otherworldly — even as He ministers among earthly people.

verses.kjv_text: And there shall be an hole in the top of it, in the midst thereof: it shall have a binding of woven work round about the hole of it, as it were the hole of an habergeon, that it be not rent.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה פִי־רֹאשׁוֹ בְּתוֹכוֹ שָׂפָה יִהְיֶה לְפִיו סָבִיב מַעֲשֵׂה אֹרֵג כְּפִי תַחְרָא יִהְיֶה־לּוֹ לֹא יִקָּרֵעַ׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah fi-rosho betokhov safah yihyeh lefiv saviv ma'aseh oreg kefi tachra yihyeh-lo lo yiqqare'a.
verses.study_note: The robe has a woven binding around its neck opening to prevent tearing — 'that it be not rent' (lo yiqqare'a). The comparison is to a habergeon (tachra — a coat of armor). The reinforcement is both practical (preventing fraying) and symbolic: the priestly robe must not be torn. The high priest's garments represent his office, and a torn garment represents a broken office. When Caiaphas 'rent his clothes' at Jesus' trial (Matt 26:65), he unwittingly performed a prophetic act — the Aaronic high priesthood was being torn, destroyed, and superseded by the one true High Priest standing before him. Leviticus 21:10 explicitly forbids the high priest from tearing his garments.

verses.kjv_text: And beneath upon the hem of it thou shalt make pomegranates of blue, and of purple, and of scarlet, round about the hem thereof; and bells of gold between them round about:
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ עַל־שׁוּלָיו רִמֹּנֵי תְכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי עַל־שׁוּלָיו סָבִיב וּפַעֲמֹנֵי זָהָב בְּתוֹכָם סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita al-shulav rimmonei tekhelet ve'argaman vetola'at shani al-shulav saviv ufa'amonei zahav betokham saviv.
verses.study_note: The hem of the robe bears an alternating pattern of pomegranates and golden bells. Pomegranates (rimmonim) — rich with seeds, a symbol of fruitfulness, abundance, and life. They are made in three of the Tabernacle colors: blue, purple, and scarlet (no gold or white). Golden bells (pa'amonei zahav) are interspersed between them. The alternating pattern — bell, pomegranate, bell, pomegranate — creates a rhythm of sound and symbol around the priest's feet. Pomegranates are found in the Song of Solomon (4:3,13; 6:7), carved on the Temple pillars (1 Kings 7:18-20), and listed among the blessings of the Promised Land (Deut 8:8). They speak of the fruitfulness that flows from the priestly presence.
verses.doctrinal_note: The pomegranates and bells together express the dual nature of priestly ministry: fruitfulness and proclamation. The pomegranate represents the fruit of the Spirit-filled life; the bell represents the voice that announces the priest's presence and ministry. Christ's ministry produces both: abundant fruit (John 15:5,8) and authoritative proclamation (John 7:46). The alternating pattern suggests that fruitfulness and testimony are inseparable — every good work speaks, and every true word bears fruit.

verses.kjv_text: A golden bell and a pomegranate, a golden bell and a pomegranate, upon the hem of the robe round about.
verses.original_text: פַּעֲמֹן זָהָב וְרִמּוֹן פַּעֲמֹן זָהָב וְרִמּוֹן עַל־שׁוּלֵי הַמְּעִיל סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Pa'amon zahav verimmon pa'amon zahav verimmon al-shulei hamme'il saviv.
verses.study_note: The pattern is stated with rhythmic repetition: 'a golden bell and a pomegranate, a golden bell and a pomegranate' — the text itself echoes the alternating pattern of the hem. This rhythmic quality is liturgical: the reader hears what the priest wears. The repetition emphasizes the ordered, beautiful regularity of the design. Nothing is random in God's sacred architecture — every element has its assigned place and sequence. The hem of the robe, the lowest and most visible part of the garment, is decorated with the greatest care — beauty extends to the very edges of priestly ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be upon Aaron to minister: and his sound shall be heard when he goeth in unto the holy place before the LORD, and when he cometh out, that he die not.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה עַל־אַהֲרֹן לְשָׁרֵת וְנִשְׁמַע קוֹלוֹ בְּבֹאוֹ אֶל־הַקֹּדֶשׁ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה וּבְצֵאתוֹ וְלֹא יָמוּת׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah al-Aharon lesharet venishma qolo bevo'o el-haqqodesh lifnei YHWH uvetzeto velo yamut.
verses.study_note: The bells serve a life-or-death purpose: 'his sound shall be heard' (venishma qolo) — the bells announce the priest's movement within the holy place. 'That he die not' (velo yamut) — the bells are protective. As long as the people hear the ringing, they know the priest is alive and moving in God's presence. If the bells fall silent, the priest has died. The ringing sound is both announcement (the priest is ministering) and assurance (he is alive). Entering God's presence is not casual — it is mortal work. The priest's sound going in and coming out means Israel knows their mediator has been accepted and has returned safely. This is the Old Testament pattern that makes Christ's resurrection so significant: our High Priest entered the heavenly sanctuary and came out alive — 'he ever liveth' (Heb 7:25).
verses.doctrinal_note: The bells that ensure 'he die not' reveal the peril of standing before holy God as a sinful mediator. Aaron can die in God's presence — his sons Nadab and Abihu will prove this (Lev 10:1-2). The ringing bells are testimony that the priest has survived the encounter. Christ enters the ultimate holy place — not an earthly copy but heaven itself (Heb 9:24) — and His resurrection is the proof that He has been accepted: 'Who is he that condemneth? It is Christ that died, yea rather, that is risen again' (Rom 8:34). The empty tomb is the cosmic equivalent of the ringing bells: our High Priest lives.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make a plate of pure gold, and grave upon it, like the engravings of a signet, HOLINESS TO THE LORD.
verses.original_text: וְעָשִׂיתָ צִּיץ זָהָב טָהוֹר וּפִתַּחְתָּ עָלָיו פִּתּוּחֵי חֹתָם קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'asita tzitz zahav tahor ufittachta alav pittuchei chotam qodesh laYHWH.
verses.study_note: The crown jewel of the priestly attire: a plate (tzitz — literally 'blossom, flower, shining thing') of pure gold, engraved with the words 'HOLINESS TO THE LORD' (qodesh laYHWH). 'Tzitz' shares the root of 'tzitzit' (the fringes of Numbers 15:38) and carries connotations of shining forth, blossoming, radiating. The inscription uses signet-quality engraving (pittuchei chotam) — the same quality demanded for the tribal names. These two words — QODESH LAYHWH — are arguably the most important two words on any priestly garment. Worn on the forehead (v.38), they are the first thing visible as the priest approaches. Everything about the high priest's ministry is summarized in this inscription: he is consecrated to YHWH; his entire being is dedicated to holiness. The golden plate crowns the mitre (turban) as holiness crowns the priestly life.
verses.doctrinal_note: HOLINESS TO THE LORD (qodesh laYHWH) is the defining inscription of the entire priestly system. Holiness is not one attribute among many but the fundamental reality of God's character — 'Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty' (Rev 4:8). The plate on the forehead means holiness governs the mind: every thought, decision, and intention of the priest is placed under the banner of divine holiness. Zechariah prophesies that in the Messianic kingdom, 'HOLINESS UNTO THE LORD' will be inscribed even on the bells of horses (Zech 14:20) — holiness will pervade all of life. Christ is Himself 'the Holy One of God' (Mark 1:24; John 6:69). His holiness is not inscribed externally but is His essential nature.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put it on a blue lace, that it may be upon the mitre; upon the forefront of the mitre it shall be.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתָּ אֹתוֹ עַל־פְּתִיל תְּכֵלֶת וְהָיָה עַל־הַמִּצְנָפֶת אֶל־מוּל פְּנֵי הַמִּצְנֶפֶת יִהְיֶה׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamta oto al-petil tekhelet vehayah al-hammitznafet el-mul penei hammitznefet yihyeh.
verses.study_note: The golden plate is attached to the turban (mitznefet) by a blue cord (petil tekhelet) — the same heavenly blue that binds the breastplate to the ephod (v.28). Blue connects every sacred element: the color of heaven ties holiness to the priest's brow, the people's names to his heart, and his heart to his shoulders. The plate is positioned on the 'forefront' (el-mul penei — literally 'opposite the face of') — the most prominent, visible position. Anyone approaching the high priest sees first and foremost: HOLINESS TO THE LORD. This is the first impression of the priestly office — and the lasting one.

verses.kjv_text: And it shall be upon Aaron’s forehead, that Aaron may bear the iniquity of the holy things, which the children of Israel shall hallow in all their holy gifts; and it shall be always upon his forehead, that they may be accepted before the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהָיָה עַל־מֵצַח אַהֲרֹן וְנָשָׂא אַהֲרֹן אֶת־עֲוֹן הַקֳּדָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר יַקְדִּישׁוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְכָל־מַתְּנֹת קָדְשֵׁיהֶם וְהָיָה עַל־מִצְחוֹ תָּמִיד לְרָצוֹן לָהֶם לִפְנֵי יְהוָה׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayah al-metzach Aharon venasa Aharon et-avon haqqodashim asher yaqdishu benei Yisra'el lekhol-mattenot qodsheihem vehayah al-mitzcho tamid leratzon lahem lifnei YHWH.
verses.study_note: Perhaps the most theologically dense verse in the chapter. Three staggering statements: (1) Aaron 'bears the iniquity of the holy things' (nasa et-avon haqqodashim) — even Israel's holiest acts of worship contain imperfection and sin. Their best offerings are tainted. The priest bears this contamination, absorbing the impurity of flawed worship so that it becomes acceptable. (2) The plate is 'always upon his forehead' (al-mitzcho tamid) — perpetual, unceasing coverage for perpetually imperfect worship. (3) 'That they may be accepted before the LORD' (leratzon lahem lifnei YHWH) — 'ratzon' means acceptance, favor, delight. The priest's holiness makes the people's offerings acceptable. Without the mediator's covering, even sacred gifts would be rejected.
verses.doctrinal_note: This verse is the clearest Old Testament expression of substitutionary bearing of sin in worship. Aaron 'bears' (nasa) Israel's iniquity — the same verb used in Isaiah 53:4 ('He hath borne our griefs') and 53:12 ('he bare the sin of many'). Even our holiest acts — our worship, prayers, and gifts — are contaminated by sin and require a mediator to make them acceptable. Christ is the permanent reality of what the golden plate symbolized: 'he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him' (Heb 7:25). The 'always' (tamid) speaks of Christ's unceasing intercession. The 'accepted' (ratzon) speaks of the Father's delight in the Son: 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' (Matt 3:17). In Christ, believers are 'accepted in the beloved' (Eph 1:6).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt embroider the coat of fine linen, and thou shalt make the mitre of fine linen, and thou shalt make the girdle of needlework.
verses.original_text: וְשִׁבַּצְתָּ הַכְּתֹנֶת שֵׁשׁ וְעָשִׂיתָ מִצְנֶפֶת שֵׁשׁ וְאַבְנֵט תַּעֲשֶׂה מַעֲשֵׂה רֹקֵם׃
verses.transliteration: Veshibbatzta hakketonet shesh ve'asita mitznefet shesh ve'avnet ta'aseh ma'aseh roqem.
verses.study_note: Three garments made of fine linen (shesh): the coat (ketonet — the woven tunic worn closest to the body), the mitre (mitznefet — the turban), and the girdle (avnet — the sash, made with embroidered/needlework). The coat of fine linen is the foundation garment — pure white, representing righteousness and purity. It is worn directly against the priest's skin, between his body and the outer garments of glory. The mitre (turban) wraps the head and bears the golden plate. The girdle binds everything together. These 'inner' garments of white linen complement the 'outer' garments of colored splendor — righteousness underlies glory.

verses.kjv_text: And for Aaron’s sons thou shalt make coats, and thou shalt make for them girdles, and bonnets shalt thou make for them, for glory and for beauty.
verses.original_text: וְלִבְנֵי אַהֲרֹן תַּעֲשֶׂה כֻתֳּנֹת וְעָשִׂיתָ לָהֶם אַבְנֵטִים וּמִגְבָּעוֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה לָהֶם לְכָבוֹד וּלְתִפְאָרֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Velivnei Aharon ta'aseh kuttonot ve'asita lahem avnetim umigba'ot ta'aseh lahem lekhavod uletif'aret.
verses.study_note: Aaron's sons — the ordinary priests — receive three garments: coats (kuttonot — linen tunics), girdles (avnetim — sashes), and bonnets (migba'ot — caps, distinct from the high priest's mitznefet/turban). Their garments are simpler than the high priest's but share the same purpose: 'for glory and for beauty' (lekhavod uletif'aret) — the identical phrase from v.2. Every priest, not only the high priest, serves in garments designed for glory. The ordinary priests represent the broader priesthood of all believers (1 Pet 2:9) — every servant of God is clothed by grace for sacred service.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put them upon Aaron thy brother, and his sons with him; and shalt anoint them, and consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priest’s office.
verses.original_text: וְהִלְבַּשְׁתָּ אֹתָם אֶת־אַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ וְאֶת־בָּנָיו אִתּוֹ וּמָשַׁחְתָּ אֹתָם וּמִלֵּאתָ אֶת־יָדָם וְקִדַּשְׁתָּ אֹתָם וְכִהֲנוּ לִי׃
verses.transliteration: Vehilbashta otam et-Aharon achikha ve'et-banav itto umashachta otam umille'ta et-yadam veqiddashta otam vekhihanu li.
verses.study_note: Three acts of consecration: (1) 'anoint them' (umashachta — anointing with oil, the visible sign of the Spirit's empowerment; the root of 'mashiach' / Messiah / Christ), (2) 'consecrate them' (umille'ta et-yadam — literally 'fill their hands,' the idiom for ordination/installation in office), and (3) 'sanctify them' (veqiddashta — set them apart as holy). The three-fold process transforms ordinary men into sacred servants. 'Fill their hands' (mille et-yad) is particularly vivid: the priest's hands, previously empty, are filled with the instruments of sacrifice — his hands become dedicated tools of mediation. The entire sequence points to Christ: anointed with the Spirit (Luke 4:18), consecrated by the Father (John 10:36), sanctified for His people (John 17:19).
verses.doctrinal_note: The three acts — anointing, ordination, and sanctification — correspond to Christ's three offices: Prophet (anointed to speak), Priest (consecrated to mediate), and King (sanctified to rule). 'Mashach' (anoint) is the root of 'Mashiach' (Messiah/Christ — the Anointed One). Jesus is anointed 'with the oil of gladness above thy fellows' (Heb 1:9; Ps 45:7). His hands are 'filled' with the perfect sacrifice — Himself. He is sanctified by the Father: 'him, whom the Father hath sanctified, and sent into the world' (John 10:36). Every element of Aaronic consecration finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt make them linen breeches to cover their nakedness; from the loins even unto the thighs they shall reach:
verses.original_text: וַעֲשֵׂה לָהֶם מִכְנְסֵי־בָד לְכַסּוֹת בְּשַׂר עֶרְוָה מִמָּתְנַיִם וְעַד־יְרֵכַיִם יִהְיוּ׃
verses.transliteration: Va'aseh lahem mikhnesei-vad lekhassot besar ervah mimmotnayim ve'ad-yerekhayim yihyu.
verses.study_note: Linen breeches (mikhnesei-vad) — undergarments of white linen to cover the priest's nakedness (ervah). This connects directly to Exodus 20:26 where God prohibited steps on the altar 'that thy nakedness be not discovered.' 'Ervah' (nakedness/shame) is the vocabulary of the Fall (Gen 3:7,10,21). God's first act of grace after the Fall was clothing Adam and Eve (Gen 3:21); here He clothes the priests who serve in His presence. The breeches cover 'from the loins even unto the thighs' — the area of generation and vulnerability. The priest's entire person, from innermost undergarment to outermost golden plate, is clothed by divine design.

verses.kjv_text: And they shall be upon Aaron, and upon his sons, when they come in unto the tabernacle of the congregation, or when they come near unto the altar to minister in the holy place; that they bear not iniquity, and die: it shall be a statute for ever unto him and his seed after him.
verses.original_text: וְהָיוּ עַל־אַהֲרֹן וְעַל־בָּנָיו בְּבֹאָם אֶל־אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד אוֹ בְגִשְׁתָּם אֶל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לְשָׁרֵת בַּקֹּדֶשׁ וְלֹא יִשְׂאוּ עָוֹן וָמֵתוּ חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לוֹ וּלְזַרְעוֹ אַחֲרָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vehayu al-Aharon ve'al-banav bevo'am el-ohel mo'ed o vegishtam el-hammizbe'ach lesharet baqqodesh velo yis'u avon vametu chuqqat olam lo ulezar'o acharav.
verses.study_note: The chapter's final verse ties everything together with a solemn warning: the garments are not optional adornments but essential protection. Without them, 'they bear iniquity, and die' (velo yis'u avon vametu). The verb 'nasa avon' (bear iniquity) here means to bear the guilt of their own sin — the opposite of v.38 where Aaron bears the iniquity of the people's holy things. Without proper garments, the priest's own sin remains uncovered and brings death. 'A statute for ever' (chuqqat olam) — a permanent ordinance binding Aaron's line through all generations. The phrase 'unto him and his seed after him' (lo ulezar'o acharav) establishes the hereditary, perpetual nature of the priestly regulations. The chapter that began with glory and beauty ends with life and death: sacred service is simultaneously the highest privilege and the gravest responsibility.
verses.doctrinal_note: The final verse reiterates the gravity of priestly ministry: approach God improperly and die. The garments are not decoration but theology worn on the body — they represent the righteousness, holiness, and mediation that alone permit approach to God. Stripped of these, the priest is exposed in his sinful humanity and condemned. The typological implication is profound: no one approaches God in their own natural condition. Every person needs to be 'clothed' in righteousness not their own. This is the gospel in Old Testament form: 'Put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ' (Rom 13:14); 'ye have put on Christ' (Gal 3:27); 'clothed with the robe of righteousness' (Isa 61:10). Christ is the garment of glory and beauty in which believers are dressed for acceptance before God.

meta.overview: Exodus 28 contains God's detailed instructions for the garments of the high priest and the ordinary priests — clothing designed not by human fashion but by divine blueprint. Every thread, color, stone, and bell carries theological weight. Aaron is to be clothed 'for glory and for beauty' (lekhavod uletif'aret, v.2) — his garments visually proclaim the holiness, majesty, and mediatorial role of the one who stands between God and Israel. The chapter describes six garments for the high priest: the ephod (a richly woven vest of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen), the breastplate of judgment (bearing twelve precious stones engraved with the names of the twelve tribes, worn over the heart), the robe of the ephod (entirely blue, with golden bells and pomegranates on the hem), the mitre (turban) with the golden plate inscribed HOLINESS TO THE LORD, the embroidered coat, and the girdle. Aaron bears Israel on his shoulders (the onyx stones of the ephod, v.12) and over his heart (the breastplate stones, v.29) — the priest carries his people into God's presence physically and symbolically. The breastplate also contains the Urim and Thummim, the mysterious instruments of divine guidance. The chapter concludes with instructions for the garments of Aaron's sons: coats, girdles, and bonnets. The entire priestly wardrobe is a theological statement in fabric: the high priest is a walking Tabernacle, a living symbol of mediation, a type of Christ who is 'holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens' (Heb 7:26). Every garment Aaron wears points forward to the one true High Priest who needs no external garments of glory because He is 'the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person' (Heb 1:3).
meta.theme: The Priestly Garments: Clothing the Mediator for Glory and Beauty
meta.keyWordMeaning: Glory and Beauty — the dual purpose of the priestly garments stated in v.2. 'Kavod' (glory, weight, honor) and 'tif'eret' (beauty, splendor, adornment) together express the visible magnificence of the priestly office. The high priest's garments make the invisible holiness of God visible through sacred craftsmanship. These same terms describe God Himself (Ps 96:6) and are fulfilled in Christ, 'the brightness of his glory' (Heb 1:3).
outline: The Call of Aaron and His Sons to the Priesthood (v.1)
Garments for Glory and Beauty: The Craftsmen Commissioned (vv.2-5)
The Ephod: Gold, Blue, Purple, Scarlet and Fine Linen (vv.6-14)
The Breastplate of Judgment: Twelve Stones Over the Heart (vv.15-30)
The Robe of the Ephod: All Blue with Bells and Pomegranates (vv.31-35)
The Golden Plate: HOLINESS TO THE LORD (vv.36-38)
The Coat, Mitre, Girdle & Garments for Aaron's Sons (vv.39-43)

verses.kjv_text: And this is the thing that thou shalt do unto them to hallow them, to minister unto me in the priest’s office: Take one young bullock, and two rams without blemish,
verses.original_text: וְזֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר־תַּעֲשֶׂה לָהֶם לְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתָם לְכַהֵן לִי לְקַח פַּר אֶחָד בֶּן־בָּקָר וְאֵילִם שְׁנַיִם תְּמִימִם׃
verses.transliteration: Vezeh haddavar asher-ta'aseh lahem leqaddesh otam lekahen li leqach par echad ben-baqar ve'eilim shenayim temimim.
verses.study_note: The opening verse establishes the threefold purpose: (1) "to hallow them" (leqaddesh otam) — to consecrate, set apart as holy; (2) "to minister unto me" (lekahen li) — the priestly verb, to serve as priest; (3) implicitly, to mediate between God and Israel. Three animals are required: one young bull (par — for the sin offering, vv.10-14) and two rams (eilim — one for the burnt offering, vv.15-18, and one for the consecration, vv.19-28). "Without blemish" (temimim) — physically perfect, representing moral and spiritual wholeness. Every sacrifice pointing to Christ must be unblemished, because He is "a lamb without blemish and without spot" (1 Pet 1:19).
verses.doctrinal_note: The requirement of unblemished animals is the consistent biblical principle of substitutionary perfection. The substitute must be what the sinner is not — perfect. This eliminates any possibility that the sacrifice is merely disposal of a defective animal. The best is offered, not the worst. Christ, the ultimate sacrifice, was "holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners" (Heb 7:26). The perfection required of Levitical sacrifices was a shadow of His sinless perfection.

verses.kjv_text: And unleavened bread, and cakes unleavened tempered with oil, and wafers unleavened anointed with oil: of wheaten flour shalt thou make them.
verses.original_text: וְלֶחֶם מַצּוֹת וְחַלֹּת מַצּוֹת בְּלוּלֹת בַּשֶּׁמֶן וּרְקִיקֵי מַצּוֹת מְשֻׁחִים בַּשֶּׁמֶן סֹלֶת חִטִּים תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתָם׃
verses.transliteration: Velechem matztzot vechallot matztzot belulot bashshemen urqiqei matztzot meshuchim bashshemen solet chittim ta'aseh otam.
verses.study_note: Three types of unleavened bread: (1) matztzot — plain unleavened bread; (2) challot matztzot belulot bashshemen — unleavened cakes mixed with oil (the oil is incorporated throughout); (3) reqiqei matztzot meshuchim bashshemen — unleavened wafers anointed with oil (oil applied on the surface). Leaven is consistently a symbol of sin and corruption in Scripture (Matt 16:6; 1 Cor 5:6-8); its absence represents purity and sincerity. The oil (shemen), representing the Holy Spirit, is either mixed in (internal filling) or anointed upon (external empowerment). "Wheaten flour" (solet chittim) — the finest grade, sifted and refined. Everything offered in consecration must be the purest quality.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put them into one basket, and bring them in the basket, with the bullock and the two rams.
verses.original_text: וְנָתַתָּ אוֹתָם עַל סַל אֶחָד וְהִקְרַבְתָּ אֹתָם בַּסָּל וְאֶת־הַפָּר וְאֵת שְׁנֵי הָאֵילִם׃
verses.transliteration: Venatatta otam al sal echad vehiqravta otam bassal ve'et-happar ve'et shenei ha'eilim.
verses.study_note: All the bread is placed in one basket (sal echad) — the unity of the offering. "And bring them" (vehiqravta) — from qarav, to draw near, bring near, the standard term for presenting an offering. The same root gives us qorban (offering/sacrifice). Everything needed for consecration is gathered together and brought near at once: the bread, the bull, and the two rams. The totality of the offering — grain and animal, bread and blood — represents the completeness of consecration. Nothing is left out; no dimension of life remains unconsecrated.

verses.kjv_text: And Aaron and his sons thou shalt bring unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and shalt wash them with water.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת־בָּנָיו תַּקְרִיב אֶל־פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְרָחַצְתָּ אֹתָם בַּמָּיִם׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-Aharon ve'et-banav taqriv el-petach ohel mo'ed verachatzta otam bammayim.
verses.study_note: The first act of consecration: washing with water (rachatz) at the door (petach) of the Tabernacle. Before anything else — before garments, anointing, or sacrifice — there must be cleansing. The priests are washed entirely (not just hands and feet, as in daily service at the laver, 30:19-20). This is the once-for-all washing of ordination, distinct from the daily washings that follow. "The door of the tabernacle of the congregation" (petach ohel mo'ed) — literally "the opening of the tent of meeting." The consecration is public, visible, and takes place at the threshold between the common world and the sacred space.
verses.doctrinal_note: The once-for-all washing of the priests at ordination corresponds to regeneration — the one-time spiritual cleansing that initiates the believer into priestly service. Jesus told Peter: "He that is washed (louo — bathed entirely) needeth not save to wash (nipto — wash parts) his feet" (John 13:10). The initial, total washing is salvation/regeneration; the daily foot-washing is ongoing sanctification. Paul applies the priestly washing to believers: "He saved us, by the washing of regeneration" (Titus 3:5). Baptism corresponds to this ordination washing — the public initiation into the priesthood of all believers.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take the garments, and put upon Aaron the coat, and the robe of the ephod, and the ephod, and the breastplate, and gird him with the curious girdle of the ephod:
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ אֶת־הַבְּגָדִים וְהִלְבַּשְׁתָּ אֶת־אַהֲרֹן אֶת־הַכֻּתֹּנֶת וְאֵת מְעִיל הָאֵפֹד וְאֶת־הָאֵפֹד וְאֶת־הַחֹשֶׁן וְאָפַדְתָּ לוֹ בְּחֵשֶׁב הָאֵפֹד׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta et-habbegadim vehilbashta et-Aharon et-hakkuttonet ve'et me'il ha'efod ve'et-ha'efod ve'et-hachoshen ve'afadta lo becheshev ha'efod.
verses.study_note: After washing, clothing — the garments of glory and beauty (Exod 28:2). Five items are layered in order: (1) the coat/tunic (kuttonet) — the white linen undergarment worn next to the skin; (2) the robe of the ephod (me'il ha'efod) — the blue robe with golden bells and pomegranates at the hem; (3) the ephod — the outer vest with shoulder straps bearing the onyx stones; (4) the breastplate (choshen) — the twelve gemstones over the heart; (5) the curious girdle (cheshev) — the woven band that binds the ephod in place. Each garment has been described in detail in chapter 28. Aaron does not dress himself — Moses dresses him. Consecration is not self-initiated but God-given through God's appointed agent.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt put the mitre upon his head, and put the holy crown upon the mitre.
verses.original_text: וְשַׂמְתָּ הַמִּצְנֶפֶת עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ וְנָתַתָּ אֶת־נֵזֶר הַקֹּדֶשׁ עַל־הַמִּצְנָפֶת׃
verses.transliteration: Vesamta hammitznefet al-rosho venatatta et-nezer haqqodesh al-hammitznafet.
verses.study_note: The mitre (mitznefet) — the priestly turban — is placed on Aaron's head, and upon it the "holy crown" (nezer haqqodesh). This crown is the golden plate inscribed "HOLINESS TO THE LORD" (Qodesh laYHWH, Exod 28:36). The nezer is the same word used for the Nazirite's consecration (Num 6:7) — it means both crown and separation/dedication. The inscription on the forehead declares the priest's total dedication to YHWH and identifies his entire ministry as holy. The crown covers the forehead — the seat of the will and mind — symbolizing that the priest's thinking and intentions are consecrated to God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The golden plate reading "Holiness to the LORD" on the high priest's forehead is typologically fulfilled in Christ, who is "holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and made higher than the heavens" (Heb 7:26). But it also points forward to the New Jerusalem, where the saints bear God's name on their foreheads (Rev 22:4) — the culmination of priestly consecration. What was true of Aaron alone becomes true of all God's people: "Ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation" (1 Pet 2:9).

verses.kjv_text: Then shalt thou take the anointing oil, and pour it upon his head, and anoint him.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ אֶת־שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וְיָצַקְתָּ עַל־רֹאשׁוֹ וּמָשַׁחְתָּ אֹתוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta et-shemen hammishchah veyatzaqta al-rosho umashachta oto.
verses.study_note: The anointing — the climactic act of priestly consecration. The sacred oil (shemen hammishchah, formula in Exod 30:22-33) is poured (yatzaq — not dabbed but poured abundantly) upon Aaron's head. Psalm 133:2 describes it flowing "down upon the beard, even Aaron's beard: that went down to the skirts of his garments" — a lavish, overflowing anointing. The verb mashach (to anoint) is the root of Mashiach (Messiah) — the Anointed One. Every anointing of a priest points to the ultimate Anointing: "God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Ghost and with power" (Acts 10:38). The oil descends from the head downward — anointing flows from Christ the Head to His body the church.
verses.doctrinal_note: The anointing of the high priest is the definitive Old Testament type of Christ's anointing with the Holy Spirit. The word "Christ" (Christos in Greek) is the exact translation of "Messiah" (Mashiach in Hebrew) — both mean "The Anointed One." At Jesus' baptism, the Spirit descended "like a dove" upon Him (Matt 3:16) — His priestly anointing for public ministry. The oil poured on Aaron's head flowing down to his garments (Ps 133:2) pictures the Spirit given to Christ flowing to His body the church: "Of his fulness have all we received, and grace for grace" (John 1:16).

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt bring his sons, and put coats upon them.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־בָּנָיו תַּקְרִיב וְהִלְבַּשְׁתָּם כֻּתֳּנֹת׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-banav taqriv vehilbashtam kuttonot.
verses.study_note: Aaron's sons receive their garments — the kuttonot (tunics/coats). Unlike Aaron, they do not receive the ephod, breastplate, or holy crown — those are unique to the high priest. The sons wear white linen tunics, linen breeches, bonnets, and girdles (Exod 28:40). The distinction between the high priest's elaborate garments and the sons' simpler vestments reflects the unique mediatorial role of the high priest. Yet even the sons are "brought near" (taqriv) — the same verb used for offering sacrifices. The priests themselves are living offerings, consecrated to God.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt gird them with girdles, Aaron and his sons, and put the bonnets on them: and the priest’s office shall be theirs for a perpetual statute: and thou shalt consecrate Aaron and his sons.
verses.original_text: וְחָגַרְתָּ אֹתָם אַבְנֵט אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו וְחָבַשְׁתָּ לָהֶם מִגְבָּעֹת וְהָיְתָה לָהֶם כְּהֻנָּה לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם וּמִלֵּאתָ יַד־אַהֲרֹן וְיַד־בָּנָיו׃
verses.transliteration: Vechagarta otam avnet Aharon uvanav vechabashta lahem migba'ot vehaytah lahem kehunnah lechuqqat olam umileta yad-Aharon veyad-banav.
verses.study_note: Two final elements: girdles (avnet — the sash/belt) and bonnets (migba'ot — head coverings for the sons, distinct from Aaron's mitre). Then the summary statement: "the priest's office shall be theirs for a perpetual statute" (kehunnah lechuqqat olam) — the Aaronic priesthood is established as a permanent institution by divine decree. The chapter's key phrase appears: "thou shalt consecrate" — literally "thou shalt fill the hand of" (umileta yad). The Hebrew idiom for ordination is "filling the hand" (mille yad), meaning to authorize, empower, and commission. An empty hand receives the instruments and authority of office.
verses.doctrinal_note: The idiom "fill the hand" (mille yad) for ordination reveals that priestly authority is received, not seized. The priest's hand is empty until God fills it with the tools and authority of service. This counters all presumptuous claims to spiritual authority. Aaron did not volunteer for the priesthood; God called and filled him (Heb 5:4). Christ likewise "glorified not himself to be made an high priest; but he that said unto him, Thou art my Son" (Heb 5:5). All legitimate spiritual authority is divinely conferred, not self-appointed.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt cause a bullock to be brought before the tabernacle of the congregation: and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the bullock.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְרַבְתָּ אֶת־הַפָּר לִפְנֵי אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְסָמַךְ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת־יְדֵיהֶם עַל־רֹאשׁ הַפָּר׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqravta et-happar lifnei ohel mo'ed vesamakh Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh happar.
verses.study_note: The sin offering begins. The bull is brought before the Tabernacle, and Aaron and his sons lay their hands (samakh — to lean, press firmly) upon its head. This is not a light touch but a deliberate pressing — transferring identification from person to animal. The laying on of hands (semikhah) is the foundational act of substitutionary atonement: the offerer's sin and guilt are symbolically transferred to the animal, which then dies in his place. The priests, though chosen by God, are themselves sinners who need atonement before they can atone for others. Even the holiest human mediator requires cleansing.
verses.doctrinal_note: The laying on of hands (semikhah) establishes the substitutionary principle that runs through all of Scripture: the innocent dies in place of the guilty. The sin is transferred by identification — pressing hands on the head represents union between offerer and offering. This is the Old Testament foundation for the New Testament doctrine of imputation: Christ was "made sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him" (2 Cor 5:21). Our sins were laid upon Him; His righteousness was credited to us. The transfer runs in both directions.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt kill the bullock before the LORD, by the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַטְתָּ אֶת־הַפָּר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד׃
verses.transliteration: Veshachateta et-happar lifnei YHWH petach ohel mo'ed.
verses.study_note: The bull is slaughtered (shachat — ritually killed by cutting the throat) "before the LORD" (lifnei YHWH) — in God's presence, at the entrance of the Tabernacle. The killing is not hidden but performed openly before God, acknowledging that sin's penalty is death and that this death occurs under divine witness. The location — "the door" (petach) — is the threshold between the common world and sacred space. Death occurs at the boundary, creating the passage from profane to holy. Without this death, the priests cannot enter God's presence.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take of the blood of the bullock, and put it upon the horns of the altar with thy finger, and pour all the blood beside the bottom of the altar.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ מִדַּם הַפָּר וְנָתַתָּה עַל־קַרְנֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ בְּאֶצְבָּעֶךָ וְאֶת־כָּל־הַדָּם תִּשְׁפֹּךְ אֶל־יְסוֹד הַמִּזְבֵּחַ׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta middam happar venatattah al-qarnot hammizbeach be'etzba'ekha ve'et-kol-haddam tishpokh el-yesod hammizbeach.
verses.study_note: Two applications of blood: (1) some blood is placed on the horns of the altar (qarnot hammizbeach) with Moses' finger (be'etzba'ekha) — the horn represents power, and blood on the four horns consecrates the altar's authority in all four directions. The finger application is personal and deliberate, not casual. (2) All remaining blood is poured (tishpokh — poured out abundantly) at the base (yesod — foundation) of the altar. Blood at the foundation means the altar itself is established upon sacrifice. The entire sacrificial system rests on a base saturated with blood — there is no approach to God except through atoning death.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take all the fat that covereth the inwards, and the caul that is above the liver, and the two kidneys, and the fat that is upon them, and burn them upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ אֶת־כָּל־הַחֵלֶב הַמְכַסֶּה אֶת־הַקֶּרֶב וְאֵת הַיֹּתֶרֶת עַל־הַכָּבֵד וְאֵת שְׁתֵּי הַכְּלָיֹת וְאֶת־הַחֵלֶב אֲשֶׁר עֲלֵיהֶן וְהִקְטַרְתָּ הַמִּזְבֵּחָה׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta et-kol-hachelev hamkhasseh et-haqqerev ve'et hayyoteret al-hakkaved ve'et shttei hakkelayot ve'et-hachelev asher aleihen vehiqtarta hammizbechah.
verses.study_note: The internal fats and specific organs are burned on the altar. The fat (chelev) covering the internal organs, the fatty lobe (yoteret) on the liver, and the two kidneys (kelayot) with their fat — these represent the innermost, hidden parts of the animal. Fat in the ancient world was considered the richest, most vital portion. Leviticus 3:16 declares: "All the fat is the LORD's" — the best and richest belongs to God. The kidneys are associated with the emotions and inner thoughts in Hebrew (cf. Ps 7:9; Jer 17:10; Rev 2:23). Burning the internal organs means offering to God the deepest, most hidden aspects of one's being.

verses.kjv_text: But the flesh of the bullock, and his skin, and his dung, shalt thou burn with fire without the camp: it is a sin offering.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־בְּשַׂר הַפָּר וְאֶת־עֹרוֹ וְאֶת־פִּרְשׁוֹ תִּשְׂרֹף בָּאֵשׁ מִחוּץ לַמַּחֲנֶה חַטָּאת הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-besar happar ve'et-oro ve'et-pirsho tisrof ba'esh michutz lammachaneh chattat hu.
verses.study_note: The remainder — flesh, skin, and dung — is burned "without the camp" (michutz lammachaneh). The sin offering is not consumed on the altar or eaten by priests but expelled from the community and burned in an unclean place. This is the most graphic expression of sin's treatment: utter rejection, removal from sacred space, and destruction by fire outside the boundaries of the holy community. "It is a sin offering" (chattat hu) — the word chattat is from the root chata (to miss the mark, to sin). The offering literally becomes "sin" — it takes on the identity of what it bears.
verses.doctrinal_note: Hebrews 13:11-13 directly applies this verse to Christ's crucifixion: "The bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burned without the camp. Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate." Christ was crucified outside Jerusalem's walls — "without the camp." He became the sin offering that was expelled, rejected, and consumed by God's judgment in the place of uncleanness. Paul echoes this: "He made him to be sin for us" (2 Cor 5:21). The bull burned outside the camp is one of the most explicit types of the cross in the Old Testament.

verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt also take one ram; and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאַיִל הָאֶחָד תִּקָּח וְסָמְכוּ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת־יְדֵיהֶם עַל־רֹאשׁ הָאָיִל׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'ayil ha'echad tiqqach vesamkhu Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil.
verses.study_note: The second sacrifice: the first ram, for the burnt offering (olah). Again, hands are laid on the head — the identification principle repeats. Unlike the sin offering (where the flesh was expelled), the burnt offering will be entirely consumed on the altar (v.18). The ram (ayil) is the animal of substitution — it was a ram that replaced Isaac on Mount Moriah (Gen 22:13). The laying of hands by all the priests (Aaron and his sons together) demonstrates corporate identification — the whole priestly family, the entire priesthood, is represented in this one sacrifice.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt slay the ram, and thou shalt take his blood, and sprinkle it round about upon the altar.
verses.original_text: וְשָׁחַטְתָּ אֶת־הָאָיִל וְלָקַחְתָּ אֶת־דָּמוֹ וְזָרַקְתָּ עַל־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב׃
verses.transliteration: Veshachateta et-ha'ayil velaqachta et-damo vezaraqta al-hammizbeach saviv.
verses.study_note: The ram is slaughtered and its blood is sprinkled (zaraq — thrown, dashed) "round about upon the altar" (al-hammizbeach saviv). Unlike the sin offering's blood (applied to the horns with a finger, v.12), the burnt offering's blood is thrown against the altar on all sides. The different application methods distinguish the offerings: the sin offering targets specific points (horns), while the burnt offering covers the entire altar. Zaraq implies force — the blood is hurled, not gently placed. The comprehensive coverage — "round about" (saviv) — ensures every side of the altar is marked with blood.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt cut the ram in pieces, and wash the inwards of him, and his legs, and put them unto his pieces, and unto his head.
verses.original_text: וְאֶת־הָאַיִל תְּנַתֵּחַ לִנְתָחָיו וְרָחַצְתָּ קִרְבּוֹ וּכְרָעָיו וְנָתַתָּ עַל־נְתָחָיו וְעַל־רֹאשׁוֹ׃
verses.transliteration: Ve'et-ha'ayil tenattach lintachav verachatzta qirbo ukhera'av venatatta al-netachav ve'al-rosho.
verses.study_note: The ram is cut into pieces (natach — to dismember, portion out). The internal organs and legs are washed — cleansed of any impurity before being offered to God. Then all the pieces are arranged together with the head. The dismembering and reassembling creates a picture of total examination: every part is separated, washed, and then reunited in offering. Nothing is hidden; every part is exposed, cleansed, and presented. This speaks to the thoroughness of consecration — God examines every dimension of the life offered to Him.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt burn the whole ram upon the altar: it is a burnt offering unto the LORD: it is a sweet savour, an offering made by fire unto the LORD.
verses.original_text: וְהִקְטַרְתָּ אֶת־כָּל־הָאַיִל הַמִּזְבֵּחָה עֹלָה הוּא לַיהוָה רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ אִשֵּׁה לַיהוָה הוּא׃
verses.transliteration: Vehiqtarta et-kol-ha'ayil hammizbechah olah hu laYHWH reiach nichoach ishsheh laYHWH hu.
verses.study_note: The entire ram — every piece — is burned on the altar. "Burnt offering" (olah — from alah, to go up) — it wholly "ascends" to God in smoke. Nothing is retained; nothing is eaten. This is the offering of total dedication: everything given, nothing held back. "A sweet savour" (reiach nichoach — literally "a soothing/restful aroma") — the ascending smoke is pleasing to God. This phrase first appears after Noah's sacrifice (Gen 8:21) and becomes the standard expression for an offering God accepts. "An offering made by fire" (ishsheh) — the fire transforms the physical sacrifice into ascending worship. The burnt offering represents the worshipper's total consecration to God.
verses.doctrinal_note: The burnt offering (olah) is the foundational sacrifice expressing total consecration — everything ascends to God, nothing is withheld. This is the offering Christ fulfilled supremely: "Christ also hath loved us, and hath given himself for us an offering and a sacrifice to God for a sweetsmelling savour" (Eph 5:2). Paul uses the exact Septuagint vocabulary of the olah (osme euodias — "sweet savour") to describe Christ's cross. The entire ram on the altar is a picture of Christ's total, unreserved self-offering to the Father.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt take the other ram; and Aaron and his sons shall put their hands upon the head of the ram.
verses.original_text: וְלָקַחְתָּ אֵת הָאַיִל הַשֵּׁנִי וְסָמַךְ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֶת־יְדֵיהֶם עַל־רֹאשׁ הָאָיִל׃
verses.transliteration: Velaqachta et ha'ayil hashsheni vesamakh Aharon uvanav et-yedeihem al-rosh ha'ayil.
